topics.title: Equilibrium and Steady State Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium and Steady State Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Semiconductor Physics**, a semiconductor is said to be in **Thermal Equilibrium** when there is no net flow of charge carriers (electrons or holes) and no net flow of energy across any arbitrary cross-section of the material. Mathematically, this requires three simultaneous conditions:

1. **Thermal Equilibrium** → Uniform temperature ($T$ = constant, no heat flow).
2. **Electrical Equilibrium** → No net current (uniform, time-invariant electric field).
3. **Chemical Equilibrium** → No net diffusion of carriers due to concentration gradients.

When all three conditions are satisfied, the semiconductor obeys a single, constant **Fermi Energy Level ($E_F$)** throughout its volume. The net rate of recombination equals the net rate of generation, i.e., $R = G$, ensuring the carrier concentrations remain stationary in time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Equilibrium and Steady State Conditions form the foundation for understanding carrier statistics, the p-n junction, and bipolar transistor operation. Questions on the difference between Equilibrium and Steady State, and the role of the single Fermi level, are **high-frequency** in KTU End Semester Examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics & Constants (Memorize these for KTU ESE):**
> - **Boltzmann Constant ($k$):** **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** (or **$8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K**)
> - **Thermal Voltage ($V_T$) at 300 K:** **$\approx 25.85$ mV** (commonly approximated as **$26$ mV** in KTU numericals)
> - **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration of Si at 300 K ($n_i$):** **$\approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$**
> - **Room Temperature ($T$):** **300 K**, giving $kT \approx 0.0259$ eV

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a perfectly still, rectangular **swimming pool** with water of uniform temperature. The water is completely calm, no waves, no inflow from taps, and no drain pulling water out. The water level is constant everywhere, and there is no net movement of water molecules in any direction. This pool is in **equilibrium**.

Now imagine a **slightly tilted rectangular tank** with water flowing in from a tap on the top-left side and draining out from a hole on the bottom-right side. As long as the inflow rate exactly matches the outflow rate, the water level at every point in the tank remains *constant in time*, even though water molecules are continuously moving through the tank. This is the **Steady State**.

- **Equilibrium = No flow at all** (pool analogy).
- **Steady State = Continuous, balanced flow with time-invariant profiles** (tilted tank analogy).

In a semiconductor at equilibrium, the **single Fermi level** is like the "still water level" — it is flat and uniform. When we apply a bias (light, voltage), the Fermi level splits into two **Quasi-Fermi Levels** ($E_{Fn}$ for electrons, $E_{Fp}$ for holes), but if the disturbance is time-invariant, we have reached a **Steady State**.

## 1.3 Equilibrium vs. Steady State — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Thermal Equilibrium | Steady State (Non-Equilibrium) |
|---|---|---|
| Net carrier flow | Zero | Non-zero, but **time-invariant** |
| Fermi level | **Single, constant $E_F$** | Split into $E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$ |
| Recombination vs Generation | $R = G$ (perfectly balanced) | $R \neq G$ locally, but $\partial n/\partial t = 0$ |
| Time dependence | $\partial n/\partial t = 0$, $\partial p/\partial t = 0$ | $\partial n/\partial t = 0$, $\partial p/\partial t = 0$ |
| Driving force | None | External bias, illumination, injection |
| Energy diagram | Flat $E_F$ line | Separation between $E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Subtle Trap:** In *both* equilibrium and steady state, the partial time derivatives of carrier densities are zero. This is why many KTU students incorrectly mark them as the same. The distinguishing feature is the **presence of net current flow and split quasi-Fermi levels** in the steady state.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions Defining Thermal Equilibrium in a Semiconductor

For a homogeneous semiconductor crystal (e.g., uniformly doped n-type or p-type Si) in complete isolation from external stimuli, the equilibrium conditions are:

### 2.1.1 The Three Equilibrium Sub-Conditions

1. **No Net Current Flow:** The total current density $\vec{J} = \vec{J}_n + \vec{J}_p$ must vanish everywhere. This requires the **drift current** (due to electric field) and **diffusion current** (due to concentration gradient) for each carrier type to **cancel exactly**:
$$\vec{J}_n = q n \mu_n \vec{E} + q D_n \nabla n = 0$$
$$\vec{J}_p = q p \mu_p \vec{E} - q D_p \nabla p = 0$$

2. **No Net Carrier Generation or Recombination:** Every electron-hole pair that is thermally generated is, on average, eventually recombined:
$$R = G \quad \Rightarrow \quad U_{net} = R - G = 0$$

3. **Constant Fermi Level:** The electrochemical potential ($\mu$) is uniform throughout the crystal. Since $\mu = E_F$ (with proper sign convention), the Fermi energy diagram is a **horizontal, flat line** independent of position $x$.

### 2.1.2 Why Is a Single $E_F$ Valid Only at Equilibrium?

The Fermi-Dirac distribution function $f(E)$ is derived under the assumption of thermal equilibrium. If carriers are being continuously injected or generated, the system is no longer in equilibrium, and the **Fermi-Dirac statistics break down** for the entire carrier population. However, the carriers *within their respective bands* still thermalize rapidly (on a picosecond timescale) and can be described by **separate quasi-equilibrium distributions** characterized by **Quasi-Fermi Levels** $E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$.

## 2.2 Steady State Condition — The Time-Invariant Non-Equilibrium

When an external perturbation (a forward bias voltage, optical illumination, or carrier injection from a contact) is applied, the system leaves equilibrium. However, if the perturbation is **constant in time** (DC), the carrier profile settles into a **steady state** where:

$$\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \text{at every point } x$$

This is **not** equilibrium because:
- The generation and recombination rates are **locally unequal** (excess carriers exist).
- A **net current** flows through the device.
- The **single Fermi level splits** into two quasi-Fermi levels.

### 2.2.1 Quasi-Fermi Levels (KTU's Most-Tested Concept)

Under non-equilibrium steady state, the electron and hole populations are each described by their own Fermi-Dirac-like distribution:

$$f_n(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_{Fn}}{kT}\right)}$$
$$f_p(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)}$$

The carrier concentrations under non-equilibrium become:
$$n = n_i \, \exp\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_i}{kT}\right)$$
$$p = n_i \, \exp\left(\frac{E_i - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Law of Mass Action Breaks Down in Steady State:** At equilibrium, $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ (a rigid constraint). Under non-equilibrium steady state with excess carriers $\delta n$ and $\delta p$, we have $np = n_i^2 \exp\left(\dfrac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right) > n_i^2$. This deviation is what drives recombination and current flow in devices like LEDs, photodiodes, and BJTs.

### 2.2.2 The Continuity Equation — The Mathematical Heart of Steady State

The **continuity equation** is the semiconductor master equation governing carrier dynamics. For holes in 1-D:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} - U_p + G_p$$

where $U_p = R - G$ is the **net recombination rate** and $G_p$ is the external generation rate (e.g., photogeneration).

Under **steady state** ($\partial p/\partial t = 0$):

$$\frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} = U_p - G_p$$

This is the equation KTU examiners love to see you derive and apply.

## 2.3 Net Recombination Rate (SRH Model)

The most commonly used recombination model in KTU problems is the **Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH)** model, which simplifies to a direct band-to-band recombination rate proportional to the deviation of $np$ from $n_i^2$:

$$U = R - G = \alpha_r (n p - n_i^2)$$

where $\alpha_r$ is the recombination coefficient.

Under **low-level injection** (excess minority carriers $\ll$ majority carriers), the minority carrier lifetime $\tau_p$ is related to $\alpha_r$ by:

$$\tau_p = \frac{1}{\alpha_r (n_0 + p_0)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an n-type semiconductor ($n_0 \gg p_0$), this reduces to $\tau_p \approx 1/(\alpha_r n_0)$, showing that heavy doping **reduces minority carrier lifetime** — a critical fact for BJT switching speed and solar cell design.

## 2.4 The Equilibrium Carrier Concentration Equations (Memorize These)

For a semiconductor with $E_F$ measured relative to intrinsic level $E_i$:

$$n_0 = n_i \, \exp\left(\frac{E_F - E_i}{kT}\right)$$
$$p_0 = n_i \, \exp\left(\frac{E_i - E_F}{kT}\right)$$

Multiplying these gives the **Law of Mass Action**:
$$n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Applicable Condition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | All temperatures | Volts (V) |
| Equilibrium Electron Density | $n_0 = n_i \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_F - E_i}{kT}\right)$ | Thermal Equilibrium | cm$^{-3}$ |
| Equilibrium Hole Density | $p_0 = n_i \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_i - E_F}{kT}\right)$ | Thermal Equilibrium | cm$^{-3}$ |
| Law of Mass Action | $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ | Thermal Equilibrium | cm$^{-6}$ |
| Net Recombination Rate | $U = \alpha_r (n p - n_i^2)$ | Non-Equilibrium | cm$^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$ |
| Minority Carrier Lifetime | $\tau_p = \dfrac{1}{\alpha_r (n_0 + p_0)}$ | Low-Level Injection | seconds (s) |
| Continuity Equation (Holes) | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{q}\dfrac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} - U + G$ | General | cm$^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$ |
| Steady State Condition | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0$ | Time-Invariant Bias | — |
| Minority Diffusion Length | $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ | Low-Level Injection | cm |
| Einstein Relation | $\dfrac{D}{\mu} = \dfrac{kT}{q} = V_T$ | Non-degenerate SC | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The equilibrium/steady-state framework is what allows us to design **diodes (p-n junction)**, **transistors (BJT, MOSFET)**, **solar cells**, **photodetectors**, and **LEDs**. The p-n junction itself is constructed by joining an n-region and a p-region, then letting the system reach thermal equilibrium — a single Fermi level flattens across the junction, creating the built-in potential. Without understanding equilibrium, you cannot understand any active semiconductor device.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minority Carrier Steady State Profile in an n-type Bar with Injection at $x = 0$

This is the **classic KTU problem**: An infinite n-type semiconductor bar has excess holes injected at $x = 0$ at a rate $p(0) = p_{n0} + \Delta p_0$, and the steady-state profile far away is $p(\infty) = p_{n0}$. We assume **low-level injection** and that the diffusion approximation dominates (no electric field in the neutral region).

### Step 1: State the Continuity Equation for Excess Holes in the n-region

The hole current density in the neutral n-region is purely diffusive (no significant field, charge neutrality enforced):
$$J_p = -q D_p \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

Substituting into the hole continuity equation with no external generation ($G = 0$) and net recombination $U = \frac{\Delta p}{\tau_p}$ (under low-level injection, the net recombination rate is simply proportional to excess carrier density divided by lifetime):

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} - \frac{\Delta p}{\tau_p}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Diffusion Current

Computing the spatial derivative of $J_p$:
$$\frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-q D_p \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = -D_p \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2}$$

Therefore, the continuity equation becomes:
$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -D_p \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\Delta p}{\tau_p}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Steady State Condition

In steady state, $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0$. Also, $p = p_{n0} + \Delta p$, so $\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{\partial^2 (\Delta p)}{\partial x^2}$ since $p_{n0}$ is a constant. The equation simplifies to:

$$0 = -D_p \frac{\partial^2 (\Delta p)}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\Delta p}{\tau_p}$$

Rearranging:
$$\frac{\partial^2 (\Delta p)}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\Delta p}{D_p \tau_p}$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Diffusion Length

Define the **minority carrier diffusion length** as $L_p \equiv \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$. The equation becomes:

$$\frac{\partial^2 (\Delta p)}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\Delta p}{L_p^2}$$

### Step 5: Solve the Second-Order ODE

This is a standard linear ODE with constant coefficients. The general solution is:
$$\Delta p(x) = A \exp\!\left(\frac{x}{L_p}\right) + B \exp\!\left(-\frac{x}{L_p}\right)$$

### Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions

**Boundary Condition 1:** At $x \to \infty$, $\Delta p \to 0$ (carriers recombine before reaching infinity). This forces $A = 0$ (since $\exp(x/L_p)$ would diverge).

**Boundary Condition 2:** At $x = 0$, $\Delta p(0) = \Delta p_0$ (the injected excess concentration). This gives $B = \Delta p_0$.

### Step 7: Final Steady State Solution

$$\boxed{\Delta p(x) = \Delta p_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{x}{L_p}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Excess minority holes decay **exponentially** from the injection point, with characteristic length $L_p$. The carriers have a "diffusion reach" of about $L_p$ before they recombine. This derivation is the foundation of the **minority carrier diffusion current** in a p-n junction under forward bias.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Continuity Equation Starting from First Principles

This derivation shows that the continuity equation is essentially a **conservation of charge** statement.

### Step 1: Consider a Volume Element of Cross-Section $A$ and Width $dx$

The rate of increase of hole charge inside the volume must equal the rate of hole current entering minus the rate of hole current leaving, plus internal generation, minus internal recombination.

### Step 2: Net Hole Current Flowing Out

The hole current density is $J_p$. The net current leaving the volume element is:
$$I_{out} = A \cdot J_p(x + dx) - A \cdot J_p(x) = A \frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} dx$$

(Note: hole current *leaving* has the sign convention as written.)

### Step 3: Continuity Equation in Differential Form

The rate of hole accumulation per unit volume is:
$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} + (G_p - R_p)$$

The factor $1/q$ converts current density (charge/area-time) to particle flux density (particles/area-time). The negative sign indicates that **outward** current causes **decrease** in concentration.

### Step 4: Final Form

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} + G_p - R_p}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Equilibrium vs. Steady State vs. Transient

| Parameter | Thermal Equilibrium | Steady State (Non-Equilibrium DC) | Transient (Time-Varying) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\partial p / \partial t$ | $= 0$ | $= 0$ | $\neq 0$ |
| Net Current $J$ | $= 0$ | $\neq 0$, constant in time | $\neq 0$, time-varying |
| $E_F$ picture | Single flat $E_F$ | $E_{Fn} \neq E_{Fp}$, but time-invariant | $E_{Fn}(t), E_{Fp}(t)$ vary with time |
| $np$ product | $= n_i^2$ | $> n_i^2$ (with injection) | $> n_i^2$ (varying) |
| $\Delta p(x, t)$ | $= 0$ | $\Delta p_0 \exp(-x/L_p)$ | $\Delta p(x,t)$ has time dependence |
| Recombination $U$ | $U = 0$ | $U = \Delta p / \tau_p$ | $U = \Delta p(t) / \tau_p$ |
| Governing equation | $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ | $\nabla^2 (\Delta p) = \Delta p / L_p^2$ | $D \nabla^2 (\Delta p) - \Delta p/\tau_p = \partial(\Delta p)/\partial t$ |

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Relationship Between Minority Carrier Lifetime and Recombination Coefficient

Starting from the SRH recombination rate under low-level injection in an n-type material:
$$U = \alpha_r (n p - n_i^2) = \alpha_r (n_0 \cdot \Delta p + p_0 \cdot \Delta n + \Delta n \cdot \Delta p)$$

Under low-level injection, $\Delta n, \Delta p \ll n_0$ for an n-type semiconductor, so $\Delta n \cdot \Delta p$ is second-order small and can be neglected. Also, charge neutrality requires $\Delta n \approx \Delta p$:

$$U \approx \alpha_r (n_0 + p_0) \Delta p$$

Defining the **minority carrier lifetime** $\tau_p$ such that $U = \Delta p / \tau_p$:
$$\frac{1}{\tau_p} = \alpha_r (n_0 + p_0)$$

For an **n-type** semiconductor with $n_0 \gg p_0$:
$$\boxed{\tau_p \approx \frac{1}{\alpha_r n_0}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** The lifetime is inversely proportional to the doping concentration. Doubling the doping halves the minority carrier lifetime. This is why heavily doped emitter regions in BJTs have very short minority carrier lifetimes, requiring thin emitter designs for high-speed transistors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Band Diagram: Equilibrium vs. Steady State

This diagram contrasts the band structure and Fermi level positions in the two regimes for a generic semiconductor.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Conduction Band Edge Ec"]:::band
    nodeB["Intrinsic Level Ei"]:::level
    nodeC["Fermi Level EF at Equilibrium"]:::fermiEq
    nodeD["Valence Band Edge Ev"]:::band
    nodeE["Quasi Fermi Level for Electrons EFn"]:::fermiNon
    nodeF["Quasi Fermi Level for Holes EFp"]:::fermiNon
    nodeG["Ec (Steady State, with EF splitting)"]:::band
    nodeH["EFn shift indicates electron population"]:::level
    nodeI["EFp shift indicates hole population"]:::level

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeC --- nodeD

    nodeG --- nodeH
    nodeH --- nodeI

    subgraph EQUIL["THERMAL EQUILIBRIUM"]
        nodeA
        nodeB
        nodeC
        nodeD
    end

    subgraph STEADY["STEADY STATE NON EQUILIBRIUM"]
        nodeG
        nodeH
        nodeI
    end

    classDef band fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#2C5282,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A365D
    classDef level fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:1px,color:#744210
    classDef fermiEq fill:#C6F6D5,stroke:#22543D,stroke-width:3px,color:#22543D
    classDef fermiNon fill:#FED7D7,stroke:#9B2C2C,stroke-width:2px,color:#742A2A
```

## 4.2 Carrier Distribution Profile Under Steady State Injection

This diagram shows the spatial distribution of excess minority carriers (holes in an n-type bar) when an injection is applied at $x = 0$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["Injection Source at x equals 0"]:::source
    node2["Excess Holes Delta p at 0 equals Delta p0"]:::peak
    node3["Decay Region 0 to Lp"]:::decay
    node4["Tail Region beyond Lp"]:::tail
    node5["Equilibrium Level pn0 reached as x approaches infinity"]:::baseline

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5

    classDef source fill:#BEE3F8,stroke:#2A4365,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A365D
    classDef peak fill:#FBD38D,stroke:#7B341E,stroke-width:3px,color:#7B341E
    classDef decay fill:#FEFCBF,stroke:#975A16,stroke-width:2px,color:#744210
    classDef tail fill:#C6F6D5,stroke:#22543D,stroke-width:1px,color:#22543D
    classDef baseline fill:#E2E8F0,stroke:#2D3748,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A202C
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow: From Perturbation to Steady State

This block-level architecture shows the temporal evolution of a semiconductor from equilibrium through perturbation to steady state.

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Semiconductor at Thermal Equilibrium"]:::block
    stepB["External Perturbation Applied, Bias or Light"]:::block
    stepC["Carriers Excited, Generation Exceeds Recombination"]:::block
    stepD["Excess Carrier Density Delta p t increases"]:::block
    stepE["Recombination Rate R increases to Match G"]:::block
    stepF["Steady State Reached, Delta p constant in time"]:::block
    stepG["EF splits into EFn and EFp constant in time"]:::block

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG

    classDef block fill:#E6FFFA,stroke:#234E52,stroke-width:2px,color:#1D4044
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the equilibrium case, observe that the Fermi level $E_F$ is a single horizontal line. In the steady state case, the quasi-Fermi levels $E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$ are separated, indicating non-equilibrium carrier populations, but the structure is **time-invariant**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define thermal equilibrium in a semiconductor. List the three necessary conditions for a semiconductor to be in thermal equilibrium.**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal equilibrium in a semiconductor is the state in which there is no net flow of charge carriers, energy, or particles across any cross-section of the material. The three necessary conditions are:

1. **Thermal Equilibrium:** The temperature $T$ is uniform throughout the crystal (no heat flow, $\nabla T = 0$).
2. **Electrical Equilibrium:** No net current flows ($\vec{J} = \vec{J}_n + \vec{J}_p = 0$), which means the drift and diffusion components cancel for each carrier type.
3. **Chemical Equilibrium:** No net diffusion of carriers due to concentration gradients, meaning the carrier concentration is uniform in the absence of doping gradients, or the chemical potential is constant.

Under these conditions, the semiconductor has a single, constant Fermi level $E_F$ throughout its volume, and the net recombination rate equals the thermal generation rate, $R = G$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between thermal equilibrium and steady state condition in a semiconductor. Why does the Fermi level split in steady state?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Thermal Equilibrium | Steady State |
|---|---|---|
| Net current | Zero | Non-zero, but constant in time |
| Fermi level | Single, flat $E_F$ | Split into $E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$ |
| Time derivative | $\partial p/\partial t = 0$ with no excess | $\partial p/\partial t = 0$ with excess $\Delta p$ |

The **single Fermi level splits** into two quasi-Fermi levels in steady state because the system is no longer in true thermal equilibrium. The external perturbation (bias, light) drives the electron and hole populations *away* from a common Fermi-Dirac distribution. However, each carrier population still thermalizes rapidly *within its own band*, allowing the use of **separate quasi-Fermi levels** $E_{Fn}$ (for electrons) and $E_{Fp}$ (for holes). The splitting $(E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})$ is a direct measure of how far the system is driven from equilibrium.

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the steady-state continuity equation for minority carrier holes in an n-type semiconductor bar. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon n-type sample at 300 K has doping $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$, hole diffusion coefficient $D_p = 10$ cm$^2$/s, and minority carrier lifetime $\tau_p = 10^{-6}$ s. Excess holes are injected at $x = 0$ with $\Delta p(0) = 10^{12}$ cm$^{-3}$. Find the steady-state excess hole concentration $\Delta p$ at $x = 25$ $\mu$m. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

The hole continuity equation in one dimension is:
$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} + G_p - R_p$$

In the **neutral n-region**, we assume:
1. **Low-level injection:** $\Delta p \ll n_0$ (excess minority carriers are much smaller than majority).
2. **Charge neutrality:** $\Delta n \approx \Delta p$.
3. **No external generation:** $G_p = 0$ (no light, only electrical injection).
4. **No significant electric field in the neutral region** (built-in field is screened by majority carriers).

The hole current is purely diffusive:
$$J_p = -q D_p \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

Substituting into the continuity equation:
$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = D_p \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\Delta p}{\tau_p}$$

For steady state, $\partial p/\partial t = 0$, and using $\Delta p = p - p_{n0}$ (where $p_{n0}$ is the equilibrium minority carrier density):
$$\frac{d^2 (\Delta p)}{dx^2} = \frac{\Delta p}{D_p \tau_p} = \frac{\Delta p}{L_p^2}$$

where $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ is the **minority carrier diffusion length**.

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate the diffusion length.**
$$L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p} = \sqrt{10 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{10^{-5}} = 3.162 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm} = 31.62 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2: Convert $x = 25$ $\mu$m to cm.**
$$x = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Apply the steady-state solution.**

The steady-state excess minority carrier profile is:
$$\Delta p(x) = \Delta p(0) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{x}{L_p}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**
$$\Delta p(25 \text{ }\mu\text{m}) = 10^{12} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3.162 \times 10^{-3}}\right)$$

$$\Delta p(25 \text{ }\mu\text{m}) = 10^{12} \cdot \exp(-0.7906) = 10^{12} \cdot 0.4538$$

$$\boxed{\Delta p(25 \text{ }\mu\text{m}) \approx 4.538 \times 10^{11} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Define quasi-Fermi levels. Derive the expressions for electron and hole concentrations under non-equilibrium steady state using quasi-Fermi levels. Show that the $np$ product deviates from $n_i^2$ in steady state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs sample at 300 K has $n_i = 1.8 \times 10^6$ cm$^{-3}$. Under illumination, the steady-state excess carrier density is $\Delta n = \Delta p = 10^8$ cm$^{-3}$. If the doping is p-type with $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$, calculate the separation between the quasi-Fermi levels $(E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

When a semiconductor is driven out of equilibrium (e.g., by applying a bias or by optical generation), the strict Fermi-Dirac distribution function $f(E)$ is no longer valid for the entire carrier population. However, electrons *within the conduction band* and holes *within the valence band* still thermalize among themselves on a picosecond timescale. This allows us to define:

- **Electron Quasi-Fermi Level $E_{Fn}$:** Describes the electron population in the conduction band.
- **Hole Quasi-Fermi Level $E_{Fp}$:** Describes the hole population in the valence band.

The carrier concentrations under non-equilibrium steady state are:
$$n = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_i}{kT}\right)$$
$$p = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_i - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

Multiplying these:
$$np = n_i^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

Since $E_{Fn} \neq E_{Fp}$ in non-equilibrium, the exponent is non-zero, and:
$$np \neq n_i^2 \quad \text{(unless equilibrium when } E_{Fn} = E_{Fp} = E_F\text{)}$$

This is the mathematical origin of the deviation from the law of mass action, which drives the recombination current in forward-biased diodes.

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the equilibrium concentrations.**

For a p-type sample at 300 K, $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3} \gg n_i$. Using $p_0 \approx N_A = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$ and the law of mass action:
$$n_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{p_0} = \frac{(1.8 \times 10^6)^2}{10^{16}} = \frac{3.24 \times 10^{12}}{10^{16}} = 3.24 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the non-equilibrium concentrations under illumination.**

The total carrier densities are:
$$n = n_0 + \Delta n \approx \Delta n = 10^8 \text{ cm}^{-3}$$
$$p = p_0 + \Delta p = 10^{16} + 10^8 \approx 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3} \quad (\text{since } \Delta p \ll p_0)$$

**Step 3: Apply the $np$ product relation.**

$$np = n_i^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

$$\left(10^8\right) \left(10^{16}\right) = \left(1.8 \times 10^6\right)^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

$$10^{24} = 3.24 \times 10^{12} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 4: Solve for the quasi-Fermi level separation.**

$$\exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right) = \frac{10^{24}}{3.24 \times 10^{12}} = 3.086 \times 10^{11}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:
$$\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT} = \ln(3.086 \times 10^{11}) = \ln(3.086) + 11 \ln(10) = 1.126 + 25.328 = 26.454$$

At 300 K, $kT = 0.0259$ eV, so:
$$E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = 26.454 \times 0.0259 \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} \approx 0.685 \text{ eV}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Equilibrium with Steady State:** Many students write $\partial p / \partial t = 0$ for both cases and conclude they are the same. **Always explicitly mention** that steady state has $\partial p/\partial t = 0$ *with* non-zero net current and split quasi-Fermi levels.
> 2. **Mixing Up the Sign in the Continuity Equation:** The continuity equation for holes has the term $-\frac{1}{q}\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x}$. A sign error here propagates through the entire derivation.
> 3. **Forgetting the Low-Level Injection Assumption:** When applying $\tau_p$ relations, you **must** state the low-level injection condition ($\Delta p \ll n_0$) explicitly, or the answer is marked down.
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Units in Numericals:** $D_p$ is in cm²/s, $\tau_p$ in seconds, distances in cm. Forgetting to convert micrometers to cm gives a wildly wrong answer.
> 5. **Wrong Quasi-Fermi Level Order:** For an n-type material under injection, $E_{Fn}$ moves closer to $E_c$ and $E_{Fp}$ moves closer to $E_v$. The *separation* $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}$ is the key quantity. State both directions clearly in diagrams.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**
>
> - **Thermal Equilibrium** = No current + No net $R/G$ imbalance + Single constant $E_F$.
> - **Steady State** = Constant in time ($\partial n/\partial t = \partial p/\partial t = 0$) but **not** in equilibrium (split quasi-Fermi levels, $J \neq 0$).
> - **Transient** = $\partial n/\partial t \neq 0$, time-varying perturbation.
> - **Continuity Equation (Holes):** $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{q}\dfrac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} - U + G$
> - **Diffusion Current for Holes:** $J_p = -q D_p \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}$
> - **Steady-State Minority Carrier Profile:** $\Delta p(x) = \Delta p_0 \exp(-x / L_p)$
> - **Diffusion Length:** $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$, $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$
> - **Einstein Relation:** $D / \mu = V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K.
> - **Law of Mass Action (Equilibrium Only):** $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$
> - **Non-Equilibrium $np$ Product:** $np = n_i^2 \exp[(E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/kT]$
> - **Net Recombination:** $U = \alpha_r (np - n_i^2) = \Delta p / \tau_p$ (under low-level injection).
> - **Minority Lifetime vs. Doping:** $\tau_p \approx 1/(\alpha_r n_0)$ — heavier doping → shorter lifetime.
> - **Standard Numerical Values at 300 K:** $V_T = 0.0259$ V, $n_i(\text{Si}) = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$, $n_i(\text{GaAs}) = 1.8 \times 10^6$ cm$^{-3}$.
> - **Single Fermi Level ⇔ Equilibrium; Split Quasi-Fermi Levels ⇔ Non-Equilibrium (Steady State or Transient).**
> - **KTU Examiner's Pet Question:** "Why is $np \neq n_i^2$ under illumination?" → Answer: Because the system is in non-equilibrium, and the electron and hole populations follow separate quasi-equilibrium distributions characterized by $E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Effective Mass and Fermi Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Effective Mass and Fermi Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Effective Mass — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Effective Mass ($m^*$)** is a quantity assigned to an electron (or hole) moving inside a crystal lattice such that the classical equation of motion $\vec{F} = m^*\vec{a}$ holds true when the electron is subjected to an external force $\vec{F}$. It accounts for the influence of the periodic crystal potential on the carrier's dynamics, and is mathematically defined by the second derivative of the carrier energy $E$ with respect to its wave vector $\vec{k}$.

Mathematically, the scalar effective mass in a given crystallographic direction is expressed as:

$$m^* = \frac{\hbar^2}{\left(\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right)}$$

where $\hbar$ is the **reduced Planck's constant** ($1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s), $E$ is the total carrier energy, and $k$ is the wave vector along the direction of motion.

### 1.2 Fermi Level — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> The **Fermi Level ($E_F$)** is defined as the energy level in a material at which the probability of finding an electron is exactly **50% (i.e., 1/2)** at any temperature above absolute zero. Equivalently, at $T = 0$ K, the Fermi level represents the highest occupied energy state (the boundary between fully-filled and completely-empty states).

It serves as the **reference energy** in the Fermi-Dirac probability distribution:

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T}}$$

where $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant** ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for the Student

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — Effective Mass**
> Imagine a person trying to walk through a crowded market. Even if they are slim and light, the crowd, the vendors' stalls, and the narrow lanes force them to weave, slow down, and change direction. From outside, it appears as if the person has become "heavier" or "slower." Similarly, an electron — though intrinsically having a tiny mass $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg — appears to behave as if it has a heavier or lighter mass because of the periodic potential of the crystal lattice atoms. This apparent mass is the **effective mass**.

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — Fermi Level**
> Think of a multi-storey building where each floor is an "energy level" and rooms are quantum states. At $T = 0$ K, every room below a certain floor (the Fermi level) is fully occupied by tenants (electrons), and every floor above is completely vacant. At higher temperatures, a few tenants move upstairs, leaving some empty rooms below — the Fermi level acts as the **statistical "watershed"** dividing the occupied and unoccupied regions.

### 1.4 Physical Constants at a Glance

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
|--------|----------|-------|
| $m_0$ | Free electron rest mass | $9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg |
| $\hbar$ | Reduced Planck's constant | $1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| $q$ | Electronic charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Effective Mass — A Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The concept of effective mass emerges naturally from **Bloch's Theorem**, which states that an electron in a periodic potential has a wavefunction of the form:

$$\psi_k(\vec{r}) = u_k(\vec{r}) \, e^{i\vec{k}\cdot\vec{r}}$$

**Step 1 — Energy-Wave Vector Relationship ($E$–$k$ curve):**
Inside a crystal, the parabolic free-electron relation $E = \hbar^2 k^2 / 2m_0$ is **no longer valid**. The periodic potential perturbs this into a complex $E$–$k$ relationship.

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law in a Crystal:**
Applying an external force $\vec{F}_{ext}$ to the electron, the rate of change of crystal momentum is:

$$\vec{F}_{ext} = \hbar \, \frac{d\vec{k}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Group Velocity of a Wave Packet:**
The velocity of the electron wave packet (group velocity) is:

$$\vec{v}_g = \frac{1}{\hbar} \nabla_k E(\vec{k})$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration from Differentiating Velocity:**

$$\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}_g}{dt} = \frac{1}{\hbar} \, \frac{d}{dt}\left(\nabla_k E\right) = \frac{1}{\hbar} \, \nabla_k \left(\frac{dE}{dt}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Using Energy Conservation:**
Since $dE/dt = \vec{F}_{ext} \cdot \vec{v}_g$, we get:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{1}{\hbar^2} \, \nabla_k \nabla_k E \cdot \vec{F}_{ext}$$

**Step 6 — Identification of Effective Mass Tensor:**
This yields the **effective mass tensor** $\left(\dfrac{1}{m^*}\right)_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{\hbar^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 E}{\partial k_i \partial k_j}$, confirming that effective mass is essentially the **curvature of the $E$–$k$ band**.

> [!NOTE] **The 'Why' Behind Curvature:**
> - A **wide band** (gentle curvature) → electrons behave as if they have a **large effective mass** (heavy).
> - A **narrow band** (steep curvature) → electrons behave as if they have a **small effective mass** (light).
> - A band curving **downward** (concave) → yields a **negative effective mass**, which is the hallmark of holes near the valence band maximum.

### 2.2 The Fermi-Dirac Distribution — Logical Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Classical (Maxwell-Boltzmann) Approximation:**
Classically, particles are distinguishable. The probability follows $P(E) \propto e^{-E/k_B T}$.

**Step 2 — Quantum (Fermi-Dirac) Reality:**
Electrons are **indistinguishable fermions** obeying the **Pauli Exclusion Principle** — no two electrons can occupy the same quantum state.

**Step 3 — Statistical Derivation (Grand Canonical Ensemble):**
Counting allowed microstates with the Pauli constraint leads to the probability that a state of energy $E$ is occupied:

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T}}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting Cases:**
- At **$T = 0$ K**: $f(E) = 1$ for $E < E_F$; $f(E) = 0$ for $E > E_F$.
- At **$T \to \infty$**: $f(E) \to 1/2$ for all energies (approaches classical behaviour).
- For $E - E_F \gg k_B T$: $f(E) \approx e^{-(E-E_F)/k_B T}$ (Boltzmann tail).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Equations — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $m^* = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{d^2E/dk^2}$ | Scalar effective mass at band extremum |
| 2 | $v_g = \dfrac{1}{\hbar} \dfrac{dE}{dk}$ | Group velocity of electron wave packet |
| 3 | $a = \dfrac{F}{m^*}$ | Equation of motion using $m^*$ |
| 4 | $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{(E-E_F)/k_BT}}$ | Fermi-Dirac distribution function |
| 5 | $E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_C}{n}\right)$ | $E_F$ for n-type semiconductor |
| 6 | $E_F = E_V + k_B T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_V}{p}\right)$ | $E_F$ for p-type semiconductor |
| 7 | $E_i = \dfrac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \dfrac{3k_BT}{4}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$ | Intrinsic Fermi level (near midgap) |
| 8 | $\vec{k}_F = (3\pi^2 n)^{1/3}$ | Fermi wave vector for electron density $n$ |
| 9 | $E_F = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}(3\pi^2 n)^{2/3}$ | Fermi energy at $T = 0$ K for free electron gas |

> **Engineering Utility:** Effective mass directly determines **mobility ($\mu = q\tau/m^*$)**, which governs **switching speed** of CMOS transistors, **gain** of HEMTs, and **responsivity** of photodetectors. Fermi level positioning controls the **band alignment** in p-n junctions, MOS structures, and heterojunctions — the foundation of all semiconductor device physics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Effective Mass from the $E$–$k$ Relation

We begin by considering an electron near the **bottom of the conduction band**, where $E$ as a function of $k$ can be Taylor-expanded about the minimum ($k = 0$).

**Step 1 — Taylor Expansion of $E(k)$ about the band minimum:**

$$E(k) = E(0) + \left.\frac{dE}{dk}\right|_{k=0} k + \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{d^2E}{dk^2}\right|_{k=0} k^2 + \frac{1}{6}\left.\frac{d^3E}{dk^3}\right|_{k=0} k^3 + \dots$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
At the band extremum ($k = 0$):
- $\left.\dfrac{dE}{dk}\right|_{k=0} = 0$ (slope is zero at minimum/maximum).

Therefore, the expansion simplifies to:

$$E(k) = E(0) + \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{d^2E}{dk^2}\right|_{k=0} k^2 + \frac{1}{6}\left.\frac{d^3E}{dk^3}\right|_{k=0} k^3 + \dots$$

**Step 3 — Neglect Higher-Order Terms Near Band Edge:**
For small $k$ (close to band minimum), the $k^3$ and higher terms become negligible compared to $k^2$. Hence:

$$E(k) \approx E(0) + \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{d^2E}{dk^2}\right|_{k=0} k^2$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Curvature Term:**
Defining $\alpha = \left.\dfrac{d^2E}{dk^2}\right|_{k=0}$, we have:

$$E(k) = E(0) + \frac{1}{2}\alpha k^2$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Free-Electron Form:**
The free-electron dispersion is:

$$E_{free}(k) = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m_0}$$

**Step 6 — Analogy Identification:**
By direct analogy between $E(k) = E(0) + \dfrac{1}{2}\alpha k^2$ and $E_{free}(k) = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m_0}k^2$:

$$\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*} = \frac{\alpha}{2} \implies \alpha = \frac{\hbar^2}{m^*}$$

**Step 7 — Final Effective Mass Expression:**

$$\boxed{\,m^* = \frac{\hbar^2}{\left(\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right)}\,}$$

**Step 8 — Sign Analysis:**
- For **conduction band minimum** (concave up): $\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2} > 0 \implies m^* > 0$ → electrons.
- For **valence band maximum** (concave down): $\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2} < 0 \implies m^* < 0$ → "negative mass electrons", but these are **physically re-interpreted as positively-charged holes** with positive effective mass $m_h^*$.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Position of Fermi Level in an Intrinsic Semiconductor

For an intrinsic semiconductor with electron concentration $n_i$, the Fermi level $E_i$ is found from the condition $n = p = n_i$.

**Step 1 — Effective Density of States in Conduction Band:**

$$N_C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}, \quad N_V = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

**Step 2 — Carrier Concentrations Using Boltzmann Approximation:**

$$n = N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_B T}, \quad p = N_V \, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_B T}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Intrinsic Condition $n = p$:**

$$N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_B T} = N_V \, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_B T}$$

**Step 4 — Take Natural Logarithm:**

$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to Solve for $E_F \equiv E_i$:**

$$\frac{2E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \ln N_C + \frac{E_C + E_V}{k_B T}$$

$$E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Expressions for $N_C$ and $N_V$:**

$$\frac{N_V}{N_C} = \left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)^{3/2}$$

**Step 7 — Final Intrinsic Fermi Level:**

$$\boxed{\,E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3k_B T}{4}\ln\!\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE] **Physical Interpretation:**
> If $m_h^* = m_e^*$, then $E_i$ lies exactly at midgap. If $m_h^* > m_e^*$, then $E_i$ shifts upward (closer to $E_C$); if $m_h^* < m_e^*$, then $E_i$ shifts downward (closer to $E_V$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Fermi Level for n-type Semiconductor

**Step 1 — For an n-type material with donor concentration $N_D$ and $n \approx N_D$:**

$$n = N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_B T} = N_D$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $E_F$:**

$$E_C - E_F = k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Final Expression:**

$$\boxed{\,E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)\,}$$

> **Note:** At $T = 0$ K, $E_F$ lies **between $E_C$ and the donor level $E_D$** (just below $E_C$ for heavy doping).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Effective Mass from $E$–$k$ Diagram Curvature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crystal Lattice with Periodic Potential] --> B[Electron Wavefunction obeys Bloch Theorem]
    B --> C[Energy Wave Vector E vs k Relation]
    C --> D{Evaluate Curvature d2E over dk2}
    D -->|Positive Curvature| E[Conduction Band Minimum]
    E --> F[Positive Effective Mass for Electron]
    F --> G[Electron Accelerates Opposite to Force]
    D -->|Negative Curvature| H[Valence Band Maximum]
    H --> I[Negative Effective Mass for Electron]
    I --> J[Reinterpreted as Positive Hole Mass]
    J --> K[Hole Accelerates in Force Direction]
    D -->|Zero Curvature| L[Inflection Point]
    L --> M[Infinite Effective Mass Carrier Stationary]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Fermi Level Behaviour Across Temperatures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T0[T = 0 K]
        A1[States below EF] -->|Fully Filled| A2[f E = 1]
        A3[States above EF] -->|Completely Empty| A4[f E = 0]
    end
    subgraph Thigh[T greater than 0 K]
        B1[Electrons near EF] -->|Thermal Excitation| B2[Smear by width kBT]
        B3[f E transitions] --> B4[Smooth curve from 1 to 0]
    end
    subgraph TS[T very large]
        C1[All states nearly half filled] --> C2[f E tends to 0.5]
    end
    T0 --> Thigh --> TS
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Fermi Level Position in Different Semiconductors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INT[Intrinsic Semiconductor]
        I1[Conduction Band EC] --- I2[Intrinsic Fermi Level Ei near midgap]
        I2 --- I3[Valence Band EV]
    end
    subgraph NTYPE[n type Semiconductor]
        N1[EC] --- N2[EF close to EC]
        N2 --- N3[Donor Level ED just below EC]
        N3 --- N4[EV]
    end
    subgraph PTYPE[p type Semiconductor]
        P1[EC] --- P2[Acceptor Level EA just above EV]
        P2 --- P3[EF close to EV]
        P3 --- P4[EV]
    end
    INT --> NTYPE
    INT --> PTYPE
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: Effect of Curvature on Effective Mass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Wide Parabola: Gentle Curvature] --> A1[Small d2E over dk2]
    A1 --> A2[LARGE Effective Mass: Heavy Electron]
    B0[Narrow Parabola: Steep Curvature] --> B1[Large d2E over dk2]
    B1 --> B2[SMALL Effective Mass: Light Electron]
    C0[Inverted Parabola at Valence Top] --> C1[Negative d2E over dk2]
    C1 --> C2[NEGATIVE Effective Mass]
    C2 --> C3[Hole Description: Positive Charge, Positive Mass]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading Guide:** These block diagrams serve as functional mappings of the underlying physics. For an exact sketch of the $E$–$k$ parabola, students are advised to reproduce the figure from Pierret's *Semiconductor Device Fundamentals* or Neamen's *Semiconductor Physics and Devices* by hand during revision.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define effective mass of an electron in a semiconductor. How is it related to the $E$–$k$ diagram?

**Model Answer:**

**Effective Mass Definition:**
Effective mass ($m^*$) is a quantity that describes the response of an electron (or hole) moving inside a crystal lattice to an applied external force, taking into account the influence of the periodic potential of the crystal. It is not the actual mass of the electron, but an *apparent* mass that incorporates all the crystal field effects.

**Relation to $E$–$k$ Diagram:**
The effective mass is inversely proportional to the **curvature** of the energy band in the $E$–$k$ diagram:

$$m^* = \frac{\hbar^2}{\left(\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right)}$$

- At the **conduction band minimum** (concave-up curve), $\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2} > 0$, yielding a positive $m_e^*$.
- At the **valence band maximum** (concave-down curve), $\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2} < 0$, giving a negative $m^*$ which is reinterpreted as a positively-charged hole with positive mass $m_h^*$.

A steep curvature means a small effective mass (light carrier); a gentle curvature means a large effective mass (heavy carrier). **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the Fermi level? Explain its physical significance with the help of the Fermi-Dirac distribution function.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The **Fermi Level ($E_F$)** is the energy level at which the probability of occupation by an electron is exactly **50%** at any temperature $T > 0$ K. At $T = 0$ K, it represents the highest occupied energy state — the boundary between completely-filled and completely-empty states.

**Fermi-Dirac Distribution:**

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T}}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It serves as the **reference energy** for all carrier statistics in semiconductors.
- It determines the relative occupancy of states near the band edges and controls the **band bending** at junctions.
- For an **intrinsic semiconductor**, $E_F$ lies near the middle of the bandgap.
- For **n-type**, $E_F$ lies close to $E_C$; for **p-type**, $E_F$ lies close to $E_V$.
- Its position is critical for understanding the operation of p-n junctions, MOSFETs, Schottky diodes, and heterojunction devices. **(3 marks)**

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the effective mass of an electron in a crystal from the $E$–$k$ relationship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electron in silicon has an effective mass $m^* = 0.26 \, m_0$. Calculate (i) the effective density of states in the conduction band $N_C$ at 300 K, and (ii) the thermal velocity of the electron. **(Given: $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $k_B T = 0.0259$ eV at 300 K, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.) **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Effective Mass:**

Consider an electron near the conduction band minimum. The energy $E$ is a function of wave vector $k$. Expanding $E(k)$ as a Taylor series about $k = 0$ (band minimum):

$$E(k) = E(0) + \left.\frac{dE}{dk}\right|_{k=0} k + \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right|_{k=0} k^2 + \frac{1}{6}\left.\frac{d^3 E}{d k^3}\right|_{k=0} k^3 + \dots$$

At the band minimum, the slope vanishes: $\left.\dfrac{dE}{dk}\right|_{k=0} = 0$.

For $k$ near zero, higher-order terms beyond $k^2$ are negligible:

$$E(k) \approx E(0) + \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right|_{k=0} k^2$$

The group velocity is:

$$v_g = \frac{1}{\hbar}\frac{dE}{dk} = \frac{1}{\hbar}\left.\frac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right|_{k=0} k$$

The acceleration is:

$$a = \frac{dv_g}{dt} = \frac{1}{\hbar}\left.\frac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right|_{k=0} \frac{dk}{dt}$$

Using $\hbar \, dk/dt = F$:

$$a = \frac{F}{\hbar^2 / \left(\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right)}$$

Comparing with Newton's law $F = m^* a$:

$$\boxed{\,m^* = \frac{\hbar^2}{\left(\dfrac{d^2 E}{d k^2}\right)}\,}$$

**(7 marks awarded for complete derivation with all intermediate steps.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

**Part (i) — Effective Density of States $N_C$ at 300 K:**

$$N_C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

Substitute $m_e^* = 0.26 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} = 2.3686 \times 10^{-31}$ kg.

$$\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} = \frac{2\pi \times 2.3686 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}$$

Numerator: $2\pi \times 2.3686 \times 10^{-31} \times 4.14 \times 10^{-21} = 2\pi \times 9.806 \times 10^{-52} = 6.162 \times 10^{-51}$.

Denominator: $(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2 = 4.39 \times 10^{-67}$.

Ratio: $\dfrac{6.162 \times 10^{-51}}{4.39 \times 10^{-67}} = 1.404 \times 10^{16}$ m$^{-2}$.

Raising to the 3/2 power:

$$(1.404 \times 10^{16})^{3/2} = 1.404^{1.5} \times 10^{24} = 1.663 \times 10^{24}$$

Multiply by 2:

$$\boxed{\,N_C \approx 3.33 \times 10^{24} \, \text{m}^{-3} = 3.33 \times 10^{18} \, \text{cm}^{-3}\,}$$

(This matches the standard textbook value $2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$ within rounding tolerance.) **(4 marks)**

**Part (ii) — Thermal Velocity:**

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{3 k_B T}{m_e^*}}$$

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{2.3686 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{1.242 \times 10^{-20}}{2.3686 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{5.244 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_{th} \approx 2.29 \times 10^{5} \, \text{m/s}\,}$$

**(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Define Fermi level. Obtain the expression for the position of the Fermi level in an intrinsic semiconductor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an n-type silicon sample at 300 K, the donor concentration is $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$. Given $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$ and $E_g = 1.12$ eV, calculate: (i) the position of the Fermi level below the conduction band, and (ii) the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ if $E_F - E_i = 0.25$ eV. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Derivation of Intrinsic Fermi Level:**

The **Fermi level** is defined as the energy at which the probability of electron occupation is 1/2. For an intrinsic semiconductor, charge neutrality demands $n = p = n_i$.

From carrier statistics (Boltzmann approximation):

$$n = N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_B T}, \quad p = N_V \, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_B T}$$

Setting $n = p$:

$$N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_B T} = N_V \, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_B T}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

Solving for $E_F \equiv E_i$:

$$E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

Substituting $N_V/N_C = (m_h^*/m_e^*)^{3/2}$:

$$\boxed{\,E_i = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3 k_B T}{4} \ln\!\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)\,}$$

For silicon ($m_h^* > m_e^*$), $E_i$ lies slightly above midgap. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

**Part (i) — Fermi Level Position in n-type Silicon:**

$$E_C - E_F = k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

$$E_C - E_F = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{2.8 \times 10^{19}}{10^{16}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(2800)$$

$$\ln(2800) = \ln(2.8 \times 10^3) = \ln(2.8) + 3\ln(10) = 1.0296 + 6.9078 = 7.937$$

$$E_C - E_F = 0.0259 \times 7.937 \approx 0.2056 \, \text{eV}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_C - E_F \approx 0.206 \, \text{eV}\,}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Part (ii) — Intrinsic Carrier Concentration:**

Using $E_F - E_i = k_B T \ln(N_D/n_i)$:

$$0.25 = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{n_i}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{n_i}\right) = \frac{0.25}{0.0259} = 9.653$$

$$\frac{10^{16}}{n_i} = e^{9.653} = 1.55 \times 10^{4}$$

$$n_i = \frac{10^{16}}{1.55 \times 10^{4}} \approx 6.45 \times 10^{11} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\,n_i \approx 6.45 \times 10^{11} \, \text{cm}^{-3}\,}$$

(This is reasonably close to the standard value of $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$, with deviation due to assumed parameters.) **(4 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of effective mass** — Always state whether $m^*$ is positive (electron at conduction band minimum) or negative (electron at valence band maximum, reinterpreted as a hole). **(Common 1-mark deduction)**
> 2. **Skipping the Taylor expansion step** in the derivation — Examiners specifically look for the step "$\left.\dfrac{dE}{dk}\right|_{k=0} = 0$" at the band extremum. **(1–2 marks lost)**
> 3. **Unit confusion in $N_C$** — Always express in consistent units: either cm$^{-3}$ or m$^{-3}$. Mixing both in the same expression is a frequent error. **(1 mark lost)**
> 4. **Using Boltzmann approximation wrongly** — The Boltzmann approximation $f(E) \approx e^{-(E-E_F)/k_BT}$ is valid only when $E - E_F \gg 3 k_B T$. Don't apply it inside the heavily-doped degenerate regime.
> 5. **Sign convention in $E_i$ expression** — Note that $\ln(m_h^*/m_e^*)$ is positive when $m_h^* > m_e^*$ (true for Si), pushing $E_i$ *above* midgap. Many students write the wrong sign.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Effective mass** is **not** a real mass — it is a **mathematical construct** that allows Newton's laws to apply inside a crystal.
- ✅ Always invoke the **Taylor expansion** + boundary condition $\left.\dfrac{dE}{dk}\right|_{k=0} = 0$ when deriving $m^*$.
- ✅ **Heavy mass** ↔ flat band ↔ **low mobility**; **light mass** ↔ steep band ↔ **high mobility**.
- ✅ **Negative $m^*$** at the valence band top ⇒ electrons behaving like holes. We *redefine* them as positive holes with positive mass.
- ✅ **Fermi level** is the **50% occupation probability marker** at $T > 0$ and the **highest filled state** at $T = 0$.
- ✅ Fermi-Dirac function reduces to a **step function at $T = 0$** and to the **Boltzmann tail** when $E - E_F \gg k_B T$.
- ✅ Intrinsic Fermi level $E_i$ is at midgap only if $m_h^* = m_e^*$; otherwise it shifts slightly.
- ✅ **n-type**: $E_F$ near $E_C$; **p-type**: $E_F$ near $E_V$; **intrinsic**: $E_F$ near midgap.
- ✅ Fermi level position directly controls **band bending**, **contact potential**, and **threshold voltage** in devices.
- ✅ For KTU exams, **memorize the four key equations**: $m^*$ formula, $f(E)$, $E_i$ expression, and $E_F$ for n-type.
- ✅ Always check whether to use the **Boltzmann approximation** or the **full Fermi-Dirac** integral — the latter is required for heavily-doped (degenerate) semiconductors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Density of States and Effective Density of States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Density of States and Effective Density of States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Density of States (DOS) — Formal Definition**
> The **Density of States**, denoted as $g(E)$, is defined as the *number of available electron states per unit volume per unit energy interval* at an energy $E$ in a semiconductor crystal. Mathematically:
> $$g(E) = \frac{dN}{dE \cdot V}$$
> where $dN$ is the number of states in the energy range $E$ to $E + dE$ and $V$ is the crystal volume. The SI unit is $\mathbf{J^{-1} \cdot m^{-3}}$, while in cgs/practical use, it is commonly expressed in $\mathbf{eV^{-1} \cdot cm^{-3}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Density of States (Nc & Nv) — Formal Definition**
> The **Effective Density of States** is the *thermally equivalent* number of states concentrated at the conduction band edge ($E_C$) for electrons ($N_C$) or at the valence band edge ($E_V$) for holes ($N_V$). It replaces the entire distribution of states near the band edge with a single, equivalent lumped value that yields the same carrier concentration under the Boltzmann approximation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

**Analogy — "Seating in a Multiplex Theatre":**

Imagine a movie theatre (the conduction band) with thousands of seats. The seats are *not* all at the same price level — front-row seats (low energy) are cheap, balcony seats (high energy) are expensive. The **Density of States** $g(E)$ tells you *how many seats are available at each price level*.

However, when you ask *"How many people actually buy tickets and come to the show?"*, you must also account for the **probability** that a customer is willing to pay that price. That probability is the **Fermi-Dirac distribution** $f(E)$.

The product $g(E) \cdot f(E)$ gives the *actual number of occupied seats* at each price.

Now, the theatre manager doesn't want to count every single seat-by-seat sale. She wants a **single round number** that represents the total audience assuming all seats were "effectively" priced at the lowest (most attractive) rate. This single round number is the **Effective Density of States** $N_C$.

- $N_C$ = how many people would show up if all $N_C$ "effective" seats were placed right at the band edge.
- $N_V$ = the same idea, but for empty seats in the balcony (valence band), which represent holes.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants (Bold for Quick Recall)

| Symbol | Constant | Numerical Value |
|:------:|:---------|:----------------|
| **$h$** | Planck's constant | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** |
| **$\hbar$** | Reduced Planck's constant ($h/2\pi$) | **$1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** |
| **$m_0$** | Free electron rest mass | **$9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg** |
| **$k$** | Boltzmann's constant | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| **$q$** | Electronic charge | **$1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 Scheme paper routinely gives constants in the data sheet, but examiners expect you to *remember* the **Nc formula structure** — not the value of $h$. Always reduce $N_C$ to its compact form: $N_C = 2 \left( \frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does DOS Exist? (The "Why" Behind the Concept)

A solid-state crystal contains $\sim 10^{22}$ atoms/cm³. Each atom contributes discrete energy levels, but in a crystal these levels **broaden into bands** due to overlapping atomic orbitals (Pauli exclusion + periodicity of the lattice). Within an allowed band, the *quantum states* of an electron behave like a 3-D **Particle-in-a-Box** problem — but with the electron's inertial mass replaced by the **effective mass** $m^*$, which captures the periodic potential of the lattice.

**Key logical chain:**

1. A free electron in a 3-D box of side $L$ has allowed wavevectors $k_x, k_y, k_z = \frac{\pi}{L}(n_x, n_y, n_z)$ with positive integers.
2. The number of states in $k$-space per unit volume is $\frac{V}{8\pi^3}$ (one octant because $n_x, n_y, n_z > 0$ for standing waves; or full $\frac{V}{(2\pi)^3}$ with periodic boundary conditions).
3. Each $k$-state is **doubly degenerate** due to electron spin ($\pm \frac{1}{2}$), multiplying by **2**.
4. Converting from $k$-space to energy $E$ via the dispersion $E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m^*}$ yields the parabolic DOS function.

### 2.2 The "How" — Derivation Roadmap (Bulleted Logic)

- **Step 1:** Apply 3-D infinite potential well (or periodic boundary) quantization to obtain $k$-states.
- **Step 2:** Count states in a thin spherical shell of $k$-space: $4\pi k^2 \, dk$.
- **Step 3:** Multiply by state density $\frac{V}{4\pi^3}$ (already including spin factor of 2).
- **Step 4:** Substitute $k = \frac{\sqrt{2m^*E}}{\hbar}$ and compute $dk = \frac{m^*}{\hbar^2 k}dE$.
- **Step 5:** Express DOS per unit volume by dividing by $V$.
- **Step 6:** For **Nc**: integrate $g(E) \cdot f(E)$ from $E_C$ to $\infty$ using the Boltzmann approximation.
- **Step 7:** Use the Gamma function identity $\int_0^\infty \sqrt{x} e^{-x} dx = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table — No Vertical Pipes in Cells)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:--------|
| 1 | DOS in 3-D (general) | $g(E) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi^{2}} \left(\dfrac{2m^{*}}{\hbar^{2}}\right)^{3/2}\!\sqrt{E}$ | $\text{J}^{-1}\,\text{m}^{-3}$ | $E$ measured from band edge; $\hbar^{2}$ in numerator |
| 2 | DOS using $h$ | $g(E) \;=\; \dfrac{4\pi \left(2m^{*}\right)^{3/2}}{h^{3}}\sqrt{E}$ | $\text{J}^{-1}\,\text{m}^{-3}$ | Substitute $\hbar = h/2\pi$ |
| 3 | Effective DOS — CB | $N_{C} \;=\; 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_{n}^{*} kT}{h^{2}}\right)^{3/2}$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | Or $\text{cm}^{-3}$ if $h$ in erg·s and $m^*$ in grams |
| 4 | Effective DOS — VB | $N_{V} \;=\; 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_{p}^{*} kT}{h^{2}}\right)^{3/2}$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | $m_p^*$ is hole effective mass |
| 5 | Electron concentration | $n \;=\; N_{C}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{C}-E_{F}}{kT}\right)$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | Boltzmann approximation |
| 6 | Hole concentration | $p \;=\; N_{V}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{F}-E_{V}}{kT}\right)$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | Boltzmann approximation |
| 7 | Mass-action law | $n \cdot p \;=\; n_i^{2} \;=\; N_{C} N_{V}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{g}}{kT}\right)$ | $\text{m}^{-6}$ | Independent of $E_F$ |
| 8 | Spin degeneracy factor | Factor of **2** in $N_C, N_V$ | — | Comes from $\pm 1/2$ electron spin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for the DOS Formulas:**
> The parabolic DOS $g(E) \propto \sqrt{E}$ is valid **only within the parabolic band approximation** (i.e., near the band edge where the $E$–$k$ curve is a parabola). For energies far from $E_C$ or $E_V$ (e.g., high-energy tail or $L$-valley electrons in Si), a non-parabolic correction is required.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MOSFET & BJT Design:** $N_C$ and $N_V$ are essential for computing threshold voltage, built-in potential, and minority-carrier injection.
- **Solar Cell Optimization:** Determining open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current requires accurate $n$ and $p$, which depend on $N_C, N_V$.
- **LED / Laser Engineering (GaAs, InGaAsP):** $N_C$ and $N_V$ define the population inversion threshold for direct-bandgap devices.
- **Semiconductor Process Simulation (TCAD tools like Sentaurus, ATLAS):** Built-in material libraries contain $N_C, N_V$ values at 300 K that scale as $T^{3/2}$.
- **TCO (Transparent Conducting Oxides) & 2-D Materials:** Modified DOS forms (2-D, 1-D, 0-D) drive modern nanoelectronic device design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 3-D Density of States $g(E)$

**Starting Point:** An electron in a 3-D crystal of volume $V = L_x L_y L_z$ behaves as a quasi-free particle. The allowed wavevectors under periodic boundary conditions are:

$$
k_x = \frac{2\pi n_x}{L_x}, \quad k_y = \frac{2\pi n_y}{L_y}, \quad k_z = \frac{2\pi n_z}{L_z}
$$

where $n_x, n_y, n_z \in \mathbb{Z}$ (positive, negative, zero).

**Volume per state in $k$-space:** Each state occupies a cube of side $\frac{2\pi}{L}$ in $k$-space. Therefore the density of states in $k$-space is:

$$
\rho_k = \frac{V}{(2\pi)^3}
$$

**Including spin degeneracy:** Since each $k$-state has two possible spin orientations ($+\hbar/2$ and $-\hbar/2$), we multiply by 2:

$$
\rho_k^{\text{spin}} = \frac{V}{4\pi^3}
$$

**Counting states in a spherical shell:** The number of states with wavevector magnitude between $k$ and $k + dk$ equals the volume of a thin spherical shell of radius $k$ and thickness $dk$:

$$
dN(k) = \rho_k^{\text{spin}} \cdot 4\pi k^2 \, dk = \frac{V}{4\pi^3} \cdot 4\pi k^2 \, dk = \frac{V k^2}{\pi^2} dk
$$

**Energy–wavevector relationship (parabolic band):**

$$
E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m^*} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{\sqrt{2m^* E}}{\hbar}
$$

**Differential of $k$ with respect to $E$:**

$$
dk = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2m^* E}} \cdot \frac{2m^*}{\hbar} dE = \frac{m^*}{\hbar^2 k} dE
$$

**Substituting $k$ and $dk$ into $dN$:**

$$
dN(E) = \frac{V k^2}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{m^*}{\hbar^2 k} dE = \frac{V k m^*}{\pi^2 \hbar^2} dE
$$

**Replacing $k = \sqrt{2m^*E}/\hbar$:**

$$
dN(E) = \frac{V m^*}{\pi^2 \hbar^2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2m^*E}}{\hbar} dE = \frac{V m^* \sqrt{2m^*E}}{\pi^2 \hbar^3} dE
$$

**DOS per unit volume:**

$$
g(E) = \frac{dN(E)}{V \, dE} = \frac{m^* \sqrt{2m^*E}}{\pi^2 \hbar^3}
$$

**Factoring into the canonical form:**

$$
\boxed{\,g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}\,}
$$

> **Logic step:** The factor $\frac{1}{2\pi^2}$ emerges from $\frac{1}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot (2)^{1/2} \cdot 2^{3/2} = \frac{2^{3/2} \cdot 2^{1/2}}{2\pi^2} = \frac{4}{2\pi^2} = \frac{2}{\pi^2}$ … (algebra verified by writing $m^* \sqrt{2m^*} = (2m^*)^{3/2}/2$).

**Equivalent form using $h$:** Substituting $\hbar = h/(2\pi)$:

$$
g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left( \frac{2m^* \cdot 4\pi^2}{h^2} \right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E} = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \cdot \frac{(2m^*)^{3/2} (4\pi^2)^{3/2}}{h^3} \sqrt{E}
$$

Since $(4\pi^2)^{3/2} = 8\pi^3$:

$$
\boxed{\,g(E) = \frac{4\pi(2m^*)^{3/2}}{h^3}\sqrt{E}\,}
$$

> **Logic step:** $\frac{1}{2\pi^2} \cdot 8\pi^3 = 4\pi$, confirming the cleaner textbook form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Density of States $N_C$

The **electron concentration** in the conduction band is obtained by integrating the *occupied* DOS over all energies above $E_C$:

$$
n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g(E) \, f(E) \, dE
$$

**Applying the Boltzmann approximation** (valid when $E_C - E_F \gg kT$, i.e., non-degenerate semiconductor):

$$
f(E) \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{kT}\right)
$$

**Substituting $g(E)$ (with $E$ measured from $E_C$):**

$$
n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E - E_C} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{kT}\right) dE
$$

**Substitution:** Let $x = \dfrac{E - E_C}{kT}$, so $E - E_C = kT x$ and $dE = kT \, dx$. Also $E - E_F = (E - E_C) + (E_C - E_F) = kT x + (E_C - E_F)$.

$$
n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (kT)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right) \int_0^{\infty} \sqrt{x} \, e^{-x} \, dx
$$

**Evaluating the integral** using the Gamma function identity:

$$
\int_0^{\infty} x^{1/2} e^{-x} dx = \Gamma\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (kT)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right)
$$

$$
n = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{4\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^* kT}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right)
$$

$$
n = \frac{1}{4\pi^{3/2}}\left(\frac{2m_n^* kT}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right)
$$

**Replacing $\hbar$ with $h$:** Since $\hbar = h/(2\pi)$, we get $\hbar^2 = h^2/(4\pi^2)$. Substituting:

$$
\left(\frac{2m_n^* kT}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} = \left(\frac{2m_n^* kT \cdot 4\pi^2}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} = \left(\frac{8\pi^2 m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}
$$

Combining with the $\dfrac{1}{4\pi^{3/2}}$ prefactor:

$$
N_C = \frac{1}{4\pi^{3/2}} \cdot (8\pi^2)^{3/2} \cdot \left(\frac{m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} = \frac{(8\pi^2)^{3/2}}{4\pi^{3/2}} \left(\frac{m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}
$$

Since $(8\pi^2)^{3/2} = 8^{3/2} \cdot \pi^3 = 16\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi^3$ and dividing by $4\pi^{3/2}$:

$$
N_C = \frac{16\sqrt{2} \, \pi^3}{4 \pi^{3/2}} \left(\frac{m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} = 4\sqrt{2} \, \pi^{3/2} \left(\frac{m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}
$$

**Final canonical form:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}\,}
$$

> **Logic step:** $2 \cdot (2\pi)^{3/2} = 2 \cdot 2^{3/2} \cdot \pi^{3/2} = 4\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi^{3/2}$, matching the previous expression. ✓

---

### 3.3 Numerical Demonstration (Standard 300 K Values for Silicon)

Using $m_n^* = 1.08 m_0$, $m_p^* = 0.56 m_0$ for Si, $T = 300$ K:

$$
N_C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi \cdot 1.08 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 300}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}\right)^{3/2}
$$

**Stepwise calculation:**

- Numerator inside bracket: $2\pi \times 1.08 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \approx 2.557 \times 10^{-50}$
- Denominator: $(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2 = 4.39 \times 10^{-67}$
- Ratio: $2.557 \times 10^{-50} / 4.39 \times 10^{-67} = 5.825 \times 10^{16}$ (units: m⁻²)
- $(5.825 \times 10^{16})^{3/2} = 1.408 \times 10^{25}$ m⁻³
- $N_C = 2 \times 1.408 \times 10^{25} = 2.82 \times 10^{25}$ m⁻³ $= 2.82 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³

$$
\boxed{\,N_C(\text{Si, 300 K}) \approx 2.8 \times 10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Reference Values** (rounded — these appear in the KTU data booklet):
> - Si: $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³, $N_V = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³
> - GaAs: $N_C = 4.7 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $N_V = 7.0 \times 10^{18}$ cm⁻³
> - Ge: $N_C = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³, $N_V = 6.0 \times 10^{18}$ cm⁻³
