<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define quasi-Fermi levels. Under what conditions do they reduce to the ordinary Fermi level?**

**Model Answer:**

Quasi-Fermi levels are fictitious energy levels that replace the single equilibrium Fermi level when a semiconductor is driven out of thermal equilibrium. Two such levels are defined: the **electron quasi-Fermi level** $E_{Fn}$, which describes the occupation statistics of electrons in the conduction band, and the **hole quasi-Fermi level** $E_{Fp}$, which describes the occupation statistics of holes in the valence band.

Mathematically, the carrier densities are expressed as:

$$n = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right) \quad ; \quad p = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fi} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

**Condition for reduction to ordinary Fermi level:** When the system is in **thermal equilibrium**, no external excitation or bias is present, and the rates of generation and recombination are in detailed balance. In this case, $E_{Fn} = E_{Fp} = E_F$, the quasi-Fermi levels coalesce into the ordinary equilibrium Fermi level $E_F$, and the carrier densities revert to the standard expressions $n_0 = n_i \exp((E_F - E_{Fi})/kT)$ and $p_0 = n_i \exp((E_{Fi} - E_F)/kT)$.

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Write the non-equilibrium mass-action law and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **non-equilibrium mass-action law** states that the product of the electron and hole concentrations in a non-equilibrium semiconductor is given by:

$$np = n_i^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

**Physical significance:**

1. **Generalization of equilibrium law:** In thermal equilibrium, $E_{Fn} = E_{Fp}$, the exponential becomes 1, and the law reduces to the familiar $np = n_i^2$. Thus, the non-equilibrium law is a strict generalization of the equilibrium mass-action law.

2. **Measure of non-equilibrium:** The factor $\exp((E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/kT)$ quantifies how far the carrier product deviates from its equilibrium value. The greater the splitting between $E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$, the larger the deviation.

3. **Foundation for device physics:** This law underpins the operation of all bipolar devices. For example, in a forward-biased p-n diode, the minority-carrier injection across the junction is governed entirely by the quasi-Fermi level splitting. In a solar cell under illumination, the photo-generated excess carriers cause the quasi-Fermi levels to separate, producing the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} = (E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/q$.

4. **Population inversion condition:** For laser action, stimulated emission requires $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} > E_g$, which corresponds to $np > n_i^2 \exp(E_g/kT)$ — a condition impossible under thermal equilibrium.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You are required to attempt **one** full question out of the two alternatives (OR choice). Sub-part (a) typically tests derivation/theory and sub-part (b) tests numerical application or device-level reasoning.

---

### **Question B — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]`

#### **B-A (a)** — 7 Marks

**Starting from the Fermi-Dirac distribution and the Boltzmann approximation, derive the expressions for electron and hole concentrations in a non-equilibrium semiconductor in terms of quasi-Fermi levels.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the equilibrium expressions.**

Under the Boltzmann approximation, the equilibrium electron density in the conduction band is:

$$n_0 = N_C \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

where $N_C$ is the effective density of states in the conduction band and $E_F$ is the equilibrium Fermi level.

**Step 2 — Recall the intrinsic carrier density expression.**

When the Fermi level is at $E_{Fi}$ (intrinsic position):

$$n_i = N_C \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Divide (1) by (2).**

$$\frac{n_0}{n_i} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{E_F - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right)$$

Hence:

$$n_0 = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_F - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to non-equilibrium.**

The Boltzmann-approximated Fermi-Dirac integral depends only on the *position* of the reference level, not on the existence of detailed balance. Since electron-electron scattering is much faster than recombination, the electrons in the CB are internally in equilibrium and can be described by a Fermi-Dirac distribution with reference level $E_{Fn}$. Replacing $E_F$ by $E_{Fn}$ in equation (3):

$$\boxed{\,n = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right)\,}$$

**Step 5 — Analogous derivation for holes.**

By the same reasoning applied to the valence band, the hole density becomes:

$$\boxed{\,p = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fi} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)\,}$$

**Step 6 — State the physical meaning.**

The expressions are formally identical to their equilibrium counterparts; the *only* change is the replacement of the equilibrium Fermi level $E_F$ by the appropriate quasi-Fermi level ($E_{Fn}$ for electrons, $E_{Fp}$ for holes). This compact replacement is the central elegance of the quasi-Fermi level formalism.

---

#### **B-A (b)** — 7 Marks

**In a silicon sample at 300 K, the electron and hole densities are $n = 10^{15}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$ and $p = 10^{5}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$ respectively. Given $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$, $kT/q = 0.0259\;\text{V}$, calculate (i) the quasi-Fermi level positions $E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$ relative to $E_{Fi}$, and (ii) the quasi-Fermi level separation $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}$. Also comment on whether the sample is in equilibrium or non-equilibrium.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 10^{15}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$, $p = 10^{5}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$, $T = 300\;\text{K}$, $V_T = 0.0259\;\text{V}$.

**(i) Position of $E_{Fn}$ relative to $E_{Fi}$:**

From the master equation $n = n_i \exp((E_{Fn} - E_{Fi})/kT)$:

$$E_{Fn} - E_{Fi} = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{n}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{15}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right)$$

$$\frac{10^{15}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}} = 6.667 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\ln(6.667 \times 10^{4}) = \ln(6.667) + 4\ln(10) = 1.897 + 9.210 = 11.107$$

$$E_{Fn} - E_{Fi} = 0.0259 \times 11.107 = 0.2877\;\text{eV}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{Fn} - E_{Fi} \approx 0.288\;\text{eV (above } E_{Fi}\text{)}\,}$$

**Position of $E_{Fp}$ relative to $E_{Fi}$:**

$$E_{Fi} - E_{Fp} = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{5}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right)$$

$$\frac{10^{5}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}} = 6.667 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\ln(6.667 \times 10^{-6}) = \ln(6.667) - 6\ln(10) = 1.897 - 13.816 = -11.919$$

$$E_{Fi} - E_{Fp} = 0.0259 \times (-11.919) = -0.3087\;\text{eV}$$

This means:

$$\boxed{\,E_{Fp} - E_{Fi} \approx -0.309\;\text{eV, i.e., } E_{Fp}\text{ is } 0.309\;\text{eV below } E_{Fi}\,}$$

**(ii) Quasi-Fermi level separation:**

$$E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = (E_{Fn} - E_{Fi}) + (E_{Fi} - E_{Fp}) = 0.2877 + 0.3087 = 0.5964\;\text{eV}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} \approx 0.596\;\text{eV}\,}$$

**Comment on equilibrium:**

At equilibrium, we would expect $np = n_i^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{20}\;\text{cm}^{-6}$.  
Actual value: $np = 10^{15} \times 10^{5} = 10^{20}\;\text{cm}^{-6}$.  
Since $np \neq n_i^2$, the sample is clearly in **non-equilibrium**. Equivalently, $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = 0.596\;\text{eV} \neq 0$, confirming the system is driven away from equilibrium (likely by optical excitation or external injection).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $E_{Fp}$ formula:** Students often write $E_{Fp} = E_{Fi} + kT \ln(p/n_i)$, which is **wrong**. The correct relation is $E_{Fp} = E_{Fi} - kT \ln(p/n_i)$. (The minus sign comes from the fact that the hole density *decreases* as $E_{Fp}$ moves deeper into the valence band.)
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Always quote energies in **eV** and use $kT = 0.0259\;\text{eV}$ at 300 K, not $1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$, to avoid conversion errors.
> 3. **Missing equilibrium check:** Examiners award 1–2 marks for explicitly verifying $np \neq n_i^2$ to justify the non-equilibrium comment.

---

### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]`

#### **B-B (a)** — 7 Marks

**Derive the non-equilibrium mass-action law $np = n_i^2 \exp((E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/kT)$ from the expressions for $n$ and $p$ in terms of quasi-Fermi levels. Discuss its significance in the context of a forward-biased p-n junction.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the two master equations:**

$$n = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$p = n_i \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fi} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply (i) and (ii):**

$$np = n_i^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fi} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Combine the exponential terms using the identity $e^A \cdot e^B = e^{A+B}$:**

$$np = n_i^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fi} + E_{Fi} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — The $E_{Fi}$ terms cancel:**

$$np = n_i^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}}{kT}\right)$$

This is the **non-equilibrium mass-action law**.

**Step 5 — Equilibrium limit:** Setting $E_{Fn} = E_{Fp}$ recovers $np = n_i^2$.

**Step 6 — Significance in a forward-biased p-n junction:**

In the neutral n-region, the equilibrium electron density is $n_{n0}$. Under forward bias $V$, the injected minority-hole density at the edge of the depletion region is:

$$p_n = p_{n0} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right)$$

The minority-electron density in the p-region similarly becomes:

$$n_p = n_{p0} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right)$$

Using the non-equilibrium mass-action law at the junction edge and recognizing that $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = qV$ (as derived earlier), the carrier product becomes $np = n_i^2 \exp(qV/kT)$, which is enhanced by a factor of $\exp(qV/kT)$ over its equilibrium value. This enhanced minority-carrier population is the **physical origin of forward-bias current** in a diode.

**Step 7 — Population inversion criterion:** The non-equilibrium law also gives the condition for population inversion in laser diodes: $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} > E_g$ ⟹ $np > n_i^2 \exp(E_g/kT)$.

---

#### **B-B (b)** — 7 Marks

**A GaAs sample at 300 K is illuminated uniformly, producing excess carriers $\Delta n = \Delta p = 10^{16}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$. The thermal-equilibrium electron and hole concentrations are $n_0 = 10^{17}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$ and $p_0 = 10^{7}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$. Given $n_i = 1.8 \times 10^{6}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$ for GaAs, calculate the separation $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}$ under illumination. Also state whether population inversion is achieved, given $E_g = 1.42\;\text{eV}$ for GaAs.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\Delta n = \Delta p = 10^{16}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$, $n_0 = 10^{17}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$, $p_0 = 10^{7}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$, $n_i = 1.8 \times 10^{6}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$, $T = 300\;\text{K}$, $V_T = 0.0259\;\text{V}$, $E_g = 1.42\;\text{eV}$.

**Step 1 — Total carrier densities under illumination:**

$$n = n_0 + \Delta n = 10^{17} + 10^{16} = 1.1 \times 10^{17}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$$

$$p = p_0 + \Delta p = 10^{7} + 10^{16} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{16}\;\text{cm}^{-3}$$

(We neglect $p_0$ in $p$ because $\Delta p \gg p_0$.)

**Step 2 — Apply the non-equilibrium mass-action law:**

$$E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{np}{n_i^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $np$:**

$$np = (1.1 \times 10^{17}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{16}) = 1.1 \times 10^{33}\;\text{cm}^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $n_i^2$:**

$$n_i^2 = (1.8 \times 10^{6})^2 = 3.24 \times 10^{12}\;\text{cm}^{-6}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{np}{n_i^2} = \frac{1.1 \times 10^{33}}{3.24 \times 10^{12}} = 3.395 \times 10^{20}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(3.395 \times 10^{20}) = \ln(3.395) + 20\ln(10) = 1.222 + 46.052 = 47.274$$

**Step 7 — Compute $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}$:**

$$E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = 0.0259 \times 47.274 = 1.224\;\text{eV}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} \approx 1.224\;\text{eV}\,}$$

**Step 8 — Population inversion check:**

The condition for population inversion is $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} > E_g$.

We have $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = 1.224\;\text{eV}$ and $E_g = 1.42\;\text{eV}$.

Since $1.224\;\text{eV} < 1.42\;\text{eV}$:

$$\boxed{\,E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} < E_g \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Population inversion is NOT achieved.}\,}$$

The illuminated GaAs is in a non-equilibrium state with substantial excess carriers, but the splitting is still less than the bandgap. To achieve lasing, a higher pump intensity (larger $\Delta n, \Delta p$) would be required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $\Delta n$ to $n_0$:** Many students use $n_0$ and $p_0$ directly in the formula instead of the *total* concentrations $n = n_0 + \Delta n$ and $p = p_0 + \Delta p$. This gives a wildly wrong answer.
> 2. **Dropping the $n_0$ term in $n$:** Since $\Delta n = 10^{16}$ is an order of magnitude smaller than $n_0 = 10^{17}$, you *cannot* drop it. Only $p_0$ can be neglected next to $\Delta p$.
> 3. **Concluding inversion too quickly:** Always compare $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}$ with $E_g$ explicitly, and state the inequality in words.
> 4. **Logarithm mistakes:** Use $\ln(10) = 2.3026$ and $\ln(10^x) = x \cdot 2.3026$ for clean computation. Examiners give full credit only when the logarithm step is shown explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Single Fermi level ($E_F$)** exists *only* in **thermal equilibrium**. [Essential]
- **Quasi-Fermi levels ($E_{Fn}$ and $E_{Fp}$)** replace $E_F$ when the semiconductor is **driven out of equilibrium** by bias, light, or injection. [Essential]
- The carrier density formulas $n = n_i \exp((E_{Fn} - E_{Fi})/kT)$ and $p = n_i \exp((E_{Fi} - E_{Fp})/kT)$ are the **master equations** — memorize both. [Essential]
- The **non-equilibrium mass-action law** $np = n_i^2 \exp((E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/kT)$ generalizes the equilibrium $np = n_i^2$. [Essential]
- **Equilibrium limit:** $E_{Fn} = E_{Fp} \Rightarrow np = n_i^2$. [Essential]
- **Physical reason for splitting:** electron-electron thermalization is ~$10^{6}$ times faster than electron-hole recombination. [Important]
- **Population inversion condition (laser):** $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} > E_g$. [High-yield]
- **Forward-biased p-n junction:** $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = qV$ across the depletion region. [High-yield]
- **Solar cell open-circuit voltage:** $V_{oc} = (E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/q$ under illumination. [Important]
- **Sign convention:** $E_{Fn} > E_{Fi} > E_{Fp}$ is typical under forward bias / illumination. [Important]
- **Use $kT = 0.0259\;\text{eV}$** at 300 K for clean numerical computation; avoid mixing SI and eV units. [KTU exam tip]
- **Conversion check:** Always verify $np \neq n_i^2$ to confirm a non-equilibrium situation before applying quasi-Fermi level formulas. [Valuation tip]

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carrier Transport in Semiconductors: Drift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carrier Transport in Semiconductors: Drift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carrier Transport by Drift (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> **Drift** is the ordered motion of charge carriers (free electrons in the conduction band and holes in the valence band) through a semiconductor crystal caused by the application of an external **electric field (E)**. Under the influence of this field, carriers acquire a net average velocity superimposed on their random thermal motion, which results in a macroscopic current known as the **drift current**.

In thermal equilibrium, the random thermal motion of charge carriers produces *zero net current* because the motion is equally probable in all directions. When a DC or low-frequency electric field is applied, two opposing physical processes govern the motion:

1. **Acceleration by the electric field** – Carriers gain kinetic energy between collisions.
2. **Randomizing collisions** with lattice vibrations (phonons), impurity ions, and crystal defects – These scatter the carriers and randomize their velocities.

The competition between these two processes yields a constant **average velocity** called the **drift velocity ($v_d$)**, which is proportional to the applied electric field.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bumper Car" Analogy for Drift**
> Imagine a large, crowded bumper-car arena. The cars (charge carriers) are continuously colliding and bouncing in completely random directions due to their own engines (thermal energy from lattice vibrations) — this random motion corresponds to the thermal velocity of carriers and produces no net flow of traffic. Now, suppose the floor is gently tilted so that all cars experience a slight slope pulling them toward the east side of the arena. Each car still bumps randomly, but on average, every collision now has a slight eastward bias. The arena now shows a steady stream of cars flowing east — this average, biased flow is exactly what we call **drift velocity**, and the tilt of the floor is the **electric field (E)**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities Introduced

The following fundamental quantities are defined for the first time in this topic and form the backbone of transport physics:

- **Drift Velocity ($v_d$)** – The average net velocity acquired by a carrier along the direction of the applied field. Units: **m/s** or **cm/s**.
- **Mobility ($\mu$)** – The proportionality constant between drift velocity and the applied electric field, representing *how easily* a carrier moves through the crystal. Units: **m²/(V·s)** or **cm²/(V·s)**.
- **Mean Free Time / Relaxation Time ($t_{rn}$ or $\tau_c$)** – The average time between two successive scattering collisions of a carrier. Units: **seconds (s)**.
- **Mean Free Path ($\lambda$)** – The average distance a carrier travels between successive collisions. Units: **m** or **cm**.
- **Conductivity ($\sigma$)** – The macroscopic material property linking current density to applied field. Units: **S/m** or **S/cm**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the *qualitative* relationship between mobility and temperature/doping. Memorize the trend — mobility *decreases* with increasing temperature (lattice scattering) and *decreases* with increasing doping (impurity scattering).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Microscopic Origin of Drift

When an electric field **E** is applied across a semiconductor, the equation of motion for a carrier of charge $q$ and effective mass $m^*$ is given by Newton's second law:

$$F = qE = m^* a$$

This produces a continuous acceleration of the carrier. However, in a real crystal, the carrier undergoes **scattering events** at an average time interval of $\tau_c$ (mean free time). Between two consecutive collisions, the carrier accelerates, gaining a velocity component in the direction of **E** that we call the **incremental drift velocity**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Drift Analysis

The standard textbook derivation follows this rigorous four-step logical chain (a KTU favourite):

1. **Step 1 — Random Thermal Motion (No Field):** Without an electric field, a carrier undergoes perfectly random collisions. Its velocity after the $n^{th}$ collision is denoted $v_{n}$. The average velocity over many collisions is exactly **zero** because of the random directionality:
$$\langle v_{n} \rangle = 0$$

2. **Step 2 — Acceleration Between Collisions:** The instant after the $n^{th}$ collision (which we label $t = 0$), the carrier starts with a random thermal velocity $v_{n0}$. During the time interval $0 < t < t_c$ before the next collision, the carrier accelerates in the direction of **E**:
$$v_{n}(t) = v_{n0} + \frac{qE}{m^*} t$$

3. **Step 3 — Average Over All Carriers (Boltzmann Statistics):** The average thermal velocity over the carrier ensemble remains zero: $\langle v_{n0} \rangle = 0$. Therefore, the net average velocity at any instant in time is the average of the additional component:
$$\langle v_{n}(t) \rangle = \frac{qE}{m^*} t$$

4. **Step 4 — Time Average Over the Mean Free Path:** We now average this velocity over the average time $\tau_c$ between collisions to obtain the steady-state drift velocity:
$$v_d = \langle v_n \rangle_{avg} = \frac{1}{\tau_c} \int_{0}^{\tau_c} \frac{qE}{m^*} t \, dt$$

Evaluating the integral:
$$v_d = \frac{qE}{m^*} \cdot \frac{\tau_c}{2}$$

Because the choice of the starting instant $t = 0$ is statistically equivalent to any other instant (steady-state), the factor of ½ cancels with a corresponding factor of 2 from the average over a full collision cycle, yielding the clean linear result:

$$v_d = \frac{q\tau_c}{m^*} E$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Critical Insight:** Drift velocity is *linearly proportional* to the applied electric field. The proportionality constant is the **mobility**:
> $$\mu = \frac{q\tau_c}{m^*} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_d = \mu E$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every drift-related formula you must know for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Each equation is exam-grade and high-frequency.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Definition of Symbols | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drift Velocity (general) | $v_d = \mu E$ | $v_d$: drift velocity, $\mu$: mobility, $E$: electric field | m/s, m²/V·s, V/m |
| 2 | Electron Drift Velocity | $v_{dn} = -\mu_n E$ | $\mu_n$: electron mobility (negative sign due to charge) | m/s |
| 3 | Hole Drift Velocity | $v_{dp} = +\mu_p E$ | $\mu_p$: hole mobility (positive sign) | m/s |
| 4 | Mobility Definition (microscopic) | $\mu = \frac{q\tau_c}{m^*}$ | $\tau_c$: mean free time, $m^*$: effective mass | m²/V·s |
| 5 | Conductivity | $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ | $n,p$: carrier concentrations | S/m |
| 6 | Resistivity | $\rho = \frac{1}{\sigma} = \frac{1}{q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)}$ | $\rho$: resistivity | Ω·m |
| 7 | Drift Current Density (Electrons) | $J_{n,drift} = -qnv_{dn} = qn\mu_n E$ | $J_{n,drift}$: electron drift current density | A/m² |
| 8 | Drift Current Density (Holes) | $J_{p,drift} = qpv_{dp} = qp\mu_p E$ | $J_{p,drift}$: hole drift current density | A/m² |
| 9 | Total Drift Current Density | $J_{drift} = J_{n,drift} + J_{p,drift} = \sigma E$ | Total current density by Ohm's Law form | A/m² |
| 10 | Mean Free Path | $\lambda = v_{th} \tau_c$ | $v_{th}$: thermal velocity, $\tau_c$: mean free time | m |
| 11 | Thermal Velocity (Maxwell-Boltzmann) | $v_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{8k_B T}{\pi m^*}}$ | $k_B$: Boltzmann constant, $T$: absolute temperature | m/s |
| 12 | Average Thermal Energy | $\frac{1}{2} m^* v_{th}^2 = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$ | Equipartition principle | J |
| 13 | Matthiessen's Rule | $\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_{lattice}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{impurity}}$ | Combines lattice and impurity scattering | m²/V·s |
| 14 | Velocity Saturation (Empirical) | $v_{sat} = \frac{\mu_0 E}{1 + \frac{\mu_0 E}{v_{sat,\infty}}}$ | Empirical fit to saturation behavior | m/s |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The drift mechanism is the foundational working principle behind virtually every semiconductor device:

- **Resistors (Semiconductor Type):** Linear current–voltage response governed directly by $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$.
- **Field-Effect Transistors (FETs):** The current flowing between source and drain in a MOSFET is fundamentally a *drift current* controlled by the gate-induced channel field.
- **Junction Field-Effect Transistors (JFETs):** Current modulation by gate-depletion width directly controls the cross-sectional area available for drift.
- **Hall Effect Sensors:** Drift velocity of carriers in a magnetic field is the central measurable quantity.
- **Power Devices (IGBTs, Thyristors):** High-field drift in the drift region determines breakdown voltage and on-state losses.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** "**Drift Depends Directly on μ, but Mobility is Limited by Collisions.**" — This is the conceptual backbone of every drift problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Drift Velocity from the Equation of Motion

**Problem Setup:** Consider an electron of effective mass $m_n^*$ and charge $-q$ in a semiconductor under an applied electric field $E$ along the $x$-direction. Assume that the electron experiences collisions at random time intervals, with an average time between collisions equal to $\tau_c$. Derive the expression for steady-state drift velocity.

**Step 1 — Write Newton's Second Law for the carrier:**

The force on the electron due to the applied field is $F = -qE$ (the negative sign reflects the negative charge of the electron, but we will absorb the sign convention into the mobility). For simplicity and generality, we write the equation for a generic carrier of charge $q$:

$$m^* \frac{dv}{dt} = qE$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that collisions introduce damping (Drude Model):**

A carrier that has just undergone a collision has its velocity randomized. Its *momentum* decays between collisions due to the randomizing action of scattering. The simplest phenomenological way to model this is the **Drude relaxation-time approximation**:

$$m^* \frac{dv}{dt} = qE - \frac{m^* v}{\tau_c}$$

The term $-\frac{m^* v}{\tau_c}$ represents the rate at which momentum is lost to the lattice (or impurities) through scattering events.

**Step 3 — Solve the differential equation in steady state:**

In steady state, the carrier velocity reaches a constant value, so $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$. Setting this to zero:

$$0 = qE - \frac{m^* v_d}{\tau_c}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for $v_d$:**

$$\frac{m^* v_d}{\tau_c} = qE$$

$$v_d = \frac{q\tau_c}{m^*} E$$

**Step 5 — Define the mobility $\mu$:**

The proportionality constant between $v_d$ and $E$ is the mobility $\mu$:

$$\mu \equiv \frac{q\tau_c}{m^*}$$

Therefore:

$$v_d = \mu E$$

This is the **defining equation of drift transport** in semiconductors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the factor of ½ from the time-averaging disappear in steady state?** In the steady-state Drude model, we are *not* tracking the velocity profile of an individual carrier. Instead, we are asking: "What is the typical *instantaneous* velocity of a randomly chosen carrier at an arbitrary moment?" Because the system is statistically stationary, the probability that a randomly sampled carrier has been accelerating for a time $t$ since its last collision is proportional to $e^{-t/\tau_c}$, and the average over this exponential distribution gives exactly $\langle t \rangle = \tau_c$ — no factor of ½.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Conductivity of an Intrinsic/Extrinsic Semiconductor

**Problem Setup:** A semiconductor contains $n$ free electrons per unit volume (cm⁻³) and $p$ free holes per unit volume. Under an applied field $E$, derive the conductivity of the material.

**Step 1 — Write the drift current density contribution from electrons:**

Each electron moves with average drift velocity $v_{dn} = -\mu_n E$ (the negative sign reflects the negative charge of the electron). The current density is the charge crossing per unit area per unit time:

$$J_{n,drift} = (-q)(n)(v_{dn}) = (-q)(n)(-\mu_n E) = qn\mu_n E$$

**Step 2 — Write the drift current density contribution from holes:**

Holes drift in the direction of the field with velocity $v_{dp} = +\mu_p E$. Their charge is positive $+q$:

$$J_{p,drift} = (+q)(p)(v_{dp}) = qp\mu_p E$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions (superposition principle):**

$$J_{drift} = J_{n,drift} + J_{p,drift} = qn\mu_n E + qp\mu_p E$$

$$J_{drift} = (qn\mu_n + qp\mu_p) E$$

**Step 4 — Identify the conductivity via Ohm's Law in local form:**

By definition, $J = \sigma E$. Therefore, the conductivity is:

$$\sigma = qn\mu_n + qp\mu_p$$

**Step 5 — Special case: Intrinsic semiconductor ($n = p = n_i$):**

For an intrinsic semiconductor, the electron and hole concentrations are both equal to the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$. The conductivity becomes:

$$\sigma_i = qn_i(\mu_n + \mu_p)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The equation $\sigma = qn\mu_n + qp\mu_p$ is **the single most important formula** in the entire drift transport module. It directly connects carrier concentration (a *statistical* property) with mobility (a *dynamical* property). It will appear in *every* drift-related numerical problem.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Temperature Dependence of Mobility

**Problem Setup:** Show that the mobility due to lattice (phonon) scattering follows $\mu_L \propto T^{-3/2}$, while mobility due to ionized impurity scattering follows $\mu_I \propto T^{+3/2}$.

**Step 1 — Acoustic Phonon Scattering (Lattice Mobility):**

The mean free path for acoustic phonon scattering at temperature $T$ is given by:

$$\lambda_L = \frac{h^4 \rho v_s^2}{16 \pi^6 m^{*2} (k_B T) D_{ac}^2}$$

where $\rho$ is crystal density, $v_s$ is the velocity of sound, and $D_{ac}$ is the deformation potential. Substituting $\lambda_L = v_{th} \tau_c$ and using $v_{th} \propto T^{1/2}$:

$$\tau_{c,L} = \frac{\lambda_L}{v_{th}} \propto \frac{T^{-1}}{T^{1/2}} = T^{-3/2}$$

Therefore, mobility is:

$$\mu_L = \frac{q\tau_{c,L}}{m^*} \propto T^{-3/2}$$

**Step 2 — Ionized Impurity Scattering (Impurity Mobility):**

Using the Conwell-Weisskopf formula (or Brooks-Herring, more refined):

$$\mu_I = \frac{128 \sqrt{2\pi} \epsilon^2 (k_B T)^{3/2}}{N_I Z^2 q^3 \sqrt{m^*} \ln\left[1 + \left(\frac{12\pi \epsilon k_B T}{q^2 N_I^{1/3}}\right)^2 \right]}$$

The dominant temperature scaling is:

$$\mu_I \propto T^{+3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Matthiessen's Rule (Combination of Independent Scattering):**

When both mechanisms are present, the inverse mobilities add:

$$\frac{1}{\mu_{total}} = \frac{1}{\mu_L} + \frac{1}{\mu_I}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**

- At **low temperatures**, $\mu_I$ is small (carriers move slowly, impurities deflect them strongly). $\mu_I$ dominates and increases as $T$ rises.
- At **high temperatures**, $\mu_L$ is small (lattice vibrations are violent). $\mu_L$ dominates and decreases as $T$ rises.
- The mobility vs. temperature curve has a **maximum** at some intermediate temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common trick question is: *"Does mobility increase or decrease with temperature in silicon at room temperature?"* The answer is **decreases** because lattice (phonon) scattering dominates at $T \geq 300$ K in lightly doped Si.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** Silicon at $T = 300$ K is doped with $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³ donors (fully ionized). Given $\mu_n = 1350$ cm²/V·s, $\mu_p = 480$ cm²/V·s, and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, calculate: (a) the electron and hole concentrations, (b) the conductivity, and (c) the resistivity of the material.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using the mass-action law $np = n_i^2$ and assuming $n \approx N_D$ (since $N_D \gg n_i$):

$$n = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

$$p = \frac{n_i^2}{n} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}{10^{16}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{20}}{10^{16}} = 2.25 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Part (b):** Conductivity:

$$\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$$

$$= (1.6 \times 10^{-19})[10^{16}(1350) + (2.25 \times 10^4)(480)]$$

$$= (1.6 \times 10^{-19})[1.35 \times 10^{19} + 1.08 \times 10^7]$$

$$= (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.35 \times 10^{19}) \approx 2.16 \, \text{S/cm}$$

**Part (c):** Resistivity:

$$\rho = \frac{1}{\sigma} = \frac{1}{2.16} \approx 0.463 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The hole contribution is completely negligible ($p\mu_p \ll n\mu_n$). This is true for virtually all n-type semiconductors where $N_D \gg n_i$. KTU examiners will often ask you to identify which term dominates and justify dropping the minority contribution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Drift Process Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete functional architecture of the drift process, from the applied electric field down to the resulting macroscopic current density.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Voltage Source V_applied] --> B[Electric Field E established across semiconductor]
    B --> C[Force F = qE acts on every charge carrier]
    C --> D[Carrier accelerates between collisions]
    D --> E{Scattering Event Occurs?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Randomization of velocity direction]
    F --> G[New random thermal velocity assigned]
    G --> D
    E -- No --> H[Time interval exceeds tau_c?]
    H --> I[Velocity drift component accumulates]
    I --> J[Ensemble average: v_d = mu * E]
    J --> K[Drift current density J = q n v_d]
    K --> L[Macroscopic current I = J * A measurable at terminals]
    L --> M[Output: Conductivity sigma = J / E]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Diagram: Mobility vs. Temperature Regimes

The following diagram is a structured flowchart describing the two dominant scattering regimes and their corresponding mobility behavior.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Electric Field E] --> B[Carrier Motion in Crystal]
    B --> C1[Phonon Lattice Vibrations]
    B --> C2[Ionized Impurity Centers]
    B --> C3[Crystal Defects and Neutral Impurities]
    C1 --> D1[Lattice Mobility mu_L proportional to T to the power minus 3/2]
    C2 --> D2[Impurity Mobility mu_I proportional to T to the power plus 3/2]
    C3 --> D3[Defect Mobility mu_def approximately T independent]
    D1 --> E[Matthiessen Sum: 1 over mu_total equals 1 over mu_L plus 1 over mu_I plus 1 over mu_def]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Effective Drift Velocity v_d equals mu_total times E]
```

### 4.3 Analytical Block Diagram: Microscopic vs. Macroscopic View of Drift

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MICROSCOPIC_LEVEL
        M1[Individual Carrier Mass m star] --> M2[Mean Free Time tau_c between collisions]
        M2 --> M3[Instantaneous Velocity from acceleration a equals qE over m star]
    end
    MICROSCOPIC_LEVEL --> MACROSCOPIC_LEVEL
    subgraph MACROSCOPIC_LEVEL
        MA1[Ensemble Mobility mu equals q tau_c over m star] --> MA2[Average Drift Velocity v_d equals mu times E]
        MA2 --> MA3[Total Current Density J equals sigma times E]
    end
    MA3 --> OUT[Engineering Measurable Quantity: Current I equals J times Cross Section Area]
```

### 4.4 Velocity Saturation Block Architecture

At very high electric fields ($E > 10^4$ V/cm in Si), the linear $v_d = \mu E$ relationship breaks down. The following flowchart shows the transition to velocity saturation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Low Field E less than 10 to power 3 V per cm] --> B[Linear Region: v_d equals mu_0 times E]
    A1[Intermediate Field E between 10 to power 3 and 10 to power 5] --> C[Sub-linear Region: mobility decreases]
    A2[High Field E greater than 10 to power 5 V per cm] --> D[Saturation Region: v_d approaches v_sat]
    B --> E[Ohmic regime: J proportional to E]
    C --> F[Hot carrier regime: optical phonon emission]
    D --> G[Velocity Saturation: v_d equals v_sat approximately 10 to power 7 cm per s in Si]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define drift velocity and mobility in a semiconductor. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Drift Velocity ($v_d$):** The average net velocity acquired by charge carriers (electrons or holes) due to the application of an external electric field, superimposed on their random thermal motion.
>
> **Mobility ($\mu$):** The proportionality constant relating the drift velocity of carriers to the applied electric field. It is a measure of *how easily* carriers can move through the crystal lattice under the influence of an electric field.
>
> **Relationship:** $v_d = \mu E$, where $E$ is the applied electric field.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of drift velocity: 1 mark
- Definition of mobility: 1 mark
- Relationship $v_d = \mu E$: 1 mark

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the effect of temperature on the mobility of charge carriers in a semiconductor?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The effect of temperature on mobility is governed by the dominant scattering mechanism:
>
> 1. **At higher temperatures (near and above room temperature):** Lattice (phonon) scattering dominates. Mobility **decreases** with temperature as $\mu_L \propto T^{-3/2}$.
>
> 2. **At lower temperatures (below ~100 K in lightly doped Si):** Ionized impurity scattering dominates. Mobility **increases** with temperature as $\mu_I \propto T^{+3/2}$.
>
> 3. **The combined behavior** is captured by **Matthiessen's rule**:
> $$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_L} + \frac{1}{\mu_I}$$
>
> The total mobility shows a peak at some intermediate temperature.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Mention of phonon vs. impurity scattering: 1.5 marks
- Temperature dependence (sign and exponent): 1 mark
- Matthiessen's rule: 0.5 mark

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the drift velocity of charge carriers in a semiconductor under a low applied electric field. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An n-type silicon sample at $T = 300$ K has a donor concentration of $N_D = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻³ and the donor states are completely ionized. Given $\mu_n = 1300$ cm²/V·s, $\mu_p = 450$ cm²/V·s, and $n_i = 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, calculate:
- (i) The electron and hole concentrations.
- (ii) The conductivity of the sample.
- (iii) The drift current density when an electric field of $E = 100$ V/cm is applied. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Drift Velocity:**

Consider an electron of effective mass $m_n^*$ subjected to an applied electric field $E$. The equation of motion including the phenomenological scattering (relaxation-time) term is:

$$m_n^* \frac{dv}{dt} = -qE - \frac{m_n^* v}{\tau_c}$$

where $\tau_c$ is the mean free time (average time between collisions). In steady state, $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = -qE - \frac{m_n^* v_d}{\tau_c}$$

Solving for $v_d$:

$$v_d = -\frac{q\tau_c}{m_n^*} E$$

The negative sign indicates that the electron drifts *opposite* to the field direction. The magnitude of drift velocity is:

$$|v_d| = \frac{q\tau_c}{m_n^*} E = \mu_n E$$

where the **mobility** is defined as $\mu_n = \frac{q\tau_c}{m_n^*}$.

**Assumptions Used:**
1. The electric field is sufficiently low (ohmic regime) that the carrier energy is not significantly altered.
2. The mean free time $\tau_c$ is independent of the applied field.
3. The effective mass $m^*$ is constant (parabolic band approximation).
4. All donors are fully ionized.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Carrier Concentrations:**

Since the sample is n-type with $N_D \gg n_i$, the electron concentration is approximately equal to the donor concentration:

$$n \approx N_D = 5 \times 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

Using the mass-action law $np = n_i^2$:

$$p = \frac{n_i^2}{n} = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{10})^2}{5 \times 10^{15}} = \frac{10^{20}}{5 \times 10^{15}} = 2 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**(ii) Conductivity:**

$$\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$$

The hole contribution is negligible ($p\mu_p = 2 \times 10^4 \times 450 = 9 \times 10^6 \ll n\mu_n = 6.5 \times 10^{18}$):

$$\sigma \approx qn\mu_n = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(5 \times 10^{15})(1300)$$

$$\sigma = 1.04 \, \text{S/cm}$$

**(iii) Drift Current Density:**

By Ohm's law in local form:

$$J_{drift} = \sigma E = (1.04)(100) = 104 \, \text{A/cm}^2$$

**Mark Distribution:**
- Part (a) Equation of motion setup: 2 marks
- Steady-state solution: 2 marks
- Mobility definition: 1 mark
- Assumptions: 2 marks
- Part (b)(i) n and p: 2 marks
- Part (b)(ii) σ: 2 marks
- Part (b)(iii) J: 3 marks

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the effect of the following on the mobility of carriers in a semiconductor: (i) Temperature, (ii) Doping concentration. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Two silicon samples A and B are characterized as follows:
- Sample A: Intrinsic, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, $\mu_n = 1350$ cm²/V·s, $\mu_p = 480$ cm²/V·s.
- Sample B: n-type with $N_D = 10^{14}$ cm⁻³, $\mu_n = 1350$ cm²/V·s, $\mu_p = 480$ cm²/V·s.

Calculate the resistivity of each sample and comment on the result. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Effect of Temperature and Doping on Mobility:**

**Temperature Effect:**
At higher temperatures, the amplitude of lattice vibrations (phonons) increases, leading to more frequent scattering events. The lattice (phonon) scattering-limited mobility follows:

$$\mu_L \propto T^{-3/2}$$

As temperature increases, $\mu_L$ decreases. At lower temperatures, ionized impurity scattering dominates:

$$\mu_I \propto T^{+3/2}$$

The overall mobility is governed by **Matthiessen's rule**:

$$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_L} + \frac{1}{\mu_I}$$

The mobility-temperature curve exhibits a peak at an intermediate temperature.

**Doping Concentration Effect:**
Higher doping introduces more ionized impurity centers, which act as additional scattering sites. The impurity-limited mobility follows:

$$\mu_I \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{N_I \ln(\ldots)}$$

As doping $N_I$ increases, $\mu_I$ decreases. Therefore, **mobility decreases with increasing doping concentration**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Sample A (Intrinsic):**

$$\sigma_A = qn_i(\mu_n + \mu_p) = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.5 \times 10^{10})(1350 + 480)$$

$$\sigma_A = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.5 \times 10^{10})(1830) = 4.392 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{S/cm}$$

$$\rho_A = \frac{1}{\sigma_A} = \frac{1}{4.392 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.28 \times 10^{5} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$$

**Sample B (n-type, $N_D = 10^{14}$ cm⁻³):**

Assuming $N_D \gg n_i$ and full ionization, $n \approx N_D = 10^{14}$ cm⁻³, $p = n_i^2/N_D = 2.25 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³.

$$\sigma_B = qn\mu_n = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{14})(1350) = 2.16 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{S/cm}$$

$$\rho_B = \frac{1}{\sigma_B} \approx 46.3 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$$

**Comment:** The intrinsic sample (A) has a resistivity ~5000 times higher than the lightly doped n-type sample (B). This dramatic difference demonstrates the power of doping in controlling the conductivity of semiconductors — the foundation of all modern electronic devices.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Part (a) Temperature effect with formula: 2 marks
- Part (a) Doping effect with formula: 2 marks
- Part (a) Matthiessen's rule: 1 mark
- Part (a) Diagram description: 2 marks
- Part (b) Sample A calculation: 3 marks
- Part (b) Sample B calculation: 3 marks
- Part (b) Comparison comment: 1 mark

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Drift Problems (PCECT302)**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in Drift Velocity:** For electrons, $v_{dn} = -\mu_n E$ (the negative sign is critical). For holes, $v_{dp} = +\mu_p E$. Forgetting the sign or applying it incorrectly leads to zero marks for the velocity term.
>
> 2. **Confusing Drift with Diffusion:** Drift is caused by an **electric field** and is proportional to mobility. Diffusion is caused by a **concentration gradient** and is proportional to the diffusion coefficient. KTU examiners frequently frame "trick" questions where students confuse the two mechanisms.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Charge in Conductivity:** The conductivity formula is $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$, NOT $(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$. Dropping the $q$ factor results in a unit mismatch and a -1 mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Neglecting the Minority Carrier Term:** In lightly doped intrinsic or near-intrinsic samples, BOTH $n$ and $p$ contributions matter. In heavily doped n-type samples, the $p\mu_p$ term is negligible. Always check the ratio before simplifying.
>
> 5. **Wrong Temperature Exponent:** Lattice scattering gives $T^{-3/2}$ (decreasing). Impurity scattering gives $T^{+3/2}$ (increasing). Reversing these signs is a very common KTU error.
>
> 6. **Mixing Up Mobility Units:** SI units are **m²/(V·s)**. CGS units (commonly used in KTU problems) are **cm²/(V·s)**. The conversion is $1 \, m^2/(V \cdot s) = 10^4 \, cm^2/(V \cdot s)$. Forgetting this conversion will yield an answer off by a factor of $10^4$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Drift Transport in Semiconductors**

- **Drift velocity** is the *average* velocity of carriers in the direction of the applied electric field, superimposed on their random thermal motion.
- **Mobility** $\mu$ is defined by $v_d = \mu E$ and microscopically equals $\frac{q\tau_c}{m^*}$.
- The **drift current density** is $J_{drift} = \sigma E$, where $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$.
- The **resistivity** is the reciprocal of conductivity: $\rho = 1/\sigma$.
- **Mean free time** $\tau_c$ is the average time between two successive scattering collisions.
- **Mean free path** $\lambda$ is the average distance between collisions: $\lambda = v_{th} \tau_c$.
- **Thermal velocity** is $v_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{8k_B T}{\pi m^*}}$ for a 3D Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution.
- **Lattice (phonon) scattering** dominates at *high* temperature: $\mu_L \propto T^{-3/2}$.
- **Ionized impurity scattering** dominates at *low* temperature: $\mu_I \propto T^{+3/2}$.
- **Matthiessen's rule** combines independent scattering mechanisms: $\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_L} + \frac{1}{\mu_I}$.
- Mobility **decreases** with increasing doping concentration.
- Typical room-temperature electron mobility in Si: $\mu_n \approx 1350 \, cm^2/(V \cdot s)$.
- Typical room-temperature hole mobility in Si: $\mu_p \approx 480 \, cm^2/(V \cdot s)$.
- At very high electric fields, $v_d$ **saturates** at $v_{sat} \approx 10^7 \, cm/s$ in silicon.
- Drift current is **unidirectional** with the field; diffusion current flows from high to low concentration.
- The **direction** of electron drift is *opposite* to the electric field; the direction of hole drift is *along* the electric field.
- Always remember: $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, C$, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K$.
- Intrinsic carrier concentration at 300 K in Si: $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \, cm^{-3}$.
- The **mass-action law** $np = n_i^2$ is valid at thermal equilibrium and is essential for relating $n$ and $p$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Drift problems in Module 1 almost always follow the same pattern: (1) identify the doping type, (2) determine $n$ and $p$ from $N_D$/$N_A$ and $n_i$, (3) compute $\sigma$ from the conductivity formula, (4) compute $J$ from $J = \sigma E$ or $v_d = \mu E$. Master this four-step pipeline and you will secure full marks on every drift numerical in the KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conductivity and Mobility
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conductivity and Mobility

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrical Conductivity ($\sigma$)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Electrical Conductivity** is defined as the measure of a material's ability to conduct electric current. It is the reciprocal of **resistivity ($\rho$)** and is quantified as the ratio of the current density ($J$) flowing through the material to the applied electric field ($E$).
> 
> Mathematically: $\sigma = \dfrac{J}{E}$ and has SI units of **Siemens per meter (S/m)** or equivalently **$(\Omega \cdot m)^{-1}$**.

In semiconductor physics, conductivity is **not a constant** like in pure metals — it depends **strongly** on:
- The concentration of free charge carriers
- The temperature
- The type and amount of dopants added

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, conductivity is treated as a **tensor property** in advanced analysis, but for Module 1, we restrict it to an **isotropic scalar** property.

## 1.2 Carrier Mobility ($\mu$)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Carrier Mobility** is defined as the **steady-state drift velocity** acquired by a charge carrier per unit applied electric field. It quantifies *how quickly* a carrier can move through the crystal lattice under the influence of an external field.
> 
> Mathematically: $\mu = \dfrac{v_d}{E}$ with units of **$m^2/(V \cdot s)$** or **$cm^2/(V \cdot s)$** (commonly used in textbooks).

**Physical meaning**: A high mobility means carriers zip through the semiconductor quickly; a low mobility means they lumber along, even under a strong field.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Pipe" Model

Imagine a horizontal water pipe with water flowing through it:

| Water Pipe Concept | Semiconductor Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Diameter of the pipe** | Cross-sectional area of the conductor |
| **Number of water molecules flowing** | Charge carrier concentration ($n$ for electrons, $p$ for holes) |
| **Speed of water flow** | Drift velocity ($v_d$) |
| **Pressure difference across pipe** | Applied electric field ($E$) |
| **Ease of water flow** | Conductivity ($\sigma$) |
| **Smoothness of pipe interior** | Carrier Mobility ($\mu$) — fewer bumps = higher mobility |

- A **wider pipe (more carriers)** carries more water → more current.
- A **smoother pipe (higher mobility)** allows faster water flow.
- **Conductivity** captures BOTH effects (number of carriers AND how fast they move).

> [!TIP]
> If you remember only one thing: **$\sigma = n \cdot q \cdot \mu$** — conductivity is the product of *how many* carriers and *how fast* they move.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drift Current — The Foundation

When an external electric field $E$ is applied across a semiconductor, the free carriers (electrons and holes) acquire a **net directional motion** superimposed on their random thermal motion. This directed motion constitutes the **drift current**.

The **drift velocity** for each carrier type is:

$$v_{d,n} = \mu_n \cdot E \quad \text{(for electrons)}$$

$$v_{d,p} = \mu_p \cdot E \quad \text{(for holes)}$$

Where:
- $\mu_n$ = **electron mobility** (electrons move opposite to $E$)
- $\mu_p$ = **hole mobility** (holes move in the direction of $E$)
- $E$ = applied electric field magnitude

> [!NOTE]
> In a typical semiconductor like Silicon: $\mu_n \approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ and $\mu_p \approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$. Electrons are always more mobile than holes because their effective mass is smaller.

## 2.2 Drift Current Density Equations

The drift current density contributed by **electrons**:

$$J_{n,\text{drift}} = n \cdot q \cdot \mu_n \cdot E$$

The drift current density contributed by **holes**:

$$J_{p,\text{drift}} = p \cdot q \cdot \mu_p \cdot E$$

Where:
- $n$ = free electron concentration (per $m^3$)
- $p$ = free hole concentration (per $m^3$)
- $q$ = magnitude of electronic charge = **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**

**Total drift current density** (both carriers moving):

$$J_{\text{drift}} = J_{n,\text{drift}} + J_{p,\text{drift}} = \left(n \cdot q \cdot \mu_n + p \cdot q \cdot \mu_p\right) \cdot E$$

## 2.3 The Master Conductivity Equation

Comparing with Ohm's law in point form ($J = \sigma E$), we arrive at the **most important equation in this module**:

$$\boxed{\sigma = q \left( n \cdot \mu_n + p \cdot \mu_p \right)}$$

This is the **KTU board exam favorite** — it will appear directly in derivations and numerical problems.

### 2.3.1 Special Case: Intrinsic Semiconductor

In an **intrinsic (pure)** semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$, so:

$$\sigma_i = q \cdot n_i \left(\mu_n + \mu_p\right)$$

### 2.3.2 Special Case: n-type Extrinsic Semiconductor

In an **n-type** semiconductor, $n \gg p$ (electrons are *majority carriers*). The minority holes contribute negligibly:

$$\sigma_n \approx n \cdot q \cdot \mu_n \approx N_D \cdot q \cdot \mu_n$$

Where $N_D$ is the donor concentration (since $n \approx N_D$ for full ionization).

### 2.3.3 Special Case: p-type Extrinsic Semiconductor

In a **p-type** semiconductor, $p \gg n$:

$$\sigma_p \approx p \cdot q \cdot \mu_p \approx N_A \cdot q \cdot \mu_p$$

Where $N_A$ is the acceptor concentration.

## 2.4 Scattering Mechanisms — Why Mobility Has Limits

If the crystal were perfect, mobility would be **infinite**. In reality, carriers collide with various imperfections. KTU 2024 requires knowledge of:

### 2.4.1 Lattice (Phonon) Scattering
- Caused by **thermal vibrations of lattice atoms**
- Dominates at **high temperatures** ($T > 200$ K in Si)
- Mobility dependence: $\mu_L \propto T^{-3/2}$
- Higher temperature → more lattice vibration → more collisions → lower mobility

### 2.4.2 Ionized Impurity Scattering
- Caused by collisions with **dopant ions** (charged $N_D^+$ or $N_A^-$)
- Dominates at **low temperatures** (dopants are ionized, lattice is calm)
- Mobility dependence: $\mu_I \propto \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{N_I}$ where $N_I$ is ionized impurity concentration

### 2.4.3 Total Mobility — Mathiessen's Rule (Approximate)

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\mu_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_L} + \dfrac{1}{\mu_I}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **NOT a strict physical law** but an empirical approximation that works well when the two mechanisms are independent. KTU examiners love asking: *"Why is total mobility always LESS than the smaller of the two mobilities?"* — Answer: The two scattering mechanisms add resistances in series.

## 2.5 Temperature Dependence of Conductivity

The temperature dependence of $\sigma$ is dominated by the **carrier concentration** (exponential) rather than mobility (power law):

$$\sigma(T) \propto \exp\left(\dfrac{-E_g}{2 k_B T}\right) \quad \text{(intrinsic regime)}$$

For extrinsic semiconductors:
- At low $T$: freeze-out region — carriers not yet ionized
- At mid $T$: extrinsic region — $\sigma$ roughly constant or slowly varying
- At high $T$: intrinsic region — $\sigma$ rises sharply

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of $\sigma$ and $\mu$ |
|---|---|
| **MOSFET design** | Channel mobility determines ON-current and switching speed |
| **Solar cells** | High mobility → longer carrier lifetime → better collection efficiency |
| **High-frequency devices (HEMT, HBT)** | GaAs, InGaAs used because $\mu_n$ is 5–10× higher than Si |
| **Power electronics** | Wide-bandgap (SiC, GaN) trade off mobility for breakdown voltage |
| **Integrated circuit interconnects** | Cu (high $\sigma$) replaces Al to reduce RC delay |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_d = \mu E$ | Drift velocity | $m/s$ |
| 2 | $J_n = nq\mu_n E$ | Electron drift current density | $A/m^2$ |
| 3 | $J_p = pq\mu_p E$ | Hole drift current density | $A/m^2$ |
| 4 | $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ | General conductivity | S/m |
| 5 | $\sigma_i = qn_i(\mu_n + \mu_p)$ | Intrinsic conductivity | S/m |
| 6 | $\sigma_n \approx N_D q \mu_n$ | n-type conductivity | S/m |
| 7 | $\sigma_p \approx N_A q \mu_p$ | p-type conductivity | S/m |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{\mu} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_L} + \dfrac{1}{\mu_I}$ | Mathiessen's rule | $m^2/(V\cdot s)$ |
| 9 | $\mu_L \propto T^{-3/2}$ | Lattice mobility scaling | — |
| 10 | $\mu_I \propto T^{3/2}/N_I$ | Impurity mobility scaling | — |
| 11 | $\rho = 1/\sigma$ | Resistivity | $\Omega \cdot m$ |
| 12 | $R_s = \rho / t$ | Sheet resistance | $\Omega/\square$ |

> [!TIP]
> The symbol `\vert` (rendered as $\vert$) is used in exam scripts for "magnitude" — but in your **KTU answer sheet**, plain vertical bars are fine. The formula sheet above uses $\vert$ notation only for clean rendering.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: The Conductivity Equation from First Principles

### Objective
Derive the relationship $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ starting from the definition of drift current.

### Given
- An n-type semiconductor bar of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$
- Applied voltage $V$, producing electric field $E = V/L$
- Electron concentration $n$, hole concentration $p$

### Step 1: Identify Total Charge Crossing a Plane

Consider an imaginary cross-section perpendicular to current flow. In time $\Delta t$, each electron drifts a distance $v_{d,n} \cdot \Delta t$. Therefore, the **volume** of material swept through the plane (per unit area) is $v_{d,n} \cdot \Delta t$.

The **number of electrons** crossing per unit area in time $\Delta t$ is:

$$N_{n,\text{cross}} = n \cdot v_{d,n} \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 2: Compute the Charge Crossing

Each electron carries charge $-q$. So the charge due to electrons crossing per unit area in time $\Delta t$:

$$Q_{n} = -q \cdot n \cdot v_{d,n} \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 3: Compute the Electron Current Density

Current density is charge per unit area per unit time:

$$J_{n,\text{drift}} = \dfrac{Q_n}{A \cdot \Delta t} = -q \cdot n \cdot v_{d,n}$$

Wait — but current is conventionally taken in the direction of positive charge flow. Since electrons flow opposite to $E$, we write the **magnitude** of electron current density in the direction of $E$ as:

$$J_{n,\text{drift}} = n \cdot q \cdot v_{d,n} = n \cdot q \cdot \mu_n \cdot E$$

(We absorbed the sign into the definition of $\mu_n$ being positive for the magnitude of drift.)

### Step 4: Repeat for Holes

Holes drift *with* the field, so:

$$J_{p,\text{drift}} = p \cdot q \cdot v_{d,p} = p \cdot q \cdot \mu_p \cdot E$$

### Step 5: Total Current Density

Since both carrier types contribute current in the same direction (by convention):

$$J_{\text{total}} = J_{n,\text{drift}} + J_{p,\text{drift}} = \left(n \cdot q \cdot \mu_n + p \cdot q \cdot \mu_p\right) E$$

### Step 6: Compare with Ohm's Law

By Ohm's law in point form: $J = \sigma \cdot E$. Equating the two expressions:

$$\sigma \cdot E = q \left( n \mu_n + p \mu_p \right) E$$

Dividing both sides by $E$ (assuming $E \neq 0$):

$$\boxed{\sigma = q \left( n \mu_n + p \mu_p \right)} \qquad \text{Q.E.D.}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II: Why Mobility Has a Velocity Saturation

### Objective
Show that drift velocity is **not** proportional to $E$ for arbitrarily large fields.

### Step 1: Low-Field Regime

At low fields ($E < 10^3$ V/cm for Si), mobility is constant:

$$v_d = \mu \cdot E \quad \text{(linear, Ohmic regime)}$$

### Step 2: High-Field Regime

At high fields, carriers gain energy faster than they can lose it to the lattice via phonon emission. The carrier effective temperature rises above the lattice temperature. This **reduces** mobility.

Empirically:

$$v_d = \dfrac{\mu_0 E}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 E}{v_{\text{sat}}}\right)^{\beta}\right]^{1/\beta}}$$

For Silicon: $v_{\text{sat}} \approx 1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s and $\beta \approx 2$.

### Step 3: Limiting Behavior

As $E \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{E \to \infty} v_d = v_{\text{sat}}$$

This is **velocity saturation**, a critical concept for short-channel MOSFETs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 typically asks students to plot $v_d$ vs $E$ and identify the saturation region. The effective mobility drops as $E$ increases.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Temperature Coefficient of Conductivity

### Objective
Show that $\dfrac{1}{\sigma} \dfrac{d\sigma}{dT}$ is a critical engineering parameter.

### Step 1: Write Conductivity for Extrinsic n-type

$$\sigma_n(T) = N_D \cdot q \cdot \mu_n(T)$$

### Step 2: Take the Logarithm and Differentiate

$$\ln \sigma_n = \ln(N_D q) + \ln \mu_n(T)$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$:

$$\dfrac{1}{\sigma_n} \dfrac{d\sigma_n}{dT} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n} \dfrac{d\mu_n}{dT}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Power-Law Mobility

For lattice-scattering dominated mobility, $\mu_n \propto T^{-3/2}$, so $\dfrac{d\mu_n}{dT} = -\dfrac{3}{2} \mu_n T^{-1}$. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{1}{\sigma_n} \dfrac{d\sigma_n}{dT} = -\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{1}{T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For intrinsic regime, the carrier concentration term dominates, and the temperature coefficient becomes **positive and large** (conductivity *increases* with temperature — opposite to metals).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Scenario | Dominant Carriers | Conductivity Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic Si, 300 K | $n = p = n_i$ | $\sigma_i = qn_i(\mu_n + \mu_p)$ | $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ |
| Lightly doped n-type | Electrons majority | $\sigma \approx qN_D\mu_n$ | $N_D \gg n_i$ |
| Lightly doped p-type | Holes majority | $\sigma \approx qN_A\mu_p$ | $N_A \gg n_i$ |
| Compensated semiconductor | Both types significant | $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ | Use full expression |
| High temperature (intrinsic) | $n = p$ regained | $\sigma_i = qn_i(\mu_n + \mu_p)$ | Even with doping |
| Low temperature (freeze-out) | $n \approx 0$ | $\sigma \to 0$ | Carriers not yet ionized |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Block Architecture of Conductivity Determinants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conductivity sigma] --> B[Carrier Concentration n or p]
    A --> C[Charge q]
    A --> D[Carrier Mobility mu]
    B --> B1[Intrinsic Carriers ni]
    B --> B2[Dopant Concentration ND or NA]
    B --> B3[Temperature T]
    D --> D1[Lattice Scattering mu L]
    D --> D2[Impurity Scattering mu I]
    D1 --> D1a[Phonon Vibration]
    D1 --> D1b[High T Dominates]
    D2 --> D2a[Ionized Dopants]
    D2 --> D2b[Low T Dominates]
    D1 --> E[Mathiessen Combination]
    D2 --> E
    E --> A
    B --> A
    C --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **causal dependencies** of conductivity. Use it as a mental model for answering *"What factors affect $\sigma$?"* questions in KTU exams.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Flowchart: Determining Dominant Carriers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Given Semiconductor] --> Q1{Is it pure or doped?}
    Q1 -->|Pure| Pure[Intrinsic: n equals p equals ni]
    Q1 -->|Doped| Q2{Type of dopant?}
    Q2 -->|Donor ND| Ntype[n type: n approx ND, p minor]
    Q2 -->|Acceptor NA| Ptype[p type: p approx NA, n minor]
    Pure --> Eq1[sigma i equals q ni mu n plus mu p]
    Ntype --> Eq2[sigma n approx q ND mu n]
    Ptype --> Eq3[sigma p approx q NA mu p]
    Eq1 --> Output[Apply Correct Conductivity Formula]
    Eq2 --> Output
    Eq3 --> Output
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Scattering Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Carrier in Motion] --> SC{Which scattering dominates?}
    SC -->|T greater than 200K in Si| LS[Lattice Scattering mu L]
    SC -->|T less than 100K with dopants| IS[Impurity Scattering mu I]
    SC -->|Mid range| Both[Both contribute]
    LS --> LR[mu L proportional to T to power minus 1.5]
    IS --> IR[mu I proportional to T to power 1.5 over NI]
    Both --> MR[Mathiessen 1 over mu total equals 1 over mu L plus 1 over mu I]
    LR --> Final[Total Mobility]
    IR --> Final
    MR --> Final
    Final --> Conductivity[sigma equals q n mu total]
```

        ─── ─── ─── ─── ─── ─── ─── ─── ─── ─── ─── (no acceptor levels in n-type)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define **carrier mobility** in a semiconductor. Explain why electron mobility is greater than hole mobility in Silicon.

**Model Answer:**

> **Carrier Mobility** is defined as the steady-state drift velocity acquired by a charge carrier per unit applied electric field.
> 
> $$\mu = \dfrac{v_d}{E} \quad \text{units: } m^2/(V \cdot s)$$
> 
> **Why $\mu_n > \mu_p$ in Silicon:**
> 
> 1. **Effective mass difference**: The effective mass of electrons in Si is $m_n^* = 1.08 \, m_0$, while the effective mass of holes is $m_p^* = 0.56 \, m_0$ (for heavy holes, it is even higher).
> 2. **Mobility is inversely proportional to effective mass**:
> 
> $$\mu = \dfrac{q\tau}{m^*}$$
> 
> 3. Since holes have higher effective mass, their mobility is lower.
> 4. Additionally, holes suffer more inter-valley scattering due to the degenerate valence band structure (heavy-hole, light-hole, and split-off bands).
> 
> **Result**: In Si at 300 K, $\mu_n \approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ and $\mu_p \approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$.

**[Marks Distribution: 1 mark definition, 2 marks explanation]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

Distinguish between **intrinsic** and **extrinsic** conductivity. Give the relevant expressions.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Intrinsic Conductivity | Extrinsic Conductivity |
|---|---|---|
| Material purity | Pure semiconductor | Doped semiconductor |
| Carrier source | Thermal generation across $E_g$ | Thermal ionization of dopants |
| Carrier equality | $n = p = n_i$ | $n \neq p$ |
| Conductivity formula | $\sigma_i = qn_i(\mu_n + \mu_p)$ | $\sigma_n = qN_D\mu_n$ (n-type) or $\sigma_p = qN_A\mu_p$ (p-type) |
| Magnitude | Very low at 300 K ($\sim 10^{-6}$ S/m for Si) | Much higher ($\sim 1$ to $10^3$ S/m) |
| Temperature dependence | Strong exponential rise with $T$ | Depends on regime (freeze-out, extrinsic, intrinsic) |

**[Marks Distribution: 2 marks comparison, 1 mark expressions]**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **electrical conductivity of an intrinsic semiconductor** in terms of carrier concentration and mobility. Explain the physical significance of each term. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A silicon sample at 300 K has electron and hole mobilities of $1350 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ and $480 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ respectively. The intrinsic carrier concentration is $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$. Calculate:
- (i) The intrinsic conductivity.
- (ii) The resistivity.
- (iii) The drift velocity of electrons under an applied field of $100$ V/cm. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1**: Consider a piece of intrinsic semiconductor of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ with applied voltage $V$ producing an electric field:

$$E = \dfrac{V}{L}$$

**Step 2**: Under the field, electrons drift opposite to $E$ with velocity $v_{d,n} = \mu_n E$, and holes drift along $E$ with velocity $v_{d,p} = \mu_p E$.

**Step 3**: Current density due to electrons:

$$J_n = n \cdot q \cdot \mu_n \cdot E$$

**Step 4**: Current density due to holes:

$$J_p = p \cdot q \cdot \mu_p \cdot E$$

**Step 5**: Total current density:

$$J = J_n + J_p = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)E$$

**Step 6**: Comparing with Ohm's law $J = \sigma E$:

$$\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$$

**Step 7**: For intrinsic case, $n = p = n_i$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_i = q n_i (\mu_n + \mu_p)}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- $q$: charge per carrier — larger $q$ means each carrier transports more charge.
- $n_i$: carrier concentration — more carriers means more current for the same field.
- $\mu_n, \mu_p$: mobility — higher mobility means carriers move faster, producing more current.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $\mu_n = 1350 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s}) = 1350 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$
- $\mu_p = 480 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s}) = 480 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3} = 1.5 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^{-3}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $E = 100$ V/cm $= 10^4$ V/m

**(i) Intrinsic conductivity:**

$$\sigma_i = q n_i (\mu_n + \mu_p)$$

$$\sigma_i = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{16}) \times (1350 + 480) \times 10^{-4}$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$q n_i = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{16}) = 2.403 \times 10^{-3} \text{ C/m}^3$$

$$\mu_n + \mu_p = (1350 + 480) \times 10^{-4} = 1830 \times 10^{-4} = 0.183 \text{ m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$$

$$\sigma_i = (2.403 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.183) = 4.398 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S/m}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_i \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S/m}}$$

**(ii) Resistivity:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i} = \dfrac{1}{4.4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\rho \approx 2272.7 \text{ } \Omega \cdot m \approx 2.27 \times 10^3 \text{ } \Omega \cdot m}$$

**(iii) Drift velocity of electrons:**

$$v_{d,n} = \mu_n \cdot E = 1350 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s}) \times 100 \text{ V/cm}$$

$$\boxed{v_{d,n} = 1.35 \times 10^5 \text{ cm/s} = 1.35 \times 10^3 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** What is **carrier mobility**? Discuss the various **scattering mechanisms** that limit mobility in a semiconductor. Explain **Mathiessen's rule** for combining mobilities. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An n-type silicon sample at 300 K is doped with $N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ donors. Given $\mu_n = 1350 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ and $\mu_p = 480 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$, calculate:
- (i) The conductivity of the sample.
- (ii) The hole concentration at equilibrium (use $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$).
- (iii) Comment on the contribution of holes to the total conductivity. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Theory [7 marks]:**

**Definition of Carrier Mobility [2 marks]:**

Carrier mobility is the steady-state drift velocity per unit applied electric field:

$$\mu = \dfrac{v_d}{E} \quad \text{units: } \text{cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$$

It represents how *swiftly* a charge carrier moves through the crystal under an applied field.

**Scattering Mechanisms [4 marks]:**

**1. Lattice (Phonon) Scattering:**
- Thermal vibrations of lattice atoms create quantized lattice waves called **phonons**.
- Carriers collide with phonons and lose momentum.
- **Dominant at high temperatures** (above 200 K in Si).
- Temperature dependence: $\mu_L \propto T^{-3/2}$
- As $T$ increases, lattice vibrations increase → more collisions → $\mu_L$ decreases.

**2. Ionized Impurity Scattering:**
- Dopant atoms (donors or acceptors) become ionized ($N_D^+$ or $N_A^-$) in the lattice.
- These charged centers create **Coulomb potential wells** that deflect passing carriers.
- **Dominant at low temperatures** and in heavily doped samples.
- Temperature dependence: $\mu_I \propto \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{N_I}$
- As doping $N_I$ increases, scattering increases, and $\mu_I$ decreases.

**3. Other Scattering (brief mention):**
- Neutral impurity scattering (very low T)
- Surface scattering (in thin films / MOSFET channels)
- Carrier-carrier scattering (in degenerate semiconductors)

**Mathiessen's Rule [1 mark]:**

The combined effect of independent scattering mechanisms:

$$\dfrac{1}{\mu_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_L} + \dfrac{1}{\mu_I} + \dfrac{1}{\mu_{\text{others}}}$$

The total mobility is **always less** than the smallest individual mobility (analogous to resistors in series).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$
- $\mu_n = 1350 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$
- $\mu_p = 480 \text{ cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**(i) Conductivity:**

For n-type: $n \approx N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $p$ is negligible.

$$\sigma = q \cdot N_D \cdot \mu_n$$

$$\sigma = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (10^{15} \times 10^6) \times (1350 \times 10^{-4})$$

Converting units: $N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3} = 10^{21} \text{ m}^{-3}$ and $\mu_n = 0.135 \text{ m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$:

$$\sigma = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (10^{21}) \times (0.135)$$

$$\sigma = (1.602 \times 10^{2}) \times (0.135) = 21.6 \text{ S/m}$$

Working in CGS to avoid conversions:

$$\sigma = (1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) \times (10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}) \times (1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$$

$$\sigma = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15} \times 1350$$

$$\sigma = 1.602 \times 10^{-4} \times 1350 = 0.2163 \text{ S/cm}$$

Converting to S/m:

$$\boxed{\sigma = 0.2163 \times 100 = 21.63 \text{ S/m}}$$

**(ii) Hole concentration:**

Using the mass-action law $n \cdot p = n_i^2$:

$$p = \dfrac{n_i^2}{N_D} = \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}{10^{15}} = \dfrac{2.25 \times 10^{20}}{10^{15}}$$

$$\boxed{p = 2.25 \times 10^5 \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

**(iii) Comment on hole contribution:**

$$J_p = q \cdot p \cdot \mu_p = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (2.25 \times 10^5) \times (480)$$

$$J_p \propto p \cdot \mu_p = 2.25 \times 10^5 \times 480 = 1.08 \times 10^8$$

For electrons:

$$J_n \propto n \cdot \mu_n = 10^{15} \times 1350 = 1.35 \times 10^{18}$$

Ratio:

$$\dfrac{J_p}{J_n} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^8}{1.35 \times 10^{18}} \approx 8 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Comment:** The hole contribution is **negligibly small** (about $10^{-10}$ times the electron contribution) because:
- Hole concentration $p$ is **5 orders of magnitude lower** than $n$.
- Hole mobility $\mu_p$ is also about **3× smaller** than $\mu_n$.

This justifies the approximation $\sigma \approx q N_D \mu_n$ for extrinsic n-type semiconductors.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks for):**
> 
> 1. **Unit conversion errors**: $\mu$ is often given in $cm^2/(V\cdot s)$ but $n$ in $cm^{-3}$ and $\sigma$ in S/m. Mixed units cause dimensional chaos. Convert **all** quantities to consistent SI or CGS before plugging in.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the charge of hole carriers**: In the drift current expression $J_p = p \cdot q \cdot \mu_p \cdot E$, the $q$ is the magnitude of the electronic charge (always $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C). Students often drop it or use the wrong sign.
> 
> 3. **Confusing $\mu$ and $\sigma$**: Mobility is a property of *one* carrier; conductivity includes *all* carriers and the charge.
> 
> 4. **Applying Mathiessen's rule in the wrong direction**: It is $\dfrac{1}{\mu} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\mu_i}$, NOT $\mu = \sum \mu_i$.
> 
> 5. **In intrinsic calculations, students write $n_i$ in cm⁻³ but $\mu$ in SI without conversion**, leading to off-by-4 orders of magnitude.
> 
> 6. **Missing the velocity saturation concept** for high-field problems.
> 
> 7. **In Part B (b) numericals**, examiners expect a clear step-by-step substitution with unit cancellation. Writing just the final answer loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Key Definitions
- **Conductivity ($\sigma$)**: $J/E$ — measures ease of current flow. Units: **S/m**.
- **Mobility ($\mu$)**: $v_d/E$ — measures carrier drift speed per unit field. Units: **$m^2/(V\cdot s)$**.
- **Resistivity ($\rho$)**: $1/\sigma$. Units: $\Omega \cdot m$.
- **Drift velocity ($v_d$)**: $\mu E$ — net directional motion of carriers.

### Master Equations (Must Memorize)
- $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ — **the most important equation**
- $\sigma_i = qn_i(\mu_n + \mu_p)$ — intrinsic case
- $\sigma_n \approx qN_D\mu_n$ — n-type extrinsic
- $\sigma_p \approx qN_A\mu_p$ — p-type extrinsic
- $\dfrac{1}{\mu_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_L} + \dfrac{1}{\mu_I}$ — Mathiessen's rule

### Physical Constants (Commit to Memory)
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $n_i (\text{Si, 300 K}) = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³
- $\mu_n (\text{Si, 300 K}) \approx 1350$ cm²/(V·s)
- $\mu_p (\text{Si, 300 K}) \approx 480$ cm²/(V·s)
- $v_{\text{sat}} (\text{Si}) \approx 10^7$ cm/s

### Trends to Remember
- $\mu_n > \mu_p$ always (electrons have smaller effective mass)
- $\mu \propto T^{-3/2}$ at high $T$ (lattice scattering)
- $\mu \propto T^{3/2}$ at low $T$ (impurity scattering, dopants ionized)
- $\sigma$ **decreases** with $T$ in metals, **increases** with $T$ in semiconductors
- $v_d$ saturates at high $E$ (velocity saturation)

### Engineering Insights
- High-mobility materials (GaAs, InSb, InGaAs) chosen for high-speed devices
- Heavy doping → low mobility but high conductivity (used in contacts)
- Sheet resistance $R_s = \rho/t$ is the practical figure-of-merit for thin films

### Common KTU Question Patterns
1. **Derive** $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ from first principles.
2. **Compare** $\mu_n$ and $\mu_p$ with physical reasoning.
3. **Plot** $\ln \sigma$ vs $1/T$ to identify regimes.
4. **Calculate** conductivity / resistivity / drift velocity given doping.
5. **Discuss** scattering mechanisms and Mathiessen's rule.

### One-Line Summary
> **Conductivity = how much + how fast + how much charge** — i.e., $\sigma = q \cdot n \cdot \mu$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variation of Mobility with Temperature and Doping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variation of Mobility with Temperature and Doping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carrier Mobility ($\mu$)** is formally defined as the steady-state drift velocity ($\vec{v_d}$) acquired by a charge carrier per unit applied electric field ($\vec{E}$):

$$\vec{v_d} = \mu \, \vec{E}$$

For electrons (drift velocity opposite to field):
$$\vec{v_{d,n}} = -\mu_n \, \vec{E}$$

For holes (drift velocity along the field):
$$\vec{v_{d,p}} = \mu_p \, \vec{E}$$

In a semiconductor crystal, the carrier does not accelerate indefinitely. It undergoes frequent **scattering events** due to imperfections in the periodic lattice potential. The **mean time between successive scattering events** is called the **Mean Free Time** or **Relaxation Time** ($\tau$), measured in **seconds (s)**. Mobility is mathematically related to $\tau$ by:

$$\mu = \frac{q \tau}{m^*}$$

where $q$ is the elementary charge (**1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**) and $m^*$ is the **effective mass** of the carrier (kg).

The variation of mobility with temperature ($T$) and doping concentration ($N$) is determined by the dominant **scattering mechanisms** active in the crystal at any given operating condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> Students must be able to analyze and quantify how mobility $\mu$ changes with temperature (in Kelvin) and impurity concentration (in $\text{cm}^{-3}$), and identify which scattering mechanism dominates in a given temperature-doping regime.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are walking through a crowded market (a semiconductor crystal). You are the **charge carrier** (electron or hole) trying to move toward an exit (the applied electric field direction). Two things can slow you down:

1. **Thermal Vibrations of the Stalls (Phonon/Lattice Scattering):** When the market gets hot (high temperature), the stall owners start jumping, dancing, and waving their hands.
