| Einstein Relation (holes) | D_p / μ_p | kT / q = V_T | V | Same thermal voltage |
| Thermal Voltage (300 K) | V_T | kT/q | ≈ 25.85 mV | Often rounded to 26 mV |
| Diffusion Coeff. (Si, 300 K) | D_n (μ_n ≈ 1350) | μ_n × V_T | cm²/s | ≈ 35 cm²/s for Si |
| Diffusion Coeff. (Si, 300 K) | D_p (μ_p ≈ 480) | μ_p × V_T | cm²/s | ≈ 12.5 cm²/s for Si |
| Boltzmann constant | k | 1.38 × 10⁻²³ | J/K | Always positive |
| Elementary charge | q | 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ | C | Magnitude used |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar cells (photovoltaics):** The Einstein relation governs the diffusion length L = √(Dτ) of photogenerated carriers, which directly determines the **quantum efficiency**.
- **CMOS technology:** Used in deriving subthreshold swing, threshold voltage sensitivity, and short-channel effects in nanoscale MOSFETs.
- **Bipolar junction transistors (BJTs):** The minority carrier diffusion currents in the base region are computed using D_n, D_p derived from the Einstein relation.
- **Semiconductor sensors & photodetectors:** Response time and bandwidth depend on the diffusion transit time, which is fundamentally tied to the mobility-diffusion link.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Einstein Relation

We start from the **total electron current density** equation in a non-degenerate semiconductor under the influence of an electric field **E** and a concentration gradient **dn/dx**:

$$J_n = q n \mu_n E + q D_n \frac{dn}{dx}$$

**Line-by-line logical breakdown:**

- **Line 1 — Apply thermal equilibrium condition:** At equilibrium, the total current must be zero (no net flow of charge), hence:

$$J_n = 0$$

- **Line 2 — Substitute and divide by q:**

$$n \mu_n E + D_n \frac{dn}{dx} = 0$$

- **Line 3 — Rearrange to isolate the concentration gradient term:**

$$D_n \frac{dn}{dx} = -n \mu_n E$$

- **Line 4 — Divide both sides by n and D_n:**

$$\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dx} = -\frac{\mu_n}{D_n} E$$

- **Line 5 — Use the Boltzmann statistics relation:** In a non-degenerate semiconductor at equilibrium, the carrier concentration is governed by:

$$n(x) = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C(x) - E_F}{kT}\right)$$

Since E_F is constant at equilibrium, taking the logarithmic derivative:

$$\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dx} = -\frac{1}{kT} \frac{dE_C}{dx}$$

- **Line 6 — Relate dE_C/dx to the electric field E:** The conduction band edge energy is related to the electrostatic potential V by:

$$E_C = -qV + \text{constant}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dE_C}{dx} = -q \frac{dV}{dx} = qE$$

(using E = -dV/dx, but flipping the sign because we define E along the field direction).

- **Line 7 — Combine Steps 5 and 6:**

$$\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dx} = -\frac{1}{kT}(qE) = -\frac{qE}{kT}$$

- **Line 8 — Substitute back into the equilibrium equation (Line 4):**

$$-\frac{qE}{kT} = -\frac{\mu_n}{D_n} E$$

- **Line 9 — Cancel E on both sides (assuming E ≠ 0 in general):**

$$\frac{q}{kT} = \frac{\mu_n}{D_n}$$

- **Line 10 — Invert to obtain the final Einstein relation:**

$$\boxed{\frac{D_n}{\mu_n} = \frac{kT}{q} = V_T}$$

By symmetry, repeating the entire derivation for **holes** (using J_p = 0) yields:

$$\boxed{\frac{D_p}{\mu_p} = \frac{kT}{q} = V_T}$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Evaluation at Room Temperature (T = 300 K)

Let us evaluate the thermal voltage numerically:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K})(300 \text{ K})}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$$

- **Step 1 — Numerator:** kT = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ × 300 = 4.14 × 10⁻²¹ J
- **Step 2 — Division:** 4.14 × 10⁻²¹ / 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 0.025875 V
- **Step 3 — Final result:** V_T ≈ 25.875 mV ≈ **25.85 mV** (often taken as **26 mV** in KTU numericals).

For silicon at 300 K:
- μ_n ≈ 1350 cm²/V·s → D_n = 1350 × 0.02585 = **34.9 cm²/s**
- μ_p ≈ 480 cm²/V·s → D_p = 480 × 0.02585 = **12.4 cm²/s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** Whenever a problem gives mobility, you can **always** find the diffusion coefficient using the Einstein relation with V_T = 26 mV. No need to memorise D_n or D_p values!

---

### 3.3 Verification by Dimensional Analysis

- [μ] = cm² / (V·s)
- [D] = cm² / s
- [D/μ] = (cm²/s) / (cm²/(V·s)) = V
- [kT/q] = (J/K × K) / C = J/C = V ✓

The relation is **dimensionally consistent**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — Connecting Drift and Diffusion via Einstein Relation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Semiconductor at Thermal Equilibrium] --> B{Two Transport Mechanisms}
    B --> C[Drift Mechanism]
    B --> D[Diffusion Mechanism]
    C --> C1[Cause: Electric Field E]
    C --> C2[Carrier velocity v = mu x E]
    C --> C3[Current density J_drift = q n mu E]
    D --> D1[Cause: Concentration Gradient dn/dx]
    D --> D2[Flux phi = -D x dn/dx]
    D --> D3[Current density J_diff = q D dn/dx]
    C3 --> E[Total Current J_n = J_drift + J_diff]
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[At Equilibrium: J_n = 0]
    F --> G[Apply Boltzmann Statistics]
    G --> H[Use n x proportional to exp -E_C over kT]
    H --> I[Substitute and Simplify]
    I --> J[Einstein Relation: D over mu = kT over q = V_T]
    J --> K[Applies to Electrons and Holes]
    K --> L[Thermal Voltage V_T approx 26 mV at 300 K]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Transport Phenomena Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Gradients]
        A1[Electric Field E]
        A2[Concentration Gradient dn/dx]
    end
    subgraph MECHANISM[Physical Transport Mechanisms]
        B1[Drift Engine]
        B2[Diffusion Engine]
    end
    subgraph COUPLING[Equilibrium Coupling]
        C1[Boltzmann Carrier Distribution]
        C2[Zero Net Current Condition]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Relations]
        D1[D_n = mu_n x V_T]
        D2[D_p = mu_p x V_T]
        D3[V_T = kT over q]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation — Energy Band Tilted by Electric Field

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EQUIL[Semiconductor at Thermal Equilibrium]
        E1[Conduction Band E_C tilts downward in -x direction]
        E2[Fermi Level E_F is CONSTANT flat line across device]
        E3[Valence Band E_V parallel to E_C]
        E4[Slope of bands gives E = 1 by q dE_C over dx]
    end
    E1 --> F1[Carrier density n x increases along E_C descent]
    E2 --> F2[dn over dx in equilibrium follows Boltzmann law]
    E3 --> F3[Slope tied to E]
    E4 --> F4[Einstein Relation emerges from equating drift and diffusion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `nodeA`, `B1`, `C2`). No reserved keywords are used. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold or italics formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Einstein relation in a semiconductor. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Einstein relation is a fundamental thermodynamic relation that connects the diffusion coefficient (D) and mobility (μ) of charge carriers in a non-degenerate semiconductor at thermal equilibrium:

$$\frac{D_n}{\mu_n} = \frac{D_p}{\mu_p} = \frac{kT}{q} = V_T$$

where k is Boltzmann's constant, T is absolute temperature, q is the electron charge, and V_T is the thermal voltage (≈ 26 mV at 300 K).

**Significance:**
- It bridges drift and diffusion transport mechanisms (1 mark).
- It enables the conversion of mobility to diffusion coefficient, which is essential for minority carrier diffusion current calculations in p–n junctions, BJTs, and MOSFETs (1 mark).
- It is derived from the requirement of zero net current at thermal equilibrium, combined with Boltzmann statistics (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is the Einstein relation not valid for heavily doped (degenerate) semiconductors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Einstein relation is derived under the assumption of a **non-degenerate semiconductor**, where the Fermi level lies at least 3kT away from the band edges and the Boltzmann approximation n = N_C exp(−(E_C − E_F)/kT) holds (1 mark).

In **heavily doped (degenerate) semiconductors**, the Fermi level moves into the conduction band (n-type) or valence band (p-type), and the Boltzmann approximation fails (1 mark).

The correct generalisation uses **Fermi–Dirac statistics**, which introduces an additional correction factor (ratio of Fermi–Dirac integrals F_{1/2}(η) / F_{−1/2}(η)), so that the simple proportionality D/μ = kT/q no longer holds (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the Einstein relation for electrons in a non-degenerate semiconductor starting from the condition of zero current at thermal equilibrium. (7 marks)

**(b)** For silicon at 300 K, the electron mobility is μ_n = 1350 cm²/V·s and the hole mobility is μ_p = 480 cm²/V·s. Calculate the diffusion coefficients D_n and D_p, and the minority carrier diffusion lengths in a p-type region with minority carrier lifetime τ_n = 1 μs. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The total electron current density in a semiconductor is:

$$J_n = q n \mu_n E + q D_n \frac{dn}{dx} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** At thermal equilibrium, J_n = 0:

$$q n \mu_n E + q D_n \frac{dn}{dx} = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Rearranging:

$$D_n \frac{dn}{dx} = -n \mu_n E$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Divide by n·D_n:

$$\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dx} = -\frac{\mu_n}{D_n} E \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply Boltzmann statistics. The electron density in a non-degenerate semiconductor at equilibrium is:

$$n(x) = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C(x) - E_F}{kT}\right)$$

Since E_F is constant at equilibrium, the logarithmic derivative gives:

$$\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dx} = -\frac{1}{kT}\frac{dE_C}{dx}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Relate dE_C/dx to E. Since E_C = −qV (plus constant) and E = −dV/dx:

$$\frac{dE_C}{dx} = qE$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dx} = -\frac{qE}{kT} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Equating (2) and (3):

$$-\frac{\mu_n}{D_n} E = -\frac{qE}{kT}$$

$$\frac{\mu_n}{D_n} = \frac{q}{kT}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final result:

$$\boxed{\frac{D_n}{\mu_n} = \frac{kT}{q} = V_T}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate thermal voltage at T = 300 K:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585 \text{ V} \approx 26 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Einstein relation to find D_n:

$$D_n = \mu_n \cdot V_T = 1350 \times 0.02585 = 34.9 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply Einstein relation to find D_p:

$$D_p = \mu_p \cdot V_T = 480 \times 0.02585 = 12.4 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Calculate minority carrier diffusion length in p-type region (for electrons):

$$L_n = \sqrt{D_n \cdot \tau_n} = \sqrt{34.9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$L_n = \sqrt{3.49 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.91 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm} = 59.1 \text{ \mu m}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Starting from the Einstein relation, show that the minority carrier diffusion current density in a p–n junction can be expressed in terms of mobility. (7 marks)

**(b)** An n-type silicon sample at 300 K has electron concentration n = 10¹⁶ cm⁻³. If a steady-state electric field of 100 V/cm is applied, calculate (i) the drift current density, (ii) the diffusion current density if the concentration gradient is dn/dx = 10¹⁸ cm⁻⁴, and (iii) verify that under equilibrium, the drift and diffusion currents exactly cancel. Given μ_n = 1350 cm²/V·s. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Theoretical Linkage (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The minority carrier (hole) diffusion current density in a p-region is:

$$J_p = -q D_p \frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Einstein relation D_p = μ_p · V_T:

$$J_p = -q \mu_p V_T \frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** This shows that diffusion current can be expressed as an "equivalent drift" term with an effective field:

$$E_{eff} = V_T \cdot \frac{1}{p}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Hence the total hole current becomes:

$$J_p = q p \mu_p E - q \mu_p V_T \frac{dp}{dx} = q \mu_p \left(p E - V_T \frac{dp}{dx}\right)$$

This is the **drift-diffusion formulation** used universally in semiconductor device modelling (e.g., in SPICE).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** n = 10¹⁶ cm⁻³, E = 100 V/cm, dn/dx = 10¹⁸ cm⁻⁴, μ_n = 1350 cm²/V·s, T = 300 K.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Thermal voltage V_T = 26 mV = 0.026 V.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Drift current density:

$$J_{drift} = q n \mu_n E = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{16})(1350)(100)$$

$$J_{drift} = 216 \text{ A/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Diffusion current density (first compute D_n using Einstein relation):

$$D_n = \mu_n \cdot V_T = 1350 \times 0.026 = 35.1 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$J_{diff} = q D_n \frac{dn}{dx} = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(35.1)(10^{18})$$

$$J_{diff} = 5.616 \text{ A/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Verification of equilibrium (Einstein relation confirms cancellation when E and dn/dx are in the right ratio):

At equilibrium, the relation is:

$$\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dx} = \frac{qE}{kT} = \frac{E}{V_T}$$

Check: E / V_T = 100 / 0.026 ≈ 3846 cm⁻¹
and (1/n)(dn/dx) = 10¹⁸ / 10¹⁶ = 100 cm⁻¹

Since the two are not equal, this is **not an equilibrium** configuration. The Einstein relation provides the *unique* gradient that exactly cancels drift for a given E.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors (–2 marks typical):** Students often forget the sign of the diffusion current for electrons. Always write J_n,total = drift + diffusion with **explicit sign convention** from your textbook.
> 2. **Confusing D_n with D_p (–1 to –2 marks):** In a p-type region, the **minority carrier** is the electron, so use D_n (not D_p).
> 3. **Forgetting units (–1 mark):** Mobility in cm²/V·s requires E in V/cm and D in cm²/s. Mixing SI and CGS units is the **#1 reason** for wrong answers in KTU numericals.
> 4. **Stating only the formula, not the derivation (–3 to –5 marks):** A 7-mark derivation question **must** show the Boltzmann statistics step and the dE_C/dx = qE relationship. Simply writing D/μ = kT/q without justification fetches **zero** marks in KTU.
> 5. **Using V_T = 25 mV vs 26 mV:** KTU generally accepts either, but for consistency use **V_T = 0.02585 V** when T = 300 K is given explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Einstein Relations (Module 1):**

- **Core Equation:** D_n / μ_n = D_p / μ_p = kT / q = V_T ≈ **26 mV at 300 K**.
- **Derivation backbone:** Start from J_n = 0 → use Boltzmann statistics → relate (1/n)(dn/dx) to E via dE_C/dx = qE.
- **Validity:** Only for **non-degenerate** semiconductors under **thermal equilibrium** (or local quasi-equilibrium).
- **Failure condition:** Heavily doped / degenerate semiconductors require **Fermi–Dirac correction factors**.
- **Per-carrier independence:** The relation holds separately for electrons and holes — they share the same V_T but have different D and μ.
- **Thermal voltage rule of thumb:** V_T increases linearly with T → at T = 300 K, V_T ≈ 25.85 mV; at T = 400 K, V_T ≈ 34.5 mV.
- **Engineering utility:** Used in (i) minority carrier diffusion current of p–n diodes, (ii) base transport factor of BJTs, (iii) subthreshold swing of MOSFETs, (iv) photodetector response time.
- **Master conversion:** Given μ (mobility), you can ALWAYS find D using D = μ × V_T — never memorise D_n, D_p separately.
- **Dimensional check:** [D/μ] = [V] and [kT/q] = [J/C] = [V] — both are voltages.
- **Linked concepts:** Boltzmann statistics, drift-diffusion formalism, Fermi-Dirac integrals (advanced), quasi-Fermi levels.
- **Most common KTU question pattern:** "Derive the Einstein relation" (7 marks) + "Calculate D_n, D_p, L_n, L_p using given μ and τ" (7 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Poisson Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Poisson's Equation** is a second-order partial differential equation of elliptic type that relates a scalar potential field to its source — namely, the charge density distribution in space. In the context of solid-state electronics, it serves as the master governing equation that links the **electrostatic potential** $\psi(x,y,z)$ to the **local space-charge density** $\rho(x,y,z)$ within a semiconductor crystal.

The general three-dimensional form of Poisson's equation is:

$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

where:
- $\nabla^2$ is the **Laplacian operator** (divergence of the gradient).
- $\psi$ is the electrostatic potential in **Volts (V)**.
- $\rho$ is the volumetric charge density in **Coulombs per cubic meter ($\text{C/m}^3$)**.
- $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ is the **permittivity** of the semiconductor medium in **Farads per meter (F/m)**.
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ is the **vacuum permittivity**.

For one-dimensional analysis of planar devices (such as p-n junctions and MOS capacitors), the equation reduces to the simplified scalar form:

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$$

where $\varepsilon_s$ is the **semiconductor permittivity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Poisson's equation forms the mathematical backbone for analyzing the **space-charge region**, **depletion width**, **built-in potential**, and **electric field distribution** in all semiconductor junctions. Mastery of this equation is mandatory for solving KTU numerical problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Ball-Bearing on a Stretched Rubber Sheet"**

Imagine a flat, stretched rubber sheet representing empty space. Now, scatter ball-bearings on it. Where there are NO ball-bearings, the sheet remains flat (zero charge → flat potential). But where you drop a **HEAVY cluster** of ball-bearings (analogous to **positive charge density**), the sheet sags downward sharply — creating a steep depression (steep potential gradient → strong electric field).

Poisson's equation mathematically says: **"The curvature of the potential surface at any point is directly proportional to the local charge density."**

A second analogy uses **water flow**:
- The **electric potential** $\psi$ behaves like the **height of water** in a tank.
- The **electric field** $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla \psi$ behaves like the **slope** (steepness) of the water surface.
- The **charge density** $\rho$ behaves like a **water source or sink** — pouring water in (positive charge) creates a hump; draining water (negative charge) creates a dip.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook for KTU Exams:**
> - **No charge** ($\rho = 0$) → Equation becomes **Laplace's equation** ($\nabla^2 \psi = 0$) → potential varies **linearly**.
> - **Uniform charge** ($\rho = \text{const}$) → potential varies **parabolically**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are **must-memorize** for KTU numericals:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Vacuum permittivity | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}}$ |
| $\varepsilon_s$ | Semiconductor permittivity (Si) | $\mathbf{11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F/m}}$ |
| $q$ | Elementary charge | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$ |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}}$ |
| $T$ | Room temperature | $\mathbf{300 \text{ K}}$ |
| $V_T$ | Thermal voltage $k_BT/q$ | $\mathbf{\approx 0.0259 \text{ V}}$ at 300 K |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Pathway: From Gauss's Law to Poisson's Equation

Poisson's equation is **not** an empirical rule — it is a direct mathematical consequence of **Gauss's Law for Electricity**, one of the four foundational Maxwell's equations. The logical chain is:

1. **Start with Gauss's Law in differential form** (relates divergence of $\mathbf{E}$ to charge density):
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

2. **Recognize that the electric field is the negative gradient of the scalar potential** (a fundamental identity from electrostatics):
$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla \psi$$

3. **Substitute the gradient relation into Gauss's Law**:
$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla \psi) = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

4. **Recognize the divergence of a gradient as the Laplacian operator**:
$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

This elegant reduction converts a **vector field equation** into a **scalar field equation** — far easier to solve for boundary-value problems in semiconductor devices.

## 2.2 Why Poisson's Equation is Indispensable in Solid-State Devices

- It governs the **electric field profile** within depletion regions.
- It determines the **band bending** (potential variation) at junctions.
- It enables calculation of the **depletion width** $W$.
- It underlies the derivation of the **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$.
- It is the starting point for deriving the **capacitance-voltage (C-V) characteristics** of p-n and MOS structures.

## 2.3 Special Case: Laplace's Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the semiconductor region is **completely depleted** of mobile carriers (i.e., the space-charge region consists ONLY of ionized dopants), and the doping profile is **uniform**, then $\rho = qN_D$ or $\rho = -qN_A$ is **constant**. In the **neutral region** (far from the junction), $\rho \approx 0$ and the equation reduces to **Laplace's equation**:
> $$\nabla^2 \psi = 0$$

This contrast — Poisson inside the depletion region vs. Laplace outside — is a **favourite KTU question pattern**.

## 2.4 Charge Density Components in a Semiconductor

The total space-charge density in a semiconductor is the algebraic sum of four contributions:

$$\rho(x) = q\left(p - n + N_D^+ - N_A^-\right)$$

where:
- $p$ = **hole concentration** (mobile positive charge carriers)
- $n$ = **electron concentration** (mobile negative charge carriers)
- $N_D^+$ = **ionized donor concentration** (fixed positive charge)
- $N_A^-$ = **ionized acceptor concentration** (fixed negative charge)

> [!NOTE]
> **Under the Depletion Approximation** (the standard assumption in KTU problems), the mobile carrier concentrations ($n$ and $p$) are considered **negligibly small** within the depletion region, leaving only the **ionized dopants** as the source of charge.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\nabla^2 \psi = -\rho / \varepsilon$ | General 3-D Poisson's equation | Master equation |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$ | 1-D Poisson's equation | Planar device analysis |
| 3 | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ | Laplace's equation (charge-free region) | Neutral region analysis |
| 4 | $\rho = q(N_D - n + p - N_A)$ | Total charge density expression | Setting up Poisson's eq. |
| 5 | $\mathbf{E} = -\dfrac{d\psi}{dx}\,\hat{x}$ | Electric field from potential | Field profile |
| 6 | $E(x) = \dfrac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)$ | Field in uniform n-region | Step junction |
| 7 | $\psi(x) = -\dfrac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)^2$ | Potential in uniform n-region | Parabolic profile |
| 8 | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$ | Depletion width (abrupt junction) | p-n junction |
| 9 | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$ | Built-in potential | Junction barrier |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ | Semiconductor permittivity | Material property |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Always check whether the problem requires **depletion approximation** (most do) or **full depletion with mobile carriers**. This determines whether you use $\rho = qN_D$ or the full $\rho$ expression.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: From Gauss's Law to Poisson's Equation (Full Algebraic Walkthrough)

**Step 1:** State the integral form of Gauss's Law for a closed surface $S$ enclosing volume $V$:

$$\oint_S \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\int_V \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** (Gauss's theorem for converting surface integral to volume integral):

$$\oint_S \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two volume integrals (valid for any arbitrary volume $V$):

$$\int_V (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) \, dV = \int_V \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon} \, dV$$

**Step 4:** Since the equality holds for **any** volume, the integrands must be equal point-wise:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

This is the **differential form of Gauss's Law**.

**Step 5:** Recall the defining relation between electric field and scalar potential:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla \psi$$

The negative sign indicates that the electric field points from **high potential to low potential** (a fundamental convention in electrostatics).

**Step 6:** Compute the divergence of the gradient of $\psi$. By definition, this is the **Laplacian operator**:

$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla \psi) = -\nabla^2 \psi$$

**Step 7:** Substitute into the differential Gauss's Law:

$$-\nabla^2 \psi = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 8:** Multiply both sides by $-1$ to obtain the canonical form:

$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **Poisson's Equation** — derived rigorously from Gauss's Law, the Divergence Theorem, and the definition of electrostatic potential. **This derivation is a high-weight KTU question** (typically Part A, 3 marks).

## 3.2 Derivation II: Solving 1-D Poisson's Equation in a Uniformly Doped Depletion Region

**Problem Setup:** Consider an abrupt p-n junction with donor concentration $N_D$ on the n-side. The depletion region extends from $x = 0$ (metallurgical junction) to $x = x_n$ on the n-side. The space charge density is uniform: $\rho(x) = +qN_D$.

**Step 1:** Write the 1-D Poisson equation:

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = -\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate once with respect to $x$ to obtain the electric field $E(x) = -\dfrac{d\psi}{dx}$:

$$\frac{d\psi}{dx} = -\int \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,dx = -\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,x + C_1$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **boundary condition** that the electric field vanishes at the edge of the depletion region, $E(x_n) = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,x_n + C_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_1 = \frac{qN_D\,x_n}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $C_1$ back to obtain the electric field profile:

$$E(x) = -\frac{d\psi}{dx} = -\left(-\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,x + \frac{qN_D\,x_n}{\varepsilon_s}\right)$$

$$E(x) = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)$$

**Step 5:** Integrate a second time to get the potential:

$$\psi(x) = \int E(x)\,dx = \int \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)\,dx$$

$$\psi(x) = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\left(\frac{x^2}{2} - x_n x\right) + C_2$$

**Step 6:** Apply the boundary condition that $\psi(0) = 0$ at the metallurgical junction (reference potential):

$$0 = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,(0 - 0) + C_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_2 = 0$$

**Step 7:** Final potential expression:

$$\psi(x) = \frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}\left(x^2 - 2x_n x\right) = -\frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)^2 + \frac{qN_D\,x_n^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

This **parabolic** potential profile is a hallmark of a uniformly doped depletion region.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Total Built-in Potential from Poisson's Equation

**Step 1:** Apply the same procedure on the p-side, where $\rho = -qN_A$ and depletion extends to $x = -x_p$:

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = +\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 2:** Integrating with boundary conditions yields a similar parabolic profile on the p-side.

**Step 3:** The total built-in potential $V_{bi}$ equals the total potential drop across the entire depletion region:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s}\left(N_D x_n^2 + N_A x_p^2\right)$$

**Step 4:** Use **charge neutrality** of the depletion region: $N_D x_n = N_A x_p$.

**Step 5:** Combine with the depletion width $W = x_n + x_p$ to obtain the **famous KTU formula**:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{q N_D N_A W^2}{2\varepsilon_s (N_D + N_A)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Key Equation Forms

| Region | Charge Density $\rho$ | Governing Equation | Solution Type | Profile Shape |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| Neutral n-region | $\rho \approx 0$ | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ (Laplace) | Linear | Flat band |
| n-side depletion | $\rho = +qN_D$ | $\nabla^2 \psi = -qN_D/\varepsilon_s$ | Parabolic | Band bends up |
| Neutral p-region | $\rho \approx 0$ | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ (Laplace) | Linear | Flat band |
| p-side depletion | $\rho = -qN_A$ | $\nabla^2 \psi = +qN_A/\varepsilon_s$ | Parabolic | Band bends down |
| Inversion layer (MOS) | $\rho$ complex | Modified Poisson | Numerical | Steep bend |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Solve Strategy:** When asked to find the electric field or potential in a depletion region, immediately: (1) write 1-D Poisson, (2) integrate ONCE for $E(x)$, (3) integrate AGAIN for $\psi(x)$, (4) apply boundary conditions. This is a guaranteed 7-mark pattern.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Logical Flow from Physical Law to Device Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Maxwell Gauss Law Integral Form"] --> nodeB["Apply Divergence Theorem"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Differential Form Div grad E equals rho over epsilon"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Use Identity E equals minus grad psi"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Poisson Equation Laplacian psi equals minus rho over epsilon"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Region Type Decision"}
    nodeF -- "Charge Free Region rho = 0" --> nodeG["Laplace Equation Laplacian psi = 0"]
    nodeF -- "Uniform Charge rho = qN" --> nodeH["Solve 1D Poisson Integrate Twice"]
    nodeF -- "Non Uniform Charge" --> nodeI["Numerical Integration"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Linear Potential Profile"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Parabolic Potential Profile"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["Numerical Solution Required"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["Apply to P N Junction Depletion Width"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["Apply to MOS Structure Band Bending"]
    nodeK --> nodeO["Apply to Schottky Barrier Analysis"]
    nodeM --> nodeP["Device Performance Predictions"]
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeP
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Poisson-Based Device Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput1["Input Doping Profile N_D x and N_A x"] --> nodeProc1["Block A Set Up Charge Density Function rho x"]
    nodeProc1 --> nodeProc2["Block B Write 1D Poisson Equation"]
    nodeProc2 --> nodeProc3["Block C First Integration yields E x"]
    nodeProc3 --> nodeProc4["Block D Apply Boundary Condition E = 0 at depletion edge"]
    nodeProc4 --> nodeProc5["Block E Second Integration yields psi x"]
    nodeProc5 --> nodeProc6["Block F Apply Boundary Condition psi = 0 at junction"]
    nodeProc6 --> nodeOutput1["Output 1 Electric Field Profile E x"]
    nodeProc6 --> nodeOutput2["Output 2 Potential Profile psi x"]
    nodeProc6 --> nodeOutput3["Output 3 Depletion Width W"]
    nodeProc6 --> nodeOutput4["Output 4 Built in Potential V_bi"]
    nodeOutput1 --> nodeFinal["Final Result Device Characterization"]
    nodeOutput2 --> nodeFinal
    nodeOutput3 --> nodeFinal
    nodeOutput4 --> nodeFinal
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Poisson Equation Solution Pathways Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start Poisson Equation Del squared psi = minus rho over epsilon"] --> nodeQ1{"Is rho = 0?"}
    nodeQ1 -- "YES Neutral Region" --> nodeLap["Use Laplace Equation Linear Solution"]
    nodeQ1 -- "NO Space Charge Region" --> nodeQ2{"Is rho = constant?"}
    nodeQ2 -- "YES Uniform Doping" --> nodePara["Parabolic Solution Analytical Closed Form"]
    nodeQ2 -- "NO Graded Doping" --> nodeQ3{"Is rho = a times x?"}
    nodeQ3 -- "YES Linear Doping" --> nodeCubic["Cubic Solution Analytical Closed Form"]
    nodeQ3 -- "NO Arbitrary Profile" --> nodeNum["Numerical Methods Finite Difference or Finite Element"]
    nodeLap --> nodeEnd["Solution psi x Obtained"]
    nodePara --> nodeEnd
    nodeCubic --> nodeEnd
    nodeNum --> nodeEnd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box represents a logical step or decision point. The arrows (edges) represent the flow of mathematical reasoning. The "diamond" boxes (with question marks) are decision points where the analysis path branches depending on the form of $\rho(x)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Poisson's equation. State its significance in the analysis of semiconductor devices.**

**Model Answer:**

Poisson's equation is a fundamental second-order partial differential equation of electrostatics that relates the spatial variation of the electrostatic potential $\psi$ to the local charge density $\rho$:

$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Significance in semiconductor devices:**
1. It is the master equation for determining the **electric field distribution** within the depletion region of p-n junctions, Schottky barriers, and MOS structures.
2. It enables calculation of the **potential profile** and hence the **band bending** at semiconductor interfaces.
3. It forms the theoretical foundation for deriving the **depletion width**, **built-in potential**, and **junction capacitance**.
4. It is essential for modeling **space-charge-limited current** in semiconductor devices.

*(3 marks — exact KTU expected answer length)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Poisson's equation and Laplace's equation. Under what condition does Poisson's equation reduce to Laplace's equation?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Poisson's Equation | Laplace's Equation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Form** | $\nabla^2 \psi = -\rho/\varepsilon$ | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ |
| **Charge density** | Non-zero ($\rho \neq 0$) | Zero ($\rho = 0$) |
| **Region type** | Space-charge region (depletion) | Charge-free / neutral region |
| **Solution type** | Parabolic, cubic, or numerical | Linear (in 1-D) |
| **Physical meaning** | Curvature is finite | Potential varies without curvature |

**Condition for reduction:** Poisson's equation reduces to Laplace's equation when the **charge density $\rho = 0$** throughout the region of interest, i.e., in a charge-free or fully depleted region outside the space-charge zone.

*(3 marks — tabular format preferred in KTU valuation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive Poisson's equation starting from Gauss's law in integral form. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a one-sided abrupt p$^+$-n junction with $N_A \gg N_D$, derive the expression for the depletion width $W$ in terms of the applied reverse bias $V_R$ and built-in potential $V_{bi}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Assumption 1:** We work in the **electrostatic (time-independent) regime** — no time-varying magnetic fields.

**Assumption 2:** The medium is **linear, homogeneous, and isotropic**, so that $\varepsilon$ is a constant scalar (not a tensor).

**Step 1:** Gauss's Law in integral form:

$$\oint_S \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\int_V \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Divergence Theorem to convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$\oint_S \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two volume integrals:

$$\int_V (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) \, dV = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\int_V \rho \, dV$$

**Step 4:** Since the equality holds for any arbitrary volume, the integrands must be equal point-wise:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5:** Use the electrostatic potential relation $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla \psi$:

$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla \psi) = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize $\nabla \cdot \nabla \psi = \nabla^2 \psi$ (the Laplacian):

$$-\nabla^2 \psi = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 7:** Final canonical form:

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \psi = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

This is Poisson's equation, derived rigorously. *(7 marks — full marks require all 7 steps)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Write 1-D Poisson's equation on the lightly-doped n-side (depletion approximation):

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = -\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate once to get the electric field. Let the depletion edge on the n-side be at $x = x_n$:

$$E(x) = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)$$

**Step 3:** Integrate again to get the potential. Use the reference $\psi(0) = 0$ at the metallurgical junction:

$$\psi(x) = -\frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)^2 + \frac{qN_D x_n^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4:** The total potential drop across the n-side depletion region (from $x = 0$ to $x = x_n$) is:

$$V_{n} = \psi(0) - \psi(x_n) = \frac{qN_D x_n^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 5:** For a one-sided p$^+$-n junction, $N_A \gg N_D$, so virtually all the depletion region extends into the n-side. Therefore $W \approx x_n$ and:

$$V_{n} = \frac{qN_D W^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 6:** The total voltage across the junction is $V_{bi} - V_R$ (where $V_R$ is the reverse bias, taken as positive for reverse bias):

$$V_{bi} - V_R = \frac{qN_D W^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

Wait — in a one-sided junction, the p$^+$-side drop is negligible, so $V_{bi} - V_R$ is sustained entirely by the n-side.

**Step 7:** Solving for $W$:

$$\boxed{W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_R)}{qN_D}}}$$

with the built-in potential:

$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

*(7 marks — full marks require derivation, NOT just the final formula)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from Poisson's equation, derive the expression for the electric field and potential in a uniformly doped n-type depletion region. Sketch the variation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An abrupt silicon p-n junction at 300 K has $N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ and $N_A = 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$. Calculate: (i) built-in potential $V_{bi}$, (ii) depletion width $W$ at zero bias, (iii) depletion width at a reverse bias of 5 V. Take $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \varepsilon_0$, and $V_T = 0.0259$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1:** 1-D Poisson's equation in the n-depletion region (charge density $\rho = +qN_D$):

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = -\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 2:** First integration (E-field). Boundary condition: $E(x_n) = 0$:

$$E(x) = -\frac{d\psi}{dx} = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)$$

**Step 3:** Maximum field at $x = 0$:

$$E_{\max} = -\frac{qN_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4:** Second integration (Potential). Boundary condition: $\psi(0) = 0$:

$$\psi(x) = \frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}(x^2 - 2x_n x)$$

**Step 5:** Sketch: $E(x)$ is a **straight line** with slope $qN_D/\varepsilon_s$, starting at $E_{\max}$ at $x=0$ and reaching zero at $x = x_n$. $\psi(x)$ is an **inverted parabola** rising from zero at the junction to $V_n$ at the depletion edge.

*(7 marks — sketch is mandatory for full marks in KTU)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $N_A = 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $T = 300$ K, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $V_T = 0.0259$ V, $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm $= 1.036 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm.

**(i) Built-in potential:**

$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right)$$

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{33}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(4.44 \times 10^{12})$$

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times 29.12 = 0.754 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Depletion width at zero bias:**

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

Since $N_A \gg N_D$, the $1/N_A$ term is negligible:

$$W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.754}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15}}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{1.562 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{9.76 \times 10^{-9}} = 9.88 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 0.988 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

**(iii) Depletion width at $V_R = 5$ V:**

The total voltage across the junction is $V_{bi} + V_R = 0.754 + 5 = 5.754$ V (reverse bias adds to the built-in potential):

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 5.754}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15}}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{1.193 \times 10^{-11}}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{7.45 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.73 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 2.73 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

*(7 marks — calculator-friendly numbers; KTU expects 3 significant figures)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Poisson's Equation:** Many students write $\nabla^2 \psi = +\rho/\varepsilon$ instead of $-\rho/\varepsilon$. The **negative sign is mandatory** and carries 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting Boundary Conditions:** Stating the final $E(x)$ or $\psi(x)$ formula WITHOUT applying boundary conditions loses 2-3 marks. Always explicitly state: $E(x_n) = 0$ and $\psi(0) = 0$.
> 3. **Confusing $V_{bi} - V_R$ vs. $V_{bi} + V_R$:** Under **forward bias**, use $V_{bi} - V_F$. Under **reverse bias**, use $V_{bi} + V_R$. This is a notorious 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Units Mismatch:** Convert $N_D$ from $\text{cm}^{-3}$ to $\text{m}^{-3}$ (multiply by $10^6$) when $\varepsilon_s$ is in F/m. OR keep everything in CGS — **pick one system and stick to it**.
> 5. **Missing Sketch:** For "sketch" type questions, omitting the diagram costs 2 marks even if the analytical part is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Poisson's Equation (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Master Equation:** $\nabla^2 \psi = -\rho/\varepsilon$ (1-D form: $d^2\psi/dx^2 = -\rho(x)/\varepsilon_s$).
- ✅ **Origin:** Derived from **Gauss's Law** + **Divergence Theorem** + $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla\psi$.
- ✅ **Special Case (Laplace):** When $\rho = 0$, equation reduces to $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ — potential varies **linearly**.
- ✅ **Uniformly Doped Depletion:** Potential varies **parabolically**; Electric field varies **linearly**.
- ✅ **Charge Neutrality Condition:** $N_D x_n = N_A x_p$ — must be used to relate depletion widths on the two sides of an abrupt junction.
- ✅ **Depletion Approximation:** $n \approx p \approx 0$ inside the depletion region — only ionized dopants contribute to $\rho$.
- ✅ **Built-in Potential:** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ — depends on doping and temperature.
- ✅ **Depletion Width Formula:** $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q}\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$ (reverse bias).
- ✅ **One-Sided Junction Shortcut:** If $N_A \gg N_D$ (or vice versa), simplify to $W \approx \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R) / (qN_{\text{light}})}$.
- ✅ **Maximum Electric Field:** Occurs at the metallurgical junction; magnitude $|E_{\max}| = qN_D x_n / \varepsilon_s = 2(V_{bi} + V_R)/W$.
- ✅ **Physical Constants to Memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V_T = 0.0259$ V at 300 K.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Electric field points from n to p in the built-in depletion region (from high to low potential).
- ✅ **Order of Magnitude Check (Si):** Typical depletion width is in **$\mu$m** range; built-in potential is **~0.7 V** for silicon p-n junctions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuity Equation** is a fundamental conservation law in semiconductor physics that describes how the concentration of charge carriers (free electrons and holes) evolves with time at any point inside a semiconductor crystal, accounting for three physical mechanisms simultaneously:

1. **Drift and Diffusion** — the net carrier flux entering or leaving the volume.
2. **Generation** — the creation of electron–hole pairs by thermal, optical, or high-energy processes.
3. **Recombination** — the annihilation of electron–hole pairs.

Mathematically, the continuity equations for electrons and holes are written as:

$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q}\,\nabla \cdot \vec{J_n} + (G_n - R_n)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\,\frac{1}{q}\,\nabla \cdot \vec{J_p} + (G_p - R_p)
$$

where **q** is the elementary charge ($1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$), **n** and **p** are the electron and hole concentrations (cm⁻³), **J_n** and **J_p** are the electron and hole current densities (A/cm²), and **G_n, R_n, G_p, R_p** are the respective generation and recombination rates (cm⁻³ s⁻¹).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECT302 – Module 1):** Continuity equations form the mathematical backbone for analyzing the *transient* and *steady-state* behavior of excess carriers in semiconductors. Mastering this topic is essential for understanding **pn-junction diodes, BJT base-transit time, and MOS device switching**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank with Inlet and Outlet"

Imagine a **cylindrical water tank** that already has a small hole leaking water out the bottom (recombination) and is being filled by a tap whose flow can be turned up or down (generation). At the same time, water can also *flow into* or *out of* the tank from a neighbouring region through a pipe (drift + diffusion).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Continuity Equation = "Conservation of water molecules in the tank."**
> *Rate of water-level change* = *Net inflow from pipe* + *Tap inflow* − *Leak outflow*.

Translating this to semiconductor physics:
- The **tank** is a small control volume $\Delta V$ inside the crystal.
- The **pipe** is the current density flux (drift + diffusion) crossing the surface.
- The **tap** is the generation process (creating e⁻–h⁺ pairs).
- The **leak** is the recombination process (destroying e⁻–h⁺ pairs).

Just as the water level cannot change unless there is a net flow or a source/sink, the **local carrier concentration cannot change without flux divergence or generation-recombination imbalance**. This is the essence of charge-carrier continuity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin and Derivation Logic

The continuity equation is built from two pillars of classical physics:

### Pillar 1 — Conservation of Charge (Kirchhoff's Current Law in Differential Form)
For a control volume of cross-section $A$ and thickness $\Delta x$, the net current flowing **into** the volume must equal the rate of charge accumulation **minus** any internal charge creation:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(q\,n\,A\,\Delta x) = I_n(x) - I_n(x+\Delta x) + q\,A\,\Delta x\,(G_n - R_n)
$$

Dividing by $q\,A\,\Delta x$ and taking the limit $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q}\,\frac{\partial J_n}{\partial x} + (G_n - R_n)
$$

> The **negative sign for holes** appears because the conventional current direction of holes is opposite to their physical flow direction. Hence:
> $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q}\,\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} + (G_p - R_p)$$

### Pillar 2 — Current Density Equation (Drift + Diffusion)
The total current density for each carrier type is the superposition of drift (field-driven) and diffusion (concentration-gradient-driven) components:

$$
\vec{J_n} = q\,n\,\mu_n\,\vec{E} + q\,D_n\,\nabla n
$$

$$
\vec{J_p} = q\,p\,\mu_p\,\vec{E} - q\,D_p\,\nabla p
$$

where $\mu_n$ and $\mu_p$ are mobilities (cm²/V·s) and $D_n$ and $D_p$ are diffusivities (cm²/s).

### Combining the Two Pillars
Substituting the drift-diffusion current density into the continuity equation produces the **Generalized Continuity Equation** (also called the **Ambipolar Transport Equation** in its full form).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Equation:**
> 1. Why is there a $\frac{1}{q}$? → It converts *particle flux divergence* into *current density divergence* using $I = q \times \text{(particle flow rate)}$.
> 2. Why is the sign opposite for holes? → Holes move in the direction of conventional current but opposite to electron flow.
> 3. Why are drift and diffusion inseparable? → In a real semiconductor, **both** mechanisms operate simultaneously and the carrier motion is the vector sum of both effects.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Form |
|---|----------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{q}\nabla\!\cdot\!\vec{J_n} + G_n - R_n$ | Electron continuity | General (1-D/3-D) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{1}{q}\nabla\!\cdot\!\vec{J_p} + G_p - R_p$ | Hole continuity | General (1-D/3-D) |
| 3 | $\vec{J_n} = q\,n\,\mu_n\,\vec{E} + q\,D_n\,\nabla n$ | Electron drift + diffusion current | Current density |
| 4 | $\vec{J_p} = q\,p\,\mu_p\,\vec{E} - q\,D_p\,\nabla p$ | Hole drift + diffusion current | Current density |
| 5 | $\dfrac{D}{\mu} = \dfrac{kT}{q} = V_T$ | Einstein Relation (at thermal equilibrium) | $V_T \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ @ 300 K |
| 6 | $R_n - G_n = \dfrac{\delta n}{\tau_n}$ | Net recombination (excess carriers) | Low-level injection |
| 7 | $R_p - G_p = \dfrac{\delta p}{\tau_p}$ | Net recombination (excess carriers) | Low-level injection |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\partial (\delta n)}{\partial t} = D_n\,\dfrac{\partial^2 (\delta n)}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{\delta n}{\tau_n} + g_n$ | Minority electron diffusion eqn. (n-type) | 1-D, no electric field |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\partial (\delta p)}{\partial t} = D_p\,\dfrac{\partial^2 (\delta p)}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{\delta p}{\tau_p} + g_p$ | Minority hole diffusion eqn. (p-type) | 1-D, no electric field |
| 10 | $\delta n\,\tau_n = L_n^2$ | Diffusion length relation | $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ |
| 11 | $\delta p\,\tau_p = L_p^2$ | Diffusion length relation | $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols to remember:** $\delta n = n - n_0$ (excess electron concentration), $\delta p = p - p_0$ (excess hole concentration), $\tau_n, \tau_p$ (carrier lifetimes in seconds), $L_n, L_p$ (diffusion lengths in cm).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The continuity equation is not merely a textbook abstraction — it is the workhorse equation in semiconductor device simulation tools such as **Sentaurus TCAD, Silvaco Atlas, and COMSOL**. Every modern IC (CPU, GPU, smartphone SoC) is designed by solving coupled continuity + Poisson equations. Specific applications include:

- **Photodetectors & Solar Cells** — Calculating photocurrent by setting $g_n = g_p = G_L$ (optical generation rate).
- **pn-Junction Diodes** — Deriving the minority carrier diffusion current $I = I_0\,(e^{V/V_T}-1)$.
- **BJT Base Transit Time** — Computing the storage time $t_s = \tau_p \ln(\dots)$ for switching.
- **MOSFET / CMOS Inverter Delay** — Estimating the propagation delay using diffusion-limited discharge.
- **LEDs & Laser Diodes** — Analyzing carrier injection and recombination dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General 1-D Continuity Equation for Electrons

**Step 1 — Define the Control Volume**
Consider a thin slice of semiconductor of cross-sectional area $A$ and infinitesimal thickness $\Delta x$ along the x-axis. Let $n(x,t)$ denote the electron concentration (cm⁻³) within the slice.

**Step 2 — Charge Accumulation Rate**
The number of electrons inside the slice is $n(x,t)\,A\,\Delta x$. The corresponding charge is $-q\,n(x,t)\,A\,\Delta x$ (note the negative sign because electrons carry negative charge).

The **time rate of change of charge** inside the volume is:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\big[-q\,n(x,t)\,A\,\Delta x\big]
$$

**Step 3 — Net Current Flowing In**
The conventional current entering at $x$ is $I_n(x)$ and the current leaving at $x+\Delta x$ is $I_n(x+\Delta x)$. The net conventional current **flowing into** the volume is:

$$
I_n(x) - I_n(x+\Delta x)
$$

**Step 4 — Internal Generation and Recombination**
Inside the volume, electrons are generated at rate $G_n$ (cm⁻³ s⁻¹) and recombine at rate $R_n$. The net charge added per unit time from these processes is:

$$
+q\,A\,\Delta x\,(G_n - R_n)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Conservation of Charge**
By Kirchhoff's Current Law (charge is neither created nor destroyed, only moved or generated/recombined):

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\big[-q\,n\,A\,\Delta x\big] = I_n(x) - I_n(x+\Delta x) + q\,A\,\Delta x\,(G_n - R_n)
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by $-q\,A\,\Delta x$**

$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{I_n(x+\Delta x) - I_n(x)}{q\,A\,\Delta x} - (G_n - R_n)
$$

**Step 7 — Take the Limit $\Delta x \to 0$**
Recognize that $I_n / A = J_n$ (current density):

$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q}\,\frac{\partial J_n}{\partial x} - (G_n - R_n)
$$

**Step 8 — Convention for "Net Recombination"**
Most textbooks define $U_n = R_n - G_n$ as the **net recombination rate** (positive when recombination exceeds generation). Rewriting:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q}\,\frac{\partial J_n}{\partial x} - U_n\;}
$$

This is the **general electron continuity equation in 1-D**.

## 3.2 Substitution of the Drift-Diffusion Current

**Step 1 — Start with the 1-D electron current density:**

$$
J_n = q\,n\,\mu_n\,E_x + q\,D_n\,\frac{\partial n}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the spatial derivative $\partial J_n / \partial x$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J_n}{\partial x} = q\,\mu_n\,E_x\,\frac{\partial n}{\partial x} + q\,n\,\mu_n\,\frac{\partial E_x}{\partial x} + q\,D_n\,\frac{\partial^2 n}{\partial x^2}
$$

(We treat $\mu_n$ and $D_n$ as position-independent for a uniform material.)

**Step 3 — Substitute into the continuity equation:**

$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \mu_n\,E_x\,\frac{\partial n}{\partial x} + n\,\mu_n\,\frac{\partial E_x}{\partial x} + D_n\,\frac{\partial^2 n}{\partial x^2} - U_n
$$

**Step 4 — Factor the diffusion term and recognize the three physical contributions:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = D_n\,\frac{\partial^2 n}{\partial x^2} + \mu_n\,E_x\,\frac{\partial n}{\partial x} + n\,\mu_n\,\frac{\partial E_x}{\partial x} - U_n\;}
$$

| Term | Physical Interpretation |
|------|------------------------|
| $\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t}$ | Rate of change of electron density at point x |
| $D_n\,\dfrac{\partial^2 n}{\partial x^2}$ | Diffusion contribution (concentration gradient) |
| $\mu_n\,E_x\,\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial x}$ | Drift contribution (electric field acting on concentration gradient) |
| $n\,\mu_n\,\dfrac{\partial E_x}{\partial x}$ | Field-gradient term (non-uniform E-field) |
| $-U_n$ | Net recombination loss |

## 3.3 Specialization: Minority Carrier Diffusion Equation (Low-Level Injection)

For most KTU problems, the following simplifying assumptions are made:

1. **Low-level injection:** $\delta n \ll n_0$ (in n-type) or $\delta p \ll p_0$ (in p-type).
2. **No external electric field** in the neutral region: $E_x = 0$.
3. **Uniform material** and **1-D analysis**.
4. **Steady-state excitation** (uniform generation): $g_n = g_p = g$ (cm⁻³ s⁻¹).

With these, the minority electron equation in a **p-type** semiconductor becomes:

$$
\frac{\partial (\delta n)}{\partial t} = D_n\,\frac{\partial^2 (\delta n)}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\delta n}{\tau_n} + g_n
$$

**Derivation of the recombination term** — In low-level injection, the net recombination rate is proportional to the excess carrier density:

$$
R_n - G_n = \frac{\delta n}{\tau_n} \quad\Rightarrow\quad U_n = \frac{\delta n}{\tau_n}
$$

This **linear lifetime model** is the foundation for nearly all KTU exam problems.

## 3.4 Steady-State Form and Solution

**Steady-state:** $\dfrac{\partial (\delta n)}{\partial t} = 0$. The equation reduces to:

$$
D_n\,\frac{d^2 (\delta n)}{d x^2} - \frac{\delta n}{\tau_n} + g_n = 0
$$

Dividing through by $D_n$:

$$
\frac{d^2 (\delta n)}{d x^2} - \frac{\delta n}{L_n^2} + \frac{g_n}{D_n} = 0
$$

where the **diffusion length** is:

$$
L_n = \sqrt{D_n\,\tau_n}
$$

**Particular (forced) solution** — For uniform generation $g_n$ in a semi-infinite semiconductor ($x \to \infty$, $\delta n$ finite), the particular solution is $\delta n_p = g_n \tau_n$.

**Homogeneous (natural) solution** — The complementary solution is $\delta n_h = A\,e^{x/L_n} + B\,e^{-x/L_n}$.

**General solution:**

$$
\delta n(x) = A\,e^{-x/L_n} + B\,e^{x/L_n} + g_n \tau_n
$$

**Boundary conditions** typically yield $A = \delta n(0)$ and $B = 0$ (boundedness at infinity), giving:

$$
\boxed{\;\delta n(x) = \delta n(0)\,e^{-x/L_n} + g_n\,\tau_n\;}
$$

This is the **canonical exponential decay profile** of excess minority carriers — a KTU-board favorite for derivation problems.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Scenario | Equation Form | Use Case in KTU |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| General 3-D | $\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{q}\nabla\!\cdot\!\vec{J_n} + G_n - R_n$ | Theoretical questions |
| 1-D + drift-diffusion | $D_n \dfrac{\partial^2 n}{\partial x^2} + \mu_n E \dfrac{\partial n}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\delta n}{\tau_n} = \dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t}$ | Device analysis |
| Minority, no field, time-dep. | $D_p \dfrac{\partial^2 (\delta p)}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{\delta p}{\tau_p} = \dfrac{\partial (\delta p)}{\partial t}$ | Switching transients |
| Steady-state minority | $D_p \dfrac{d^2 (\delta p)}{d x^2} - \dfrac{\delta p}{\tau_p} = 0$ | pn-junction analysis |
| Steady-state with generation | $D_p \dfrac{d^2 (\delta p)}{d x^2} - \dfrac{\delta p}{\tau_p} + g_p = 0$ | Photodiode / solar cell |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Continuity Equation

The continuity equation is best visualized as an **input–processing–output block diagram** showing how different physical phenomena combine to determine the local carrier concentration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drift Current q n mu E] --> S[Summation Block: Total Current Density J]
    B[Diffusion Current q D dn/dx] --> S
    S --> C[Flux Divergence: dJ/dx]
    C --> D[Conservation Law: 1/q times dJ/dx]
    E[Generation Process G] --> F[Net Source Sink: G minus R]
    G[Recombination Process R] --> F
    F --> H[Add to Conservation Term]
    D --> H
    H --> I[Output: dn/dt Time Evolution of Carrier Density]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Process Flow for Solving a KTU Continuity Equation Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Given doping type and bias condition] --> P2[Identify majority and minority carriers]
    P2 --> P3[Check if low-level injection applies]
    P3 --> P4{Is there an external E field in the neutral region?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Include drift term mu E dn/dx]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Drop drift term, use minority diffusion equation]
    P5 --> P7{Is the problem time-dependent or steady-state?}
    P6 --> P7
    P7 -- Steady-state --> P8[Set partial derivative wrt t to 0]
    P7 -- Time-dependent --> P9[Keep partial derivative wrt t]
    P8 --> P10[Form 2nd order ODE in x]
    P9 --> P11[Form PDE in x and t]
    P10 --> P12[Apply boundary conditions: surface x=0, infinity x=infinity]
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[Solve for excess carrier profile delta n or delta p]
    P13 --> P14[Compute diffusion length L = sqrt D tau]
    P14 --> P15[Output final solution: delta n x = delta n0 exp minus x over L]
    style P1 fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style P15 fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Cause–Effect Mapping (Generation–Recombination–Diffusion Triangle)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS[Physical Inputs to the Crystal]
        G1[Thermal Generation G_th]
        G2[Optical Generation G_opt]
        G3[Impact Ionization G_ii]
    end
    subgraph LOSSES[Carrier Loss Mechanisms]
        R1[Radiative Recombination R_rad]
        R2[SRH Recombination R_SRH]
        R3[Auger Recombination R_aug]
    end
    subgraph TRANSPORT[Transport Mechanisms]
        T1[Drift due to Electric Field E]
        T2[Diffusion due to Concentration Gradient dn dx]
    end
    INPUTS --> CE[Continuity Equation]
    LOSSES --> CE
    TRANSPORT --> CE
    CE --> OUT[Output: Carrier Profile n x t and p x t]
    style CE fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
