## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Carrier Transport via Quasi-Fermi Gradient

The following Mermaid diagram maps out the **physical and logical flow** of how the quasi-Fermi level gradient drives current in a non-equilibrium semiconductor device. This represents the **complete block-level functional architecture** of the transport process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Stimulus: V_applied or Optical Input] --> B[Non-Equilibrium Established in Device]
    B --> C[Carrier Injection: n and p Deviate from n0 and p0]
    C --> D[Single Fermi Level Splits into Fn and Fp]
    D --> E[Spatial Variation of Fn and Fp Arises]
    E --> F[Compute Gradient: grad Fn and grad Fp]
    F --> G[Apply Constitutive Relations]
    G --> G1[Jn = q mu_n n grad Fn]
    G --> G2[Jp = - q mu_p p grad Fp]
    G1 --> H[Total Current Density J_total = Jn + Jp]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Continuity Equation Couples to Poisson]
    I --> J[Numerical Solver: Gummel / Newton Iteration]
    J --> K[Self-Consistent Solution: Potential, Fn, Fp]
    K --> L[Device Characteristics: I-V, C-V, Transit Time]
    L --> M[Engineering Output: Solar Cell, LED, MOSFET, BJT]
```

**Node-Description Table (for the architecture above):**

| Node ID | Function |
|---------|----------|
| A | External bias or optical generation — source of non-equilibrium |
| B | Breaks the law of mass action; $np \neq n_i^2$ |
| C | Carrier deviation from thermal equilibrium values |
| D | Fermi level bifurcates into electron and hole quasi-levels |
| E | Spatial profile of $F_n(x)$ and $F_p(x)$ develops |
| F | Gradient operator applied to obtain driving force |
| G | Constitutive (transport) equations applied |
| G1 | Electron current component (in direction of $\nabla F_n$) |
| G2 | Hole current component (opposite to $\nabla F_p$) |
| H | Total current — sum of drift, diffusion, generation currents |
| I | Continuity + Poisson coupled system |
| J | Iterative numerical solver used in TCAD |
| K | Converged solution set: $\psi, F_n, F_p$ |
| L | Extracted device figures of merit |
| M | Real-world device applications |

### 4.2 Conceptual Energy-Band Diagram (Schematic Overview)

The following block diagram represents the **band diagram logic** of a p-n junction under forward bias, showing the quasi-Fermi level splitting — which is a direct visualization of the topic:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[P-side Region: High Hole Density] --> D[Depletion / Quasi-Neutral Boundary]
    D --> N[N-side Region: High Electron Density]
    P -. "Fp HIGH here" .-> FpBand[Quasi-Fermi Level Fp Line]
    N -. "Fn HIGH here" .-> FnBand[Quasi-Fermi Level Fn Line]
    FpBand --> Split[Fp - Fn Splitting = qV_applied]
    FnBand --> Split
    Split --> Grad[Gradient of Fn: Electron Current Driver]
    Split --> Grad2[Gradient of Fp: Hole Current Driver]
    Grad --> Jtotal[Total Device Current J = Jn + Jp]
    Grad2 --> Jtotal
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In a **forward-biased p-n junction**, $F_p$ is high on the p-side and $F_n$ is high on the n-side, with $F_n - F_p = qV$ across the depletion region. This splitting is the **direct visual signature** of the gradient of quasi-Fermi level that drives current.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define quasi-Fermi levels for electrons and holes. Under what condition do they coincide with the equilibrium Fermi level?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under non-equilibrium conditions, the Fermi level splits into two quasi-Fermi levels:

- **Electron Quasi-Fermi Level ($F_n$)**: Energy level whose Fermi-Dirac occupation gives the electron concentration, $n = n_i \exp[(F_n - E_i)/kT]$.
- **Hole Quasi-Fermi Level ($F_p$)**: Energy level whose Fermi-Dirac occupation gives the hole concentration, $p = n_i \exp[(E_i - F_p)/kT]$.

In **thermal equilibrium** (no external bias, no optical generation), the generation and recombination rates are balanced, and $np = n_i^2$. In this case, $F_n = F_p = E_F$ (the equilibrium Fermi level), and the gradient of the quasi-Fermi level is zero everywhere, leading to **zero net current**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**State the relation between the gradient of the electron quasi-Fermi level and the electron current density. Why is this relation considered more fundamental than the drift-diffusion form?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$J_n = q \mu_n n \, \nabla F_n$$

This relation is more fundamental because:

1. It **unifies** drift and diffusion into a single gradient quantity, eliminating the need to separately consider electric field and concentration gradient.
2. It is **valid under all non-equilibrium conditions**, including high-field, hot-carrier, and sub-micron device regimes where drift-diffusion breaks down.
3. It provides a clear **physical picture**: current flows wherever $F_n$ is non-uniform, and the magnitude of current is proportional to the local gradient of $F_n$.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for electron current density $J_n$ in terms of the gradient of the electron quasi-Fermi level $F_n$, starting from the standard drift-diffusion equation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a piece of silicon at 300 K, the electron concentration varies as $n(x) = 2 \times 10^{16} \, e^{5000x}$ cm$^{-3}$, where $x$ is in cm. The applied electric field is $E = 100$ V/cm in the $+x$ direction. Given $\mu_n = 1350$ cm$^2$/V·s and $D_n = 35$ cm$^2$/s, calculate the electron current density at $x = 0$ using **(i)** the drift-diffusion method, and **(ii)** the quasi-Fermi level gradient method. Verify that both methods yield the same result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

We start with the standard drift-diffusion electron current density (assume low-field, constant mobility, non-degenerate semiconductor):

$$J_n = q D_n \nabla n + q \mu_n n E \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1**: Express $n$ in terms of quasi-Fermi level $F_n$:

$$n = n_i \exp\left(\frac{F_n - E_i}{kT}\right) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2**: Differentiate Eq. (2) with respect to position (assuming $T$ and $n_i$ spatially constant):

$$\nabla n = \frac{n}{kT} \left( \nabla F_n - \nabla E_i \right) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3**: Relate the electric field to the gradient of the intrinsic level. For the band-bending convention, $E_i = -q\psi + \text{const}$, hence:

$$\nabla E_i = -q \nabla \psi = qE \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Therefore $E = \nabla E_i / q$.

**Step 4**: Apply the Einstein relation: $D_n = \mu_n (kT/q)$, so $q D_n = \mu_n kT$.

**Step 5**: Substitute Eq. (3) and (4) into Eq. (1):

$$J_n = (\mu_n kT) \cdot \frac{n}{kT} \left( \nabla F_n - \nabla E_i \right) + q \mu_n n \left( \frac{\nabla E_i}{q} \right)$$

$$J_n = \mu_n n \left( \nabla F_n - \nabla E_i \right) + \mu_n n \, \nabla E_i$$

**Step 6**: The $\nabla E_i$ terms cancel:

$$\boxed{J_n = q \mu_n n \, \nabla F_n} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Assumptions:**

- Low-injection non-equilibrium
- Boltzmann approximation (non-degenerate)
- Constant temperature across the device
- Constant mobility (low-field)
- Homojunction (constant $n_i$)
- 1-D analysis

**(7 marks — 1 for start, 2 for gradient of n, 2 for substitution, 1 for Einstein, 1 for cancellation/final box)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $n(x) = 2 \times 10^{16} \, e^{5000x}$ cm$^{-3}$, $E = 100$ V/cm, $\mu_n = 1350$ cm$^2$/V·s, $D_n = 35$ cm$^2$/s, $T = 300$ K, $x = 0$.

**Step 1: Compute $\nabla n$ at $x = 0$**:

$$\nabla n = \frac{dn}{dx} = 2 \times 10^{16} \times 5000 \times e^{5000x} = 10^{20} e^{5000x}$$

At $x = 0$: $\nabla n = 10^{20}$ cm$^{-4}$.

**Step 2: Compute $n$ at $x = 0$**:

$$n(0) = 2 \times 10^{16} \times e^0 = 2 \times 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Method (i) — Drift-Diffusion**:

$$J_n = q D_n \nabla n + q \mu_n n E$$

$$= (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(35)(10^{20}) + (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(1350)(2 \times 10^{16})(100)$$

$$= 5.607 \times 10^{2} + 4.3254 \times 10^{2}$$

$$J_n = 560.7 + 432.54 = 993.24 \text{ A/cm}^2$$

**Step 4: Method (ii) — Quasi-Fermi Level Gradient**:

$$\nabla F_n = \frac{kT}{n} \nabla n = (0.0259)(10^{20} / 2 \times 10^{16}) = (0.0259)(5000) = 129.5 \text{ eV/cm}$$

$$J_n = q \mu_n n \nabla F_n = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(1350)(2 \times 10^{16})(129.5)$$

$$= (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(1350)(2 \times 10^{16})(129.5)$$

$$= (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times 3.4966 \times 10^{21}$$

$$J_n \approx 993.2 \text{ A/cm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification:** Both methods yield $J_n \approx 993.2$ A/cm², confirming the equivalence.

**(7 marks — 1 for $\nabla n$, 1 for $n$, 2 for drift-diffusion calculation, 2 for quasi-Fermi calculation, 1 for verification/comparison)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Derive an expression for the electron current density $J_n$ in a semiconductor in terms of the gradient of the electron quasi-Fermi level. Show that this formulation is equivalent to the standard drift-diffusion equation. State clearly the significance of this representation in the context of non-equilibrium semiconductor devices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider an n-type silicon sample of length $L = 1 \, \mu$m with donor concentration $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$ at 300 K. A voltage of 0.5 V is applied across it, creating a uniform electric field. The electron mobility is $\mu_n = 1350$ cm$^2$/V·s. Calculate:
   (i) The thermal voltage $V_T$.
   (ii) The electron drift velocity.
   (iii) The magnitude of the gradient of the electron quasi-Fermi level $\nabla F_n$ across the sample.
   (iv) Comment on the relationship between $\nabla F_n$ and the applied bias. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the derivation in Question A (a). The final result is:

$$J_n = q \mu_n n \, \nabla F_n$$

**Significance:**

1. The quasi-Fermi level form provides a **unified transport framework** where both drift and diffusion effects are encapsulated in a single gradient.
2. It is particularly **useful in non-uniformly doped** and **heterojunction** devices where electric field and concentration gradients act simultaneously.
3. It forms the **mathematical foundation** of numerical device simulation (drift-diffusion, hydrodynamic, and Boltzmann transport equations).
4. In **equilibrium**, $\nabla F_n = 0$ and hence $J_n = 0$ — consistent with the absence of net current.
5. The **splitting** $F_n - F_p = qV$ is a direct measure of the **non-equilibrium driving force**.

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Given data**
- $L = 1 \, \mu\text{m} = 10^{-4}$ cm
- $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$ (so $n \approx N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$)
- $V = 0.5$ V, $\mu_n = 1350$ cm$^2$/V·s, $T = 300$ K

**(i) Thermal voltage:**

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{0.0259 \text{ eV}}{q} = 0.02585 \text{ V} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$$

**(1 mark)**

**(ii) Electron drift velocity:**

Electric field:

$$E = \frac{V}{L} = \frac{0.5}{10^{-4}} = 5000 \text{ V/cm}$$

Drift velocity:

$$v_d = \mu_n E = 1350 \times 5000 = 6.75 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm/s}$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**(iii) Magnitude of $\nabla F_n$:**

Current density (drift, since concentration is uniform):

$$J_n = q n v_d = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(10^{16})(6.75 \times 10^{6})$$

$$J_n = 1.081 \times 10^{4} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 10.81 \text{ kA/cm}^2$$

Using $J_n = q \mu_n n \, \nabla F_n$:

$$\nabla F_n = \frac{J_n}{q \mu_n n} = \frac{10.81 \times 10^{3}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 1350 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$\nabla F_n = \frac{10810}{2.163 \times 10^{0}} = \frac{10810}{2.163} = 4998 \text{ eV/cm} \approx 5000 \text{ eV/cm}$$

**(3 marks)**

**(iv) Comment:**

The gradient of the quasi-Fermi level equals the applied voltage divided by the device length:

$$\nabla F_n = \frac{V}{L} = \frac{0.5}{10^{-4}} = 5000 \text{ V/cm} = 5000 \text{ eV/cm}$$

(after multiplying by $q$ to convert volts to eV). This confirms that the **gradient of $F_n$ scales linearly with the applied bias** and inversely with the device length. In shorter devices, the gradient (and hence the current-driving force) becomes enormous, illustrating the **scaling challenges** in nanoscale semiconductor devices.

**(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Sign errors in derivation**: Students often forget the **sign of $\nabla E_i$** when relating it to the electric field. Remember: $\nabla E_i = +qE$ (with $E$ as the conventional electric field vector, $E = -\nabla \psi$).
> 
> 2. **Mixing up $F_n$ and $F_p$ current relations**: The signs in $J_n = q\mu_n n \nabla F_n$ and $J_p = -q\mu_p p \nabla F_p$ are **NOT the same**. The minus sign for holes comes from the fact that holes have positive charge and their quasi-Fermi level decreases in the direction of hole flow (from p to n under forward bias).
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by $q$ in numerical problems**: If $F_n$ is in eV, the formula is $J_n = q \mu_n n \nabla F_n$. Students often write $J_n = \mu_n n \nabla F_n$, missing the factor of $q$ and getting answers off by ~$1.6 \times 10^{-19}$.
> 
> 4. **Confusing drift and diffusion currents**: In a uniform-doped sample, $\nabla n = 0$ but $J_n$ is still nonzero due to drift. The quasi-Fermi form handles this automatically because $\nabla F_n$ includes both field and concentration effects.
> 
> 5. **Not stating assumptions**: KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** if assumptions (low injection, Boltzmann statistics, constant $T$, constant $\mu$) are not explicitly listed in derivation questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Gradient of Quasi-Fermi Level**

- ✅ The quasi-Fermi level **splits** ($F_n \neq F_p$) only under **non-equilibrium** (bias or illumination).
- ✅ In **thermal equilibrium**, $F_n = F_p = E_F$, and **no current flows** (zero gradient).
- ✅ The **gradient** $\nabla F_n$ (or $\nabla F_p$) is the **fundamental driver** of carrier current in a non-equilibrium semiconductor.
- ✅ The unified current relations: $J_n = q\mu_n n \nabla F_n$ and $J_p = -q\mu_p p \nabla F_p$.
- ✅ These relations **encapsulate both drift and diffusion** — no need to compute them separately.
- ✅ Quasi-Fermi level splitting is related to applied bias: $F_n - F_p = qV$.
- ✅ The carrier statistics in non-equilibrium: $n = n_i \exp[(F_n - E_i)/kT]$, $p = n_i \exp[(E_i - F_p)/kT]$.
- ✅ Generalized mass action: $np = n_i^2 \exp[(F_n - F_p)/kT]$.
- ✅ In **ohmic contacts**, $F_n$ and $F_p$ are pinned to the contact metal's Fermi level.
- ✅ The continuity equation in quasi-Fermi form: $0 = \nabla \cdot (D_n \nabla n + \mu_n n \nabla F_n) + G - R$ (steady state).
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: solar cells (Voc from $F_n - F_p$ splitting), LEDs (population inversion), MOSFETs (channel transport), BJTs (base minority gradient).
- ✅ **Always remember** the Einstein relation $D = \mu kT/q$ is the bridge between the two formulations.
- ✅ For **exam derivations**: explicitly list assumptions, show every algebraic step, and box the final answer.
- ✅ **TCAD foundation**: every commercial device simulator (Sentaurus, ATLAS, COMSOL) solves the coupled Poisson-Continuity system using the quasi-Fermi level formulation.
- ✅ The gradient of quasi-Fermi level is the **modern, device-physics-grade description** of current flow — preferred over the classical drift-diffusion form for clarity and generality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contact Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Contact Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

The **Contact Potential** (also called the **Built-in Potential**, denoted $V_{bi}$ or $V_0$) of a $p\text{-}n$ junction is the **internal electrostatic potential difference** that spontaneously develops across the depletion region when a $p$-type and an $n$-type semiconductor are brought into intimate thermodynamic contact, **before any external bias is applied**.

At equilibrium, this potential exactly balances the carrier concentration gradient, so that the **net current across the junction is zero** (drift current = diffusion current for both electrons and holes).

$$V_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A \, N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right) \; \text{volts}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants at 300 K (Standard KTU Values):**
> - Thermal Voltage $V_T = kT/q = \mathbf{0.0259 \; V}$ (≈ **25.85 mV**)
> - Boltzmann constant $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \; J/K}$
> - Electronic charge $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \; C}$
> - Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si at 300 K: $n_i = \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{10} \; cm^{-3}}$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Connected Water Tanks"

Imagine two giant water tanks connected by a thin pipe at the bottom:
- **Tank A (n-side):** filled with water (majority electrons) at a **HIGH level**.
- **Tank B (p-side):** filled with water (majority holes) at a **LOW level**.

When the valve opens, water rushes from A → B (analogous to **diffusion of majority carriers** across the metallurgical junction). After a short time, the levels equalize — but in the process, the pipe region is "depleted" of water and the two tanks now sit at an **intermediate equilibrium level**. The **height difference** between the original levels is the **Contact Potential** — an inherent property that arose *spontaneously* from the concentration gradient, even though no external pump was ever used.

The same physics governs the $p\text{-}n$ junction: carrier diffusion creates ionized dopant charges that build up an **internal electric field** (the "wall" that stops further flow) and establishes $V_{bi}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (High-Yield for KTU):**
> $V_{bi}$ exists **only across the depletion region**. Outside the depletion region, the semiconductor is **quasi-neutral**, and the field is essentially zero. The total band bending across the junction equals $qV_{bi}$ in energy units (eV).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why and How" — Formation Mechanism

- **Step 1 — Concentration Gradient:** When the two materials touch, holes from the $p$-side and electrons from the $n$-side are present in huge numbers near the junction. Nature abhors a gradient → **diffusion** of majority carriers begins.
- **Step 2 — Recombination & Space Charge:** Diffusing carriers **recombine** near the junction. What is left behind are the **immobile, ionized dopants** (negative acceptors on the $p$-side, positive donors on the $n$-side). This creates the **space-charge (depletion) region** of width $W$.
- **Step 3 — Internal Field:** The fixed positive charge (donors) on the $n$-side and fixed negative charge (acceptors) on the $p$-side generate a **built-in electric field** $\mathcal{E}$ pointing from $n \to p$.
- **Step 4 — Drift Counter-Current:** This field pushes holes back to the $p$-side and electrons back to the $n$-side — i.e., it drives a **drift current opposite** to the diffusion current.
- **Step 5 — Dynamic Equilibrium:** Diffusion continues until the drift current grows large enough to **cancel** the diffusion current for both carrier types. At this moment, the **Fermi level $E_F$ becomes flat** (constant) across the entire device — the defining signature of thermal equilibrium.
- **Step 6 — Contact Potential Born:** The integrated field across the depletion region equals the **contact potential** $V_{bi}$. It is "contact" because it appears at the point where the two materials touch.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU numerical problems on the $p\text{-}n$ junction. **Memorize the boxed quantities.**

| # | Quantity | Formula (LaTeX) | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|-----------------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | V (≈ 0.0259 V @ 300 K) | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 2 | **Contact Potential** | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$ | V | Core formula |
| 3 | Alternative form | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{p0}}{p_{n0}}\right) = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{n_{n0}}{n_{p0}}\right)$ | V | Useful in derivations |
| 4 | Total Depletion Width | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}\right)}$ | cm or μm | Abrupt junction |
| 5 | One-sided ($N_A \gg N_D$) | $W \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}$ | cm | $p^+n$ or $n^+p$ case |
| 6 | Depletion width in $n$ | $x_n = W \dfrac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}$ | cm | Charge neutrality |
| 7 | Depletion width in $p$ | $x_p = W \dfrac{N_D}{N_A + N_D}$ | cm | $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$ |
| 8 | Peak Electric Field | $\vert \mathcal{E}_{max} \vert = \dfrac{2V_{bi}}{W} = \dfrac{q N_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s}$ | V/cm | Triangular profile |
| 9 | Junction Capacitance | $C_j = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s A}{W}$ | F | $A$ = junction area |
| 10 | Energy Band Bending | $qV_{bi} = E_{i,n} - E_{i,p}$ | eV | Difference in intrinsic levels |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The contact potential is the **fundamental design parameter** of every semiconductor device:
- **Solar Cells / Photodiodes:** The open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ is bounded above by $V_{bi}$; higher doping → higher $V_{bi}$ → higher efficiency.
- **LEDs:** The turn-on voltage of a diode is essentially $V_{bi}$; understanding it helps select materials (GaAs vs. Si vs. GaN).
- **BJTs & MOSFETs:** The built-in potentials of the base-emitter and base-collector junctions set the threshold and switching behavior.
- **Schottky Diodes:** The metal-semiconductor contact potential (barrier height) is engineered by choosing appropriate metal work functions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Contact Potential $V_{bi}$

**Starting Point:** Under thermal equilibrium, the **Fermi level $E_F$ is constant** throughout the $p\text{-}n$ structure.

#### Step 1 — Express $E_F$ in the bulk $p$-region (relative to intrinsic level $E_i$)

In non-degenerate $p$-type bulk: $p_{p0} = N_A$ and the Fermi level lies **below** the intrinsic level by an amount $kT \ln(N_A/n_i)$.

$$E_{F} = E_i - kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) \quad \text{(in bulk p-side)}$$

#### Step 2 — Express $E_F$ in the bulk $n$-region

In non-degenerate $n$-type bulk: $n_{n0} = N_D$ and the Fermi level lies **above** the intrinsic level by $kT \ln(N_D/n_i)$.

$$E_{F} = E_i + kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right) \quad \text{(in bulk n-side)}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply equilibrium condition

The difference between the intrinsic levels on the two sides gives the total band bending, which is precisely the contact potential (in energy units, $qV_{bi}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
qV_{bi} &= E_{i,n} - E_{i,p} \\[4pt]
        &= \left[E_{F} - kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right)\right] \;-\; \left[E_{F} + kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)\right] \\[4pt]
        &= kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) + kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right) \\[4pt]
        &= kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A \, N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Final Expression

Dividing both sides by $q$:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A \, N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right) = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right) \;\; \blacksquare$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** We invoked equilibrium ($E_F$ flat), substituted the standard expressions for $E_F - E_i$ in each bulk region, took the difference (which represents the total built-in energy $qV_{bi}$), and simplified using the logarithm product rule $\ln a + \ln b = \ln(ab)$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Depletion Width $W$

**Use:** Poisson's equation with the **depletion approximation** (charge density is $\pm qN$ inside the depletion region, zero outside).

**Step 1 — Set up Poisson's equation on each side**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} &= -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} \\[4pt]
\text{For } -x_p \le x \le 0: \quad \rho = -qN_A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} &= \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_s} \\[4pt]
\text{For } \;\, 0 \le x \le x_n: \quad \rho = +qN_D \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} &= -\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once to get the electric field** (apply $\mathcal{E} = -dV/dx = 0$ at the depletion edges)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{E}(x) &= -\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_s}\,(x + x_p) \quad \text{for } -x_p \le x \le 0 \\[4pt]
\mathcal{E}(x) &= \phantom{-}\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,(x - x_n) \quad \text{for } \;\, 0 \le x \le x_n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Continuity of $\mathcal{E}$ at $x = 0$** gives the **charge-neutrality relation**

$$N_A \, x_p = N_D \, x_n$$

**Step 4 — Integrate the field to get potential** (set $V(-x_p) = 0$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(x) &= -\frac{qN_A}{2\varepsilon_s}\,(x + x_p)^{2} \quad (-x_p \le x \le 0) \\[4pt]
V(x) &= \phantom{-}\frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}\,(x - x_n)^{2} - V_{bi} \quad (0 \le x \le x_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply $V(0^{-}) = V(0^{+})$** and substitute $x_p = Q/N_A$, $x_n = Q/N_D$ (where $Q = N_D x_n$ is the depletion charge per unit area)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bi} &= \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s}\!\left(N_A x_p^{2} + N_D x_n^{2}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{q Q^{2}}{2\varepsilon_s}\!\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right) = \frac{q Q^{2}}{2\varepsilon_s}\!\left(\frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $Q$** and recognize $W = x_n + x_p = Q(N_A + N_D)/(N_A N_D)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\cdot\frac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D}} \\[4pt]
W &= \frac{Q(N_A + N_D)}{N_A N_D} = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}\right)} \;\; \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Peak Electric Field

Since $\mathcal{E}(x)$ is linear (triangular profile), its maximum magnitude is at $x = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \mathcal{E}_{max} \vert &= \frac{q N_A x_p}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{q N_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_s} \\[4pt]
                              &= \frac{2 V_{bi}}{W} \;\; \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The last equality follows directly from $V_{bi} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert \mathcal{E}_{max} \vert W$ (area of the triangle).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flowchart: Genesis of the Contact Potential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[ISOLATED p-type and n-type semiconductors - each electrically neutral]
    stepB[STEP 1 - Bring into intimate atomic contact at metallurgical junction]
    stepC[STEP 2 - Huge concentration gradient at the interface - DIFFUSION begins]
    stepD[STEP 3 - Holes diffuse into n-region - Electrons diffuse into p-region]
    stepE[STEP 4 - Carriers RECOMBINE near the interface - Immobile ionized dopants left behind]
    stepF[STEP 5 - Positive donor ions on n-side - Negative acceptor ions on p-side - DEPLETION REGION forms]
    stepG[STEP 6 - Ion charges generate BUILT-IN ELECTRIC FIELD pointing from n to p]
    stepH[STEP 7 - Field pushes holes back to p-side and electrons back to n-side - DRIFT current established]
    stepI[STEP 8 - DRIFT current grows until it CANCELS diffusion current - THERMAL EQUILIBRIUM reached]
    stepJ[STEP 9 - Fermi level becomes FLAT across the device - net current is zero]
    stepK[STEP 10 - Integrated electric field across depletion region - CONTACT POTENTIAL Vbi is established]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Contact Potential in Device Physics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[INPUT - Doping concentrations N_A and N_D]
    blockB[Boltzmann Statistics - kT/q factor]
    blockC[CORE COMPUTATION - ln of doping ratio over n_i squared]
    blockD[OUTPUT - Contact Potential V_bi]
    blockE[FEEDBACK LOOP - V_bi feeds into Depletion Width W]
    blockF[FEEDBACK LOOP - V_bi feeds into Peak Field E_max]
    blockG[FEEDBACK LOOP - V_bi feeds into Junction Capacitance C_j]
    blockH[FEEDBACK LOOP - V_bi sets the open-circuit voltage of solar cells]

    blockA --> blockC
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockD --> blockF
    blockD --> blockG
    blockD --> blockH
```

**Interpretation of the Architecture:**
- The doping profile ($N_A, N_D$) and temperature are the **independent variables**.
- The contact potential $V_{bi}$ is the **central pivot** of the entire $p\text{-}n$ junction theory.
- Once $V_{bi}$ is known, **all** other key parameters ($W$, $\mathcal{E}_{max}$, $C_j$, $V_{oc}$) follow deterministically.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define contact potential. State the factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The contact potential (or built-in potential) $V_{bi}$ of a $p\text{-}n$ junction is the potential difference that develops across the depletion region due to the redistribution of charge carriers when a $p$-type and $n$-type semiconductor are brought into contact at thermal equilibrium, in the absence of any external bias. It opposes further diffusion of majority carriers and maintains zero net current.

**Factors (1 mark):** $V_{bi}$ depends on:
1. Doping concentrations $N_A$ and $N_D$
2. Intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ (which depends on the semiconductor material and temperature)
3. Temperature $T$ (via $V_T = kT/q$)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is the significance of the constant Fermi level across a $p\text{-}n$ junction at equilibrium?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **flat Fermi level** across the $p\text{-}n$ junction is the **thermodynamic signature of equilibrium**. It implies that the electrochemical potential of electrons is uniform throughout the device, which is the necessary and sufficient condition for **zero net particle flow** (no net current). The total band bending (i.e., the contact potential $V_{bi}$) is exactly the amount needed to make $E_F$ flat, given the differing doping on the two sides. Mathematically,

$$V_{bi} = \frac{1}{q}\bigl(E_{i,n} - E_{i,p}\bigr) = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{2}}\right)$$

This single principle links doping, material, and the resulting built-in potential.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Answer **ONE** full question from each module. Both choices below carry equal marks.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Series II]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the contact potential of an abrupt $p\text{-}n$ junction starting from the condition of constant Fermi level at thermal equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon $p\text{-}n$ junction at 300 K has $N_A = 10^{18} \; \text{cm}^{-3}$ and $N_D = 10^{15} \; \text{cm}^{-3}$. Calculate the contact potential and the depletion width.
Given: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \; \text{cm}^{-3}$, $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \; \text{F/cm}$, $V_T = 0.0259 \; \text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full derivation. Marks distribution:
- Step 1: State that $E_F$ is constant at equilibrium (1 mark)
- Step 2: Express $E_F$ in $p$-bulk and $n$-bulk (2 marks)
- Step 3: Equate and simplify the difference (2 marks)
- Step 4: Final expression for $V_{bi}$ (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{bi}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bi} &= V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{2}}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^{2}}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{33}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.0259 \times \ln(4.444 \times 10^{12}) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.0259 \times 29.12 \\[4pt]
       &= \mathbf{0.754 \; V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute depletion width $W$:**

Since $N_A \gg N_D$ (ratio = 1000), this is a **one-sided $p^+n$ junction**, so we use:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &\approx \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}} \\[4pt]
  &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.0359 \times 10^{-12}) \times 0.754}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15}}} \\[4pt]
  &= \sqrt{\frac{1.562 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}} \\[4pt]
  &= \sqrt{9.76 \times 10^{-9}} \\[4pt]
  &= \mathbf{9.88 \times 10^{-5} \; cm \;\; \approx \; 0.988 \; \mu m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The depletion region lies almost entirely on the lightly doped $n$-side: $x_n \approx W$, $x_p \approx 10^{-3} \, W$.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat energy-band diagram, explain the formation of the depletion region and contact potential in a $p\text{-}n$ junction at equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon $p\text{-}n$ junction has $N_A = 5 \times 10^{17} \; \text{cm}^{-3}$ and $N_D = 10^{16} \; \text{cm}^{-3}$. Calculate:
  - (i) the contact potential $V_{bi}$,
  - (ii) the total depletion width $W$,
  - (iii) the peak electric field $\vert \mathcal{E}_{max} \vert$.

Given: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \; \text{cm}^{-3}$, $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \; \text{F/cm}$, $V_T = 0.0259 \; \text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diagram + Explanation (7 marks):**

> *Diagram (3 marks):* Draw the energy band diagram of the junction at equilibrium:
> - Show $E_C, E_V, E_i$ on both $p$ and $n$ sides
> - Indicate band bending across the depletion region of total width $W = x_n + x_p$
> - Mark $qV_{bi}$ as the total band bending (difference in intrinsic levels)
> - Show a **flat (constant) Fermi level** $E_F$ across the entire structure
>
> *Explanation (4 marks):*
> - When $p$ and $n$ materials are joined, majority carriers diffuse across the junction (1 mark)
> - This leaves behind ionized dopants forming the depletion region with a built-in field (1 mark)
> - The field creates a potential barrier $V_{bi}$ that opposes further diffusion (1 mark)
> - At equilibrium, the Fermi level becomes flat, indicating zero net current (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Contact potential:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bi} &= 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{17} \times 10^{16}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^{2}}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{33}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.0259 \times \ln(2.222 \times 10^{13}) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.0259 \times 30.73 \\[4pt]
       &= \mathbf{0.796 \; V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total depletion width** (use full formula since $N_A/N_D = 50$, not strictly one-sided):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}} \\[4pt]
  &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.0359 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.796}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \cdot \frac{5.1 \times 10^{17}}{5 \times 10^{33}}} \\[4pt]
  &= \sqrt{\frac{1.649 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \cdot 1.02 \times 10^{-16}} \\[4pt]
  &= \sqrt{1.051 \times 10^{-9}} \\[4pt]
  &= \mathbf{3.24 \times 10^{-5} \; cm \; = \; 0.324 \; \mu m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Individual depletion widths** (using charge neutrality $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_n &= W \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D} = 3.24 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{50}{51} = \mathbf{3.18 \times 10^{-5} \; cm} \\[4pt]
x_p &= W \cdot \frac{N_D}{N_A + N_D} = 3.24 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{1}{51} = \mathbf{6.35 \times 10^{-7} \; cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Peak electric field:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \mathcal{E}_{max} \vert &= \frac{2V_{bi}}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.796}{3.24 \times 10^{-5}} \\[4pt]
                              &= \mathbf{4.91 \times 10^{4} \; V/cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch on $n_i^{2}$:** Always square $n_i$ correctly. $(1.5 \times 10^{10})^{2} = 2.25 \times 10^{20}$ — missing this is the **#1 reason students lose 1–2 marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{bi}$ with applied voltage:** $V_{bi}$ is an **internal, equilibrium** quantity. It does **not** vanish when external bias is zero — it is always there.
> 3. **Sign of $V_{bi}$:** The formula gives a **positive** value for $V_{bi}$. The $n$-side is at higher potential than the $p$-side.
> 4. **Approximation validity:** Use the one-sided formula $W \approx \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/(qN_D)}$ **only** when $N_A \geq 100 \, N_D$. Otherwise, use the full expression.
> 5. **Thermal voltage value:** At 300 K, $V_T = 25.85$ mV or 0.0259 V. **Do not use 0.026 V** unless the problem explicitly states it.
> 6. **Derivation marks:** In the derivation question, the examiner will look for the **constant $E_F$** condition as the starting point. Omitting this costs 1 full mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Contact Potential**

- **Definition:** $V_{bi}$ is the built-in equilibrium potential barrier across the depletion region of a $p\text{-}n$ junction, arising from carrier diffusion and the resulting space-charge separation.

- **Master Formula (Golden Equation):** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^{2})$ — this is the single most important equation in Module 2.

- **Constant Fermi Level at Equilibrium:** $E_F$ is **flat** across the junction. The band bending $qV_{bi}$ equals $E_{i,n} - E_{i,p}$ (intrinsic level shift).

- **Drift-Diffusion Balance:** Equilibrium = drift current = diffusion current (for both electrons and holes). Net current = 0.

- **Physical Constants (memorize):** $V_T = 0.0259$ V at 300 K; $n_i(\text{Si}) = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$.

- **Depletion Width:** $W = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}(N_A + N_D)/(qN_A N_D)}$. Always use full form unless $N_A \gg N_D$.

- **One-Sided Approximation:** When $N_A \gg N_D$, depletion region lies **entirely in the lightly doped side**: $W \approx \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/(qN_D)}$.

- **Charge Neutrality:** $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$ — used to find individual depletion widths.

- **Peak Field:** $\vert \mathcal{E}_{max} \vert = 2V_{bi}/W$ — occurs at the metallurgical junction ($x = 0$); field is **triangular** in shape.

- **Temperature Dependence:** $V_{bi}$ **decreases** with increasing temperature because $n_i$ grows exponentially with $T$, and $V_{bi} \propto -\ln(n_i^2)$.

- **Doping Dependence:** $V_{bi}$ **increases** logarithmically with $N_A$ and $N_D$ (more doping → larger barrier).

- **Energy Band Picture:** The conduction band $E_C$ and valence band $E_V$ both **bend upward** going from the $n$-side to the $p$-side. The amount of bending = $qV_{bi}$.

- **Engineering Impact:** $V_{bi}$ sets the **turn-on voltage** of a diode, the **open-circuit voltage** of a solar cell, and the **threshold** of MOSFET behavior.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** $V_{bi}$ is **not** zero at zero external bias — it is always present in an unbiased junction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Field ($\vec{E}$):** A vector field that exists in the region of space surrounding a charged particle or a charged body. It represents the **force per unit positive test charge** placed at any point in that region. Mathematically, it is defined as the negative gradient of the electric potential.

$$\vec{E} = \lim_{q_0 \to 0} \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0} = -\nabla V$$

The **SI unit** of electric field intensity is **Volts per meter (V/m)** or equivalently **Newtons per Coulomb (N/C)**. The constant used throughout this analysis is the **vacuum permittivity** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ and the **relative permittivity** $\varepsilon_r$ which is a dimensionless material property.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine stretching a rubber sheet horizontally and placing a heavy bowling ball in the center. The rubber sheet dips down around the ball. Now if you roll a small marble nearby, it will curve toward the bowling ball because of the depression in the sheet.

- The **bowling ball** represents an electric charge.
- The **dip in the rubber sheet** represents the electric field.
- The **marble's path** represents how a second charge would move under the influence of this field.

In a semiconductor, free electrons and holes (mobile charges) are like the marbles — they "roll" through the electric field created by ionized dopant atoms, applied voltages, or the built-in field at a p-n junction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Solid-State Devices:** The electric field is the *driving force* behind drift current, governs depletion region width in p-n junctions, controls the threshold voltage in MOSFETs, and dictates carrier velocity in all semiconductor devices. **Without $\vec{E}$, there is no drift — only diffusion.**

### 1.3 Electric Field from Coulomb's Law (Foundational Principle)

For a point charge $Q$ located at the origin, the electric field at a distance $r$ is:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^2} \hat{r}$$

where $\hat{r}$ is the unit vector pointing radially **away** from a positive charge (or **toward** a negative charge).

**Direction Convention:**
- **Positive source charge** → $\vec{E}$ points **outward** (radially away).
- **Negative source charge** → $\vec{E}$ points **inward** (radially toward).
- Test charge is **always assumed positive** by convention.

### 1.4 Electric Field Lines — Visual Properties

| Property | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Origin / Termination** | Lines start on **positive** charges, end on **negative** charges |
| **Density** | Density of lines ∝ magnitude of $\vec{E}$ |
| **Direction** | Tangent to the line gives the direction of $\vec{E}$ |
| **Never Cross** | Two field lines never intersect (would imply two field directions) |
| **Perpendicular to Conductors** | $\vec{E}$ is always $\perp$ to the surface of a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Source of the Field:**
Every electric field originates from electric charges (stationary or moving). In semiconductors, the dominant sources are: ionized donor/acceptor atoms ($N_D^+$, $N_A^-$), free carriers ($n$, $p$), and applied potentials.

**Step 2 — Superposition Principle:**
The total electric field at any point is the **vector sum** of the fields produced by all individual charges independently.

$$\vec{E}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{E}_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r} \cdot \frac{Q_i}{r_i^2} \hat{r}_i$$

This is the **"Why"** behind treating complex charge distributions as a sum of simpler ones.

**Step 3 — Gauss's Law (The "How"):**
For continuous charge distributions, Gauss's law converts the difficult vector summation into a much simpler surface integral:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$$

This works elegantly when **symmetry** (spherical, cylindrical, planar) is present.

**Step 4 — From Field to Potential:**
The electric field is conservative, meaning the work done in moving a charge between two points is path-independent. This allows defining a scalar potential:

$$V(r) = -\int_\infty^r \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 5 — Field in Semiconductor Materials:**
In a semiconductor with uniform doping, the depletion approximation treats the charge density as constant in the depletion region, leading to a **linearly varying electric field** — a critical result used in junction analysis.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Expression | Application / Context | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Electric Field Definition** | $\vec{E} = \vec{F} / q$ | Force per unit positive charge | V/m or N/C |
| **Coulomb's Law (Field form)** | $E = Q / (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r^2)$ | Field of an isolated point charge | V/m |
| **Gauss's Law** | $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enc}/\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r$ | Field from symmetric charge distribution | V·m |
| **Infinite Sheet of Charge** | $E = \sigma / (2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)$ | Field of a uniformly charged plane | V/m |
| **Parallel Plate Capacitor** | $E = \sigma / (\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r) = V/d$ | Uniform field between plates | V/m |
| **Field from Line Charge** | $E = \lambda / (2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r)$ | Field of an infinite line charge | V/m |
| **Field from Cylindrical Shell** | $E = \lambda / (2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r)$ | Field outside an infinite cylinder | V/m |
| **Potential from Field** | $V_b - V_a = -\int_a^b \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{l}$ | Potential difference between points | V |
| **Field from Potential** | $\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -(dV/dx)\hat{x}$ | 1-D gradient form | V/m |
| **Poisson's Equation** | $\nabla^2 V = -\rho/(\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)$ | Relates potential to charge density | V/m² |
| **Depletion Region Field** | $E(x) = qN_d(x + W_n)/\varepsilon_s$ | Triangular field profile in p-n junction | V/m |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For 1-D problems, Poisson's equation simplifies to:
> $$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$$
> where $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r$ is the **permittivity of the semiconductor** (typically $\varepsilon_r = 11.7$ for Si, $13.1$ for GaAs). This is the *starting point* for nearly every device problem.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

- **MOSFET Operation:** The transverse electric field under the gate oxide controls the channel inversion layer. Threshold voltage tuning relies on precise $\vec{E}$ engineering.
- **p-n Junction Depletion Width:** $W = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/q}\cdot\sqrt{(N_A+N_D)/N_A N_D}$ — derived directly from integrating $\vec{E}$.
- **Avalanche Breakdown:** When $\vec{E}$ exceeds $\sim 3\times 10^5$ V/cm in Si, impact ionization triggers breakdown — fundamental to Zener diodes and photodiodes.
- **Hall Effect Sensors:** The transverse $\vec{E}$ (Hall field) is used to measure magnetic fields, carrier type, and carrier concentration.
- **Charge-Coupled Devices (CCDs):** Pixel-level potential wells created by patterned $\vec{E}$ profiles store and transfer photo-generated charge packets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Electric Field of an Infinite Line Charge (Using Gauss's Law)

**Problem Statement:** Find $\vec{E}$ at a perpendicular distance $r$ from an infinite line charge of uniform linear charge density $\lambda$ (C/m).

**Step 1 — Identify Symmetry:**
The line charge is infinitely long and uniform. By cylindrical symmetry, the electric field must point radially outward and depend only on $r$. Therefore:

$$\vec{E} = E(r)\,\hat{r}$$

**Step 2 — Choose a Gaussian Surface:**
A cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$ coaxial with the line charge is the ideal surface — it exploits symmetry perfectly. It has three surfaces: curved side, top cap, and bottom cap.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Surface Integral:**
On the curved surface, $\vec{E}$ is parallel to $d\vec{A}$. On the top and bottom caps, $\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to $d\vec{A}$, so their contributions vanish.

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \int_{curved} E(r)\,dA = E(r)\cdot (2\pi r L)$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Enclosed Charge:**
$$Q_{enc} = \lambda L$$

**Step 5 — Apply Gauss's Law:**
$$E(r)\cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for E(r):**
$$E(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r}$$

This is the **electric field magnitude** at perpendicular distance $r$ from an infinite line charge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Reasoning:** Notice the $1/r$ dependence (not $1/r^2$ like a point charge). This is because as we move away, the enclosed charge per unit length *stays the same*, but the Gaussian surface area grows linearly with $r$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Electric Field Between Parallel Plates (Uniform Field)

**Problem Statement:** Two large parallel conducting plates separated by distance $d$ carry surface charge densities $+\sigma$ and $-\sigma$. Derive $\vec{E}$ between them.

**Step 1 — Field of a Single Infinite Sheet:**
A single infinite sheet with surface charge density $\sigma$ produces a uniform field on either side, pointing away (for $+\sigma$). Using a cylindrical Gaussian "pillbox":

$$E \cdot (2A) = \frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$$

$$E_{single\ sheet} = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$$

**Step 2 — Superpose Two Sheets:**
- Above the +σ plate: $+ \sigma/(2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)$ (away) and $-\sigma/(2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)$ (toward) **cancel**.
- Below the −σ plate: same cancellation.
- **Between the plates:** $+ \sigma/(2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)$ (toward −σ plate) and $-\sigma/(2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)$ (toward −σ plate) **add up**.

**Step 3 — Final Result:**

$$E_{between} = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$$

**Step 4 — Connect to Voltage:**
If a potential difference $V$ is applied across plates separated by distance $d$, integrating $\vec{E}$:

$$V = E \cdot d \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{V}{d}$$

Therefore: $\sigma = \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r V/d$ — this is the fundamental relation behind the **parallel-plate capacitor**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electric Field in the Depletion Region of an Abrupt p-n Junction

**Problem Statement:** For an abrupt p-n junction with donor concentration $N_D$ on the n-side and acceptor concentration $N_A$ on the p-side, derive the electric field profile assuming the depletion approximation.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry:**
Let the metallurgical junction be at $x = 0$. The depletion region extends from $-x_p$ to $+x_n$, where:

$$N_D x_n = N_A x_p \quad \text{(charge neutrality)}$$

**Step 2 — Charge Density Distribution:**
Under the depletion approximation, the charge density is:

$$\rho(x) = \begin{cases} -qN_A & \text{for } -x_p \le x \le 0 \quad (p\text{-side}) \\ +qN_D & \text{for } 0 \le x \le x_n \quad (n\text{-side}) \\ 0 & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Poisson's Equation in 1-D:**
$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Since $\vec{E} = -dV/dx$:

$$\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate on the n-side ($0 \le x \le x_n$):**

$$\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Integrating:

$$E(x) = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,x + C_1$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Condition:** $\vec{E} = 0$ at $x = x_n$ (edge of depletion region).

$$0 = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,x_n + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = -\frac{qN_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Therefore:

$$E(x) = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}\,(x - x_n) \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le x_n$$

The field is **negative** (pointing in $-x$ direction, i.e., from n to p), and reaches **maximum magnitude** at $x = 0$.

**Step 6 — Field at the Junction:**

$$E_{max} = -\frac{qN_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s} = -\frac{qN_A x_p}{\varepsilon_s}$$

The $\vert E \vert$ profile is **triangular**, peaking at the metallurgical junction and dropping to zero at both depletion edges.

**Step 7 — Built-in Potential (Using Continuity):**
Integrating $E$ across the depletion region gives the built-in voltage:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{1}{2}E_{max}\,W \quad \text{where } W = x_n + x_p$$

Using $E_{max} = qN_D x_n / \varepsilon_s$ and charge neutrality:

$$\boxed{W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\left(\frac{1}{N_A}+\frac{1}{N_D}\right)}}$$

This is one of the **most important formulas** in KTU's solid-state device syllabus.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Charge Geometries

| Geometry | Charge Distribution | Field Magnitude | Field Direction | Gauss Surface Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Point Charge** | $Q$ at origin | $\dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r^2}$ | Radial $\hat{r}$ | Sphere |
| **Infinite Line** | $\lambda$ C/m | $\dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r}$ | Radial $\hat{r}$ | Cylinder |
| **Infinite Sheet** | $\sigma$ C/m² | $\dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$ | Normal $\hat{n}$ | Pillbox |
| **Parallel Plates** | $\pm\sigma$ | $\dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$ | From + to − | Pillbox |
| **Uniform Sphere** | $\rho$ C/m³ | $\dfrac{\rho r}{3\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$ (inside) | Radial $\hat{r}$ | Sphere |
| **Depletion Region** | $qN_D$, $qN_A$ | Linear / Triangular | Across junction | Slab |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Electric Field Generation in a Semiconductor Device

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source of Charge] --> B[Charge Distribution]
    B --> C[Apply Gauss Law]
    C --> D[Solve for Electric Field E]
    D --> E[Integrate E to get Potential V]
    E --> F[Derive Device Parameters]

    A --> A1[Ionized Dopants N_D+ N_A-]
    A --> A2[Applied Bias Voltage]
    A --> A3[Free Carriers n p]
    A --> A4[Trapped Charges at Interface]

    B --> B1[Depletion Region Charge]
    B --> B2[Space Charge in Channel]
    B --> B3[Surface Charge at Oxide]

    F --> F1[Threshold Voltage V_T]
    F --> F2[Depletion Width W]
    F --> F3[Built in Potential V_bi]
    F --> F4[Drift Velocity v_d]
```

### 4.2 Electric Field Profile in Abrupt p-n Junction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P_SIDE["P-side Region"]
        direction TB
        P1[x equals minus x_p] --> P2[Negative Charge Density q N_A]
        P2 --> P3[Junction at x equals 0]
    end

    subgraph N_SIDE["N-side Region"]
        direction TB
        N1[Junction at x equals 0] --> N2[Positive Charge Density q N_D]
        N2 --> N3[x equals plus x_n]
    end

    subgraph FIELD_PROFILE["Electric Field E Profile"]
        direction TB
        F1[Zero at minus x_p] --> F2[Linear Rise to minus E_max at junction]
        F2 --> F3[Linear Drop to Zero at plus x_n]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — From Coulomb's Law to Device Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Coulombs Law F equals kQ1Q2 divided by r squared] --> S2[Define E equals F divided by q]
    S2 --> S3[Use Superposition Principle]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Gauss Law for Symmetry]
    S4 --> S5[Obtain E field inside semiconductor]
    S5 --> S6[Integrate to get V_bi]
    S6 --> S7[Compute depletion width W]
    S7 --> S8[Predict I V characteristics]
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Equipotential Lines and Field Lines (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Positive Charge Source] -->|Field lines emanate outward| B[Radial Field Region]
    B --> C[Equipotential Surface Sphere 1]
    B --> D[Equipotential Surface Sphere 2]
    B --> E[Equipotential Surface Sphere 3]
    C --> F[Same Potential V1]
    D --> G[Same Potential V2]
    E --> H[Same Potential V3]
    F -.->|E perpendicular to equipotential| G
    G -.->|E perpendicular to equipotential| H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property Illustrated:** Field lines and equipotential lines are **always perpendicular** to each other. The denser the equipotential lines, the stronger the field.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define electric field intensity. State its SI unit and mention the relation between electric field and electric potential.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Electric field intensity at a point is defined as the **force experienced per unit positive test charge** placed at that point. It is a vector quantity.
> $$\vec{E} = \lim_{q_0 \to 0}\frac{\vec{F}}{q_0}$$
> **SI Unit (1 mark):** The SI unit is **Volts per meter (V/m)** or equivalently **Newton per Coulomb (N/C)**.
> **Relation (1 mark):** Electric field is the negative gradient of electric potential:
> $$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\frac{dV}{dx}\hat{x} \quad \text{(in 1-D)}$$
> This implies the electric field points in the direction of **steepest decrease** of potential.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain Gauss's law in electrostatics. Mention any two important applications in semiconductor device analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement (1 mark):** Gauss's law states that the total electric flux through any closed surface is equal to $(1/\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)$ times the total charge enclosed by that surface.
> $$\oint_S \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$$
> **Explanation (1 mark):** This law converts the difficult problem of summing fields from many charges into a much simpler surface integral, **provided the geometry has sufficient symmetry** (spherical, cylindrical, or planar).
> **Applications (1 mark):**
> (i) Derivation of the depletion width in p-n junctions: $W = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/q \cdot (1/N_A + 1/N_D)}$.
> (ii) Calculation of the threshold voltage of a MOSFET considering oxide and depletion charges.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Define electric field. Derive the electric field intensity due to an infinite line charge of uniform linear charge density $\lambda$ at a perpendicular distance $r$ using Gauss's law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An infinite sheet of charge has a surface charge density $\sigma = 8.85\ \mu\text{C/m}^2$. Calculate the electric field intensity at a point 5 cm from the sheet in vacuum. Also, calculate the potential difference between two points 10 cm apart along the field direction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
The electric field intensity at a point in space is the **force experienced per unit positive test charge** placed at that point, the test charge being infinitesimally small so as not to disturb the source charges.

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0}$$

**Derivation (6 marks):**

*Step 1 — Setup:* Consider an infinite line charge with uniform linear charge density $\lambda$ (C/m) along the z-axis. We need the field at perpendicular distance $r$.

*Step 2 — Symmetry argument:* By cylindrical symmetry, $\vec{E}$ must point radially outward (for $\lambda > 0$) and depend only on $r$. So $\vec{E} = E(r)\hat{r}$.

*Step 3 — Gaussian surface:* Choose a coaxial cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$.

*Step 4 — Surface integral:*
$$\oint_S \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = E(r)\cdot (2\pi r L)$$
(Top and bottom caps give zero contribution as $\vec{E}\perp d\vec{A}$.)

*Step 5 — Enclosed charge:* $Q_{enc} = \lambda L$.

*Step 6 — Apply Gauss's law:*
$$E(r)\cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_0}$$

*Step 7 — Final result:*
$$\boxed{E(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $\sigma = 8.85\ \mu\text{C/m}^2 = 8.85 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C/m}^2$, $r = 5$ cm (irrelevant for sheet, but used for context), vacuum ($\varepsilon_r = 1$).

**Step 1 — Electric field of infinite sheet (2 marks):**
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0} = \frac{8.85 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$E = \frac{8.85 \times 10^{-6}}{1.7708 \times 10^{-11}} = 5.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{V/m}$$

**Step 2 — Field is uniform (1 mark):** For an infinite sheet, the field is independent of distance from the sheet. So $E$ at 5 cm is the same as at any distance.

**Step 3 — Potential difference (3 marks):**
For a uniform field, the potential difference between two points separated by distance $d = 10$ cm = 0.1 m along the field direction:
$$V = E \cdot d = 5.0 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1 = 5.0 \times 10^{4}\ \text{V} = 50\ \text{kV}$$

**Step 4 — Final answer (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{E = 5.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{V/m}, \quad \Delta V = 50\ \text{kV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.A.b):** Students often incorrectly use the parallel-plate formula $E = \sigma/\varepsilon_0$ instead of the single-sheet formula $E = \sigma/(2\varepsilon_0)$. **Memorize:** A *single sheet* gives $\sigma/(2\varepsilon_0)$; *two opposite sheets* (parallel plate) gives $\sigma/\varepsilon_0$. Also, many students forget that for an infinite sheet, the field is **constant everywhere**, so distance is irrelevant for finding $E$ (though it matters for finding $\Delta V$).

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q. (a)** Derive Poisson's equation and explain its significance in semiconductor device analysis. Starting from Poisson's equation, derive the expression for the depletion width $W$ of an abrupt p-n junction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a silicon p-n junction, $N_A = 10^{18}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $V_{bi} = 0.75$ V, $\varepsilon_r = 11.7$. Calculate: (i) depletion width $W$, (ii) maximum electric field $E_{max}$, and (iii) the depletion width on the lightly doped side. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Derive Poisson's equation from Gauss's law (3 marks):**

Starting with the differential form of Gauss's law:
$$\nabla\cdot\vec{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Using $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$:
$$\nabla\cdot(-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_s}$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_s}}$$

This is **Poisson's equation**. In 1-D: $d^2V/dx^2 = -\rho(x)/\varepsilon_s$.

**Step 2 — Significance (2 marks):**
It connects the **electric potential** (a scalar field) to the **charge density distribution** inside the semiconductor. By integrating Poisson's equation twice with the appropriate boundary conditions, we obtain the potential profile, from which the electric field and depletion widths of p-n junctions, MOSFETs, and Schottky diodes can be derived.

**Step 3 — Depletion width derivation (2 marks):**
In the depletion region of an abrupt junction, $\rho = qN_D$ on the n-side and $\rho = -qN_A$ on the p-side. Integrating Poisson's equation twice with boundary conditions $E(x_n) = E(-x_p) = 0$ and applying continuity of $V$ at $x = 0$:

$$W = x_n + x_p = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\left(\frac{1}{N_A}+\frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $N_A = 10^{18}\ \text{cm}^{-3} = 10^{24}\ \text{m}^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3} = 10^{21}\ \text{m}^{-3}$, $V_{bi} = 0.75$ V, $\varepsilon_r = 11.7$, $q = 1.6\times 10^{-19}$ C.

**Step 1 — Compute $\varepsilon_s$ (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.036 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute depletion width $W$ (2 marks):**
Since $N_A \gg N_D$, the term $1/N_D$ dominates:
$$W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{qN_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036\times 10^{-10} \times 0.75}{1.6\times 10^{-19} \times 10^{21}}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{1.554\times 10^{-10}}{1.6\times 10^{2}}} = \sqrt{9.71\times 10^{-13}} \approx 9.85\times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \approx 0.985\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute depletion width on the n-side (lightly doped) (2 marks):**
$$\frac{x_n}{W} = \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D} \approx \frac{N_A}{N_D} \cdot \frac{1}{(N_A/N_D + 1)}$$

Using exact formula:
$$x_n = \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}\,W \approx \frac{10^{18}}{10^{18} + 10^{15}}\,W \approx 0.999 \times 0.985\ \mu\text{m} \approx 0.984\ \mu\text{m}$$

Similarly:
$$x_p = \frac{N_D}{N_A + N_D}\,W \approx \frac{10^{15}}{10^{18}}\,W \approx 10^{-3} \times 0.985\ \mu\text{m} \approx 0.98\ \text{nm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum electric field (2 marks):**
$$E_{max} = \frac{2V_{bi}}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.75}{9.85\times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.52\times 10^{6}\ \text{V/m} = 1.52\times 10^{4}\ \text{V/cm}$$

**Final Results:**
$$\boxed{W \approx 0.985\ \mu\text{m},\ \ x_n \approx 0.984\ \mu\text{m},\ \ E_{max} \approx 1.52 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V/m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.B.b):** Three common mistakes students make:
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Using $N_A$ in cm⁻³ while $\varepsilon_s$ is in F/m — always convert to **SI (m⁻³)** before calculation.
> 2. **Confusion between $x_n$ and $x_p$:** The depletion region extends mostly into the **lightly doped** side. Since $N_D \ll N_A$, almost all of $W$ lies on the n-side.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** $E_{max} = 2V_{bi}/W$ comes from the *area of the triangle* (½ × base × height). Many students write $E_{max} = V_{bi}/W$ and lose marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points — Electric Field in Solid-State Devices**

- ⚡ **Electric field is a vector** pointing from + to − charges, defined as $\vec{E} = \vec{F}/q$.
- ⚡ **SI unit:** V/m or N/C. **Key constant:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- ⚡ **Coulomb's law in field form:** $E = Q/(4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r^2)$ — the foundation for everything.
- ⚡ **Gauss's law:** $\oint \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enc}/\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r$ — use **only** when symmetry is present.
- ⚡ **Point charge:** $E \propto 1/r^2$ | **Line charge:** $E \propto 1/r$ | **Infinite sheet:** $E$ = constant.
- ⚡ **Parallel-plate capacitor:** $E = V/d$ — uniform field between plates.
- ⚡ **Poisson's equation:** $\nabla^2 V = -\rho/\varepsilon_s$ — the *master equation* for device analysis.
- ⚡ **Field from potential:** $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ — field points from high to low potential.
- ⚡ **p-n junction depletion width:** $W = \sqrt{(2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/q)(1/N_A + 1/N_D)}$ — most-tested formula.
- ⚡ **Maximum field at junction:** $E_{max} = 2V_{bi}/W$ (triangular profile).
- ⚡ **Depletion extends mostly into the lightly doped side** (charge neutrality).
- ⚡ **Always convert to SI units** (m⁻³ for doping, F/m for permittivity) before computation.
- ⚡ **Field lines never cross;** they are perpendicular to conductors and to equipotential surfaces.
- ⚡ **Real-world impact:** $\vec{E}$ controls drift current, threshold voltage, breakdown, and channel formation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential and Charge Distribution at the Junction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Potential and Charge Distribution at the Junction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **p-n junction** is formed when a p-type semiconductor (with holes as majority carriers) is brought into intimate contact with an n-type semiconductor (with electrons as majority carriers) within a single crystal lattice. At the metallurgical junction, a dramatic redistribution of mobile charge carriers occurs, giving rise to three coupled physical profiles that govern all diode behavior: - **Charge Distribution (ρ(x))** — the spatial density of ionized dopants in the Space Charge Region (SCR), also called the Depletion Region. - **Electric Field Distribution (E(x))** — the internal field produced by the exposed ionized dopants, which opposes further carrier diffusion. - **Potential Distribution (V(x))** — the electrostatic potential energy landscape seen by a carrier, culminating in the **Built-in Potential (V_bi)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Built-in Potential (V_bi):** The equilibrium contact potential that develops across an unbiased p-n junction due to the diffusion of majority carriers across the junction. It represents the work done per unit charge to move a carrier from the neutral n-region to the neutral p-region against the drift field. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Crowd at a Doorway Analogy:** > Imagine a crowded hall (the n-side, full of "people" = electrons) connected to a less crowded hall (the p-side, full of "people" = holes) through a doorway (the junction). People from the crowded side rush through the doorway toward the empty side. Once they cross, the doorway itself becomes blocked because the first few "people" who passed through have left their chairs/seats behind, creating a **buffer zone** of empty chairs (ionized donors +N_D) and empty spots (ionized acceptors -N_A). No more people can pass through this blocked zone freely — this is the **depletion region**. The "pressure" (potential) difference built up across the doorway is the **built-in potential (V_bi)**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Thermal Voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV}}$ (often approximated as **26 mV**) - **Elementary Charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** - **Silicon Permittivity:** $\epsilon_s = \epsilon_r \epsilon_0 = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \approx \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}}$ - **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration of Si at 300 K:** $n_i \approx \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$ - **Germanium Permittivity:** $\epsilon_s \approx \mathbf{1.41 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}}$ - **GaAs Permittivity:** $\epsilon_s \approx \mathbf{1.11 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Reasoning (The "Why" and "How") **Step 1 — Carrier Diffusion at the Junction:** When p-type and n-type regions are brought together, there exists a steep concentration gradient for electrons (high on n-side, low on p-side) and holes (high on p-side, low on n-side). By Fick's law, electrons diffuse from n → p and holes diffuse from p → n. **Step 2 — Exposure of Ionized Dopants:** As mobile electrons leave the n-side near the junction, they leave behind **immobile, positively charged donor ions (+N_D)**. Similarly, mobile holes leaving the p-side expose **immobile, negatively charged acceptor ions (-N_A)**. A region depleted of free carriers but full of fixed charge is created — the **Space Charge Region (SCR)**. **Step 3 — Internal Electric Field Formation:** The exposed charges produce an internal **built-in electric field E(x)** pointing from the positive n-side ions to the negative p-side ions. This field opposes further diffusion (drift current balances diffusion current at equilibrium). **Step 4 — Built-in Potential Establishment:** The integral of the electric field across the depletion region yields the **built-in potential V_bi**, which acts as a "potential hill" that majority carriers must overcome. **Step 5 — Equilibrium (Zero Net Current):** At thermal equilibrium, the drift current due to E(x) exactly cancels the diffusion current due to the concentration gradient. The Fermi level E_F becomes constant across the entire structure, confirming true thermodynamic equilibrium. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Built-in Potential | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$ | Volts (V) | | 2 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | $\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K | | 3 | Total Depletion Width | $W = x_n + x_p$ | cm or μm | | 4 | Depletion Width on n-side | $x_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \dfrac{N_A}{N_D(N_A + N_D)}}$ | cm | | 5 | Depletion Width on p-side | $x_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \dfrac{N_D}{N_A(N_A + N_D)}}$ | cm | | 6 | Total Width (One-sided) | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N}$ (for $N_A \gg N_D$ or vice versa) | cm | | 7 | Charge Neutrality | $N_D x_n = N_A x_p$ | — | | 8 | Maximum Electric Field | $E_{max} = -\dfrac{q N_D x_n}{\epsilon_s} = -\dfrac{q N_A x_p}{\epsilon_s}$ | V/cm | | 9 | Charge Density (SCR) | $\rho(x) = -q N_A$ for $-x_p \leq x \leq 0$ ; $\rho(x) = +q N_D$ for $0 \leq x \leq x_n$ | C/cm³ | | 10 | Built-in Potential from Field | $V_{bi} = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert E_{max} \vert \cdot W$ | V | | 11 | With Applied Bias V_a | $V_{bi} \rightarrow V_{bi} - V_a$ (forward); $V_{bi} \rightarrow V_{bi} + V_a$ (reverse) | V | | 12 | Depletion Capacitance | $C_j = \dfrac{\epsilon_s A}{W}$ | Farads (F) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The depletion width and built-in potential are the **two most fundamental parameters** of any p-n junction diode. They determine: (a) the **breakdown voltage** of power devices, (b) the **junction capacitance** critical for high-frequency design (varactor diodes, RF mixers), and (c) the **threshold voltage** of MOSFETs. Every SPICE model of a diode uses $V_{bi}$ as a core parameter. ### 2.3 Energy Band Picture and Why E_F is Flat at Equilibrium At equilibrium, the system must be in its **lowest free-energy state**. This requires the electrochemical potential (Fermi level) to be uniform throughout the crystal. Before contact, E_F lies near the conduction band on the n-side and near the valence band on the p-side. After contact, band bending occurs near the junction such that the bands on the n-side shift downward (for electrons) by an amount exactly equal to $q V_{bi}$ relative to the p-side, unifying the Fermi level. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Built-in Potential V_bi **Starting Point:** At thermal equilibrium, the electron and hole concentrations at the edges of the depletion region must satisfy Boltzmann statistics. The relationship between carrier concentration and local potential is: $$n(x) = n_i \, e^{(E_F - E_i(x))/kT} = n_i \, e^{q\phi(x)/kT}$$ where $\phi(x)$ is the local electrostatic potential. In the neutral n-region (x = x_n), the electron concentration equals the donor concentration: $$n_n = N_D = n_i \, e^{q\phi_n / kT} \implies \phi_n = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right)$$ In the neutral p-region (x = -x_p), the hole concentration equals the acceptor concentration: $$p_p = N_A = n_i \, e^{-q\phi_p / kT} \implies \phi_p = -V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$ The built-in potential is the potential difference between n-side and p-side: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{bi} &= \phi_n - \phi_p \\ &= V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right) - \left[-V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)\right] \\ &= V_T \left[\ln\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right) + \ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)\right] \\ &= V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign convention check:** Both $N_A$ and $N_D$ exceed $n_i$, so the argument of the logarithm is greater than 1, confirming $V_{bi} > 0$ (n-side is at higher potential than p-side by $V_{bi}$). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Depletion Widths (Charge Neutrality + Poisson's Equation) Poisson's equation in 1-D for the SCR is: $$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s} = \frac{q}{\epsilon_s}\left(N_D^+ - n + p - N_A^-\right)$$ **Depletion Approximation:** Inside the SCR, $n \approx 0$ and $p \approx 0$, so only the ionized dopants contribute. This gives piecewise constant charge densities: $$\rho(x) = \begin{cases} -q N_A, & -x_p \leq x \leq 0 \\ +q N_D, & 0 \leq x \leq x_n \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 1 — Apply Charge Neutrality:** The total positive charge on the n-side must equal the total negative charge on the p-side (overall neutrality of the crystal): $$q N_D x_n = q N_A x_p \implies N_D x_n = N_A x_p$$ **Step 2 — Solve Poisson's Equation on the n-side** (0 ≤ x ≤ x_n): $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{dE}{dx} &= \frac{q N_D}{\epsilon_s} \\ E(x) &= \frac{q N_D}{\epsilon_s} x + C_1 \end{aligned} $$ Boundary condition: $E(x_n) = 0$ (field is zero in the neutral n-region). This gives $C_1 = -\frac{q N_D x_n}{\epsilon_s}$. So: $$E(x) = \frac{q N_D}{\epsilon_s}(x - x_n), \quad 0 \leq x \leq x_n$$ At x = 0 (the metallurgical junction): $$E(0) = -\frac{q N_D x_n}{\epsilon_s}$$ By symmetry, on the p-side: $$E(x) = -\frac{q N_A}{\epsilon_s}(x + x_p), \quad -x_p \leq x \leq 0$$ At x = 0: $$E(0) = -\frac{q N_A x_p}{\epsilon_s}$$ **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for E(0)** to recover charge neutrality: $$\frac{q N_D x_n}{\epsilon_s} = \frac{q N_A x_p}{\epsilon_s} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Integrate E(x) to get the potential drop.** The total potential drop across the n-side: $$V_n = -\int_0^{x_n} E(x) \, dx = -\int_0^{x_n} \frac{q N_D}{\epsilon_s}(x - x_n) \, dx = \frac{q N_D x_n^2}{2 \epsilon_s}$$ Similarly, the drop across the p-side: $$V_p = \frac{q N_A x_p^2}{2 \epsilon_s}$$ **Step 5 — Set the sum equal to V_bi:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{bi} &= V_n + V_p = \frac{q}{2\epsilon_s}\left(N_D x_n^2 + N_A x_p^2\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Use charge neutrality to eliminate one variable.** From $x_p = \frac{N_D}{N_A} x_n$, substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{bi} &= \frac{q}{2\epsilon_s}\left(N_D x_n^2 + N_A \cdot \frac{N_D^2}{N_A^2} x_n^2\right) \\ &= \frac{q N_D x_n^2}{2 \epsilon_s}\left(1 + \frac{N_D}{N_A}\right) \\ &= \frac{q N_D x_n^2}{2 \epsilon_s} \cdot \frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Solve for $x_n$:** $$x_n = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_D(N_A + N_D)}}$$ By symmetric substitution: $$x_p = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_D}{N_A(N_A + N_D)}}$$ **Step 8 — Total depletion width:** $$W = x_n + x_p = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the One-Sided Junction Approximation > [!IMPORTANT] > **The One-Sided Junction:** When $N_A \gg N_D$ (a p+n junction), nearly all the depletion region lies in the lightly doped n-side. Setting $N_A \to \infty$: $$W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}$$ This is the workhorse formula for **abrupt junction diodes** in textbook problems and exam settings. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Solution: Numerical Worked Example **Given:** A silicon p-n junction at 300 K with $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm⁻³ and $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³. **Step 1 — Compute V_bi:** $$V_{bi} = 0.02585 \times \ln\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(4.44 \times 10^{13}) = 0.02585 \times 31.72 \approx 0.820 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Compute depletion widths:** $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.820}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{10^{18}} + \frac{1}{10^{15}}\right)} \approx 1.034 \text{ μm}$$ $$x_n \approx 1.033 \text{ μm} \quad (99.9\% \text{ of } W), \quad x_p \approx 0.001 \text{ μm}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum electric field:** $$E_{max} = -\frac{q N_D x_n}{\epsilon_s} = -\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15} \times 1.033 \times 10^{-4}}{1.04 \times 10^{-12}} \approx -1.59 \times 10^4 \text{ V/cm}$$ > [!NOTE] > This example demonstrates the **one-sided junction behavior** — the depletion width is essentially concentrated in the lightly doped n-region. Such asymmetric junctions are standard in real silicon devices (e.g., p+n diodes in ICs, n+p solar cells). --- ### 3.5 Modification Under Applied Bias When an external voltage $V_a$ is applied, the potential drop across the SCR becomes $V_{bi} - V_a$.
