Therefore, every $V_{bi}$ in the depletion-width formulas is replaced by $(V_{bi} - V_a)$: $$W(V_a) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_a)}{q} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$ - **Forward bias** ($V_a > 0$): W shrinks → carriers can cross → large current. - **Reverse bias** ($V_a < 0$): W expands → almost no current flows (only drift saturation current). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Junction The following diagram shows the logical architecture of how the three coupled physical profiles (charge, field, potential) interact in the SCR. Each profile is mathematically derivable from the previous one via Poisson's equation and integration. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Neutral p-region: p = N_A, n = n_i^2 slash N_A] --> B[Metallurgical Junction x = 0] C[Neutral n-region: n = N_D, p = n_i^2 slash N_D] --> B B --> D[Space Charge Region: -x_p to x_n] D --> E[Charge Density rho x: piecewise constant] E -->|Poisson equation d2 phi over dx2 = -rho over epsilon_s| F[Electric Field E x: piecewise linear, triangular shape] F -->|Integration d phi over dx = -E| G[Potential phi x: piecewise parabolic shape] G --> H[Built-in potential V_bi at edges of SCR] E --> I[Charge neutrality: N_D x_n = N_A x_p] F --> J[Maximum field E_max at x = 0] I --> K[Depletion widths x_n and x_p] K --> L[Total depletion width W = x_n + x_p] H --> L J --> L L --> M[Junction capacitance C_j = epsilon_s A slash W] ``` ### 4.2 Physical Profile Schematic (Block Representation) The diagrams below represent the four coupled physical profiles of a one-sided p+n junction (heavily doped p-side, lightly doped n-side). Mermaid does not render physical shapes, so we map the profiles as labeled coordinate axes. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SCP["CHARGE DENSITY rho(x) (SCR)"] direction LR Pneg["rho = -q N_A (negative, on p-side)"]:::neg Zero1["rho = 0 (neutral p)"]:::zero Ppos["rho = +q N_D (positive, on n-side)"]:::pos Zero2["rho = 0 (neutral n)"]:::zero Zero1 --> Pneg Pneg --> Ppos Ppos --> Zero2 end subgraph SCE["ELECTRIC FIELD E(x)"] direction LR Ezero1["E = 0 (neutral p)"]:::zero Emin["E minimum at x = 0"]:::min Ezero2["E = 0 (neutral n)"]:::zero Ezero1 --> Emin Emin --> Ezero2 end subgraph SCV["ELECTROSTATIC POTENTIAL V(x)"] direction LR Vneg["V = -V_bi (neutral p)"]:::min Vzero["V = 0 (neutral n)"]:::zero Vneg --> Vzero end SCP --- SCE SCE --- SCV classDef neg fill:#ffd1d1,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef pos fill:#d1e8ff,stroke:#2c3e50,color:#000 classDef zero fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#7f8c8d,color:#000 classDef min fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Energy Band Diagram (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[E_c and E_v flat in neutral p-region
E_F near E_v] --> B[Bands bend upward in SCR
over distance x_p] B --> C[Metallurgical junction x = 0] C --> D[Bands bend downward in SCR
over distance x_n] D --> E[E_c and E_v flat in neutral n-region
E_F near E_c] E --> F[E_F is constant and flat across the entire structure at thermal equilibrium] F -.-> G[Total band bending = q V_bi] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the energy band diagram:** In equilibrium, the conduction band $E_C$ and valence band $E_V$ both bend by a total amount $qV_{bi}$ across the SCR. The Fermi level $E_F$ remains a single, flat horizontal line — this is the graphical signature of thermal equilibrium. ### 4.4 Symmetric vs Asymmetric Junctions (Block Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[p-n Junction Types] --> B[Symmetric: N_A = N_D] A --> C[Asymmetric one-sided: N_A >> N_D or N_D >> N_A] B --> B1[x_n = x_p = W/2] B --> B2[E_max and V_bi split equally] C --> C1[Lightly doped side dominates W] C --> C2[Useful for: varactor diodes, photodiodes, solar cells] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Define built-in potential in a p-n junction. Derive the expression for built-in potential of a silicon p-n junction with doping concentrations $N_A$ and $N_D$.** **Model Answer:** The built-in potential $V_{bi}$ is the equilibrium potential difference developed across a p-n junction due to the diffusion of majority carriers across the junction, which creates a space charge region of ionized dopants. **Derivation:** Under depletion approximation, at thermal equilibrium, the carrier concentrations at the edges of the depletion region are given by Boltzmann's statistics. In the neutral n-region, the electron concentration equals the donor concentration $N_D$, and in the neutral p-region, the hole concentration equals $N_A$. Equating the carrier concentrations to their respective Boltzmann expressions and computing the potential difference gives: $$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$ For silicon at 300 K with $V_T = 25.85$ mV and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, this typically yields $V_{bi} \approx 0.6$ to $0.9$ V. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]** **What is the depletion approximation? Explain how charge neutrality at the p-n junction leads to the relationship $N_D x_n = N_A x_p$.** **Model Answer:** The **depletion approximation** assumes that: 1. The space charge region (SCR) is completely depleted of mobile carriers (n = 0, p = 0). 2. The charge density inside the SCR is determined solely by the ionized dopants: $\rho = -qN_A$ on the p-side and $\rho = +qN_D$ on the n-side. 3. Outside the SCR, the semiconductor is electrically neutral. **Charge neutrality derivation:** The total positive charge on the n-side equals the total negative charge on the p-side. Positive charge per unit area on the n-side = $qN_D \cdot x_n$ (where $x_n$ is the depletion width on the n-side). Negative charge per unit area on the p-side = $qN_A \cdot x_p$. Setting them equal: $$qN_D x_n = qN_A x_p \implies N_D x_n = N_A x_p$$ This means the depletion region extends predominantly into the **lightly doped side** of the junction. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from Poisson's equation and the depletion approximation, derive the expressions for the depletion widths $x_n$ and $x_p$ on either side of a step p-n junction with doping concentrations $N_A$ (p-side) and $N_D$ (n-side). Clearly state all boundary conditions used. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a silicon p-n junction at T = 300 K with $N_A = 5 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³ and $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, calculate: (i) The built-in potential $V_{bi}$. (ii) The depletion widths $x_n$ and $x_p$. (iii) The maximum electric field $E_{max}$ at zero bias. Given: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, $\epsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm. **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Derivation:** See Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key results: $$x_n = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_D(N_A + N_D)}}, \quad x_p = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_D}{N_A(N_A + N_D)}}$$ Boundary conditions used: $E(x_n) = 0$, $E(-x_p) = 0$, $\phi(x_n) - \phi(-x_p) = V_{bi}$. **(b) Numerical solution:** (i) **Built-in potential:** $$V_{bi} = 0.02585 \times \ln\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{17} \times 10^{16}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(2.22 \times 10^{13}) = 0.02585 \times 30.13 \approx 0.779 \text{ V}$$ (ii) **Depletion widths:** $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}) \times 0.779}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{17}} + \frac{1}{10^{16}}\right)}$$ $$W = \sqrt{1.011 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.02 \times 10^{-16}} = \sqrt{2.04 \times 10^{-24}} = 1.43 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 1.43 \text{ μm}$$ Using $x_n = W \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}$ and $x_p = W \cdot \frac{N_D}{N_A + N_D}$: $$x_n = 1.43 \times \frac{5 \times 10^{17}}{5.1 \times 10^{17}} = 1.40 \text{ μm}, \quad x_p = 1.43 \times \frac{10^{16}}{5.1 \times 10^{17}} = 0.028 \text{ μm}$$ (iii) **Maximum electric field** (occurs at x = 0): $$E_{max} = -\frac{q N_D x_n}{\epsilon_s} = -\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16} \times 1.4 \times 10^{-4}}{1.036 \times 10^{-12}} \approx -2.16 \times 10^{5} \text{ V/cm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes by KTU Students (Valuation Pitfalls):** > 1. **Wrong sign of q:** Use $+1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C for the magnitude; the sign of E is determined by direction (n → p). > 2. **Forgetting the factor $(1/N_A + 1/N_D)$:** Many students incorrectly write $W = \sqrt{2\epsilon_s V_{bi}/(qN_D)}$ even for symmetric junctions. Always use the full formula unless explicitly told to apply the one-sided approximation. > 3. **Unit errors:** Permittivity $\epsilon_s$ in F/cm, charge $q$ in C, distances in cm → final E is in V/cm. Mixing SI and CGS units costs 1–2 marks. > 4. **Thermal voltage:** Use $V_T = 25.85$ mV (not 26 mV) for higher accuracy unless problem specifies. --- **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Sketch and explain the physical origin of (i) charge density, (ii) electric field, and (iii) electrostatic potential distributions across an abrupt p-n junction at thermal equilibrium. Mark the regions of the depletion width, the neutral regions, and the built-in potential on each sketch. **(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon p⁺n junction has $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm⁻³, $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³, cross-sectional area A = $10^{-4}$ cm², and is at T = 300 K. Compute: (i) The built-in potential $V_{bi}$. (ii) The total depletion width W at zero bias. (iii) The junction capacitance at zero bias. (iv) The depletion width when a reverse bias of 5 V is applied. **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Sketch and explanation:** (i) **Charge density ρ(x):** A step-like function. On the p-side ($-x_p \leq x \leq 0$), $\rho = -qN_A$ (negative, immobile ionized acceptors). On the n-side ($0 \leq x \leq x_n$), $\rho = +qN_D$ (positive, immobile ionized donors). Zero outside. (ii) **Electric field E(x):** Triangular shape. Linear in the SCR, zero outside. Maximum negative value at x = 0, going linearly to zero at $x = -x_p$ and $x = x_n$. (iii) **Potential V(x):** Parabolic segments in the SCR, flat outside. Rises from $-V_{bi}$ (on neutral p-side) to 0 (on neutral n-side), with curvature determined by the local charge density. The physical origin is the diffusion of majority carriers across the junction, which exposes fixed ionized dopants that produce the field and the potential barrier. **(b) Numerical solution:** (i) **Built-in potential:** $$V_{bi} = 0.02585 \times \ln\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(4.44 \times 10^{13}) \approx 0.820 \text{ V}$$ (ii) **Total depletion width** (one-sided approximation, $N_A \gg N_D$): $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.820}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15}}}$$ $$W = \sqrt{1.062 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.03 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 1.03 \text{ μm}$$ (iii) **Junction capacitance** at zero bias: $$C_j = \frac{\epsilon_s A}{W} = \frac{1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-4}}{1.03 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.006 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 1.01 \text{ pF}$$ (iv) **Depletion width at $V_R = 5$ V:** $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 5.820}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15}}}$$ $$W = \sqrt{7.54 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 2.75 \text{ μm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfalls for Question B:** > 1. **Sign convention for $V_a$:** Reverse bias adds to $V_{bi}$ in the SCR potential drop. Writing $V_{bi} - V_R$ (treating reverse as negative $V_a$) yields the same result, but students often forget to convert the sign correctly. > 2. **Units of capacitance:** C_j in Farads; for small junctions, express in pF or fF. > 3. **Sketch marks:** Examiners explicitly award marks for: (a) labeling $-x_p$ and $x_n$ on the x-axis, (b) indicating the maximum field value, (c) showing the built-in potential as the potential difference between the two flat portions of V(x). Missing any one of these loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **One-sided vs symmetric formula:** In a p⁺n junction (one-sided), use $W = \sqrt{2\epsilon_s(V_{bi}-V_a)/(qN_D)}$ where $N_D$ is the **lighter** doping. Mixing up $N_D$ and $N_A$ is a common error. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Potential and Charge Distribution at the Junction:** - [ ] **Built-in potential** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$. Always positive, $\approx 0.7\text{–}0.9$ V for silicon. - [ ] **Thermal voltage** at 300 K: $V_T = 25.85$ mV. Use 25.85, not 26, for accuracy. - [ ] **Depletion widths** $x_n$ and $x_p$ depend on $V_{bi}$, $\epsilon_s$, and doping. Always satisfy **charge neutrality**: $N_D x_n = N_A x_p$. - [ ] **Lightly doped side dominates** the depletion width. A factor-of-10 doping difference → factor-of-10 difference in width. - [ ] **One-sided junction** formula: $W = \sqrt{2\epsilon_s V_{bi}/(qN_{light})}$ — the workhorse for KTU problems. - [ ] **Charge profile ρ(x):** piecewise constant, negative on p-side, positive on n-side. - [ ] **Electric field profile E(x):** piecewise linear (triangular), zero outside SCR, maximum at the metallurgical junction. - [ ] **Potential profile V(x):** piecewise parabolic, flat in neutral regions, total drop = $V_{bi}$. - [ ] **Applied bias** replaces $V_{bi}$ with $V_{bi} - V_a$ in all SCR formulas (forward: $V_a > 0$; reverse: $V_a < 0$). - [ ] **Junction capacitance** $C_j = \epsilon_s A / W$ — directly from the parallel-plate model of the SCR. - [ ] **Energy band picture:** E_F is **flat at equilibrium**; total band bending across the SCR equals $qV_{bi}$. - [ ] **Units discipline:** $q$ in C, $\epsilon_s$ in F/cm, $N$ in cm⁻³, lengths in cm → E in V/cm, V in V, C in F. - [ ] **KTU-favorite question types:** (1) Derive $V_{bi}$, (2) Derive $x_n$ and $x_p$, (3) Sketch the three coupled profiles, (4) Numerical problems with $N_A = 10^{16}\text{–}10^{18}$ cm⁻³.

topics.title: Biasing and Energy Band Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biasing and Energy Band Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Biasing" in a PN Junction?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
> **Biasing** is the deliberate application of an external DC voltage across a PN junction diode to control the relative position of the Fermi level on the P-side and N-side, thereby shifting the junction out of its thermodynamic equilibrium condition.

In simpler words, when you connect a battery across a PN-junction diode, you are **biasing** it. Depending on which terminal of the battery touches the P-side and which touches the N-side, the diode can be in one of three states:

| Bias State | Battery Polarity | Diode Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero Bias** (Equilibrium) | No battery connected | Depletion region at natural width |
| **Forward Bias** | P → (+), N → (−) | Depletion shrinks; current flows easily |
| **Reverse Bias** | P → (−), N → (+) | Depletion widens; negligible current flows |

---

## 1.2 What is an "Energy Band Diagram"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Energy Band Diagram** is a graphical representation of electron energy (E) on the vertical axis versus position (x) along the device on the horizontal axis, used to visualize the conduction band edge (E_C), valence band edge (E_V), intrinsic Fermi level (E_i), and the Fermi level (E_F) across the device.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water-Dam Model"

Imagine a water reservoir split by a **dam** (the depletion region) holding water at different heights on either side:

- The **water level** = Electron energy (high water = conduction band)
- The **dam wall** = Potential barrier (built-in potential V_bi)
- A **pump pushing water over the dam** = Forward bias (raises water on P-side, lowers barrier)
- A **drain pulling water away** = Reverse bias (heightens the effective barrier)

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Biasing simply tilts the water levels on either side of the dam. The dam itself doesn't disappear — its *effective height as seen by the water* changes. This is exactly what happens to the built-in potential under bias.

## 1.4 The Three Critical Energy Levels

Before drawing any diagram, you must memorize these three horizontal lines on every band diagram:

1. **E_C** — Conduction Band Edge (bottom of conduction band, where free electrons sit)
2. **E_F** — Fermi Level (statistical marker; probability of occupancy = 50% at 0 K)
3. **E_V** — Valence Band Edge (top of valence band, where holes sit)

The **bandgap** $E_g = E_C - E_V$ is a material constant (e.g., **1.12 eV** for Silicon at 300 K, **0.67 eV** for Germanium, **1.42 eV** for Gallium Arsenide).

## 1.5 The Boltzmann Constant (KTU Mandated Constant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85~mV}$ at room temperature (**T = 300 K**), commonly rounded to **26 mV** in KTU numerical problems.

Where:
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** = **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $q$ = electronic charge = **1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Each Bias Condition

### 2.1.1 Zero Bias / Thermal Equilibrium

- No external voltage applied. The system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium**.
- A single **uniform Fermi level E_F** exists across the entire device (this is the "rigid ruler" rule of equilibrium).
- The bands **bend** near the junction over a depletion width $W_0$.
- The total built-in potential $V_{bi}$ appears as a step in the band edges between the neutral P-region and the neutral N-region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Rule:** A flat (horizontal) Fermi level across the whole device is the *fingerprint* of zero bias. If E_F is flat, the device is at equilibrium.

**Built-in potential formula:**

$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = acceptor concentration on P-side (cm⁻³)
- $N_D$ = donor concentration on N-side (cm⁻³)
- $n_i$ = intrinsic carrier concentration (cm⁻³)

**Equilibrium depletion width:**

$$W_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

### 2.1.2 Forward Bias

- External voltage $V_F$ is applied with **positive terminal on P-side**.
- The applied voltage **reduces** the potential barrier from $V_{bi}$ to $(V_{bi} - V_F)$.
- The Fermi level on the P-side moves **downward** by $qV_F$ relative to the N-side (since P-side potential increases).
- Depletion width shrinks.
- A **huge current** flows (exponential with $V_F$).

**Modified barrier under forward bias:**

$$V_{bi,\text{eff}} = V_{bi} - V_F$$

**Shrunk depletion width:**

$$W_F = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_F)}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)} = W_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{V_F}{V_{bi}}}$$

**Shockley Diode Equation (forward bias):**

$$I = I_S \left( e^{V_F / V_T} - 1 \right)$$

For $V_F \gg V_T$, this simplifies to $I \approx I_S e^{V_F / V_T}$.

### 2.1.3 Reverse Bias

- External voltage $V_R$ is applied with **positive terminal on N-side**.
- The applied voltage **adds** to the barrier, raising it to $(V_{bi} + V_R)$.
- The Fermi level on the P-side moves **upward** by $qV_R$ relative to the N-side.
- Depletion width expands.
- Only a tiny **reverse saturation current** $I_S$ flows (≈ µA for Si, ≈ nA for Ge).

**Modified barrier under reverse bias:**

$$V_{bi,\text{eff}} = V_{bi} + V_R$$

**Widened depletion width:**

$$W_R = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)} = W_0 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}}$$

**Reverse current (from Shockley equation):**

$$I \approx -I_S \quad \text{(for } V_R \gg V_T\text{)}$$

## 2.2 Quasi-Fermi Levels — The Big Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** When a PN junction is biased (out of equilibrium), the single Fermi level E_F splits into two distinct levels:
> - **E_Fn** (electron quasi-Fermi level) — relevant for electrons in the conduction band
> - **E_Fp** (hole quasi-Fermi level) — relevant for holes in the valence band
>
> In the **neutral regions far from the junction**, E_Fn and E_Fp rejoin the equilibrium Fermi level because the carriers have relaxed back to equilibrium there. Inside the depletion region, they remain separated.

The **separation** between E_Fn and E_Fp at any point is related to the local carrier concentration:

$$E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} = qV$$

where $V$ is the local electrostatic potential deviation from equilibrium.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table. Every numerical in KTU exams for this topic uses one or more of these formulas.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | V | Always ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K |
| 2 | Built-in potential | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ | V | Equilibrium only |
| 3 | Equilibrium depletion width | $W_0 = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}(1/N_A + 1/N_D)/q}$ | cm | Zero bias |
| 4 | Forward bias barrier | $V_{bi} - V_F$ | V | Forward bias |
| 5 | Reverse bias barrier | $V_{bi} + V_R$ | V | Reverse bias |
| 6 | Forward depletion width | $W_F = W_0 \sqrt{1 - V_F/V_{bi}}$ | cm | Forward bias |
| 7 | Reverse depletion width | $W_R = W_0 \sqrt{1 + V_R/V_{bi}}$ | cm | Reverse bias |
| 8 | Diode current (Shockley) | $I = I_S(e^{V/V_T} - 1)$ | A | General |
| 9 | Electron concentration | $n = n_i e^{(E_{Fn} - E_i)/V_T}$ | cm⁻³ | Non-equilibrium |
| 10 | Hole concentration | $p = n_i e^{(E_i - E_{Fp})/V_T}$ | cm⁻³ | Non-equilibrium |
| 11 | np product (general) | $np = n_i^2 e^{(E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/V_T}$ | cm⁻⁶ | Non-equilibrium |
| 12 | np product (equilibrium) | $np = n_i^2$ | cm⁻⁶ | Equilibrium only |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Bias-Controlled Band Diagrams

The energy band diagram is the **single most powerful visualization tool** in solid-state electronics because it directly tells you:

- **Whether a device is ON or OFF** (high or low barrier)
- **The direction of carrier flow** (carriers "roll downhill" on the band diagram)
- **Where recombination happens** (where E_Fn and E_Fp cross)
- **Why MOSFETs, BJTs, solar cells, and LEDs work** (each is a different biasing of a band diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world impact:** Photovoltaic cells operate in the *fourth state* — **photogeneration bias**, where light creates electron-hole pairs that split the Fermi levels into separate quasi-Fermi levels, generating a photovoltage. The band diagram is the reason solar cells can be designed at all.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Built-in Potential from the Depletion Approximation

**Starting Point:** In the neutral P-region, holes are the majority carriers with concentration $p_p \approx N_A$. In the neutral N-region, electrons dominate with $n_n \approx N_D$.

**Step 1:** Apply the equilibrium mass-action law $np = n_i^2$ at the edges of the depletion region:

$$p_p \cdot n_p = n_i^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_p = \dfrac{n_i^2}{N_A}$$

$$n_n \cdot p_n = n_i^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_n = \dfrac{n_i^2}{N_D}$$

**Step 2:** The built-in potential equals the difference in the **electrostatic potential** between the neutral N-region and the neutral P-region. From the Boltzmann relation for electron density:

$$n_n = n_p \, e^{V_{bi}/V_T}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values from Step 1:

$$N_D = \dfrac{n_i^2}{N_A} \cdot e^{V_{bi}/V_T}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V_{bi}$ by taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$e^{V_{bi}/V_T} = \dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}$$

**Step 5:** Take $\ln$ on both sides:

$$\dfrac{V_{bi}}{V_T} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by $V_T$:

$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Higher doping → larger $V_{bi}$. Pure intrinsic semiconductor ($N_A = N_D = n_i$) would give $V_{bi} = 0$, which makes sense — no junction, no built-in field.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equilibrium Depletion Width

**Step 1:** Total space charge neutrality requires:

$$N_A \, x_p = N_D \, x_n$$

where $x_p$ and $x_n$ are the depletion widths extending into the P-side and N-side respectively.

**Step 2:** Solve for $x_p$:

$$x_p = \dfrac{N_D}{N_A} \, x_n$$

**Step 3:** The total depletion width is:

$$W_0 = x_n + x_p = x_n\!\left(1 + \dfrac{N_D}{N_A}\right) = x_n \, \dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $x_n$:

$$x_n = \dfrac{N_A}{N_A + N_D} \, W_0$$

**Step 5:** Apply Poisson's equation in the N-side depletion region (charge density = $+qN_D$):

$$\dfrac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{\rho}{\varepsilon_s} = -\dfrac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 6:** Integrate once with the boundary condition $E = -dV/dx = 0$ at $x = x_n$:

$$E(x) = -\dfrac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x_n - x)$$

**Step 7:** The maximum field at the metallurgical junction ($x = 0$):

$$E_{\max} = -\dfrac{qN_D \, x_n}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 8:** The built-in voltage equals the negative integral of the field from $-x_p$ to $x_n$ (area under the triangular field profile):

$$V_{bi} = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert E_{\max} \vert \, W_0 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{qN_D \, x_n}{\varepsilon_s} \cdot W_0$$

**Step 9:** Substitute $x_n = \dfrac{N_A}{N_A + N_D} W_0$ from Step 4:

$$V_{bi} = \dfrac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s} \cdot \dfrac{N_A}{N_A + N_D} \cdot W_0^2$$

**Step 10:** Rearrange. The factor $\dfrac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D}$ is symmetric — we can write it as:

$$\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D} = \dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}$$

**Step 11:** Final expression for depletion width:

$$W_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Biased Depletion Width

**Step 1:** Under bias $V$, the only change in the derivation is the boundary condition. The total voltage across the depletion region becomes $V_{bi} - V$ (forward) or $V_{bi} + V$ (reverse).

**Step 2:** Following Steps 5–11 of Derivation 2 with $V_{bi}$ replaced by $(V_{bi} - V)$ for forward bias:

$$W_F = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_F)}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

**Step 3:** Express as a multiple of $W_0$:

$$W_F = W_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{bi} - V_F}{V_{bi}}} = W_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{V_F}{V_{bi}}}$$

**Step 4:** Analogous result for reverse bias:

$$W_R = W_0 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A Silicon PN junction at 300 K has $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, and $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm. Calculate (a) $V_{bi}$, (b) $W_0$, and (c) the width when 5 V reverse bias is applied.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Built-in potential**

$$V_{bi} = (0.02585) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{10^{16} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right)$$

$$= (0.02585) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{10^{31}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right)$$

$$= (0.02585) \ln(4.444 \times 10^{10})$$

$$= (0.02585)(24.52)$$

$$\boxed{V_{bi} \approx 0.634 \text{ V}}$$

**Part (b): Equilibrium depletion width**

First compute $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} = 1.036 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm.

$$\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D} = 10^{-16} + 10^{-15} = 1.1 \times 10^{-15} \text{ cm}^3$$

$$W_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.634}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times 1.1 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$= \sqrt{9.035 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 9.51 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{W_0 \approx 0.951 \text{ µm}}$$

**Part (c): Depletion width at $V_R = 5$ V**

$$W_R = W_0 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{5}{0.634}} = 0.951 \times 10^{-4} \sqrt{1 + 7.886}$$

$$= 0.951 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{8.886} = 0.951 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.981$$

$$\boxed{W_R \approx 2.835 \text{ µm}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** The reverse-bias depletion width is ~3× the equilibrium width. This is correct: $\sqrt{1 + 7.886} \approx 2.98$, matching our factor.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How the Band Diagram Changes Under Each Bias

| Feature on Diagram | Zero Bias | Forward Bias | Reverse Bias |
|---|---|---|---|
| E_F level | Single, flat | Split into E_Fn and E_Fp, E_Fp below E_Fn by $qV_F$ | Split, E_Fn below E_Fp by $qV_R$ |
| Effective barrier | $V_{bi}$ | $V_{bi} - V_F$ (reduced) | $V_{bi} + V_R$ (raised) |
| Band bending | Moderate | Smoother, less steep | Sharper, more abrupt |
| Depletion width | $W_0$ | $W_F < W_0$ | $W_R > W_0$ |
| Carrier flow | Zero net current | Large drift + diffusion → forward current | Tiny reverse saturation current |
| Electric field peak $\vert E_{\max} \vert$ | $\dfrac{2V_{bi}}{W_0}$ | Reduced | Increased |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Three Biasing States of a PN Junction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PN Junction Diode"] --> B{"Bias Condition?"}
    
    B -- "No External Voltage" --> C["Zero Bias / Equilibrium"]
    C --> C1["Single flat Fermi level E_F"]
    C --> C2["Built-in potential V_bi"]
    C --> C3["Depletion width W_0"]
    C --> C4["Net current = 0"]
    
    B -- "P connected to + terminal" --> D["Forward Bias V_F"]
    D --> D1["E_Fp shifts DOWN by qV_F relative to E_Fn"]
    D --> D2["Effective barrier = V_bi - V_F"]
    D --> D3["Depletion shrinks: W_F less than W_0"]
    D --> D4["Large forward current I = I_S times exp V_F over V_T"]
    
    B -- "N connected to + terminal" --> E["Reverse Bias V_R"]
    E --> E1["E_Fn shifts DOWN by qV_R relative to E_Fp"]
    E --> E2["Effective barrier = V_bi + V_R"]
    E --> E3["Depletion widens: W_R greater than W_0"]
    E --> E4["Tiny reverse saturation current approx -I_S"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Energy Band Diagram Features (Side-by-Side Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZERO["ZERO BIAS BAND DIAGRAM"]
        Z1["Conduction band EC bends DOWN near junction"]
        Z2["Valence band EV bends UP near junction"]
        Z3["Fermi level E_F is FLAT across device"]
        Z4["Band bending extent equals V_bi"]
    end
    
    subgraph FWD["FORWARD BIAS BAND DIAGRAM"]
        F1["E_Fp P side moves DOWN by qV_F"]
        F2["E_Fn N side stays at original level"]
        F3["Effective barrier reduced to V_bi minus V_F"]
        F4["Bands bend over smaller depletion width W_F"]
    end
    
    subgraph REV["REVERSE BIAS BAND DIAGRAM"]
        R1["E_Fp P side moves UP by qV_R"]
        R2["E_Fn N side stays at original level"]
        R3["Effective barrier increased to V_bi plus V_R"]
        R4["Bands bend over larger depletion width W_R"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Quasi-Fermi Level Splitting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Device under non-equilibrium bias"] --> Q1{"Region of device?"}
    
    Q1 -- "Neutral P region far from junction" --> QP1["E_Fn and E_Fp REJOIN equilibrium E_F"]
    Q1 -- "Inside depletion region" --> QD1["E_Fn and E_Fp SPLIT apart"]
    Q1 -- "Neutral N region far from junction" --> QN1["E_Fn and E_Fp REJOIN equilibrium E_F"]
    
    QD1 --> QDMATH["Separation equals qV applied"]
    QDMATH --> QDF["Electron density n equals n_i times exp E_Fn minus E_i over V_T"]
    QDMATH --> QDP["Hole density p equals n_i times exp E_i minus E_Fp over V_T"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The neutral regions outside the depletion layer remain at local equilibrium because carriers there have time to recombine/reach equilibrium. Only in the **active (depletion + quasi-neutral) regions near the junction** do the quasi-Fermi levels differ. This is fundamental to understanding BJT base regions, MOSFET channels, and solar cell operation.

---

## 4.4 Detailed ASCII Schematic: Energy Band Diagram (Forward Bias)

```
         Conduction Band EC

         |               \                    /  <-- Less steep bending
         |                \                  /      (smaller W_F)

         |             /                   \  
         |            /                     \ 
         |           /                       \ 
         |  E_V    /__________________________\  E_V
         |       P region      Junction     N region
         
    <-- x_p -->  <-- junction -->  <-- x_n -->
              W_F (forward, less than W_0)
              
    Effective barrier height = V_bi - V_F
    qV_F = vertical shift of E_Fp relative to E_Fn
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Define built-in potential in a PN junction. Mention its dependence on doping concentration."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ is the internal electrostatic potential difference that exists across a PN junction at thermal equilibrium, caused by the diffusion of carriers from their majority sides to the minority sides, leaving behind ionized dopants that form the depletion region.

**Dependence (1 mark):** $V_{bi}$ increases logarithmically with doping concentration. From the formula $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$, doubling both $N_A$ and $N_D$ increases $V_{bi}$ by only $V_T \ln 4 \approx 36$ mV — a slow logarithmic dependence.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"What is a quasi-Fermi level? Under what condition does it split into two distinct levels?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **quasi-Fermi level** (E_Fn for electrons, E_Fp for holes) is a statistical reference level used to describe the carrier population in a semiconductor that is **out of thermodynamic equilibrium**. It indicates the effective energy at which a 50% probability of occupancy would exist if the system were in equilibrium at that local carrier density.

**Splitting condition (1.5 marks):** The quasi-Fermi levels split into two distinct levels when the PN junction is **forward biased** (or reverse biased beyond breakdown), because external carrier injection destroys the local equilibrium. In the neutral regions far from the junction, E_Fn and E_Fp rejoin the equilibrium Fermi level.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks. The complete, step-by-step model solutions are given below.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"With the help of energy band diagrams, explain the behavior of a PN junction under (a) zero bias and (b) forward bias conditions. Derive the expression for the depletion width in each case."**

#### **Part (a) — Zero Bias / Equilibrium (7 marks)**

**Diagram description (2 marks):**
In thermal equilibrium (no external bias), the energy band diagram of a PN junction has:
- A **flat, horizontal Fermi level E_F** stretching uniformly across the entire device (from neutral P-region through the depletion region to the neutral N-region).
- The conduction band edge E_C **bends downward** as we cross from the P-side into the N-side.
- The valence band edge E_V **bends upward** by the same amount, keeping $E_g = E_C - E_V$ constant.
- The total vertical step between the P-side E_C and the N-side E_C is exactly $qV_{bi}$.

**Physical reasoning (2 marks):**
At equilibrium, the diffusion of electrons from N to P (and holes from P to N) is exactly balanced by the drift of these minority carriers back due to the built-in electric field. The net current is zero. The depletion region extends a distance $x_p$ into the P-side and $x_n$ into the N-side, with $W_0 = x_p + x_n$.

**Derivation of depletion width (3 marks):**

Apply Poisson's equation in the N-depletion region:

$$\dfrac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Integrate once with the boundary condition $E = 0$ at $x = x_n$:

$$E(x) = -\dfrac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x_n - x)$$

The maximum field at the junction ($x = 0$):

$$E_{\max} = -\dfrac{qN_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s}$$

By charge neutrality $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$, the total voltage across the depletion region is the area of the triangular field profile:

$$V_{bi} = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert E_{\max} \vert \, W_0 = \dfrac{qN_D x_n W_0}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

Solving for $W_0$ using $x_n = W_0 N_A/(N_A + N_D)$:

$$\boxed{W_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}}$$

#### **Part (b) — Forward Bias (7 marks)**

**Diagram description (2 marks):**
Under forward bias $V_F$ (positive terminal connected to P-side):
- The Fermi level is no longer flat. It splits into two quasi-Fermi levels: E_Fp on the P-side and E_Fn on the N-side, separated vertically by $qV_F$.
- The P-side E_Fp is **below** the N-side E_Fn (E_Fp moves down because the P-side is at higher potential).
- The band bending is **reduced**: the effective barrier becomes $V_{bi} - V_F$ instead of $V_{bi}$.
- The depletion width is **smaller** than at equilibrium: $W_F = W_0\sqrt{1 - V_F/V_{bi}}$.

**Physical reasoning (2 marks):**
The applied forward voltage reduces the potential barrier, allowing majority carriers to easily diffuse across the junction. Electrons from N-side and holes from P-side flood into the opposite regions, creating a **large exponential forward current** $I = I_S(e^{V_F/V_T} - 1)$.

**Derivation of forward depletion width (3 marks):**

Under forward bias, the boundary condition at the depletion edges changes: the total voltage drop is $(V_{bi} - V_F)$ instead of $V_{bi}$. Re-deriving the depletion width:

$$V_{bi} - V_F = \dfrac{q}{2\varepsilon_s}\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D}\right) W_F^2$$

Solving for $W_F$:

$$\boxed{W_F = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_F)}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)} = W_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{V_F}{V_{bi}}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake #1:** Students often write $V_F$ instead of $(V_{bi} - V_F)$ in the depletion width formula. **Always remember:** the depletion region drops the *total* potential across it, which is the built-in potential *minus* the applied forward voltage (or *plus* for reverse). Mark loss: 1–2 marks per instance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake #2:** Some students draw the forward-bias band diagram with E_Fp *above* E_Fn. **Correct picture:** E_Fp is *below* E_Fn by $qV_F$ because the P-side is at higher electrostatic potential, which lowers the electron energy there.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Explain the energy band diagram of a PN junction under (a) reverse bias and (b) derive the expression for reverse saturation current. Discuss the concept of quasi-Fermi levels."**

#### **Part (a) — Reverse Bias (7 marks)**

**Diagram description (2.5 marks):**
Under reverse bias $V_R$ (positive terminal on N-side):
- The Fermi level splits: E_Fp on the P-side moves **upward** by $qV_R$ relative to E_Fn on the N-side.
- The effective barrier height becomes $V_{bi} + V_R$ — the bands bend more steeply.
- The depletion region widens to $W_R = W_0\sqrt{1 + V_R/V_{bi}}$.
- Majority carriers cannot cross the heightened barrier; only a tiny **reverse saturation current** flows due to minority carriers generated thermally within a diffusion length of the depletion edge.

**Carrier flow analysis (1.5 marks):**
- Majority carriers are pulled away from the junction (electrons toward N-contact, holes toward P-contact).
- Minority carriers (holes in N-region, electrons in P-region) that wander to the depletion edge are **swept across** by the strong field, constituting the reverse saturation current $-I_S$.
- $I_S$ is independent of $V_R$ (saturated) but doubles for every ~10°C rise in temperature.

**Derivation of barrier (3 marks):**

Following the same Poisson's equation approach as in forward bias, with the total depletion voltage now being $(V_{bi} + V_R)$:

$$V_{bi} + V_R = \dfrac{q}{2\varepsilon_s}\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D}\right) W_R^2$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{W_R = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)} = W_0 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}}}$$

#### **Part (b) — Quasi-Fermi Levels & Reverse Saturation Current (7 marks)**

**Concept of quasi-Fermi levels (3 marks):**
When the PN junction is biased, the system is driven out of thermal equilibrium. The single Fermi level is no longer sufficient to describe carrier statistics. Two separate quasi-Fermi levels emerge:
- **E_Fn**: governs electron occupancy — $n = n_i e^{(E_{Fn} - E_i)/V_T}$
- **E_Fp**: governs hole occupancy — $p = n_i e^{(E_i - E_{Fp})/V_T}$

In the depletion region, E_Fn ≠ E_Fp. In the neutral regions far from the junction, the carriers have time to recombine and reach local equilibrium, so E_Fn = E_Fp = equilibrium E_F. The **np product** in non-equilibrium is:

$$np = n_i^2 e^{(E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/V_T}$$

**Derivation of reverse saturation current (4 marks):**

The reverse saturation current arises from minority carrier diffusion toward the depletion edge. Consider a one-sided junction (N-side lightly doped). The minority hole current in the N-region obeys the diffusion equation:

$$D_p \dfrac{d^2 p_n}{dx^2} - \dfrac{p_n - p_{n0}}{\tau_p} = 0$$

Boundary conditions:
- At the depletion edge ($x = x_n$): $p_n(x_n) = p_{n0} e^{-V_R/V_T} \approx 0$ for $V_R \gg V_T$
- At the N-contact ($x \to \infty$): $p_n = p_{n0}$

Solution: $p_n(x) = p_{n0}(1 - e^{-(x - x_n)/L_p})$ where $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ is the diffusion length.

The hole diffusion current at $x = x_n$:

$$I_p(x_n) = -qA D_p \dfrac{dp_n}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=x_n} = -\dfrac{qA D_p p_{n0}}{L_p}$$

Similarly for electrons in the P-region. The total reverse saturation current is:

$$\boxed{I_S = qA\!\left(\dfrac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} + \dfrac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p}\right) = qA n_i^2\!\left(\dfrac{D_n}{L_n N_A} + \dfrac{D_p}{L_p N_D}\right)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake #3:** Students often write $I_S = qADn_i/L$ without showing how minority carrier concentrations ($n_{p0}$ and $p_{n0}$) are substituted in terms of $n_i$ and doping. Examiners specifically award the 1-mark step $p_{n0} = n_i^2/N_D$ and $n_{p0} = n_i^2/N_A$. Missing this transition costs 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake #4:** Confusing quasi-Fermi level direction. **E_Fn drops on the P-side** and **E_Fp rises on the N-side** under forward bias — students frequently draw it inverted. The rule: E_F follows the majority carrier side; the minority side has the higher energy in terms of quasi-Fermi level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist before the exam:**

- ✅ **Three biasing states:** Zero, Forward, Reverse — know the battery polarity for each.
- ✅ **Built-in potential formula:** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ — memorize it.
- ✅ **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = 25.85$ mV at 300 K (rounded to 26 mV in KTU problems).
- ✅ **Depletion width formulas** (zero, forward, reverse) — all three versions needed.
- ✅ **Universal $W$ formula template:** $W = \sqrt{(2\varepsilon_s V_{total}/q)(1/N_A + 1/N_D)}$ where $V_{total}$ is $V_{bi}$, $V_{bi} - V_F$, or $V_{bi} + V_R$ for the three cases.
- ✅ **Shockley equation:** $I = I_S(e^{V/V_T} - 1)$ — know when to use approximation $I \approx I_S e^{V/V_T}$.
- ✅ **Fermi level rule at equilibrium:** A flat E_F is the signature of zero bias. Drawing a slanted E_F under "zero bias" is an automatic 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
- ✅ **Forward bias:** barrier reduces, depletion shrinks, current increases exponentially.
- ✅ **Reverse bias:** barrier increases, depletion widens, current saturates at $-I_S$.
- ✅ **Quasi-Fermi levels split under bias; rejoin in deep neutral regions.**
- ✅ **np product under non-equilibrium:** $np = n_i^2 \exp[(E_{Fn} - E_{Fp})/V_T]$.
- ✅ **Drawing the band diagram correctly:** E_C bends down from P to N, E_V bends up, E_F flat at equilibrium.
- ✅ **Units matter:** Concentrations in cm⁻³, lengths in cm or µm, voltages in V.
- ✅ **Reverse saturation current formula:** $I_S = qAn_i^2(D_n/(L_n N_A) + D_p/(L_p N_D))$.
- ✅ **Diode ideality factor** (advanced): $I = I_S(e^{V/(\eta V_T)} - 1)$ where $\eta = 1$ to 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final exam strategy for KTU 2024 Scheme:** This topic is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in Part B. Always draw the band diagram first (1 mark just for a correct diagram with proper labels), then derive, then state the physical interpretation. A well-labeled diagram alone can save you from losing 2–3 marks even if your derivation has a minor slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal Diode Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ideal Diode Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ideal Diode Equation** (also known as the **Shockley Diode Equation**) is the foundational mathematical model that quantitatively describes the current-voltage (I-V) relationship of a p-n junction diode under forward and reverse bias. It is derived from semiconductor physics principles, specifically the diffusion of minority carriers across the quasi-neutral regions adjacent to the depletion layer.

The equation is expressed as:

$$I_D = I_S \left[ \exp\left(\frac{V_D}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Where:
- $I_D$ → Diode current (Amperes)
- $I_S$ → Reverse saturation current / Scale current (Amperes)
- $V_D$ → Voltage applied across the diode (Volts)
- $V_T$ → Thermal voltage = **kT/q** (≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K)
- $n$ → Ideality factor (n = 1 for an ideal diode, 1 < n < 2 for real diodes)
- **k** → Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **T** → Absolute temperature in Kelvin
- **q** → Electronic charge = **1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "Ideal" in the Ideal Diode Equation refers to the **mathematical idealization** of carrier transport—specifically that ONLY diffusion current exists (no generation-recombination in the depletion region, no series resistance, and no breakdown effects). This is fundamentally different from the "Ideal Diode Switch" model where $V_D = 0$ (ON) and $I_D = 0$ (OFF).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **one-way revolving door** at the entrance of a busy building:
- **Under forward bias** (people pushing one direction): The door spins easily, and a *large* number of people (current) flow through with even a *small* push. Notice how a *tiny* extra push dramatically increases the flow—this mirrors the **exponential** nature of the equation.
- **Under reverse bias** (people pushing the wrong way): The door is locked. However, a *tiny* trickle of people always slips through (people sneaking in) — this is the **reverse saturation current ($I_S$)**, which is independent of the reverse voltage magnitude.
- The **thermal voltage ($V_T$)** acts as the "sensitivity knob"—higher temperature means people are more energetic, so less push is needed to get the same flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "-1" in the equation is not cosmetic!** It mathematically guarantees that at $V_D = 0$, the current $I_D = 0$ (satisfying thermal equilibrium), and it correctly predicts the small reverse current approaching $-I_S$ for large negative voltages.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The ideal diode equation emerges from analyzing what happens at the **edges of the depletion region** when external bias is applied. The logical flow is:

- **Step 1 — Law of the Junction:** When a voltage $V_A$ is applied, the barrier potential reduces from $V_{bi}$ to $V_{bi} - V_A$, causing minority carrier concentrations at the depletion edges to **increase exponentially**:
  $$p_n(0_n) = p_{n0} \exp(V_A / V_T)$$
  $$n_p(0_p) = n_{p0} \exp(V_A / V_T)$$

- **Step 2 — Minority Carrier Injection:** These excess minority carriers (holes on n-side, electrons on p-side) diffuse into the quasi-neutral regions, creating concentration gradients.

- **Step 3 — Diffusion Current (Fick's Law):** The gradient drives a diffusion current that decays exponentially with distance from the junction, characterized by the **diffusion length** $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ and $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$.

- **Step 4 — Current Continuity:** The total diode current is the **sum of hole and electron diffusion currents**, which are constants throughout the device (no recombination in the depletion region for the ideal model).

- **Step 5 — The Result:** Combining all effects yields the exponential I-V relationship, with the scale factor $I_S$ representing the **reverse saturation current** magnitude.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units / Typical Value** |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T$ | $V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$ | **25.85 mV at 300 K** |
| Law of Junction | $p_n(0_n)$ | $p_{n0} \exp(V_A / V_T)$ | cm⁻³ |
| Minority Carrier (Thermal) | $p_{n0}$ | $n_i^2 / N_D$ | cm⁻³ |
| Minority Carrier (Thermal) | $n_{p0}$ | $n_i^2 / N_A$ | cm⁻³ |
| Diffusion Length (Holes) | $L_p$ | $\sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ | cm |
| Diffusion Length (Electrons) | $L_n$ | $\sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ | cm |
| **Ideal Diode Equation** | $I_D$ | $I_S \left[\exp(V_D / nV_T) - 1\right]$ | Amperes |
| **Reverse Saturation Current** | $I_S$ | $qA\left[\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right]$ | **10⁻¹⁵ to 10⁻⁹ A (Si)** |
| Einstein Relation | $D / \mu$ | $D / \mu = V_T$ | Volts |
| Forward Current (approx.) | $I_D$ | $I_S \exp(V_D / V_T)$ for $V_D \gg V_T$ | Amperes |
| Dynamic Resistance | $r_d$ | $\frac{nV_T}{I_D}$ | Ohms |
| Break-even Voltage | $V_D$ | $V_D = nV_T \ln(I_D / I_S)$ | Volts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The boundary condition $I_D = 0$ when $V_D = 0$ is **automatically satisfied** by the $\left[\exp(0) - 1\right] = 0$ term—memorize this derivation, as it is a frequent KTU question.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ideal diode equation is **NOT merely theoretical**; it underpins the design of:
- **Solar cells (photovoltaics):** Operating in the fourth quadrant, the equation is rewritten as $I = I_L - I_S[\exp(V/V_T) - 1]$.
- **Rectifier circuits:** Power supply design relies on the exponential knee to predict forward voltage drop (≈ 0.7 V for Si, 0.3 V for Ge).
- **Temperature sensors:** The known temperature dependence of $V_T$ and $I_S$ allows diodes to act as precision sensors.
- **Zener diode reference circuits:** Combined with the breakdown region, the equation provides a complete I-V model.
- **Varactor diodes & mixers:** Used in RF/wireless communication systems (covered in later modules).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal Diode Equation

**Starting Point:** The ambipolar transport (continuity) equation for minority carrier holes in the n-region, with no external generation, under **steady-state** conditions ($\partial p_n / \partial t = 0$):

$$D_p \frac{\partial^2 p_n}{\partial x^2} - \frac{p_n - p_{n0}}{\tau_p} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Define Excess Carrier Concentration:**
Let the excess minority carrier concentration be $\delta p_n = p_n - p_{n0}$. The equation simplifies to:

$$D_p \frac{\partial^2 (\delta p_n)}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\delta p_n}{\tau_p}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\partial^2 (\delta p_n)}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\delta p_n}{D_p \tau_p} = \frac{\delta p_n}{L_p^2}$$

where the **diffusion length** is defined as $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ (with units of cm).

**Step 2 — Solve the Second-Order ODE:**
The general solution of this ODE is:

$$\delta p_n(x) = A \exp(-x / L_p) + B \exp(+x / L_p)$$

*Reasoning:* The exponential decay/growth terms are solutions because the second derivative equals the same exponential form (eigenfunction of the differential operator).

**Step 3 — Apply Boundary Condition at Infinity:**
As $x \rightarrow \infty$ (deep in the n-region), the excess carriers must vanish, i.e., $\delta p_n(\infty) = 0$. The term $B \exp(+x/L_p)$ would grow without bound, so we **must** set $B = 0$.

Thus:

$$\delta p_n(x) = A \exp(-x / L_p)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Condition at the Depletion Edge (Law of the Junction):**
At $x = 0$ (depletion edge in the n-region), the law of the junction states:

$$p_n(0_n) = p_{n0} \exp(V_A / V_T)$$

Therefore:

$$A = p_n(0_n) - p_{n0} = p_{n0} \left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]$$

Substituting back:

$$p_n(x) = p_{n0} + p_{n0}\left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]\exp(-x / L_p)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Fick's First Law of Diffusion:**
The hole diffusion current density is:

$$J_p(x) = -qD_p \frac{\partial p_n}{\partial x}$$

Taking the derivative:

$$\frac{\partial p_n}{\partial x} = p_{n0}\left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right] \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{L_p}\right) \exp(-x / L_p)$$

At the depletion edge ($x = 0$):

$$J_p(0) = -qD_p \cdot p_{n0}\left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right] \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{L_p}\right)$$

$$J_p(0) = \frac{qD_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]$$

**Step 6 — Total Hole Current (Multiply by Cross-Sectional Area A):**

$$I_p = A \cdot J_p(0) = \frac{qAD_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]$$

**Step 7 — By Symmetry, Electron Current in the p-Region:**
Repeating the identical analysis for electrons diffusing in the p-region:

$$I_n = \frac{qAD_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]$$

**Step 8 — Total Diode Current (Sum of Both Components):**
The total current flowing through the diode is the sum:

$$I = I_p + I_n = qA\left[\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right]\left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]$$

Defining the **reverse saturation current** as the bracketed coefficient:

$$I_S = qA\left[\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right]$$

We obtain the **Ideal Diode Equation (Shockley Equation)**:

$$\boxed{I = I_S \left[\exp\left(\frac{V_A}{V_T}\right) - 1\right]}$$

### 3.2 Limiting Cases (Behavioural Analysis Matrix)

| **Condition** | **Voltage Range** | **Current Behaviour** | **Physical Interpretation** |
|---------------|-------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Forward Bias (Strong)** | $V_A \gg V_T$ (e.g., $V_A > 0.3$ V) | $I \approx I_S \exp(V_A / V_T)$ | Exponential growth; "-1" negligible |
| **Forward Bias (Weak)** | $V_A \approx V_T$ | $I \approx 0.58 \cdot I_S \exp(V_A / V_T)$ | Both terms comparable |
| **Thermal Equilibrium** | $V_A = 0$ | $I = 0$ | No net current (cancellation of drift and diffusion) |
| **Reverse Bias** | $V_A \ll -V_T$ (e.g., $V_A < -0.3$ V) | $I \approx -I_S$ | Current saturates to $-I_S$ |

### 3.3 Temperature Dependence of $I_S$

A frequently tested KTU concept — the reverse saturation current has a strong temperature dependence:

$$I_S \propto T^3 \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{kT}\right)$$

A useful **engineering rule of thumb** for silicon diodes: $I_S$ **doubles** for every **10 °C rise** in temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Derivation Flow of the Ideal Diode Equation

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the analytical flow of the Shockley equation derivation, treating each physics principle as a functional module:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Equilibrium: Minority Carriers at p_n0 and n_p0] --> B[Apply External Bias V_A]
    B --> C[Barrier Potential Reduces: V_bi minus V_A]
    C --> D[Law of Junction: Excess Minority Carriers at Depletion Edges]
    D --> E[Excess Carriers Diffuse into Quasi-Neutral Regions]
    E --> F[Continuity Equation in Steady State]
    F --> G[Solve ODE with Boundary Conditions]
    G --> H[Exponential Carrier Decay Profile]
    H --> I[Ficks First Law: Diffusion Current Density J_p and J_n]
    I --> J[Integrate over Cross-Section A to get I_p and I_n]
    J --> K[Total Current: I equals I_p plus I_n]
    K --> L[Define Reverse Saturation Current I_S]
    L --> M[FINAL: Ideal Diode Equation I equals I_S exp V_A slash V_T minus 1]
    
    M --> N[Forward Bias: Exponential Current Growth]
    M --> O[Reverse Bias: Current Saturates to minus I_S]
    M --> P[Zero Bias: I equals 0 Equilibrium]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px
    style N fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style O fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style P fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Components of the Ideal Diode Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        V_D[Applied Voltage V_D]
        T[Absolute Temperature T]
        n_fact[Ideality Factor n]
    end
    
    subgraph COMPUTATION[Mathematical Engine]
        V_T_CALC[kT slash q equals V_T]
        EXP_TERM[Exponential of V_D slash nV_T]
        I_S_LOOKUP[Lookup I_S from Material Parameters]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output]
        I_D_OUT[Diode Current I_D]
    end
    
    V_D --> EXP_TERM
    T --> V_T_CALC
    n_fact --> EXP_TERM
    V_T_CALC --> EXP_TERM
    I_S_LOOKUP --> I_D_OUT
    EXP_TERM --> I_D_OUT
    
    style INPUT fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style COMPUTATION fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style OUTPUT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

### 4.3 V-I Characteristic Curve (Schematic Description)

Since Mermaid cannot render a continuous mathematical plot, the V-I characteristic of the ideal diode is described as a **functional map**:

- **Quadrant I (Forward):** Exponential curve starting from the origin, rising steeply. For $V_D = 0.1$ V → $I \approx 0.05$ mA; for $V_D = 0.7$ V → $I \approx 35$ mA (with $I_S = 10^{-15}$ A, $V_T = 25.85$ mV).
- **Quadrant III (Reverse):** Horizontal asymptote at $I = -I_S$ (typically a few pA to nA, virtually zero on the current scale).
- **Origin:** The curve passes exactly through $(0, 0)$, satisfying equilibrium.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define thermal voltage. Calculate its value at 300 K. State its significance in the ideal diode equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **thermal voltage** ($V_T$) is the voltage equivalent of thermal energy carried by a charge carrier at absolute temperature $T$. It arises naturally from the statistical distribution of electron energies in a semiconductor.

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$$

At $T = 300$ K:

$$V_T = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585 \text{ V} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$$

**Significance:**
- Acts as the **normalizing voltage** in the exponential term of the ideal diode equation.
- Determines the **steepness of the diode's exponential curve** — higher $V_T$ makes the diode turn on more gradually.
- Sets the fundamental **scale** for the curvature of band bending at the junction.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four key assumptions made during the derivation of the ideal diode equation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **One-dimensional current flow** — All current is assumed to flow perpendicular to the junction plane (along the x-axis).
2. **Steady-state condition** — The diode has reached equilibrium ($\partial / \partial t = 0$); no transient behaviour is considered.
3. **No generation or recombination in the depletion region** — Only minority carrier diffusion in the quasi-neutral regions contributes to current.
4. **Low-level injection** — The injected minority carrier concentration is much smaller than the majority carrier concentration (i.e., $\delta p_n \ll n_n$ and $\delta n_p \ll p_p$).
5. **No series resistance or contact effects** — The ideal diode is treated as a perfect device with no parasitic resistances.
6. **Uniform doping** — $N_A$ and $N_D$ are constant in the p and n regions, respectively.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the ideal diode equation (Shockley equation) starting from the continuity equation for minority carriers in the quasi-neutral regions of a p-n junction.**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are:

1. Write the **continuity equation** for holes in the n-region under steady-state, with no external generation:

$$D_p \frac{\partial^2 p_n}{\partial x^2} - \frac{p_n - p_{n0}}{\tau_p} = 0$$

2. Define **diffusion length** $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ and solve the second-order ODE to obtain:

$$p_n(x) = p_{n0} + p_{n0}\left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]\exp(-x / L_p)$$

3. Apply **Fick's first law** to find the hole diffusion current density and multiply by area $A$:

$$I_p = \frac{qAD_p p_{n0}}{L_p}\left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]$$

4. Repeat for electrons in the p-region to obtain $I_n$ by symmetry.

5. Sum both currents and define $I_S$ to obtain the final result:

$$\boxed{I = I_S \left[\exp(V_A / V_T) - 1\right]}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a silicon p-n junction diode, the reverse saturation current is $I_S = 10^{-13}$ A. Calculate the forward voltage required to produce a current of $I = 10$ mA. Take $n = 1$ and $T = 300$ K.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I_S = 10^{-13}$ A, $I = 10$ mA $= 10^{-2}$ A, $T = 300$ K, $n = 1$.

**Step 1 — Compute thermal voltage:**

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 25.85 \text{ mV} = 0.02585 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ideal Diode Equation:**
For $V_A \gg V_T$ (which is true since we expect ~0.6 V), the "-1" can be neglected:

$$I \approx I_S \exp(V_A / V_T)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_A$:**

$$V_A = V_T \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_S}\right)$$

$$V_A = (0.02585) \ln\left(\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-13}}\right) = (0.02585) \ln(10^{11})$$

$$V_A = (0.02585)(11)(2.3026) = (0.02585)(25.329)$$

$$\boxed{V_A \approx 0.6548 \text{ V} \approx 0.655 \text{ V}}$$

**Verification:** Including the "-1" term gives a correction of $\ln(1 + 10^{-11}) \approx 10^{-11}$, which is negligible. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With necessary diagrams, derive the expression for the reverse saturation current $I_S$ in terms of device parameters.**

**Model Solution:**

The reverse saturation current represents the **small, voltage-independent current** that flows under reverse bias. It arises from the **thermally generated minority carriers** being collected at the junction.

**Expression (derived in Section 3.1, Step 8):**

$$\boxed{I_S = qA\left[\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right]}$$

Substituting the thermal equilibrium minority carrier concentrations ($p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D$ and $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$) and the diffusion lengths ($L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$, $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$):

$$I_S = qAn_i^2\left[\frac{D_p}{N_D L_p} + \frac{D_n}{N_A L_n}\right] = qAn_i^2\left[\frac{1}{N_D}\sqrt{\frac{D_p}{\tau_p}} + \frac{1}{N_A}\sqrt{\frac{D_n}{\tau_n}}\right]$$

**Key Observations for KTU Exam:**
- $I_S$ is **proportional to $n_i^2$**, making it highly temperature-dependent (since $n_i$ itself depends exponentially on $T$).
- $I_S$ is **inversely proportional to doping concentrations** $N_A$ and $N_D$.
- $I_S$ is **directly proportional to junction area $A$**.
- Typical values: Silicon → 10⁻¹⁵ to 10⁻⁹ A; Germanium → 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁶ A.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a silicon p-n junction diode, the following parameters are given: $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm⁻³, $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³, $A = 10^{-4}$ cm², $D_p = 10$ cm²/s, $D_n = 25$ cm²/s, $\tau_p = \tau_n = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ s, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³. Calculate the reverse saturation current $I_S$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute minority carrier concentrations at equilibrium:**

$$p_{n0} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}{10^{15}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{20}}{10^{15}} = 2.25 \times 10^{5} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

$$n_{p0} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_A} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{20}}{10^{18}} = 2.25 \times 10^{2} = 225 \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Compute diffusion lengths:**

$$L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p} = \sqrt{10 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.236 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

$$L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n} = \sqrt{25 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}} = \sqrt{1.25 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.536 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the two terms inside the bracket:**

$$\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} = \frac{10 \times 2.25 \times 10^{5}}{2.236 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{6}}{2.236 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.006 \times 10^{9} \text{ cm}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} = \frac{25 \times 225}{3.536 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{5625}{3.536 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.591 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the terms (note the hole term dominates by ~3 orders of magnitude):**

$$\text{Sum} = 1.006 \times 10^{9} + 1.591 \times 10^{6} \approx 1.0076 \times 10^{9} \text{ cm}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $I_S$:**

$$I_S = qA \times (\text{Sum}) = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{-4})(1.0076 \times 10^{9})$$

$$I_S = 1.6 \times 10^{-14} \times 1.0076 \approx 1.612 \times 10^{-14} \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_S \approx 16.12 \text{ fA (femtoamperes)}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The hole-injection term ($I_{p,\text{term}}$) completely dominates because $N_D \ll N_A$ — the lightly doped n-side has far more minority carriers ($p_{n0} = 2.25 \times 10^5$) than the heavily doped p-side has electrons ($n_{p0} = 225$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1" term:** Students often write $I = I_S \exp(V/V_T)$. While acceptable for $V \gg V_T$, it loses **1 mark** in derivations where equilibrium ($V = 0 \Rightarrow I = 0$) must be proven.
> 2. **Confusing $I_S$ formula form:** The expression $I_S = qAD_p p_{n0}/L_p$ is correct; students frequently write $D_p/L_p^2$ instead of $D_p/L_p$ — losing **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Wrong unit of $L_p$:** Diffusion length is in **cm**, not cm²/s. Mixing diffusion coefficient units with length units is a classic KTU trap.
> 4. **Temperature of $I_S$:** When asked "how does $I_S$ change with temperature?", students say "it increases linearly." The correct answer is **exponentially** (proportional to $T^3 \exp(-E_g/kT)$).
> 5. **Dropping the exponential identity in step 3 of the derivation:** When solving the ODE, students must explicitly state why $B = 0$ (boundary condition at infinity). Skipping this loses the **logical completeness** mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Master Equation:** $I = I_S\left[\exp(V/nV_T) - 1\right]$ — the cornerstone of all diode analysis.
- ✅ **Thermal Voltage Value:** Always remember $V_T = 25.85$ mV at **300 K** (a KTU favorite).
- ✅ **Reverse Saturation Current:** $I_S$ is the **scale factor** of the equation, not the operating current.
- ✅ **Law of the Junction:** $p_n(0_n) = p_{n0} \exp(V_A / V_T)$ — the **starting point** of all derivations.
- ✅ **Boundary Conditions:** $x = 0$ (Law of the Junction) and $x = \infty$ (carriers vanish) — both must be **explicitly applied**.
- ✅ **Diffusion Length:** $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ and $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ — memorize the formula and units.
- ✅ **Minority Carrier Concentrations:** $p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D$ and $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$ — invert the doping!
- ✅ **Limiting Cases:** Strong forward bias → exponential; reverse bias → $-I_S$; zero bias → 0 A.
- ✅ **Temperature Behaviour:** $I_S$ has **exponential** temperature dependence; $V_T$ has **linear** temperature dependence.
- ✅ **Ideality Factor:** $n = 1$ (ideal), $n \approx 1$–$2$ (real). Default to $n = 1$ unless specified otherwise.
- ✅ **Dynamic Resistance:** $r_d = nV_T / I_D$ — used in small-signal AC analysis.
- ✅ **Rule of Thumb:** $I_S$ **doubles** for every **10 °C rise** — a quick check for sanity in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bipolar Junction Transistor: Transistor Action
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bipolar Junction Transistor: Transistor Action ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT):** A three-terminal, two-junction, current-controlled semiconductor device consisting of either a thin **p-type** base sandwiched between two **n-type** regions (NPN transistor) or a thin **n-type** base sandwiched between two **p-type** regions (PNP transistor). The term "**bipolar**" signifies that both **majority** and **minority** charge carriers participate in the conduction mechanism, distinguishing it fundamentally from unipolar (field-effect) devices. The BJT comprises two back-to-back PN junctions: 1. **Emitter-Base Junction (EBJ / J₁)** — the input junction 2. **Collector-Base Junction (CBJ / J₂)** — the output junction The three terminals are: - **Emitter (E):** Heavily doped region that **injects** charge carriers into the base. - **Base (B):** Thin, lightly doped central region that controls carrier transport. - **Collector (C):** Moderately doped region that **collects** the injected carriers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Faucet with Two Valves" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a water pipeline system. > - The **Emitter** is a high-pressure water tank (heavily doped, ready to inject). > - The **Base** is a thin, narrow, lightly-pressurized connecting pipe — its narrowness forces most water through before it can leak out sideways. > - The **Collector** is the main drainage tank at the other end, which collects almost all of the water that successfully passes through the base. > - A **small twist of the base valve** (a small base current $I_B$) controls a **massive flow of water** from the emitter to the collector (a large collector current $I_C$). This is the essence of **transistor action: current amplification via control.** Just as a small steering wheel input controls a large vehicle, a small base current modulates a large collector current. The base current is the *control signal*; the collector current is the *amplified output*. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** - **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV}}$ (commonly approximated as **26 mV**) - **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Silicon at 300 K:** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³** - **Typical current gain range:** $\alpha \approx 0.95$ to $0.99$ ; $\beta \approx 20$ to $500$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Emphasis:** The **active region** (also called the *linear* or *forward-active* region) — where the EBJ is **forward-biased** and the CBJ is **reverse-biased** — is the operational regime in which true *transistor action* (amplification) occurs. Memorize this bias condition verbatim; it is the most frequently asked 2-mark question in KTU ESE. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Sequential Physical Processes of Transistor Action When a BJT operates in the **forward-active mode** (EBJ forward-biased, CBJ reverse-biased), three cascading physical events take place: > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 — Emitter Injection (Emission):** > The forward-biased EBJ lowers the potential barrier at $J_1$. Majority carriers from the heavily doped emitter are injected across the junction into the base. For an **NPN** transistor, these are **electrons** flowing from the n-emitter into the p-base. For a **PNP** transistor, **holes** flow from the p-emitter into the n-base. The injection efficiency is high because the emitter doping ($N_E$) is much greater than the base doping ($N_B$). > [!NOTE] > **Step 2 — Base Transport (Diffusion & Recombination):** > Once injected into the thin, lightly doped base, the minority carriers (electrons in the p-base of an NPN) become **excess minority carriers**. They diffuse across the base toward the reverse-biased CBJ. Because the base is physically very thin (typically **0.1 µm to 1 µm**), and the base is lightly doped (low minority carrier lifetime controlled by doping), only a tiny fraction (~1% to 5%) of the injected carriers recombine with majority carriers in the base. This small recombination current is what constitutes the **base current $I_B$**. The remainder (~95% to 99%) successfully reaches the edge of the depletion region at $J_2$. > [!NOTE] > **Step 3 — Collector Collection (Sweeping):** > The reverse-biased CBJ has a strong built-in electric field within its depletion region. This field **sweeps** the minority carriers that have successfully diffused across the base into the collector region almost instantaneously. These collected carriers constitute the **collector current $I_C$**. The reverse bias also prevents any significant carrier injection from the collector back into the base. ### 2.2 The Why and How Behind Each Step - **Why is the emitter heavily doped?** A high emitter doping concentration maximizes the **emitter injection efficiency** $\gamma$, ensuring the maximum number of carriers are injected into the base per unit of applied forward bias. - **Why is the base thin and lightly doped?** A thin base minimizes the chance of **recombination** in the base, increasing the **base transport factor** $\alpha_T$. Light doping reduces the hole concentration available for recombination, again favoring electron transport. - **Why is the collector moderately doped?** The collector region needs to absorb heat and handle high voltages; moderate doping provides an optimal balance between breakdown voltage and on-resistance. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Memorization List for KTU 2024 ESE — Solid State Devices** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Boundary / Typical Value** | |---|---|---|---| | Kirchhoff's Current Law at BJT node | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | Emitter, Base, Collector currents | Always true in active region | | Common-Base current gain | $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ | Ratio of output to input current in CB config | $0 < \alpha < 1$, typical $\approx 0.99$ | | Common-Emitter current gain | $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | Ratio of output to input current in CE config | $20 \leq \beta \leq 500$ typical | | $\alpha$ in terms of $\beta$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{1+\beta}$ | Algebraic inter-conversion | $\alpha \to 1$ as $\beta \to \infty$ | | $\beta$ in terms of $\alpha$ | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$ | Algebraic inter-conversion | $\beta \to \infty$ as $\alpha \to 1$ | | Emitter injection efficiency | $\gamma = \dfrac{I_{E,\text{injected}}}{I_E}$ | Fraction of emitter current that is useful injection | $\gamma \to 1$ for $N_E \gg N_B$ | | Base transport factor | $\alpha_T = \dfrac{I_{C,\text{collected}}}{I_{E,\text{injected}}}$ | Fraction of injected carriers reaching collector | $\alpha_T \to 1$ for thin base | | Large-signal $\alpha$ decomposition | $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T \cdot M$ | $M$ = collector multiplication factor ($\approx 1$) | All three factors approach 1 | | Collector current with leakage | $I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO}$ | $I_{CBO}$ = CB leakage current with E open | $I_{CBO}$ is in nA to µA range | | Collector current in terms of $I_B$ | $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ | Base current drives amplified output | $(1+\beta)I_{CBO}$ is significant at high T | | Emitter current in terms of $I_B$ | $I_E = (1+\beta) I_B + I_{CBO}$ | KCL re-arrangement | Dominant term is $(1+\beta)I_B$ | | Base-width modulation (Early) | $I_C \approx I_S \left(e^{V_{BE}/V_T}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right)$ | $V_A$ = Early voltage (50–200 V) | Output resistance $r_o = V_A/I_C$ | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio amplifiers and signal conditioning circuits** in consumer electronics rely on BJT active-region operation. - **RF oscillators and mixers** in telecommunications use BJTs in the active region for high-frequency amplification. - **Switching regulators and digital logic families (TTL, ECL)** exploit BJT operation in the **cutoff** ($I_C = 0$) and **saturation** ($V_{CE} \approx 0.2$ V) regions, leaving the active region as the transition. - **Analog integrated circuits** (op-amps, comparators) use matched BJT pairs in the active region for precise current-mirroring and differential amplification. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Fundamental Relationship Between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Standard:** This is the **single most-asked derivation** in Module 2 of PCECT302 Solid State Devices. Examiners expect every algebraic step to be visible on the answer sheet. **Starting Point — Kirchhoff's Current Law Applied at the BJT Terminals:** By the conservation of charge, the current entering the emitter terminal must equal the sum of currents leaving the base and collector terminals. In the forward-active region, conventional current flows **into** the emitter, and **out of** the base and collector (for an NPN device). $$ I_E = I_B + I_C $$ **Defining the Common-Base Current Gain $\alpha$:** The fraction of emitter current that successfully reaches the collector defines the large-signal common-base current gain. By convention, this is a dimensionless ratio less than unity (but very close to it). $$ \alpha \equiv \frac{I_C}{I_E} $$ This implies that we can write the collector current as a fraction of the emitter current: $$ I_C = \alpha \, I_E $$ **Substituting Into the KCL Equation:** Substitute $I_C = \alpha I_E$ into the KCL equation $I_E = I_B + I_C$: $$ I_E = I_B + \alpha \, I_E $$ **Isolating $I_B$ on One Side of the Equation:** Move the term $\alpha I_E$ from the right-hand side to the left-hand side by subtracting it from both sides: $$ I_E - \alpha \, I_E = I_B $$ **Factoring Out the Common Factor $I_E$ on the Left-Hand Side:** Both terms on the left contain the common factor $I_E$.
