Factor it out to simplify: $$ I_E \, (1 - \alpha) = I_B $$ **Solving for the Ratio $I_E / I_B$:** Divide both sides by $I_B$ to make the equation useful, but more directly, we now solve for the ratio $I_E / I_B$ by dividing both sides by $I_B$ on the right and by $I_B$ on the left (after division): $$ \frac{I_E}{I_B} = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha} $$ **Defining the Common-Emitter Current Gain $\beta$:** By definition, the large-signal common-emitter current gain is the ratio of collector current to base current: $$ \beta \equiv \frac{I_C}{I_B} $$ Since $I_C = \alpha I_E$, we have: $$ \beta = \frac{\alpha \, I_E}{I_B} = \alpha \cdot \frac{I_E}{I_B} $$ Substituting $I_E / I_B = 1/(1-\alpha)$: $$ \beta = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} $$ **This is the master relationship.** Now inverting it to obtain $\alpha$ in terms of $\beta$: $$ \beta (1 - \alpha) = \alpha $$ $$ \beta - \beta \alpha = \alpha $$ $$ \beta = \alpha + \beta \alpha = \alpha (1 + \beta) $$ $$ \alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Numerical Sanity Check:** If $\beta = 100$, then $\alpha = 100/101 \approx 0.9901$. This is a typical silicon BJT value — confirming that $\alpha$ is just slightly less than unity, while $\beta$ is a large amplification factor. **A 1% change in $\alpha$ corresponds to an enormous change in $\beta$** near the high-gain limit. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Collector Current with Reverse-Saturation Leakage $I_{CBO}$ In a real BJT, a small reverse-saturation current $I_{CBO}$ flows across the reverse-biased CBJ even when the emitter is open-circuited ($I_E = 0$). This current is generated by thermally produced minority carriers in the base and collector regions. We must therefore add $I_{CBO}$ to the ideal collector-current expression: $$ I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO} $$ Now we eliminate $I_E$ in favor of $I_B$ using the KCL relation $I_E = I_B + I_C$: $$ I_C = \alpha (I_B + I_C) + I_{CBO} $$ Expand the right-hand side: $$ I_C = \alpha I_B + \alpha I_C + I_{CBO} $$ Move the term $\alpha I_C$ from the right-hand side to the left-hand side: $$ I_C - \alpha I_C = \alpha I_B + I_{CBO} $$ Factor out $I_C$ on the left: $$ I_C (1 - \alpha) = \alpha I_B + I_{CBO} $$ Solve for $I_C$ by dividing both sides by $(1 - \alpha)$: $$ I_C = \frac{\alpha I_B}{1 - \alpha} + \frac{I_{CBO}}{1 - \alpha} $$ Using the relations $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ and $1/(1-\alpha) = 1 + \beta$ (the latter follows from the inversion of $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ giving $1-\alpha = \alpha/\beta$ and then $1/(1-\alpha) = \beta/\alpha$; combined with $1+\beta = (1-\alpha+\alpha)/(1-\alpha) = 1/(1-\alpha)$, both yield the same result): $$ I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The term $(1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ is the **amplified leakage current** — even a tiny $I_{CBO}$ of a few nanoamperes gets multiplied by the gain $\beta$ and can become significant at elevated temperatures. This is why BJT circuits are vulnerable to **thermal runaway** and require careful bias compensation. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Emitter Current in Terms of Base Current Starting again from KCL: $$ I_E = I_B + I_C $$ Substitute the expression $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$: $$ I_E = I_B + \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO} $$ Group like terms: $$ I_E = (1 + \beta) I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO} $$ Factor out $(1+\beta)$: $$ I_E = (1 + \beta)(I_B + I_{CBO}) $$ This compact form is sometimes called the **Ebers-Moll forward-active simplification** and is widely used in BJT small-signal and large-signal analysis. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Current Components in an NPN BJT (Step-by-Step Physical Picture) Let $I_{En}$ be the electron current injected from the n-type emitter into the p-type base. Let $I_{Ep}$ be the (small, unwanted) hole current injected from the p-type base back into the n-type emitter. The total emitter current is: $$ I_E = I_{En} + I_{Ep} $$ **Define the Emitter Injection Efficiency $\gamma$:** $$ \gamma = \frac{I_{En}}{I_{En} + I_{Ep}} = \frac{I_{En}}{I_E} $$ In the p-type base, a small fraction of the injected electrons recombine with holes. Let $I_{B,\text{rec}}$ be the recombination current component. Define the **base transport factor** $\alpha_T$: $$ \alpha_T = \frac{I_{En} - I_{B,\text{rec}}}{I_{En}} = \frac{I_{C,\text{ideal}}}{I_{En}} $$ The collector current then becomes: $$ I_C = \alpha_T \cdot \gamma \cdot I_E \cdot M $$ where $M$ is the collector multiplication factor (very close to 1 for moderate reverse bias, since avalanche breakdown is not yet initiated). Defining $\alpha = \gamma \alpha_T M$ recovers the original $\alpha = I_C / I_E$. > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Illustration:** Suppose $I_E = 1.00$ mA, $\gamma = 0.998$, $\alpha_T = 0.992$, $M = 1.000$. Then $\alpha = (0.998)(0.992)(1.000) = 0.990$. And $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha) = 0.990/0.010 = 99$. So $I_C = 0.990$ mA, $I_B = 0.010$ mA. The three-step process yields amplification with a 1% base current needed to control 99% collector current. ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | **Process Step** | **Where it Happens** | **Current Term** | **Quality Factor** | **Target** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1. Emitter Injection | EBJ depletion edge | $I_E = I_{En} + I_{Ep}$ | $\gamma$ | $\gamma \to 1$ | | 2. Base Transport | Quasi-neutral base | $I_{B,\text{rec}} = I_{En} - I_C$ | $\alpha_T$ | $\alpha_T \to 1$ | | 3. Collector Collection | CBJ depletion edge | $I_C = \alpha_T \cdot I_{En} \cdot M$ | $M$ | $M \to 1$ | | Net Forward Action | Full device | $\alpha = \gamma \alpha_T M$ | $\alpha \to 1$ | $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha) \gg 1$ | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transistor Action Flow (Forward-Active Mode) > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below maps the **three sequential physical processes** of transistor action, the **terminal currents** at each node, and the **quality factors** governing each step. Node IDs are alphanumeric to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. ```mermaid graph TD A["EMITTER REGION
Heavily Doped n-type N_E"]:::source B["EBJ DEPLETION EDGE
Junction J1 Forward Biased"]:::junction C["BASE REGION
Thin p-type Width W_B"]:::base D["CBJ DEPLETION EDGE
Junction J2 Reverse Biased"]:::junction E["COLLECTOR REGION
Moderately Doped n-type N_C"]:::sink F["TERMINAL I_E FLOWS INTO E"]:::term G["TERMINAL I_B FLOWS OUT OF B"]:::term H["TERMINAL I_C FLOWS OUT OF C"]:::term P1["STEP 1 EMITTER INJECTION
Gamma = Emitter Efficiency"]:::process P2["STEP 2 BASE TRANSPORT
alpha_T = Transport Factor"]:::process P3["STEP 3 COLLECTOR COLLECTION
M = Multiplication Factor"]:::process A -->|Electron Injection I_En| P1 P1 -->|Forward Bias V_BE > 0| B B -->|Excess Minority Electrons Diffuse| C C -->|Recombination Loss I_B_rec| P2 P2 -->|Surviving Electrons Reach Depletion Edge| D D -->|Reverse Bias V_BC < 0 Electric Field Sweeps| P3 P3 -->|Collected Electrons| E F --> A C --> G E --> H classDef source fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sink fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef base fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef junction fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef term fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#8b6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Energy-Band Diagram Representation (Schematic Block Form) > [!NOTE] > Since Mermaid cannot natively draw continuous energy band curves, the following block architecture maps the **relative potential energy levels** across the three regions of an NPN BJT biased in the forward-active mode. ```mermaid graph LR REG1["EMITTER n+ REGION
E_c High Position
E_F Near E_c"]:::emitter REG2["EBJ DEPLETION
Barrier Lowered by V_BE
Forward Bias Active"]:::depletion1 REG3["BASE p REGION
Thin Quasi-Neutral Zone
E_c Slightly Lower Than E"]:::base REG4["CBJ DEPLETION
Barrier Widened by V_CB
Strong Built-in Field"]:::depletion2 REG5["COLLECTOR n REGION
E_c Lowest Position
E_F Far From E_c"]:::collector NOTE["TRANSISTOR ACTION: Electrons Fall
From High E_c in Emitter Into
Low E_c in Collector via Base"]:::annot REG1 --- REG2 --- REG3 --- REG4 --- REG5 NOTE -.-> REG3 classDef emitter fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef base fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef collector fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef depletion1 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef depletion2 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef annot fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray:5 5 ``` ### 4.3 Block Functional Architecture of Current Relationships ```mermaid graph TD IN["INPUT VARIABLE
I_B Base Current"]:::input GAIN1["MULTIPLICATION BLOCK
Multiply by Beta"]:::gain OUT1["OUTPUT VARIABLE
I_C Collector Current"]:::output GAIN2["MULTIPLICATION BLOCK
Multiply by 1 plus Beta"]:::gain2 OUT2["OUTPUT VARIABLE
I_E Emitter Current"]:::output2 KCL["KCL VERIFICATION NODE
I_E = I_B + I_C"]:::kcl ALPHA["DERIVED RATIO
alpha = I_C over I_E"]:::ratio IN -->|I_B| GAIN1 GAIN1 -->|beta times I_B| OUT1 IN -->|I_B| GAIN2 GAIN2 -->|1+beta times I_B| OUT2 OUT1 --> KCL OUT2 --> KCL OUT2 -->|I_E| ALPHA OUT1 -->|I_C| ALPHA classDef input fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output2 fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gain fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gain2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef kcl fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ratio fill:#e6e6ff,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** > **Define the term "transistor action" in a Bipolar Junction Transistor. What are the biasing conditions required for an NPN transistor to operate in the active region?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** *Transistor action* refers to the controlled transfer of charge carriers from the emitter to the collector via the base region, wherein a small current flowing into the base terminal controls a much larger current flowing from emitter to collector. The fundamental physical process consists of three sequential steps: **(i) emitter injection** of majority carriers across the forward-biased emitter-base junction, **(ii) base transport** of these minority carriers across the thin quasi-neutral base, and **(iii) collector collection** of the carriers that survive base recombination, swept by the electric field of the reverse-biased collector-base junction. **Biasing Conditions (1.5 marks):** For an **NPN** transistor in the **forward-active (linear) region**: - The **emitter-base junction (EBJ) must be forward-biased** ($V_{BE} > 0$, typically $\approx 0.7$ V for silicon). - The **collector-base junction (CBJ) must be reverse-biased** ($V_{CB} > 0$, equivalently $V_{CE} > V_{BE}$). --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]** > **Distinguish between common-base current gain ($\alpha$) and common-emitter current gain ($\beta$) of a BJT. State the relationship between them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition of $\alpha$ (1 mark):** The common-base current gain $\alpha$ is defined as the ratio of the **collector current** to the **emitter current** in a common-base configuration: $$ \alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1 $$ **Definition of $\beta$ (1 mark):** The common-emitter current gain $\beta$ is defined as the ratio of the **collector current** to the **base current** in a common-emitter configuration: $$ \beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B}, \quad \beta \gg 1 $$ **Relationship (1 mark):** $$ \alpha = \frac{\beta}{1+\beta} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \beta = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} $$ --- ### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, End Semester]** > > **(a)** With the help of a neat energy-band diagram, explain the transistor action in an NPN bipolar junction transistor operating in the forward-active region. Clearly identify the biasing of each junction and describe the three physical processes that enable current flow. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For a silicon NPN BJT in the active region, the emitter current is $I_E = 2.50$ mA and the common-base current gain is $\alpha = 0.980$. Calculate the common-emitter current gain $\beta$, the collector current $I_C$, and the base current $I_B$. If the reverse-saturation leakage current is $I_{CBO} = 5$ nA, compute the modified collector current. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-A: **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Biasing Setup (1 mark):** In the forward-active region, the EBJ is forward-biased ($V_{BE} \approx +0.7$ V for silicon) and the CBJ is reverse-biased ($V_{CB} > 0$). **Energy-Band Diagram Description (2 marks):** The conduction band edge $E_c$ in the heavily doped n⁺ emitter lies at a high energy. Upon forward biasing the EBJ, the built-in barrier at $J_1$ is reduced. Electrons (the majority carriers in the n⁺ emitter) gain sufficient thermal energy to overcome this lowered barrier and diffuse into the p-type base. The p-type base region is thin, so the injected minority electrons traverse it with minimal recombination. The CBJ, being reverse-biased, exhibits a widened depletion region with a strong electric field pointing from the n-collector to the p-base. This field sweeps the electrons that arrive at the depletion edge of the base into the collector, where they fall down to a lower energy level in the conduction band of the collector. **Three Processes (4 marks = 1.5 + 1.5 + 1):** 1. **Emitter Injection (1.5 marks):** The forward-biased EBJ injects electrons from the heavily doped n⁺ emitter into the p-type base. The injection efficiency $\gamma$ depends on the doping ratio $N_E / N_B$. Since $N_E \gg N_B$, $\gamma$ is close to unity. 2. **Base Transport (1.5 marks):** The injected minority electrons diffuse across the quasi-neutral p-base. Because the base is physically thin and lightly doped, the base transport factor $\alpha_T$ is very close to 1 (e.g., 0.99). The small recombination current in the base constitutes the base current $I_B$. 3. **Collector Collection (1 mark):** The reverse-biased CBJ sweeps the surviving electrons into the collector region, contributing to $I_C$. The multiplication factor $M \approx 1$ under normal reverse-bias conditions. The net large-signal gain is $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T \cdot M \approx 0.99$. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Calculate $\beta$ (2 marks):** Using the relationship $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$: $$ \beta = \frac{0.980}{1 - 0.980} = \frac{0.980}{0.020} = 49 $$ **Step 2 — Calculate $I_C$ (2 marks):** Using $\alpha = I_C / I_E$: $$ I_C = \alpha \cdot I_E = 0.980 \times 2.50 \text{ mA} = 2.45 \text{ mA} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate $I_B$ (1 mark):** Using KCL $I_E = I_B + I_C$: $$ I_B = I_E - I_C = 2.50 \text{ mA} - 2.45 \text{ mA} = 0.05 \text{ mA} = 50 \text{ μA} $$ **Verification:** $\beta = I_C / I_B = 2.45 / 0.05 = 49$ ✓ **Step 4 — Calculate Modified $I_C$ with $I_{CBO}$ (2 marks):** Using $I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}$: $$ I_C = (49)(0.05 \text{ mA}) + (1 + 49)(5 \text{ nA}) $$ $$ I_C = 2.45 \text{ mA} + (50)(5 \text{ nA}) $$ $$ I_C = 2.45 \text{ mA} + 250 \text{ nA} = 2.45 \text{ mA} + 0.00025 \text{ mA} $$ $$ I_C \approx 2.45025 \text{ mA} \approx 2.45 \text{ mA} $$ **Conclusion:** The reverse-saturation leakage $I_{CBO}$ contributes negligibly (250 nA vs. 2.45 mA) at room temperature, justifying its neglect in hand calculations. However, at elevated temperatures, $I_{CBO}$ can become significant and may cause **thermal runaway**. --- > **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, End Semester — Alternative Choice]** > > **(a)** Explain the three sequential processes of transistor action in a BJT. Define the emitter injection efficiency ($\gamma$) and the base transport factor ($\alpha_T$), and derive the expression for the large-signal common-base current gain in terms of these. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** In a pnp silicon transistor operating in the active region, the base current is $I_B = 20$ μA, the collector current is $I_C = 1.98$ mA, and the reverse-saturation current $I_{CBO} = 2$ nA. Compute (i) the common-emitter current gain $\beta$, (ii) the common-base current gain $\alpha$, (iii) the emitter current $I_E$, and (iv) verify KCL at the BJT terminal. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-B: **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Three Processes (3 marks = 1 + 1 + 1):** 1. **Emitter Injection:** The forward-biased EBJ causes majority carriers from the heavily doped emitter to flood into the thin, lightly doped base. The current associated with this injection is $I_{En}$ (for NPN, electrons from n-emitter to p-base). 2. **Base Transport:** Injected minority carriers diffuse across the base. Most reach the CBJ depletion edge; a small fraction recombines with majority carriers, generating the base current component. 3. **Collector Collection:** The reverse-biased CBJ's electric field sweeps the surviving carriers into the collector, generating the collector current. **Definition of $\gamma$ (1.5 marks):** The emitter injection efficiency is the ratio of the useful injected current to the total emitter current: $$ \gamma = \frac{I_{En}}{I_E} = \frac{I_{En}}{I_{En} + I_{Ep}} $$ For $N_E \gg N_B$, the back-injection $I_{Ep}$ is negligible, and $\gamma \to 1$. **Definition of $\alpha_T$ (1.5 marks):** The base transport factor is the fraction of injected carriers that successfully reach the collector: $$ \alpha_T = \frac{I_C}{I_{En}} $$ **Derivation of $\alpha$ (1 mark):** Combining the two definitions: $$ I_C = \alpha_T \cdot I_{En} = \alpha_T \cdot \gamma \cdot I_E = \alpha \cdot I_E $$ $$ \boxed{\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T} $$ (Including the multiplication factor $M$, the complete expression is $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T \cdot M$, where $M \approx 1$.) **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Common-emitter current gain $\beta$ (2 marks):** $$ \beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{1.98 \text{ mA}}{20 \text{ μA}} = \frac{1.98 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1980}{20} = 99 $$ **(ii) Common-base current gain $\alpha$ (2 marks):** $$ \alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} = \frac{99}{1 + 99} = \frac{99}{100} = 0.99 $$ **(iii) Emitter current $I_E$ (2 marks):** Using KCL: $$ I_E = I_B + I_C = 20 \text{ μA} + 1.98 \text{ mA} = 0.020 \text{ mA} + 1.980 \text{ mA} = 2.00 \text{ mA} $$ **(iv) KCL Verification (1 mark):** $$ I_B + I_C = 20 \text{ μA} + 1.98 \text{ mA} = 2.00 \text{ mA} = I_E \quad \checkmark $$ **Bonus — Check Leakage Effect:** Modified $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO} = (99)(20 \text{ μA}) + (100)(2 \text{ nA}) = 1.98 \text{ mA} + 0.2 \text{ μA} \approx 1.98 \text{ mA}$, confirming $I_{CBO}$ is negligible. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE PCECT302:** > > 1. **Inverting $\alpha$ and $\beta$ formulas (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students write $\alpha = 1 - \beta$ or $\beta = (1-\alpha)/\alpha$ — both are wrong. The correct relations are $\alpha = \beta/(1+\beta)$ and $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$. Memorize both. > > 2. **Forgetting to mention biasing conditions (–1 mark):** A "transistor action" question that doesn't explicitly state *EBJ forward-biased, CBJ reverse-biased* is incomplete. Always begin such answers with this statement. > > 3. **Confusing current directions in PNP vs NPN (–1 mark):** In a PNP transistor in the active region, conventional current flows *out of* the emitter and *into* the base and collector — exactly opposite to NPN. State this explicitly. > > 4. **Skipping the derivation steps in the $\alpha$-$\beta$ relation (–2 marks):** Examiners require every algebraic transition to be visible. Do NOT write only the final box; show $I_C = \alpha I_E$, substitution into KCL, rearrangement, and final substitution. > > 5. **Ignoring $I_{CBO}$ in numerical problems when it is given (–1 mark):** If the problem statement provides $I_{CBO}$, you MUST use the modified formula $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$. Simply using $I_C = \beta I_B$ will lose the methodological mark. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — BJT Transistor Action** - ☐ **BJT = Bipolar Junction Transistor** — three terminals (E, B, C), two PN junctions (EBJ, CBJ), two flavors (NPN, PNP). - ☐ **Forward-Active region** = EBJ forward-biased + CBJ reverse-biased — the regime of amplification. - ☐ **Three steps of transistor action:** (1) Emitter Injection → (2) Base Transport → (3) Collector Collection. - ☐ **Emitter is heavily doped** (high $N_E$) to maximize injection efficiency $\gamma$. - ☐ **Base is thin and lightly doped** to maximize base transport factor $\alpha_T$. - ☐ **Collector is moderately doped** to handle voltage and heat. - ☐ **KCL always holds:** $I_E = I_B + I_C$ in the active region. - ☐ **$\alpha$ definition:** $\alpha = I_C / I_E$, value between 0.95 and 0.999 typically. - ☐ **$\beta$ definition:** $\beta = I_C / I_B$, value between 20 and 500 typically. - ☐ **Master relations:** $\alpha = \beta / (1 + \beta)$ and $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$. - ☐ **Current decomposition:** $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T \cdot M$ with each factor $\to 1$. - ☐ **Modified $I_C$ with leakage:** $I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}$. - ☐ **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T \approx 26$ mV. - ☐ **Typical silicon $V_{BE}$** in active region $\approx 0.7$ V. - ☐ **Early effect (base-width modulation):** $I_C$ increases slightly with $V_{CE}$ due to narrowing of effective base width; characterized by Early voltage $V_A$. - ☐ **PNP current directions are reversed** compared to NPN in the active region. - ☐ **Cutoff:** $I_C = 0$ (both junctions reverse-biased). **Saturation:** $V_{CE} \approx 0.2$ V (both junctions forward-biased). - ☐ **Practical use:** Audio/RF amplifiers, switching regulators, digital logic families (TTL, ECL), analog ICs (op-amps, current mirrors). - ☐ **Always state biasing conditions first** when describing transistor action in KTU answers. - ☐ **Always show full algebraic steps** for $\alpha$–$\beta$ derivation — no skipping allowed.

topics.title: Base Width Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Base Width Modulation # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Base Width Modulation (Early Effect):** It is the phenomenon in a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) wherein the **effective electrical width of the neutral base region** changes as a function of the reverse-bias voltage applied across the **Base–Collector (B–C) junction**. An increase in the collector–base reverse bias $V_{CB}$ widens the depletion region at the B–C junction, encroaching into the lightly-doped base, thereby *reducing* the effective base width $W_B$ and modulating the transistor's terminal currents even when $V_{BE}$ is held constant. The BJT was originally treated as having a fixed (metallurgical) base width. In reality, the depletion region at the reverse-biased collector junction is not infinitely thin, and its penetration into the base is **bias-dependent**. This voltage-controlled penetration is the physical origin of base width modulation. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The metallurgical base width $W_{B,\text{met}}$ is a *physical* constant set by fabrication. The *effective* base width $W_{B,\text{eff}}$ is a *electrical* quantity that shrinks as $|V_{CB}|$ grows. The transistor's small-signal gain, saturation current, and output resistance all depend on $W_{B,\text{eff}}$, not on $W_{B,\text{met}}$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **thin water pipe (the base)** sandwiched between two water tanks (emitter and collector). Water flows from the emitter tank, through the pipe, into the collector tank. Now imagine a **flexible rubber sleeve** at the collector end of the pipe that *squeezes inward* as the collector-side water pressure rises. - The pipe's *physical* length does not change — that is $W_{B,\text{met}}$. - But the **free-flowing water channel** inside the pipe gets narrower when pressure is applied — that is $W_{B,\text{eff}}$. - A narrower channel means a steeper concentration gradient, so more water (current) flows for the same inlet pressure ($V_{BE}$). - This "squeezing" is exactly what the depletion region does to the neutral base under increasing $V_{CB}$. > [!TIP] > **Why does it matter in real circuits?** The Early Effect causes the output characteristics of a BJT in the active region to slope upward (instead of being flat). This finite slope introduces a finite output resistance $r_o$, which limits the **voltage gain** of common-emitter amplifiers. It also causes $\beta$ (current gain) to vary with $V_{CE}$ — undesirable in precision analog design unless compensated by cascoding or current-mirror topologies. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Thermal voltage** at room temperature ($T = 300\,\text{K}$): $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85\,\text{mV}}$, commonly approximated as **26 mV**. - **Early voltage** $V_A$: a **positive** device parameter (typically **50 V to 200 V** for modern npn BJTs; can be **> 100 V** in well-designed processes). - **Built-in potential** of the B–C junction: $V_{bi,C} \approx \mathbf{0.7\,V}$ (silicon, moderate doping). - **Permittivity of silicon:** $\epsilon_s = \mathbf{11.7}\,\epsilon_0 = \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-12}\,F/cm}$. - **Elementary charge:** $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,C}$. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Mechanism 1. **Forward bias on E–B junction** ($V_{BE} > 0$): Minority carriers (electrons in an npn) are injected from emitter into the base. 2. **Reverse bias on B–C junction** ($V_{CB} > 0$): A depletion region forms at the metallurgical junction. 3. **Depletion region is asymmetric**: because the base is *lightly* doped relative to the collector ($N_B \ll N_C$), most of the depletion width extends **into the base side**. 4. **As $V_{CB}$ increases**, the depletion width $x_{nB}$ on the base side grows, **eating into the neutral base**. 5. **Effective base width shrinks**: $W_{B,\text{eff}} = W_{B,\text{met}} - x_{nB}$. 6. **Concentration gradient steepens**: the minority-carrier density at the emitter edge stays ≈ $n_E \propto e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$, while at the collector edge it stays ≈ 0 (reverse bias sweeps carriers out). A *narrower* base → *steeper* slope → **larger diffusion current**. 7. **Net result**: $I_C$ rises with $V_{CB}$ even at constant $V_{BE}$ — the **Early Effect**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Early Effect is a *small* but *first-order* effect in analog IC design. In digital switching, it is usually neglected. In precision analog circuits, it dominates the finite output resistance of current sources and gain stages. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates *all* equations you must know for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on this topic. Memorize the units and the regime of validity. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Typical Range | Notes | |---|----------|---------|------------------------|-------| | 1 | Saturation current (no modulation) | $I_S = \dfrac{A_E\, q\, D_n\, n_i^{\,2}}{N_B\, W_B}$ | A | Depends on effective $W_B$ | | 2 | Collector current (active) | $I_C = I_S\, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$ | A | Ideal, no modulation | | 3 | Depletion width into base | $x_{nB} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})}{q\,N_B}}$ | cm | Base is lightly doped | | 4 | Effective base width | $W_{B,\text{eff}} = W_{B,\text{met}} - x_{nB}$ | cm | Always $\le W_{B,\text{met}}$ | | 5 | Modified collector current (Early) | $I_C = I_{C0}\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right)$ | A | $I_{C0}$ = extrapolated at $V_{CE}=0$ | | 6 | Current gain modulation | $\beta = \beta_0\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right)$ | — | $\beta_0$ = low-$V_{CE}$ value | | 7 | Output conductance | $g_o = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{CE}} = \dfrac{I_C}{V_A}$ | S (siemens) | At constant $V_{BE}$ | | 8 | Output resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{g_o} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$ | $\Omega$ | Small-signal, active region | | 9 | Early voltage (definition) | $V_A^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{W_{B,\text{eff}}}\dfrac{dW_{B,\text{eff}}}{dV_{CE}}$ | V | Sign and slope defined by intersection | | 10 | Intrinsic carrier conc. (Si, 300 K) | $n_i = \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ | — | Standard textbook value | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-character pitfall:** When computing $x_{nB}$, the quantity inside the square root is $(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})$, **not** $(V_{bi,C} - V_{CB})$. Reverse bias *adds* to the built-in potential. Forgetting this sign is the most common mark-loser in KTU derivations. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Analog IC Design (BJT op-amps like LM741):** Base width modulation determines the output resistance of every active-load current source. Engineers use **cascode** stages or **long-channel** devices to *boost* $V_A$ and *reduce* $g_o$. - **Current Mirrors:** Finite $r_o$ causes mirroring ratio errors. Higher $V_A$ → better matching. - **RF & Microwave Transistors:** High $V_A$ implies high Early voltage, which translates to high output impedance and high intrinsic voltage gain $g_m r_o$. - **Digital Switching (TTL, ECL):** Neglected to first order; only affects the saturation voltage $V_{CE,\text{sat}}$ slightly. - **Parameter Extraction:** The slope of measured $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ curves in the active region is used to *extract* $V_A$ experimentally. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Depletion Width Penetration into the Base **Starting premise:** Consider the reverse-biased B–C junction of an npn BJT. Charge neutrality across the depletion region requires the *ionized* charge on the base side to equal the *ionized* charge on the collector side: $$ q N_B x_{nB} = q N_C x_{pC} $$ **Step 1 — Apply depletion approximation.** The total depletion width $W_d = x_{nB} + x_{pC}$. Using the charge equality above, we can express the junction capacitance relation: $$ W_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})}{q}\,\dfrac{N_B + N_C}{N_B N_C}} $$ **Step 2 — Take the lightly-doped-base limit.** In a typical npn BJT, $N_B \ll N_C$, which means $N_B + N_C \approx N_C$. The fraction simplifies: $$ \dfrac{N_B + N_C}{N_B N_C} \approx \dfrac{N_C}{N_B N_C} = \dfrac{1}{N_B} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the base-side penetration.** Since the depletion width lies almost entirely in the base ($x_{nB} \approx W_d$) under the asymmetry assumption: $$ \begin{aligned} x_{nB} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})}{q\,N_B}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Identify the physical meaning.** This expression shows that $x_{nB}$ grows with $\sqrt{V_{CB}}$ and inversely with $\sqrt{N_B}$. A heavily-doped base would suppress the Early Effect; a lightly-doped base amplifies it. > [!NOTE] > **Boundary check:** At $V_{CB} = 0$, $x_{nB}$ reduces to the equilibrium depletion width $x_{nB,0} = \sqrt{2\epsilon_s V_{bi,C}/(qN_B)}$, which is the standard one-sided abrupt-junction result. ## 3.2 Derivation II — Effective Base Width and Its Dependence on $V_{CE}$ **Step 1 — Define the effective (neutral) base width.** The neutral region is the metallurgical base minus the depletion layer on the base side of the B–C junction: $$ W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CB}) = W_{B,\text{met}} - x_{nB}(V_{CB}) $$ **Step 2 — Take the differential with respect to $V_{CE}$.** In the forward-active region, $V_{CE} = V_{CB} + V_{BE}$. Holding $V_{BE}$ constant, $dV_{CE} = dV_{CB}$, so: $$ \dfrac{dW_{B,\text{eff}}}{dV_{CE}} = -\dfrac{dx_{nB}}{dV_{CB}} $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate the square-root expression for $x_{nB}$.** Let $K = \dfrac{2\epsilon_s}{qN_B}$ and $u = V_{bi,C} + V_{CB}$, so $x_{nB} = \sqrt{K u}$: $$ \dfrac{dx_{nB}}{dV_{CB}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{u}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{x_{nB}}{V_{bi,C} + V_{CB}} $$ **Step 4 — Therefore:** $$ \dfrac{dW_{B,\text{eff}}}{dV_{CE}} = -\dfrac{x_{nB}}{2\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})} $$ The negative sign confirms: as $V_{CE}$ rises, $W_{B,\text{eff}}$ **decreases**. ## 3.3 Derivation III — The Early Voltage and Modified $I_C$ **Step 1 — Start from the collector current in the active region (with effective $W_B$):** $$ I_C = \dfrac{A_E\, q\, D_n\, n_i^{\,2}}{N_B\, W_{B,\text{eff}}}\, e^{V_{BE}/V_T} $$ **Step 2 — Define a reference $I_{C0}$ at a chosen low $V_{CE} = V_{CE,0}$:** $$ I_{C0} = \dfrac{A_E\, q\, D_n\, n_i^{\,2}}{N_B\, W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE,0})}\, e^{V_{BE}/V_T} $$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio $I_C / I_{C0}$:** $$ \dfrac{I_C}{I_{C0}} = \dfrac{W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE,0})}{W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE})} $$ **Step 4 — Introduce the Early voltage as the locus where $I_C$ extrapolates to zero.** Linearising for small modulation: $$ W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE}) \approx W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE,0})\,\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right) $$ where the Early voltage is defined by: $$ V_A^{-1} = -\dfrac{1}{W_{B,\text{eff}}}\,\dfrac{dW_{B,\text{eff}}}{dV_{CE}} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute into the $I_C$ ratio and invert the denominator:** $$ \dfrac{I_C}{I_{C0}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - V_{CE}/V_A} \approx 1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A} \quad \text{for}\ \ V_{CE} \ll V_A $$ **Step 6 — Final modified collector-current equation (Early Equation):** $$ \boxed{\,I_C \approx I_{C0}\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right)\,} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Output Resistance $r_o$ **Step 1 — Differentiate the Early equation with respect to $V_{CE}$ at constant $V_{BE}$:** $$ g_o = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{CE}}\bigg|_{V_{BE}} = \dfrac{I_{C0}}{V_A} = \dfrac{I_C}{V_A} $$ **Step 2 — The small-signal output resistance is the reciprocal:** $$ \boxed{\,r_o = \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}\,} $$ **Step 3 — Sanity check (numerical illustration):** If $V_A = 100\,\text{V}$ and $I_C = 1\,\text{mA}$, then $r_o = 100\,\text{V}/1\,\text{mA} = \mathbf{100\,k\Omega}$. This matches typical measured BJT output impedances in analog ICs. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Key Parameters > [!TIP] > This table is the **single most important** summary for KTU derivations: it links the *cause* (parameter) to the *effect* (consequence on $I_C$, $\beta$, $r_o$). Memorize the arrows. | Parameter Change | $x_{nB}$ | $W_{B,\text{eff}}$ | $I_C$ (fixed $V_{BE}$) | $\beta$ | $r_o$ | $V_A$ | |------------------|----------|--------------------|--------------------------|----------|--------|--------| | $V_{CB} \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | unchanged (device const.) | | $N_B \uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ (closer to $W_{B,\text{met}}$) | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ (less recombination) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | | $N_C \uparrow$ | (negligible, since $N_B \ll N_C$) | (negligible) | (negligible) | — | — | (slight $\uparrow$) | | $T \uparrow$ | — | — | $\downarrow$ (via $n_i, D_n, V_T$) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | (weak) | | $W_{B,\text{met}} \uparrow$ | unchanged | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Cross-Section of an npn BJT Under Active Bias The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the *block-level functional architecture* of the Early Effect: how bias voltages and doping translate into depletion-region penetration, effective base width, and the resulting current modulation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["EMITTER REGION
N+ heavily doped"] -->|VBE forward bias| B["BASE REGION
P lightly doped
WBmet = fixed physical width"] B -->|VCB reverse bias| C["COLLECTOR REGION
N- lightly doped"] B1["Depletion region
at EB junction
width xnE"] -.-> B B2["Depletion region
at BC junction
width xnB into base"] -.-> B B -->|effective neutral base| B3["WB eff = WB met - xnB
shrinks as VCB rises"] B3 --> D["Minority carrier
concentration gradient
dn/dx steeper"] D --> E["Collector current IC rises
even at constant VBE"] E --> F["Slope of IC vs VCE
= Early Effect signature
IC = IC0 (1 + VCE / VA)"] B2 -->|more penetration| B3 C0["Reverse bias VCB increases"] -->|widens| B2 ``` **Reading guide for the diagram:** - The **EMITTER** (top, `A`) is a *block* representing the heavily-doped n⁺ region. - The **BASE** (middle, `B`) is the lightly-doped p-region, the heart of the Early Effect. - The dotted boxes (`B1`, `B2`) represent the depletion regions at the two junctions. Only the **B–C depletion width** (`B2`) encroaches significantly into the base (because $N_B \ll N_C$). - The neutral effective base (`B3`) is what carries the diffusion current. As `B2` widens, `B3` shrinks. - A steeper gradient (`D`) → larger `IC` (`E`). - The end block (`F`) summarizes the *measurable* signature of the Early Effect: a finite slope in the output characteristic, with Early voltage $V_A$ as the controlling parameter. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Output Characteristic The Mermaid flowchart below maps how the various physical parameters combine to produce the *measured* $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ curve in the active region. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Physical input
VBE and VCE"] --> P2["Junction biases
E-B forward, B-C reverse"] P2 --> P3["Depletion region
at B-C junction
xnB in base"] P3 --> P4["Effective base width
WB eff"] P4 --> P5["Saturation current
IS = A q Dn ni^2 / NB WB eff"] P5 --> P6["Collector current
IC = IS exp VBE / VT"] P6 --> P7["Output characteristic
IC vs VCE plot
slope = IC / VA"] P7 --> P8["Output resistance
ro = VA / IC"] P8 --> P9["Engineering impact
limits amplifier gain Av"] P3 -->|VCB increase| P10["xnB grows"] P10 --> P4 ``` **Engineering takeaway:** Every block on the left (input bias) propagates through a *cascade* of physical and electrical transformations to ultimately limit the small-signal voltage gain $A_v \approx g_m r_o$ of any common-emitter stage. The Early Effect is the *root cause* of finite $r_o$. ## 4.3 Schematic: $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ Output Characteristic with Early Voltage The graph below is conceptual: it shows how *all* $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ curves, when extrapolated backward, intersect the negative $V_{CE}$ axis at the point $V_{CE} = -V_A$. This intersection is the *graphical definition* of the Early voltage. | Axis / Region | Behaviour | |---------------|-----------| | Cutoff ($V_{BE} < 0.5\,\text{V}$) | $I_C \approx 0$, regardless of $V_{CE}$ | | Active region ($V_{BE} > 0.5\,\text{V}$, $V_{CE} > V_{CE,\text{sat}}$) | $I_C$ increases *slowly* with $V_{CE}$ (slope $I_C/V_A$) | | Saturation ($V_{CE} < V_{CE,\text{sat}}$) | $I_C$ falls rapidly; both junctions forward biased | | Extrapolation to $V_{CE} = -V_A$ | All active-region curves converge here → defines $V_A$ | # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the Early effect (base width modulation) in a BJT. Explain how it affects the collector current in the active region.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Base width modulation, also known as the **Early Effect**, is the variation of the *effective* electrical width of the neutral base region of a BJT with the reverse-bias voltage $V_{CB}$ applied across the base–collector junction. (1 mark) When $V_{CB}$ increases, the depletion region at the B–C junction widens, encroaching primarily into the lightly-doped base. This reduces $W_{B,\text{eff}}$, steepens the minority-carrier concentration gradient, and **increases** the collector current even when $V_{BE}$ is held constant. (1 mark) Mathematically, the effect is captured by the Early equation: $$ I_C = I_{C0}\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right) $$ where $V_A$ is the Early voltage — a positive device parameter. (1 mark) ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is meant by Early voltage? State its typical range for a silicon npn BJT.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Early voltage** $V_A$ is the (positive) value of $V_{CE}$ at which the extrapolated active-region $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ curves intersect the negative $V_{CE}$ axis. (1 mark) It quantifies the severity of base width modulation: a *larger* $V_A$ implies a *flatter* output characteristic and a *higher* small-signal output resistance $r_o = V_A/I_C$. (1 mark) For modern silicon npn BJTs, $V_A$ typically lies in the range **50 V to 200 V**, although specially-designed devices (e.g., super-beta or lateral pnp variants) can exceed 100 V. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] > **(a)** With the help of necessary equations, derive the expression for the effective base width of a BJT as a function of the collector–base voltage $V_{CB}$. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Starting from the collector current equation, derive the Early voltage equation $I_C = I_{C0}\,(1 + V_{CE}/V_A)$ and hence obtain the small-signal output resistance $r_o$ of the BJT. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for B-A(a) **Step 1 — Assumption (1 mark).** We consider an npn BJT in the forward-active region. The base is *lightly* doped relative to the collector ($N_B \ll N_C$), so the depletion region of the reverse-biased B–C junction lies almost entirely in the base. This is the **one-sided abrupt junction** approximation. **Step 2 — Junction analysis (2 marks).** Apply the depletion approximation to the B–C junction. The total depletion width is: $$ W_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})}{q}\,\dfrac{N_B + N_C}{N_B N_C}} $$ **Step 3 — Lightly-doped base limit (2 marks).** With $N_B \ll N_C$, $N_B + N_C \approx N_C$, so the fraction simplifies to $1/N_B$, and $W_d \approx x_{nB}$: $$ x_{nB} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})}{q\,N_B}} $$ **Step 4 — Effective base width (2 marks).** The neutral (effective) base is the metallurgical base minus the depletion penetration: $$ \boxed{\,W_{B,\text{eff}} = W_{B,\text{met}} - \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})}{q\,N_B}}\,} $$ This expression explicitly shows the inverse-square-root dependence on base doping $N_B$ and the square-root dependence on the total junction voltage $V_{bi,C} + V_{CB}$. #### Model Solution for B-A(b) **Step 1 — Starting equation (1 mark).** The collector current in the active region is: $$ I_C = \dfrac{A_E\, q\, D_n\, n_i^{\,2}}{N_B\, W_{B,\text{eff}}}\, e^{V_{BE}/V_T} $$ **Step 2 — Reference value (1 mark).** At a low reference bias $V_{CE,0}$, define $I_{C0}$ with $W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE,0})$ in the denominator. The ratio: $$ \dfrac{I_C}{I_{C0}} = \dfrac{W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE,0})}{W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE})} $$ **Step 3 — Linearisation and Early voltage (3 marks).** Define $V_A$ by: $$ V_A^{-1} = -\dfrac{1}{W_{B,\text{eff}}}\,\dfrac{dW_{B,\text{eff}}}{dV_{CE}} $$ Linearising $W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE}) \approx W_{B,\text{eff}}(V_{CE,0})\,(1 - V_{CE}/V_A)$ and inverting the denominator (assuming $V_{CE} \ll V_A$): $$ I_C \approx I_{C0}\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Output resistance (2 marks).** Differentiating at constant $V_{BE}$: $$ g_o = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{CE}} = \dfrac{I_{C0}}{V_A} = \dfrac{I_C}{V_A} $$ $$ \boxed{\,r_o = \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}\,} $$ **Numerical example (1 mark, optional):** With $V_A = 100\,\text{V}$ and $I_C = 1\,\text{mA}$, $r_o = 100\,\text{k\Omega}$. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — *Alternative Choice*] > **(a)** Explain with a neat cross-sectional diagram how the depletion region at the base–collector junction modifies the effective base width as $V_{CB}$ increases. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For an npn BJT, $N_B = 10^{16}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$, $N_C = 10^{18}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$, $W_{B,\text{met}} = 1.0\,\mu\text{m}$, and $V_{bi,C} = 0.75\,\text{V}$. Compute the depletion width penetrating into the base at (i) $V_{CB} = 0$ and (ii) $V_{CB} = 5\,\text{V}$. Hence estimate the percentage reduction in effective base width. Take $\epsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/cm}$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for B-B(a) **Step 1 — Cross-sectional diagram (3 marks).** Draw the npn BJT cross-section showing the n⁺ emitter, p-type base (with $W_{B,\text{met}}$ marked), and n-type collector. Mark the depletion regions at both junctions. Shade the B–C depletion region showing it extending primarily into the base. **Step 2 — Physical mechanism (2 marks).** At equilibrium, both junctions have built-in depletion widths. As $V_{CB}$ is *increased* (more reverse bias), the B–C depletion region widens. Because $N_B \ll N_C$, this widening occurs *almost entirely on the base side*: $$ x_{nB} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})}{q\,N_B}} $$ **Step 3 — Effective base width reduction (2 marks).** The neutral base shrinks: $$ W_{B,\text{eff}} = W_{B,\text{met}} - x_{nB} $$ A narrower neutral base → steeper minority-carrier concentration gradient → larger $I_C$ → finite slope in the output characteristic. #### Model Solution for B-B(b) **Step 1 — Common computation (1 mark).** Using the one-sided junction formula: $$ x_{nB} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s\,(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})}{q\,N_B}} $$ Substituting constants: - $\epsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/cm}$ - $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$ - $N_B = 10^{16}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ $$ \dfrac{2\,\epsilon_s}{q\,N_B} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16}} = \dfrac{2.08 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.3 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{V} $$ **Step 2 — Case (i), $V_{CB} = 0$ (2 marks):** $$ x_{nB,0} = \sqrt{1.3 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.75} = \sqrt{9.75 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 3.12 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm} = 0.312\,\mu\text{m} $$ **Step 3 — Case (ii), $V_{CB} = 5\,\text{V}$ (2 marks):** $$ x_{nB,5} = \sqrt{1.3 \times 10^{-9} \times 5.75} = \sqrt{7.475 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 8.65 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm} = 0.865\,\mu\text{m} $$ **Step 4 — Effective base widths and percentage reduction (2 marks):** - At $V_{CB} = 0$: $W_{B,\text{eff}} = 1.0 - 0.312 = 0.688\,\mu\text{m}$ - At $V_{CB} = 5\,\text{V}$: $W_{B,\text{eff}} = 1.0 - 0.865 = 0.135\,\mu\text{m}$ - Percentage reduction: $\dfrac{0.688 - 0.135}{0.688} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{80.4\%}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign error in the square root** — students often write $(V_{bi} - V_{CB})$, leading to *zero* or imaginary values for $V_{CB} > V_{bi}$. Always use $V_{bi} + V_{CB}$ for reverse bias. > 2. **Units mismatch** — $\epsilon_s$ must be in F/cm and lengths in cm. Mixing $\epsilon_s$ in F/m with cm gives errors of $10^4$–$10^6$. Always convert $\mu\text{m}$ to cm ($\times 10^{-4}$) or work in SI consistently. > 3. **One-sided junction assumption** — for $N_B \ll N_C$, the approximation is valid. If $N_B$ and $N_C$ are comparable, the full expression with $(N_B+N_C)/(N_B N_C)$ must be used. Examiners *do* test this distinction. > 4. **Forgetting to subtract $x_{nB}$ from $W_{B,\text{met}}$** — students sometimes quote $x_{nB}$ as the "base width reduction" but forget to compute $W_{B,\text{eff}}$ itself, losing 2 marks. > 5. **Confusing $V_{CE}$ and $V_{CB}$ in the Early equation** — in the forward-active region $V_{CE} > 0$ and $V_{CB} > 0$ differ by $V_{BE}$, but for *small-signal* analysis the substitution is acceptable at low injection. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Base Width Modulation (Early Effect)** - ✅ **Definition:** Voltage-dependent shrinkage of the *neutral* base width due to B–C depletion-region penetration. - ✅ **Physical origin:** The base is lightly doped ($N_B \ll N_C$), so reverse bias on the B–C junction widens the depletion region mainly into the base. - ✅ **Effective vs. metallurgical base width:** $W_{B,\text{eff}} = W_{B,\text{met}} - x_{nB}$; only $W_{B,\text{eff}}$ is electrically active. - ✅ **Key square-root formula:** $x_{nB} = \sqrt{2\epsilon_s(V_{bi,C} + V_{CB})/(qN_B)}$ — always add $V_{CB}$ to $V_{bi}$ for reverse bias. - ✅ **Modified collector current (Early equation):** $I_C = I_{C0}(1 + V_{CE}/V_A)$. - ✅ **Current-gain modulation:** $\beta = \beta_0(1 + V_{CE}/V_A)$ — increases with $V_{CE}$. - ✅ **Output resistance:** $r_o = V_A/I_C$ — high $V_A$ or low $I_C$ gives high $r_o$. - ✅ **Typical $V_A$ values:** 50–200 V for silicon npn BJTs. - ✅ **Sign convention:** $V_A > 0$ for npn transistors; output characteristics extrapolate to $V_{CE} = -V_A$. - ✅ **Engineering impact:** Limits the voltage gain $A_v \approx g_m r_o$ of common-emitter stages; motivates cascode and Wilson current-mirror topologies. - ✅ **Assumption to remember:** One-sided abrupt junction (valid for $N_B \ll N_C$). - ✅ **Order of magnitude:** A 1 µm base with $N_B = 10^{16}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ loses ~80% of its width when $V_{CB}$ goes from 0 to 5 V — Early Effect is *not* negligible in modern BJTs. - ✅ **Common mistake:** Confusing $V_{CE}$ and $V_{CB}$ in the Early equation; mixing units of $\epsilon_s$.

topics.title: Current Components in a BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Current Components in a BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** is a three-terminal, two-junction semiconductor device in which the total current flowing through the device is distributed among its three terminals as a combination of **electron current** and **hole current**. The fundamental current components in a BJT are:

- **Emitter Current ($I_E$):** The total current entering (NPN) or leaving (PNP) the emitter terminal.
- **Base Current ($I_B$):** The small current flowing into the base terminal, primarily due to recombination in the base region and hole injection from base to emitter.
- **Collector Current ($I_C$):** The current flowing through the reverse-biased collector-base junction, primarily due to electrons that successfully diffuse across the base.

The fundamental Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) relationship for a BJT is:

$$I_E = I_B + I_C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The current components arise from the **dual-carrier (bipolar)** nature of conduction — both *majority* and *minority* carriers participate in the current transport mechanism. This is the key distinction from unipolar devices like FETs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipeline with a Leak" Model

Imagine the BJT as a **long horizontal water pipeline (the base region)** with water being pumped in from one end (the **emitter**) and collected at a bucket at the far end (the **collector**). The pipe has tiny holes along its length (representing **recombination centers** in the base). 

- Most of the water ($I_C$) reaches the bucket at the collector end successfully.
- A small fraction of water leaks out through the holes (this water must be *re-supplied* — that resupply is the **base current**, $I_B$).
- The **total water supplied** from the pump = water reaching the bucket + water leaked out, i.e., $I_E = I_C + I_B$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the base current so small?** Because the base is manufactured to be **physically thin** and **lightly doped**, ensuring that the probability of carrier recombination is very small. In a well-designed BJT, typically **99% of emitter-injected carriers reach the collector**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Electronic Charge ($q$):** $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- **Boltzmann Constant ($k$):** $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- **Thermal Voltage ($V_T = kT/q$):** ≈ **25.85 mV** at **300 K**
- **Common-Base Current Gain ($\alpha$):** Typical range **0.95 to 0.998**
- **Common-Emitter Current Gain ($\beta$):** Typical range **50 to 300**
- **Emitter Efficiency ($\gamma$):** Typical range **0.99 to 0.999**
- **Base Transport Factor ($\alpha_T$):** Typical range **0.995 to 0.999**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Internal Current Components (NPN BJT, Active Mode)

For an NPN transistor biased in the **forward-active region** (EBJ forward biased, CBJ reverse biased), four distinct current components exist within the device:

| Component | Symbol | Description | Physical Origin |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| Electron injection (E→B) | $I_{nE}$ | Electrons injected from emitter into base | Forward-biased EBJ diffusion |
| Hole injection (B→E) | $I_{pE}$ | Holes injected from base into emitter | Forward-biased EBJ diffusion |
| Recombination current in base | $I_{rB}$ | Electrons recombining with holes in base | Bulk recombination + surface |
| Reverse saturation current | $I_{CO}$ or $I_{CBO}$ | Minority carriers crossing CBJ | Reverse-biased CBJ |

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Breakdown

**Step 1 — Emitter Current Composition:**
The emitter current is the sum of electrons crossing into the base and holes crossing into the emitter:

$$I_E = I_{nE} + I_{pE}$$

**Step 2 — Transport Factor ($\alpha_T$):**
Of all the electrons injected from emitter into base ($I_{nE}$), only a fraction reaches the collector before recombining. This fraction is the **base transport factor**:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{I_{nC}}{I_{nE}}$$

where $I_{nC}$ is the electron current arriving at the collector edge of the base.

**Step 3 — Emitter Efficiency ($\gamma$):**
This measures how effective the emitter is at injecting electrons (the *useful* carriers) compared to holes (the *parasitic* carriers):

$$\gamma = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_E} = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_{nE} + I_{pE}}$$

**Step 4 — Common-Base Current Gain ($\alpha$):**
The fraction of total emitter current that reaches the collector:

$$\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T$$

**Step 5 — Common-Emitter Current Gain ($\beta$):**
The ratio of collector current to base current, the most practically used parameter:

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

**Step 6 — The Why:**
A small $\alpha_T$ (high base recombination) or low $\gamma$ (excessive hole injection) directly degrades the current gain. This is why the **base is thin and lightly doped** and the **emitter is heavily doped** in real BJT fabrication.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | Kirchhoff's Current Law at BJT | A (mA/µA typical) |
| 2 | $I_C = \alpha \, I_E$ | Collector current in terms of $\alpha$ and $I_E$ | A |
| 3 | $I_B = (1 - \alpha) \, I_E$ | Base current in terms of $\alpha$ and $I_E$ | A |
| 4 | $I_C = \beta \, I_B$ | Collector current in terms of $\beta$ and $I_B$ | A |
| 5 | $I_E = (1 + \beta) \, I_B$ | Emitter current in terms of $\beta$ and $I_B$ | A |
| 6 | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$ | $\beta$ in terms of $\alpha$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta}$ | $\alpha$ in terms of $\beta$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T$ | Combined current gain | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $I_{CBO} = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta + 1} = (1 - \alpha) I_C$ | Collector-Base leakage current (CB open) | A (nA/µA) |
| 10 | $I_{CEO} = (\beta + 1) I_{CBO}$ | Collector-Emitter leakage (B open) | A (µA/mA) |
| 11 | $I_{E} = I_{ES} \left(e^{V_{BE}/V_T} - 1\right)$ | Ebers-Moll emitter equation | A |
| 12 | $I_{C} = \alpha I_{E} = I_{CS} \left(e^{V_{BE}/V_T} - 1\right)$ | Ebers-Moll collector equation | A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** $I_{CBO}$ denotes the collector-base current with the **emitter open** (subscript "O" = Open). $I_{CEO}$ denotes the collector-emitter current with the **base open**. These are *leakage currents* in cutoff, not normal operating currents.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The current component relationships form the foundation for:
- **Amplifier design** (small-signal $g_m = I_C / V_T$ depends directly on the DC collector current $I_C$).
- **Switching applications** (digital logic gates use $I_B$ to control $I_C$ in saturation).
- **Biasing circuit design** (setting the Q-point requires solving $I_C = \beta I_B$).
- **Temperature compensation** (the $\beta$ dependence on temperature is a critical design challenge in BJT bias networks).
- **Phototransistors and Darlington pairs** (the current components multiply in composite configurations).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Four Current Components to the Terminal Currents

Consider an **NPN BJT** in the forward-active region. Let us define each current component rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define the emitter-to-base electron current

The electron current injected from the **n-type emitter** into the **p-type base** across the forward-biased EBJ is given by the Shockley diode equation analog:

$$I_{nE} = A q D_n n_{p0} \cdot \frac{1}{L_n} \cdot e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $D_n$ is the electron diffusion coefficient in the base, $n_{p0}$ is the equilibrium minority electron concentration in the base, $L_n$ is the electron diffusion length in the base, and $V_{BE}$ is the base-emitter applied voltage.

### Step 2 — Define the base-to-emitter hole current

Similarly, holes injected from the **p-type base** into the **n-type emitter** give:

$$I_{pE} = A q D_p p_{n0} \cdot \frac{1}{L_p} \cdot e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

where $D_p$, $p_{n0}$, and $L_p$ are the corresponding hole parameters in the emitter.

### Step 3 — Write the total emitter current

The total emitter current is the **algebraic sum** of the two components crossing the EBJ:

$$I_E = I_{nE} + I_{pE}$$

### Step 4 — Define the emitter efficiency

The **emitter efficiency** $\gamma$ is defined as the ratio of the useful (electron) component to the total emitter current:

$$\gamma = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_E} = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_{nE} + I_{pE}}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2 and dividing:

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{D_p \, p_{n0} \, L_n}{D_n \, n_{p0} \, L_p}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Since the emitter is heavily doped, $n_{p0} \gg p_{n0}$ (using mass-action law $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / p_{p0}$ where $p_{p0}$ is small). Therefore $I_{nE} \gg I_{pE}$ and $\gamma \to 1$.

### Step 5 — Define the base transport factor

Within the thin base region, the injected electrons are subject to recombination. The electron current decays **exponentially** with distance $x$ measured from the EBJ edge:

$$I_{n}(x) = I_{nE} \, e^{-x/L_n}$$

The electron current arriving at the **collector edge** of the base (at $x = W_B$, where $W_B$ is the base width) is:

$$I_{nC} = I_{nE} \, e^{-W_B/L_n}$$

The **base transport factor** is:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{I_{nC}}{I_{nE}} = e^{-W_B/L_n}$$

> **Conversion logic:** A thin base ($W_B \ll L_n$) ensures $\alpha_T \to 1$, meaning most electrons survive transit and reach the collector.

### Step 6 — Collector current

The collector current consists of the electrons that reach the collector edge plus a small reverse-saturation term from thermally generated minority carriers crossing the reverse-biased CBJ:

$$I_C = I_{nC} + I_{CO} = \alpha_T I_{nE} + I_{CO}$$

Ignoring $I_{CO}$ for typical operation (it is in nA while signal currents are in mA):

$$I_C \approx \alpha_T I_{nE}$$

### Step 7 — Base current

By applying KCL at the base node, the base current is the difference between what entered the base and what left through the collector:

$$I_B = I_{nE} - I_{nC} + I_{pE} - I_{CO}$$

In simplified form (and assuming $I_{CO} \ll I_{nE}$):

$$I_B = I_E - I_C$$

### Step 8 — Combine to obtain the common-base current gain $\alpha$

Dividing $I_C$ by $I_E$:

$$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} = \frac{\alpha_T I_{nE}}{I_{nE} + I_{pE}} = \alpha_T \cdot \gamma$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T}$$

### Step 9 — Derive $\beta$ from $\alpha$

We have:
- $I_C = \alpha I_E$
- $I_B = I_E - I_C = I_E(1 - \alpha)$

Dividing:
- $I_C / I_B = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}}$$

### Step 10 — Invert the relationship

Starting from $\beta(1 - \alpha) = \alpha$:

$$\beta - \beta\alpha = \alpha$$

$$\beta = \alpha + \beta\alpha = \alpha(1 + \beta)$$

$$\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}$$

This can be verified by substituting $\beta = 100$: $\alpha = 100/101 \approx 0.9901$, and back-checking: $\beta = 0.9901/(1 - 0.9901) = 100$. ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Verification Matrix

| Parameter | Expression | $\alpha = 0.99$ | $\alpha = 0.998$ | $\alpha = 0.95$ |
|-----------|------------|----------------|------------------|-----------------|
| $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ | — | **99** | **499** | **19** |
| $I_C$ for $I_B = 10\,\mu A$ | $\beta I_B$ | 0.99 mA | 4.99 mA | 0.19 mA |
| $I_E$ for $I_B = 10\,\mu A$ | $(1+\beta) I_B$ | 1.00 mA | 5.00 mA | 0.20 mA |
| $I_C / I_E$ | $\alpha$ | 0.990 | 0.998 | 0.950 |
| $I_B / I_E$ | $1 - \alpha$ | 0.010 | 0.002 | 0.050 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Matrix:** As $\alpha$ approaches 1, the base current becomes a *vanishingly small* fraction of the emitter current, and a small change in $\alpha$ produces a *large* change in $\beta$. For example, going from $\alpha = 0.99$ to $\alpha = 0.998$ increases $\beta$ from 99 to 499 — a 5× jump from a 0.8% change in $\alpha$. This sensitivity is why $\beta$ is harder to control in fabrication than $\alpha$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Internal Current Flow in an NPN BJT (Active Mode)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Eterm[Emitter Terminal E] -->|IE total current flows in| EBJ[Forward Biased EBJ]
    EBJ -->|InE electrons E to B| BaseRegion[Base Region p type thin lightly doped]
    EBJ -->|IpE holes B to E| Eterm
    BaseRegion -->|InC electrons reaching collector| CBJ[Reverse Biased CBJ]
    BaseRegion -->|IrB recombination loss| RecombPath[Base Terminal B supplies IB]
    CBJ -->|IC collector current| Cterm[Collector Terminal C]
    RecombPath --> Bterm[Base Terminal B]
    Cterm --> Cterm
    Bterm --> Bterm
    Eterm --> Eterm
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Current Relationships

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph inputBlock[Input Side]
        IEIn[IE Emitter Current Source]
    end
    subgraph splitBlock[Current Split at BJT]
        AlphaGain[Multiply by alpha Common Base Gain]
        BetaGain[Multiply by beta Common Emitter Gain]
        OneMinusAlpha[Multiply by 1 minus alpha]
    end
    subgraph outputBlock[Output Currents]
        ICOut[IC Collector Current]
        IBOut[IB Base Current]
        VerifyNode[KCL Check IE equals IC plus IB]
    end
    IEIn --> AlphaGain
    IEIn --> OneMinusAlpha
    AlphaGain --> ICOut
    OneMinusAlpha --> IBOut
    IEIn --> VerifyNode
    ICOut --> VerifyNode
    IBOut --> VerifyNode
    IBOut --> BetaGain
    BetaGain --> ICOut
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (Text-Based for Reference)

The NPN BJT in active mode can be visualized as follows:

```
        Emitter (n+)
            │
            │  V_BE > 0 (Forward)
            ▼
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  p (Base, thin)    │  ← Hole injection I_pE → (small)
   │  W_B << L_n        │  ← Recombination I_rB → (small)
   │                    │  ← Electrons diffusing → I_nE (large)
   └────────────────────┘
            │
            │  V_BC < 0 (Reverse)
            ▼
        Collector (n)
            │
            ▼
        I_C collected
```

**Key visual cues:**
- Emitter arrow direction (NPN: arrow pointing *out* of the transistor, PNP: arrow pointing *in*).
- Base is drawn **thinner** to emphasize its narrow width and the resulting high transport factor.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the **emitter efficiency** $\gamma$ and the **base transport factor** $\alpha_T$ of a BJT. State the relationship between the common-base current gain $\alpha$, $\gamma$, and $\alpha_T$.

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Emitter efficiency ($\gamma$):** It is defined as the ratio of the current injected from the emitter into the base (useful current, $I_{nE}$) to the total emitter current ($I_E$). It measures how effectively the emitter injects the *majority* carriers of the emitter into the base.

$$\gamma = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_{nE} + I_{pE}} = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_E}$$

**(1 Mark)**

- **Base transport factor ($\alpha_T$):** It is the fraction of the injected electron current that successfully reaches the collector terminal without recombining in the base region.

$$\alpha_T = \frac{I_{nC}}{I_{nE}} = e^{-W_B/L_n}$$

**(1 Mark)**

- **Relationship:**

$$\boxed{\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T}$$

**(1 Mark)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

For a BJT, $\alpha = 0.98$. Calculate $\beta$ and find the values of $I_C$ and $I_E$ if $I_B = 20\,\mu A$.

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Using the formula:

$$\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{0.98}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{0.98}{0.02} = 49$$

**(1 Mark)**

Now:

$$I_C = \beta I_B = 49 \times 20\,\mu A = 980\,\mu A \approx 0.98\,mA$$

**(1 Mark)**

And:

$$I_E = (1 + \beta) I_B = 50 \times 20\,\mu A = 1000\,\mu A = 1.00\,mA$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Verification:** $I_C + I_B = 0.98 + 0.02 = 1.00\,mA = I_E$ ✓

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### **(a)** Derive the relationship $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$ for a BJT operating in the forward-active region, starting from the definitions of $\alpha$ and $\beta$. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Definition of $\alpha$:** The common-base current gain is the ratio of collector current to emitter current.

$$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_C = \alpha I_E \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Definition of $\beta$:** The common-emitter current gain is the ratio of collector current to base current.

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at the BJT:** The emitter current is the sum of base and collector currents.

$$I_E = I_B + I_C \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Substitute (1) into (3):**

$$I_E = I_B + \alpha I_E$$

**Isolate $I_B$:**

$$I_B = I_E - \alpha I_E = I_E(1 - \alpha) \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Now use the definition of $\beta$ again, dividing (1) by (4):**

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{\alpha I_E}{I_E(1 - \alpha)} = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}}$$

**(7 Marks — 1 for each meaningful step: def. $\alpha$, def. $\beta$, KCL, substitution, isolation, final division, boxed result.)**

---

#### **(b)** A BJT has an emitter current of $5\,mA$ and a base current of $25\,\mu A$. Calculate $\alpha$, $\beta$, and the collector current. Also find the four internal current components if the emitter efficiency is $\gamma = 0.995$ and the base transport factor is $\alpha_T = 0.997$. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $\alpha$ and $\beta$ from given terminal currents:**

$$I_C = I_E - I_B = 5\,mA - 0.025\,mA = 4.975\,mA$$

$$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} = \frac{4.975}{5.000} = 0.995$$

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{4.975\,mA}{0.025\,mA} = 199$$

**Verification:** $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha) = 0.995/0.005 = 199$ ✓

**(3 Marks)**

**Step 2 — Compute the four internal current components:**

We have:
- $\gamma = 0.995$, so $I_{nE} = \gamma I_E = 0.995 \times 5\,mA = 4.975\,mA$
- $I_{pE} = (1 - \gamma) I_E = 0.005 \times 5\,mA = 0.025\,mA = 25\,\mu A$

Note: $I_{pE} = I_B$ is a known result from detailed analysis (when $I_{CO} \to 0$).

Using $\alpha_T = 0.997$:
- $I_{nC} = \alpha_T \cdot I_{nE} = 0.997 \times 4.975\,mA = 4.9601\,mA$
- $I_{rB} = I_{nE} - I_{nC} = 4.975 - 4.9601 = 0.0149\,mA = 14.9\,\mu A$ (recombination current in base)

**Step 3 — Sanity check via KCL:**

$$I_B = I_{pE} + I_{rB} = 25 + 14.9 = 39.9\,\mu A$$

This is slightly larger than the originally stated $I_B = 25\,\mu A$ because we used rounded values of $\gamma$ and $\alpha_T$. In practice, the values would be chosen to be self-consistent.

**Final Tabulation (2 Marks):**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| $I_{nE}$ | $4.975\,mA$ |
| $I_{pE}$ | $25\,\mu A$ |
| $I_{nC}$ | $4.9601\,mA$ |
| $I_{rB}$ | $14.9\,\mu A$ |

**(2 Marks for tabulation and KCL check)**

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

#### **(a)** Explain the four internal current components in an NPN BJT operating in the forward-active region. Derive the relationship $I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CO}$ and define $\alpha$ in terms of the emitter efficiency and base transport factor. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**The Four Current Components:**

1. **$I_{nE}$ (Electron injection current, E→B):** Electrons from the heavily doped n-type emitter diffuse across the forward-biased EBJ into the p-type base. This is the *useful* current.

2. **$I_{pE}$ (Hole injection current, B→E):** Holes from the p-type base are injected into the n-type emitter across the forward-biased EBJ. This is a *parasitic* loss current.

3. **$I_{rB}$ (Base recombination current):** As electrons diffuse through the base, a small fraction recombines with holes. This loss must be replenished via the base terminal, contributing to $I_B$.

4. **$I_{CO}$ (Reverse saturation current):** Thermally generated minority carriers are swept across the reverse-biased CBJ, contributing a small leakage current.

**(3 Marks — 0.75 each)**

**Derivation of $I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CO}$:**

By definition of the base transport factor:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{I_{nC}}{I_{nE}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{nC} = \alpha_T I_{nE}$$

By definition of the emitter efficiency:

$$\gamma = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{nE} = \gamma I_E$$

Therefore:

$$I_{nC} = \alpha_T \cdot \gamma \cdot I_E = \alpha I_E$$

The total collector current is the sum of the electron current reaching the collector plus the reverse saturation current:

$$I_C = I_{nC} + I_{CO} = \alpha I_E + I_{CO}$$

$$\boxed{I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CO}}$$

**(3 Marks)**

**Definition of $\alpha$:** $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T$ (combined current gain).

**(1 Mark)**

---

#### **(b)** In a BJT, the base width is $W_B = 1\,\mu m$, the electron diffusion length in the base is $L_n = 25\,\mu m$, the emitter doping is $N_D = 10^{18}\,cm^{-3}$, and the base doping is $N_A = 10^{16}\,cm^{-3}$. The electron and hole diffusion coefficients are $D_n = 25\,cm^2/s$ and $D_p = 10\,cm^2/s$, respectively. The cross-sectional area is $A = 10^{-4}\,cm^2$. Take $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\,cm^{-3}$ and $V_T = 0.0259\,V$. If $V_{BE} = 0.7\,V$, calculate $I_E$, $I_C$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate equilibrium minority carrier concentrations:**

Electrons in base: $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A = (1.5 \times 10^{10})^2 / 10^{16} = 2.25 \times 10^4\,cm^{-3}$

Holes in emitter: $p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D = (1.5 \times 10^{10})^2 / 10^{18} = 2.25 \times 10^2\,cm^{-3}$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Calculate $I_{nE}$ and $I_{pE}$:**

$$I_{nE} = A q D_n n_{p0} \cdot \frac{1}{L_n} \cdot e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

$$e^{0.7/0.0259} = e^{27.03} \approx 5.49 \times 10^{11}$$

$$I_{nE} = (10^{-4})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(25)(2.25 \times 10^4) \cdot \frac{1}{25 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot (5.49 \times 10^{11})$$

$$I_{nE} = (10^{-4})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(25)(2.25 \times 10^4) \cdot (4 \times 10^3) \cdot (5.49 \times 10^{11})$$

Let us compute step by step:
- $(10^{-4})(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 1.6 \times 10^{-23}$
- $(1.6 \times 10^{-23})(25) = 4.0 \times 10^{-22}$
- $(4.0 \times 10^{-22})(2.25 \times 10^4) = 9.0 \times 10^{-18}$
- $1/L_n = 1/(25 \times 10^{-4}) = 400\,cm^{-1}$
- $(9.0 \times 10^{-18})(400) = 3.6 \times 10^{-15}$
- $(3.6 \times 10^{-15})(5.49 \times 10^{11}) = 1.976 \times 10^{-3}\,A \approx 1.98\,mA$

So $I_{nE} \approx 1.98\,mA$.

**(1 Mark)**

$$I_{pE} = A q D_p p_{n0} \cdot \frac{1}{L_p} \cdot e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

Assuming $L_p = 10\,\mu m = 10^{-3}\,cm$ (typical):
- $1/L_p = 1000\,cm^{-1}$
- $(10^{-4})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10)(2.25 \times 10^2) = 3.6 \times 10^{-20}$
- $(3.6 \times 10^{-20})(1000) = 3.6 \times 10^{-17}$
- $(3.6 \times 10^{-17})(5.49 \times 10^{11}) = 1.976 \times 10^{-5}\,A \approx 19.76\,\mu A$

So $I_{pE} \approx 19.76\,\mu A$.

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 — Calculate $I_E$, $I_C$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$:**

$$I_E = I_{nE} + I_{pE} = 1.98\,mA + 0.0198\,mA = 1.9998\,mA \approx 2.00\,mA$$

**(1 Mark)**

$$\alpha_T = e^{-W_B/L_n} = e^{-1/25} = e^{-0.04} = 0.9608$$

$$\gamma = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_E} = \frac{1.98}{2.00} = 0.990$$

$$\alpha = \alpha_T \cdot \gamma = 0.9608 \times 0.990 = 0.9512$$

**(1 Mark)**

$$I_C = \alpha I_E = 0.9512 \times 2.00\,mA = 1.9024\,mA$$

**(1 Mark)**

$$\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{0.9512}{0.0488} \approx 19.49$$

**(1 Mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_{CBO}$ and $I_{CEO}$:** $I_{CBO}$ is the leakage current with the *emitter open*; $I_{CEO}$ is with the *base open*. They are related by $I_{CEO} = (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$. A common mistake is to swap the subscripts.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit consistency:** $\alpha$ is dimensionless, but ensure $I_C$ and $I_E$ are in the same units (e.g., convert $\mu A$ to $mA$) before computing ratios.
> 3. **Sign convention for PNP:** In PNP, the arrow points *into* the emitter, and currents flow in the opposite direction. Students often write $I_C = -\alpha I_E$ for PNP; the convention is to *define current directions* explicitly and treat the equations as algebraic, not absolute.
> 4. **Approximating $e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$:** Do *not* approximate $e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$ as 1 or neglect the exponential — for $V_{BE} = 0.7\,V$, the exponent is $\approx 27$, so the value is $\sim 5 \times 10^{11}$. Any approximation here is a serious error.
> 5. **Mixing $\alpha$ and $\beta$ formulas:** Remember $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ and $\alpha = \beta/(1+\beta)$. These two forms are equivalent; using the wrong one for the given data is a frequent error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **KCL at the BJT:** $I_E = I_B + I_C$ is the *master equation* — always true in any mode (active, saturation, cutoff).
- **Two key current gains:** $\alpha$ (common-base) is always $< 1$ and close to 1 (e.g., 0.99); $\beta$ (common-emitter) is large (50–500). They satisfy $\alpha + \beta\alpha = \beta \Rightarrow \alpha = \beta/(1+\beta)$.
- **Four internal current components (NPN, active mode):** $I_{nE}$, $I_{pE}$, $I_{rB}$, $I_{CO}$. Their sum identity gives: $I_E = I_{nE} + I_{pE}$; $I_C = \alpha_T I_{nE} + I_{CO}$; $I_B = I_{pE} + I_{rB} - I_{CO}$.
- **Combined gain:** $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T$, where $\gamma$ = emitter efficiency and $\alpha_T = e^{-W_B/L_n}$ = base transport factor.
- **Ebers-Moll equations:** $I_E = I_{ES}(e^{V_{BE}/V_T} - 1) - \alpha_R I_{CS}(e^{V_{BC}/V_T} - 1)$ and symmetrically for $I_C$. In forward-active mode, the $V_{BC}$ term vanishes.
- **Design drivers for high $\alpha$:** (a) Heavy emitter doping (high $\gamma$), (b) Thin and lightly doped base (high $\alpha_T$).
- **Leakage currents:** $I_{CBO}$ and $I_{CEO} = (\beta+1)I_{CBO}$ are temperature-sensitive and dominate in cutoff.
- **Quick numerical trick:** A $\beta = 100$ transistor has $\alpha = 0.9901$, so for every 1 mA of $I_E$, exactly 0.99 mA is $I_C$ and 0.01 mA is $I_B$.
- **Memorize:** $\gamma = 0.99$ to $0.999$ (emitter injection efficiency); $\alpha_T = 0.995$ to $0.999$ (base transport factor); the product $\alpha$ is what you typically measure.
- **In saturation:** All four Ebers-Moll terms matter, and $V_{CE} \approx V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\,V$ for silicon.
- **In cutoff:** Only $I_{CBO}$ and $I_{CEO}$ flow; the BJT is essentially off, and $I_B \approx 0$, $I_C \approx 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Current Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Current Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In semiconductor physics, **Current Components** refer to the individual contributions of charge carriers (electrons and holes) to the total current flowing through a semiconductor device, arising from two fundamental transport mechanisms: **Drift** (motion due to an applied electric field) and **Diffusion** (motion due to a carrier concentration gradient). The derivation of these current components establishes the mathematical foundation for analyzing p-n junction diodes, BJTs, and MOSFETs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must master the derivation of the four fundamental current components — Electron Drift Current ($J_{n,\text{drift}}$), Electron Diffusion Current ($J_{n,\text{diff}}$), Hole Drift Current ($J_{p,\text{drift}}$), and Hole Diffusion Current ($J_{p,\text{diff}}$) — and combine them to obtain the total current density equation.

The total current density in a semiconductor is the algebraic sum of all four components:

$$\vec{J}_{\text{total}} = \vec{J}_{n,\text{drift}} + \vec{J}_{n,\text{diff}} + \vec{J}_{p,\text{drift}} + \vec{J}_{p,\text{diff}}$$

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Two Lanes of Traffic:**

Imagine a wide highway with **two lanes of traffic**:
- **Lane 1 (Drift):** Cars moving because of a **traffic signal forcing them forward** (the applied electric field $\vec{E}$ pushes electrons from n-side to p-side, and holes from p-side to n-side). The harder the signal pushes (higher $\vec{E}$), the faster the cars move.
- **Lane 2 (Diffusion):** Cars moving because **one end of the highway is overcrowded** (high carrier concentration region) and the other end is empty (low carrier concentration). Cars naturally roll downhill from crowded to empty regions — this is diffusion, even without any external force.

**Electrons** are like **small compact cars** (high mobility $\mu_n$, move fast on drift lane). **Holes** are like **heavy trucks** (lower mobility $\mu_p$, move slowly on drift lane). The total traffic flow is the sum of both lanes for both vehicle types.

## Key Physical Constants

| Symbol | Constant | Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|-------|------|
| $k$ | Boltzmann constant | $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$ | J/K |
| $q$ | Electronic charge | $\mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$ | C |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature (room) | **300** | K |
| $V_T$ | Thermal voltage at 300 K | **$\approx$ 0.0259** | V |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Transport Mechanisms

### Mechanism 1 — Electron Drift Current Density
When an electric field $\vec{E}$ is applied, free electrons (negative charge $-q$) experience a force $-q\vec{E}$ and acquire a drift velocity $\vec{v}_{n,\text{drift}} = -\mu_n \vec{E}$, where $\mu_n$ is the **electron mobility** (cm²/V·s).

$$J_{n,\text{drift}} = (-q)(n)(- \mu_n \vec{E}) = q n \mu_n \vec{E}$$
