topics.title: Electron Affinity and Work Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electron Affinity and Work Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

In solid-state device physics, two energy parameters govern the behaviour of charge carriers at material interfaces: **Electron Affinity ($\chi$)** and **Work Function ($\phi$)**. Although they are both expressed in electron-volts (eV) and both quantify energies at the vacuum boundary, they describe fundamentally different physical processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Electron Affinity ($\chi$) — Textbook Definition (Pierret / Neamen Standard)**
> The electron affinity of a semiconductor is defined as the energy difference between the vacuum level ($E_{vac}$, the energy of a free electron just outside the surface) and the conduction band edge ($E_C$) at the surface of the semiconductor. It is mathematically expressed as:
> $$\chi = E_{vac} - E_C$$
> It represents the energy gained (released) when an electron is added to a neutral atom in its lowest energy state, or equivalently, the energy required to liberate an electron from the bottom of the conduction band to the vacuum level. Typical values for common semiconductors: **Si $\approx$ 4.05 eV**, **Ge $\approx$ 4.0 eV**, **GaAs $\approx$ 4.07 eV**, **SiO₂ $\approx$ 0.9 eV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Function ($\phi$) — Standard Definition**
> The work function of a material is the minimum thermodynamic energy required to remove an electron from the Fermi level of that material to the vacuum level (an energy state at infinity, just outside the surface).
> $$\phi = E_{vac} - E_F$$
> For metals, this is the energy needed to liberate an electron from the Fermi sea. For semiconductors, since the Fermi level can be positioned anywhere within the band gap by doping, the work function is a *variable quantity* that depends on doping type and concentration. For insulators the same definition applies but $E_F$ is typically deep in the forbidden gap.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase & Skyscraper" Picture

Imagine a multi-storey parking garage (the solid material) and the open sky above it (the vacuum). 

- The **roof of the garage** is the vacuum level ($E_{vac}$).
- The **top occupied floor** of cars is the Fermi level ($E_F$).
- For a **metal**, the topmost cars (electrons) are essentially on the top floor — you only need to give them a small nudge to push them into the sky. That nudge is the **work function**.
- For a **semiconductor**, the situation is more interesting. There is an *empty* floor above the top occupied cars (the conduction band edge, $E_C$), and the top occupied cars are on the valence band edge ($E_V$) or somewhere in between depending on doping.
- The **electron affinity** is the vertical distance from the conduction band floor ($E_C$) to the roof ($E_{vac}$) — a *property of the material's conduction band only*, independent of doping.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used in This Topic**
> - **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (or **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**)
> - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 0.02585$ **V**
> - **Reference vacuum level:** $E_{vac} = 0$ eV (when measured relative to itself as datum)

### 1.4 Why These Two Quantities Matter

Both $\chi$ and $\phi$ are indispensable when analysing **metal–semiconductor junctions (Schottky and ohmic contacts)**, **MOS structures**, and **heterojunctions**. The relative magnitudes of these energies dictate whether a contact is rectifying or non-rectifying, whether band-bending occurs at the interface, and what the built-in potential of the junction will be.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Distinguishing $\chi$ from $\phi$

The following structured breakdown clarifies the conceptual split:

1. **Electron affinity ($\chi$) is an intrinsic material property of the *conduction band only*.**
   - It does not depend on doping.
   - It does not depend on Fermi level position.
   - It measures the energy cost of moving an electron from $E_C$ to $E_{vac}$.
   - It is the analogue, for a semiconductor, of the energy needed to climb from the topmost parking floor ($E_C$) to the roof ($E_{vac}$).

2. **Work function ($\phi$) depends on *both* the material's intrinsic properties *and* its doping.**
   - For metals, it is essentially constant because $E_F$ sits inside a partially filled band.
   - For semiconductors, $E_F$ slides between $E_V$ and $E_C$ depending on n-type or p-type doping — so $\phi$ varies.
   - Work function is the energy cost of moving an electron from wherever $E_F$ is located, up to the vacuum level.

3. **The mathematical bridge** between them is the position of $E_F$ relative to $E_C$:
   $$\phi = \chi + (E_C - E_F) = \chi + q\phi_n$$
   where $\phi_n = (E_C - E_F)/q$ is the bulk potential (in volts).

### 2.2 The Vacuum Level Concept

The **vacuum level** is a reference energy that exists *just outside* the surface of any material, where an electron is no longer bound by the crystal potential but has not yet escaped to infinity. By convention, $E_{vac}$ is set as the zero of energy in most textbook energy band diagrams. The interior vacuum level is shifted with respect to the exterior vacuum level by any surface dipole present.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Applicable Material** | **Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\chi = E_{vac} - E_C$ | Electron affinity | Semiconductor / Insulator | eV |
| $\phi = E_{vac} - E_F$ | Work function | Metal / Semiconductor / Insulator | eV |
| $\phi = \chi + (E_C - E_F)$ | Work function of n-type SC | n-type semiconductor | eV |
| $\phi = \chi + E_g - (E_F - E_V)$ | Work function of p-type SC | p-type semiconductor | eV |
| $q\phi_n = E_C - E_F$ | Bulk potential in volts | n-type semiconductor | eV (or V) |
| $q\phi_p = E_F - E_V$ | Bulk potential in volts | p-type semiconductor | eV (or V) |
| $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi_S$ | Schottky barrier height (n-type) | Metal–SC junction | eV |
| $\phi_{Bp} = E_g - (\phi_M - \chi_S)$ | Schottky barrier height (p-type) | Metal–SC junction | eV |
| $\phi_M > \phi_S$ (n-type) | Rectifying (Schottky) contact | Metal–SC junction | — |
| $\phi_M < \phi_S$ (n-type) | Ohmic contact | Metal–SC junction | — |
| $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S$ | Metal–SC work function difference | Junction interface | eV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever a numerical value of $\chi$ is given without specifying units, KTU convention treats it as **eV**. Always keep track of whether the question expects energy (eV) or potential (V). Since $1 \text{ eV} = q \times 1 \text{ V}$ numerically but is a different physical quantity, $E_C - E_F$ in eV must be divided by $q$ to get the equivalent voltage $\phi_n$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Photocathodes and Solar Cells:** A low work function material is chosen for the cathode so that incident photons can easily eject electrons (photoelectric effect). Conversely, a high work function is preferred for stable anodes.
- **Thermionic Emitters:** Tungsten filaments in vacuum tubes rely on a defined work function (≈ 4.5 eV) to predict emission current via the Richardson–Dushman equation.
- **CMOS Gate Engineering:** In MOS transistors, the gate material's work function relative to the silicon channel determines the threshold voltage $V_T$ — this is why modern nodes use dual-workfunction metal gates (e.g., TiN for NMOS, TiAlC for PMOS).
- **Schottky Diodes:** The barrier height, set by $\phi_M - \chi_S$, determines forward voltage drop and switching speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Work Function of a Doped Semiconductor from First Principles

We wish to derive the closed-form expression for the work function $\phi$ of an n-type semiconductor starting from its definition.

**Step 1 — Start with the formal definition of work function:**

$$\phi \equiv E_{vac} - E_F$$

*Reasoning:* This is the energy required to take an electron from the Fermi level to just outside the surface (vacuum), measured with respect to the same datum ($E_{vac}$).

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $E_C$ to introduce the conduction-band edge:**

$$\phi = (E_{vac} - E_C) + (E_C - E_F)$$

*Reasoning:* The first parenthesis is the electron affinity $\chi$ by definition. The second parenthesis is the energy gap between the conduction band and the Fermi level — for an n-type material, this is positive and equals $q\phi_n$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the definition of electron affinity:**

$$\phi = \chi + (E_C - E_F)$$

**Step 4 — Express the second term in volts:**

$$\phi = \chi + q\phi_n \quad \text{where} \quad \phi_n = \frac{E_C - E_F}{q}$$

*Result:* This is the **standard n-type work function equation**.

**Step 5 — For an n-type semiconductor in non-degenerate equilibrium, $\phi_n$ is given by:**

$$q\phi_n = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{n_0}\right)$$

*Reasoning:* Using $n_0 = N_C \exp[-(E_C - E_F)/kT]$ and solving for $E_C - E_F$.

**Step 6 — Substitute back to obtain a fully explicit form:**

$$\phi = \chi + kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{n_0}\right)$$

*Interpretation:* As donor concentration $n_0$ increases, $\phi$ *decreases* (because $E_F$ moves closer to $E_C$, making it easier — paradoxically — to remove an electron from $E_F$ to $E_{vac}$? No: actually a larger $n_0$ means $E_C - E_F$ shrinks, so the electron has *less* distance to climb — hence $\phi$ reduces).

### 3.2 Derivation: Work Function of a p-type Semiconductor

**Step 1 — Start with the definition:**

$$\phi = E_{vac} - E_F$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $E_V$, then add and subtract $E_C$:**

$$\phi = (E_{vac} - E_C) + (E_C - E_V) - (E_F - E_V)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute identities:**

$$\phi = \chi + E_g - (E_F - E_V) = \chi + E_g - q\phi_p$$

where $\phi_p = (E_F - E_V)/q$ is the bulk potential of the p-type region.

**Step 4 — Substitute $\phi_p$ in non-degenerate form:**

$$\phi = \chi + E_g - kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{p_0}\right)$$

*Interpretation:* For a p-type material, increasing acceptor concentration $p_0$ *raises* the work function because $E_F$ moves closer to $E_V$.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Silicon has electron affinity $\chi = 4.05$ eV and band gap $E_g = 1.12$ eV. It is doped n-type with $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$. Given $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$ at 300 K and the semiconductor is non-degenerate, compute the work function $\phi$ and the difference between the Fermi level and the intrinsic level $E_F - E_i$.

**Step 1 — Approximate $n_0 \approx N_D$:**

$$n_0 = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E_C - E_F$:**

$$E_C - E_F = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{n_0}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{2.8 \times 10^{19}}{10^{16}}\right) \text{ eV}$$

$$= 0.0259 \times \ln(2.8 \times 10^3) = 0.0259 \times 7.937 = 0.2056 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the work function:**

$$\phi = \chi + (E_C - E_F) = 4.05 + 0.2056 = 4.2556 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E_F - E_i$ using $E_i = (E_C + E_V)/2$:**

$$E_F - E_i = (E_C - E_F) - (E_C - E_i) = 0.2056 - \frac{E_g}{2} = 0.2056 - 0.56 = -0.3544 \text{ eV}$$

*Note:* The negative sign indicates $E_F$ lies *below* $E_i$, which is consistent with n-type doping.

### 3.4 Derivation: Schottky Barrier Height

The Schottky barrier height $\phi_{Bn}$ is the energy barrier an electron must overcome to move from the metal into the semiconductor conduction band. Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define:**

$$\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi_S$$

*Reasoning:* The metal Fermi level is at $E_{vac} - \phi_M$. The semiconductor conduction band at the surface is at $E_{vac} - \chi_S$. The difference is the barrier.

**Step 2 — For p-type material, the barrier is on the valence band side:**

$$\phi_{Bp} = E_g - (\phi_M - \chi_S)$$

*Reasoning:* The barrier for holes from metal to valence band equals the gap minus the barrier seen by electrons (due to energy conservation across the gap).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Band Diagram — Metal, n-type SC, and p-type SC Before Contact

The following Mermaid block renders the energy-band alignment for an isolated metal, an n-type semiconductor, and a p-type semiconductor, showing the relative positions of $E_{vac}$, $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_F$, $\chi$, and $\phi$.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph METAL["METAL — Work Function phi_M"]
        direction TB
        Mvac[VACUUM LEVEL E_vac]
        Mfermi[FERMI LEVEL E_F]
        Mwork[phi_M = E_vac minus E_F]
    end

    subgraph NTYPE["N-TYPE SEMICONDUCTOR"]
        direction TB
        Nvac[VACUUM LEVEL E_vac]
        Nc[CONDUCTION BAND EDGE E_C]
        Nfermi[FERMI LEVEL E_F]
        Nv[VALENCE BAND EDGE E_V]
        Nchi[chi = E_vac minus E_C]
        Nphi[phi_n = chi plus E_C minus E_F]
    end

    subgraph PTYPE["P-TYPE SEMICONDUCTOR"]
        direction TB
        Pvac[VACUUM LEVEL E_vac]
        Pc[CONDUCTION BAND EDGE E_C]
        Pfermi[FERMI LEVEL E_F]
        Pv[VALENCE BAND EDGE E_V]
        Pchi[chi = E_vac minus E_C]
        Pphi[phi_p = chi plus E_g minus E_F plus E_V]
    end
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How $\chi$ and $\phi$ Determine Contact Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Metal and Semiconductor] --> B{Compare Work Functions}
    B --> C[phi_M greater than phi_S for n-type]
    B --> D[phi_M less than phi_S for n-type]
    C --> E[Electrons flow from SC to Metal]
    E --> F[Depletion region forms in SC]
    F --> G[Band bending upward at interface]
    G --> H[Result: Rectifying Schottky Contact]
    D --> I[Electrons flow from Metal to SC]
    I --> J[Accumulation region forms in SC]
    J --> K[Band bending downward at interface]
    K --> L[Result: Ohmic Contact]

    M[Barrier Height Formula] --> N[phi_Bn equals phi_M minus chi_S]
    N --> O[Used for forward current calculation]
    O --> P[Richardson Dushman Equation]
    P --> Q[J equals A star T squared exp minus q phi_Bn over kT]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Energy Levels Visualised

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Vacuum Level E_vac equal to 0 eV datum] --> B[Electron Affinity chi]
    B --> C[Conduction Band Edge E_C]
    C --> D[Fermi Level E_F]
    D --> E[Valence Band Edge E_V]
    E --> F[Energy Reference: E_vac minus E_g]

    G[Work Function phi] --> A
    G --> D

    H[Bulk Potential phi_n for n-type] --> C
    H --> D

    I[Bulk Potential phi_p for p-type] --> D
    I --> E

    J[Band Gap E_g] --> C
    J --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for the Diagram:** Trace the arrows from $E_{vac}$ downward through $\chi$ to reach $E_C$. The same $E_{vac}$ reached by an electron starting from $E_F$ defines $\phi$. The vertical gap between $E_C$ and $E_F$ inside the semiconductor is $q\phi_n$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]:** Define electron affinity and work function. How are they related for an n-type semiconductor?

**Model Answer:**

*Electron Affinity ($\chi$):* It is the energy required to move an electron from the conduction band edge ($E_C$) of a semiconductor to the vacuum level ($E_{vac}$) just outside the surface.

$$\chi = E_{vac} - E_C$$

*Work Function ($\phi$):* It is the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the Fermi level ($E_F$) of a material to the vacuum level.

$$\phi = E_{vac} - E_F$$

*Relationship for n-type semiconductor:* Adding and subtracting $E_C$ in the work function equation gives:

$$\phi = (E_{vac} - E_C) + (E_C - E_F) = \chi + (E_C - E_F)$$

Since for n-type material $E_C - E_F = q\phi_n$, we have $\phi = \chi + q\phi_n$. Thus the work function exceeds the electron affinity by an amount equal to the Fermi level depth inside the conduction band.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]:** Explain why the work function of a metal is essentially constant while that of a semiconductor varies with doping.

**Model Answer:**

In a **metal**, the conduction band is partially filled and the Fermi level lies *inside* an allowed energy band. Removing an electron from $E_F$ requires it to climb only a fixed distance to $E_{vac}$. The density of states at $E_F$ is so high that small perturbations (impurities, temperature) cannot shift $E_F$ appreciably. Hence $\phi_M$ is an intrinsic, nearly constant property of the metal (e.g., W ≈ 4.5 eV, Au ≈ 5.1 eV, Al ≈ 4.28 eV).

In a **semiconductor**, the Fermi level lies inside the *forbidden band gap* and its position is extremely sensitive to doping. In n-type material, donors raise $E_F$ toward $E_C$, *reducing* $(E_C - E_F)$ and hence *reducing* $\phi$. In p-type material, acceptors lower $E_F$ toward $E_V$, *increasing* $\phi$. Because $n_0$ or $p_0$ can be tuned across many orders of magnitude via doping, $\phi_S$ can be varied continuously. This tunability is exploited in modern CMOS gate engineering to set threshold voltage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labelled energy band diagram, define and distinguish between **electron affinity** and **work function** for a semiconductor. Explain how the position of the Fermi level affects the work function in n-type and p-type materials. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Silicon has $\chi = 4.05$ eV, $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_V = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$. It is doped with $N_D = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$ (n-type). At $T = 300$ K, calculate:
   (i) the position of $E_F$ relative to $E_i$,
   (ii) the work function $\phi_S$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. *Energy band diagram description:* Draw three horizontal lines for an n-type semiconductor representing, from top to bottom, $E_{vac}$, $E_C$, $E_F$, $E_V$. The vertical distance between $E_{vac}$ and $E_C$ is $\chi$. The vertical distance between $E_{vac}$ and $E_F$ is $\phi$. For an n-type SC, $E_F$ is shown just below $E_C$. For a p-type SC, $E_F$ is shown just above $E_V$.

2. *Definition (electron affinity):* $\chi = E_{vac} - E_C$. It is the energy gained by a neutral atom when an extra electron is added to its conduction band, OR equivalently, the energy required to take an electron from $E_C$ to vacuum.

3. *Definition (work function):* $\phi = E_{vac} - E_F$. It is the minimum energy needed to liberate an electron from the Fermi level to the vacuum.

4. *Effect of Fermi level position:*
   - For **n-type**: $E_F$ lies close to $E_C$, so $(E_C - E_F)$ is small and positive. Therefore $\phi_n = \chi + (E_C - E_F)$ is *just slightly larger* than $\chi$. Heavy n-doping reduces $\phi$ toward $\chi$.
   - For **p-type**: $E_F$ lies close to $E_V$. Therefore $\phi_p = \chi + E_g - (E_F - E_V)$ is *significantly larger* than $\chi$, approaching $\chi + E_g$ in the heavy doping limit.

5. *Conclusion:* $\chi$ is fixed by the conduction band structure; $\phi$ is a function of doping.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Position of $E_F$ relative to $E_i$:**

Recall $E_i$ lies at:

$$E_i - E_C = -kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{n_i}\right)$$

For silicon at 300 K, $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$.

$$E_i - E_C = -0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{2.8 \times 10^{19}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = -0.0259 \times 21.4 = -0.555 \text{ eV}$$

Thus $E_C - E_i = 0.555$ eV.

For the doped material:

$$E_C - E_F = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{2.8 \times 10^{19}}{5 \times 10^{15}}\right)$$

$$= 0.0259 \times \ln(5.6 \times 10^3) = 0.0259 \times 8.630 = 0.2235 \text{ eV}$$

Therefore:

$$E_F - E_i = (E_C - E_i) - (E_C - E_F) = 0.555 - 0.2235 = 0.3315 \text{ eV}$$

The Fermi level lies **0.3315 eV above the intrinsic level**, confirming n-type behaviour.

**(ii) Work function:**

$$\phi_S = \chi + (E_C - E_F) = 4.05 + 0.2235 = 4.2735 \text{ eV}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Schottky barrier height $\phi_{Bn}$ for a metal–n-type semiconductor junction. Explain under what condition on the metal work function $\phi_M$ and semiconductor work function $\phi_S$ the contact becomes (i) rectifying and (ii) ohmic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Aluminium ($\phi_M = 4.28$ eV) is deposited on n-type silicon ($\chi_S = 4.05$ eV, $\phi_S = 4.25$ eV, $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$). Calculate:
   (i) the Schottky barrier height $\phi_{Bn}$,
   (ii) the built-in potential $V_{bi}$,
   (iii) the depletion width $W$ at zero bias, given $\varepsilon_{Si} = 11.7 \varepsilon_0$ and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Derivation of $\phi_{Bn}$:**

Step 1: Before contact, the metal Fermi level $E_F^M$ is at $E_{vac} - \phi_M$ and the semiconductor Fermi level $E_F^S$ is at $E_{vac} - \phi_S$.

Step 2: At thermal equilibrium after contact, Fermi levels must align. The barrier seen by electrons moving from metal to semiconductor is the offset between $E_F^M$ and $E_C$ at the interface:

$$\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi_S$$

Step 3: This is the **Schottky–Mott rule** (ideal, neglecting Fermi-level pinning and interface states).

**Condition for rectifying contact (n-type):**

$$\phi_M > \phi_S \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \phi_M > \chi_S$$

Electrons flow from SC to metal on contact (to align $E_F$), leaving behind a positive space-charge region in the SC, producing band bending and a built-in potential. Result: rectifying (Schottky) contact.

**Condition for ohmic contact (n-type):**

$$\phi_M < \phi_S \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \phi_M < \chi_S$$

Electrons flow from metal to SC, accumulating at the interface. No significant barrier exists for majority-carrier transport. Result: ohmic contact.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Schottky barrier height:**

$$\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi_S = 4.28 - 4.05 = 0.23 \text{ eV}$$

**(ii) Built-in potential:**

The built-in potential is the difference between the barrier height and the bulk potential:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{\phi_{Bn} - (E_C - E_F)}{q} = \frac{0.23 - 0.2056}{1} \approx 0.0244 \text{ V}$$

(Using $E_C - E_F = 0.0259 \times \ln(2.8 \times 10^{19}/10^{16}) = 0.2056$ eV.)

**(iii) Depletion width:**

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_S V_{bi}}{q N_D}}$$

Substituting values:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \times 0.0244}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16}}}$$

Compute numerator: $2 \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \times 0.0244 = 5.054 \times 10^{-14}$ F·V/cm

Denominator: $1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16} = 1.602 \times 10^{-3}$ C/cm³

Ratio: $5.054 \times 10^{-14} / 1.602 \times 10^{-3} = 3.155 \times 10^{-11}$ cm²/V

Wait — units need careful handling. Let us redo:

$$W^2 = \frac{2 \varepsilon_S V_{bi}}{q N_D} = \frac{2 \times (11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}) \times 0.0244}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16}}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 1.0359 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.0244 = 5.055 \times 10^{-14}$

Denominator: $1.602 \times 10^{-3}$

$$W^2 = \frac{5.055 \times 10^{-14}}{1.602 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.155 \times 10^{-11} \text{ cm}^2$$

$$W = \sqrt{3.155 \times 10^{-11}} = 5.617 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm} = 0.0562 \text{ µm} \approx 56.2 \text{ nm}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert eV to V (or vice versa):** When using $V_{bi}$ in the depletion-width formula, the value must be in **volts**, not eV. If you compute $(E_C - E_F) = 0.2056$ eV and use it directly as $V_{bi}$, you will get an incorrect $W$ by a factor of 1 (actually equal numerically for an eV-as-V trick, but conceptually wrong if $q$ appears again elsewhere).
> 2. **Mixing up $\chi$ and $\phi$ in the Schottky formula:** $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi_S$, **not** $\phi_M - \phi_S$. The barrier height depends on the metal work function and the semiconductor *electron affinity*, not the semiconductor work function.
> 3. **Sign error on $E_F - E_i$ for n-type vs p-type:** For n-type, $E_F > E_i$ (positive); for p-type, $E_F < E_i$ (negative). Many students write the wrong sign.
> 4. **Incorrect units of $N_C$, $N_V$:** These are per cubic centimetre, not per cubic metre. Always check whether the question uses CGS or SI and convert accordingly.
> 5. **Forgetting that $\chi$ is fixed by the conduction band, not by doping.** Do not write $\chi$ as a function of $N_D$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electron affinity $\chi$** is the energy gap between $E_{vac}$ and $E_C$; it is an *intrinsic* property of the material's conduction band, independent of doping.
- **Work function $\phi$** is the energy gap between $E_{vac}$ and $E_F$; it depends on *both* intrinsic properties and Fermi level position, hence varies with doping.
- **For n-type SC:** $\phi = \chi + (E_C - E_F) = \chi + q\phi_n$; heavier doping *reduces* $\phi$.
- **For p-type SC:** $\phi = \chi + E_g - (E_F - E_V) = \chi + E_g - q\phi_p$; heavier doping *increases* $\phi$.
- **Schottky barrier height (n-type):** $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi_S$ (Schottky–Mott rule, ideal case).
- **Rectifying contact (n-type):** $\phi_M > \phi_S$ — i.e., $\phi_M > \chi_S + q\phi_n$.
- **Ohmic contact (n-type):** $\phi_M < \phi_S$ — i.e., $\phi_M < \chi_S + q\phi_n$.
- **Typical values:** Si $\chi = 4.05$ eV, Ge $\chi = 4.0$ eV, GaAs $\chi = 4.07$ eV; metals W ≈ 4.5 eV, Au ≈ 5.1 eV, Al ≈ 4.28 eV.
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 0.0259$ V.
- **Energy gap relation for silicon:** $E_g(\text{Si}) \approx 1.12$ eV at 300 K; for germanium $E_g \approx 0.66$ eV; for GaAs $E_g \approx 1.42$ eV.
- **Units discipline:** $\chi$, $\phi$, $E_g$, $E_C - E_F$ are energies in eV; $\phi_n$, $\phi_p$, $V_{bi}$ are potentials in volts. Conversion factor: $1 \text{ eV} = q \times 1 \text{ V}$.
- **Photoelectric context:** Einstein's photoelectric equation $KE_{max} = h\nu - \phi$ makes $\phi$ directly measurable using monochromatic light sources.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ohmic and Rectifying Contacts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ohmic and Rectifying Contacts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Metal–Semiconductor (MS) Contacts – The Foundation

When a metal is brought into intimate physical contact with a semiconductor (without any native oxide layer in between), the resulting junction is called a **Metal–Semiconductor (MS) Contact**. This is one of the most fundamental interfaces in all of solid-state electronics — every transistor, diode, and integrated circuit is wired to the outside world *through* such a contact.

The electrical behaviour of an MS contact falls into one of two broad categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Definition (KTU PCECT302 – Module 3):**
> - **Ohmic Contact:** A metal–semiconductor contact that exhibits *negligible* resistance compared to the bulk semiconductor and shows a *linear* (ohmic) current–voltage (I–V) characteristic in both bias polarities. It does not rectify.
> - **Rectifying Contact (Schottky Contact):** A metal–semiconductor contact that exhibits a *non-linear* I–V characteristic with high current in one bias direction (forward) and very small current in the other (reverse) — analogous to a p–n junction diode. The resulting device is called a **Schottky Barrier Diode (SBD)**.

---

## 1.2 Why Does the Same Physics Give Two Opposite Devices?

The deciding factor is the relative alignment of two key energy levels:

| Symbol | Quantity | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\phi_M$ | **Metal Work Function** | Energy (in eV) required to free an electron from the metal's Fermi level into vacuum. |
| $\phi_S$ | **Semiconductor Work Function** | Energy from the semiconductor's Fermi level $E_F$ to the vacuum level $E_0$. |
| $\chi$ | **Electron Affinity** | Energy from the semiconductor's conduction band edge $E_C$ to the vacuum level. For Si, $\chi \approx 4.05$ eV; for GaAs, $\chi \approx 4.07$ eV. |

The two cases are:

- **n-type semiconductor:** If $\phi_M > \phi_S$ → electrons flow from semiconductor into metal → depletion region forms → **Rectifying (Schottky) contact**.
- **n-type semiconductor:** If $\phi_M < \phi_S$ → electrons accumulate at the interface → no depletion → **Ohmic contact**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
> Imagine two water tanks connected by a pipe with a one-way valve. A **rectifying contact** is exactly that valve — water (current) flows easily in one direction but is blocked in the other. An **ohmic contact** is a wide-open pipe — water flows equally well in both directions with negligible restriction. In a transistor, the source/drain electrodes are *ohmic* (open pipes), while the gate forms a *rectifying* (or capacitive) control.

---

## 1.3 Built-in Potential and Barrier Height

When the metal and semiconductor are first brought together, their Fermi levels must align at thermal equilibrium. This forces a redistribution of charge and creates a **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ and a **Schottky barrier height** $\phi_{Bn}$.

For an **n-type** semiconductor:

$$\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi$$

For a **p-type** semiconductor:

$$\phi_{Bp} = \chi + E_g - \phi_M = E_g - (\phi_M - \chi)$$

The built-in potential (for n-type) is:

$$V_{bi} = \phi_{Bn} - (E_C - E_F) = \phi_M - \phi_S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **barrier height $\phi_{Bn}$ depends only on the metal work function and the semiconductor electron affinity** — *not* on the doping concentration. This is the famous **Schottky–Mott rule** and is a direct KTU 2024 syllabus highlight.

---

## 1.4 The Two Reference Constants You Must Memorise

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Thermal voltage at 300 K | $V_T = kT/q$ | **0.0259 V** (≈ 26 mV) |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** |

These two constants appear in every derivation of contact resistance, depletion width, and current for MS junctions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formation of the Schottky Barrier (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Isolated state (before contact):**
The metal has Fermi level $E_{F,M}$ and vacuum level $E_0$. The semiconductor (n-type) has Fermi level $E_{F,S}$ close to $E_C$. The vacuum levels of both materials are aligned arbitrarily.

**Step 2 — Contact and thermal equilibrium:**
When the metal touches the semiconductor, a single Fermi level $E_F$ must be established throughout. For an n-type SC with $\phi_M > \phi_S$:

- Electrons near the SC surface have *lower* energy inside the metal.
- They "fall" from the SC conduction band into the metal.
- This leaves behind a region of positively charged, ionised donors → **space-charge / depletion region** of width $W$.

**Step 3 — Band bending:**
Inside the depletion region, the energy bands of the semiconductor bend *upward* as we approach the interface. The amount of bending equals the built-in potential $V_{bi}$.

**Step 4 — Barrier formation:**
A potential barrier $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi$ now exists for electrons trying to move from the metal into the semiconductor. The barrier seen by electrons moving from the SC to the metal is $V_{bi}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Schottky diodes switch in **picoseconds** (because they are majority-carrier devices — no minority carrier storage). This makes them indispensable in RF mixers, microwave detectors, solar cell bypass diodes, and power rectifiers (e.g., SiC Schottky diodes in switched-mode power supplies).

---

## 2.2 Current Transport Mechanisms Across an MS Junction

Four mechanisms carry current. KTU 2024 expects you to *name* all four and identify the dominant one:

1. **Thermionic Emission (TE)** — Electrons with energy $>\phi_{Bn}$ cross over the barrier. Dominant in moderately doped SBDs.
2. **Field Emission (FE) / Tunneling** — Electrons quantum-mechanically tunnel through a thin barrier. Dominant in *heavily doped* semiconductors → produces **ohmic contacts**.
3. **Thermionic-Field Emission (TFE)** — Mixed mode in intermediate doping.
4. **Recombination in the depletion region** — Analogous to p–n junction recombination current.

For a moderately doped Schottky diode, the **thermionic emission theory** gives the famous current–voltage equation:

$$I = I_S \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

with the **reverse saturation current**:

$$I_S = A \, A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)$$

where:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the diode (m²)
- $A^*$ = **Richardson constant** (A/m²·K²); for n-Si ≈ **112 A/m²·K²**, for n-GaAs ≈ **8.16 A/m²·K²**

---

## 2.3 The Depletion Region in an MS Junction (n-type)

The depletion width on the semiconductor side is:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}$$

When an external bias $V$ is applied (forward bias reduces the barrier; reverse bias widens it), replace $V_{bi}$ with $(V_{bi} - V)$ for forward and $(V_{bi} + \vert V_R \vert)$ for reverse:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q N_D}}$$

The maximum electric field (at the interface, $x = 0$) is:

$$\vert E_{max} \vert = \frac{q N_D W}{\varepsilon_s} = \sqrt{\frac{2 q N_D (V_{bi} - V)}{\varepsilon_s}}$$

The depletion capacitance (per unit area) is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_s}{W} = \sqrt{\frac{q \varepsilon_s N_D}{2 (V_{bi} - V)}}$$

A plot of $1/C^2$ versus $V$ yields a **straight line** — this is the standard KTU experimental method to extract $N_D$ and $V_{bi}$ (and hence $\phi_{Bn}$).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi$ | Schottky barrier height (n-type) |
| 2 | $\phi_{Bp} = E_g - (\phi_M - \chi)$ | Schottky barrier height (p-type) |
| 3 | $V_{bi} = \phi_{Bn} - V_n$ | Built-in potential, $V_n = (E_C - E_F)/q$ |
| 4 | $W = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V) / (q N_D)}$ | Depletion width |
| 5 | $C/A = \sqrt{q \varepsilon_s N_D / [2(V_{bi}-V)]}$ | Junction capacitance |
| 6 | $I = I_S[\exp(qV/kT) - 1]$ | TE current (Schottky diode) |
| 7 | $I_S = A A^* T^2 \exp(-q\phi_{Bn}/kT)$ | Saturation current |
| 8 | $R_c = \rho_c = \partial V/\partial J \big\vert_{V=0}$ | Specific contact resistivity |
| 9 | $\rho_c = (k/qA^*T) \exp(q\phi_{Bn}/kT)$ | TE-limited contact resistivity |
| 10 | $R_c = \rho_c / A$ | Total contact resistance |
| 11 | $\vert E_{max} \vert = \sqrt{2 q N_D V_{bi} / \varepsilon_s}$ | Peak electric field at $V=0$ |
| 12 | $\phi_M < \phi_S$ (n-type) | Condition for ohmic contact |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world significance:** In modern CMOS technology, **silicide contacts** (TiSi₂, CoSi₂, NiSi) are deliberately chosen so that $\phi_M \approx \phi_S$, giving near-ideal ohmic behaviour with $\rho_c$ as low as **10⁻⁷ Ω·cm²**. The same n-type silicon wafer can give either a rectifier or an ohmic contact purely by **changing the metal** — a classic KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Depletion Width $W$ for a Schottky Contact

We start from Poisson's equation in one dimension, valid inside the depletion region (where the charge density is $\rho = q N_D$ for a uniformly doped n-type semiconductor, completely ionised):

$$\frac{d^2 V(x)}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 1 — First integration.**
Integrate once with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{dV(x)}{dx} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} x + C_1$$

**Step 2 — Boundary condition for electric field.**
The electric field must vanish at the edge of the depletion region, $x = W$ (deep inside the neutral n-region). So $E(W) = -dV/dx = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} W + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = \frac{q N_D W}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 3 — Electric field profile.**

$$E(x) = -\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} (W - x)$$

**Step 4 — Second integration.**
Integrate $E(x) = -dV/dx$ to get potential:

$$V(x) = -\int E(x) \, dx = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} \int (W - x) \, dx = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} \left( W x - \frac{x^2}{2} \right) + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Boundary condition for potential.**
Set $V(W) = 0$ (reference at the neutral bulk):

$$0 = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} \left( W^2 - \frac{W^2}{2} \right) + C_2 = -\frac{q N_D W^2}{2 \varepsilon_s} + C_2$$

$$\Rightarrow C_2 = \frac{q N_D W^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 6 — Potential at the interface ($x = 0$).**
The interface potential is $V(0) = -V_{bi}$ (the band is bent upward by $V_{bi}$ in n-type):

$$-V_{bi} = \frac{q N_D W^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $W$.**

$$\boxed{\,W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}\,}$$

**Step 8 — Include applied bias $V$.**
For forward bias $V > 0$ (positive on metal for n-type), the barrier is reduced. Replace $V_{bi} \to (V_{bi} - V)$:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q N_D}}$$

> This derivation is essentially identical to the one for a one-sided abrupt p⁺n junction, because the metal side acts as an *infinite* reservoir of charge — exactly like a p⁺ region.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Thermionic Emission Current

**Step 1 — Flux of electrons from the semiconductor to the metal.**
Treat electrons in the conduction band as a Maxwell–Boltzmann gas. The flux crossing the barrier from the SC side (per unit area, per unit time) is given by kinetic theory:

$$J_{S \to M} = \frac{1}{2} q n_s \bar{v}_x = A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right) \exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right)$$

The factor $\exp(qV/kT)$ arises because forward bias $V$ lowers the barrier by $qV$.

**Step 2 — Flux of electrons from the metal to the semiconductor.**
The barrier $\phi_{Bn}$ for metal-to-SC electrons is *independent* of applied bias (this is the cornerstone of the Schottky model):

$$J_{M \to S} = -A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Net current density.**

$$J = J_{S \to M} - J_{M \to S} = A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right) \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the saturation current.**

$$\boxed{\,J_S = A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)\,} \quad \text{and} \quad I = I_S \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

This is the **Schottky diode equation** — identical in form to the p–n junction diode equation, but with a fundamentally different $I_S$ (it depends on $\phi_{Bn}$, not on the intrinsic carrier concentration).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Schottky vs p–n Junction Diode

| Property | Schottky (MS) Diode | p–n Junction Diode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carriers involved | Majority only | Both majority and minority |
| Storage time | **Negligible** → fast switching | Significant → slow recovery |
| Turn-on voltage $V_{on}$ | ~ 0.2 – 0.3 V (low) | ~ 0.7 V (Si p–n) |
| Reverse saturation current $I_S$ | **Much larger** (exponential in $\phi_{Bn}$) | Much smaller |
| Reverse breakdown | Limited by Schottky barrier lowering | By avalanche/Zener |
| Process steps | Single metal deposition | Diffusion / implantation |
| Dominant applications | RF, fast switching, clamping | General rectification |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Classification of MS Contacts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal Semiconductor Contact] --> B{Compare phiM and phiS}
    B -- "phiM greater phiS, n-type" --> C[Rectifying Contact]
    B -- "phiM less phiS, n-type" --> D[Ohmic Contact]
    C --> E[Schottky Barrier Diode]
    E --> F[Majority carrier device]
    F --> G[Fast switching RF mixer]
    D --> H[Low resistance interconnect]
    H --> I[Source and drain in MOSFET]
    I --> J[Modern CMOS technology]
    B -- "phiM less phiS, p-type" --> K[Rectifying Contact]
    B -- "phiM greater phiS, p-type" --> L[Ohmic Contact]
```

## 4.2 Energy Band Diagram — Before Contact (Isolated Metal and n-type Semiconductor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1[Metal Region]
        EvacM[Vacuum Level E0]
        EfM[Fermi Level EF]
        Em1[Conduction states filled up to EF]
    end
    subgraph S1[Semiconductor Region n-type]
        EcS[Conduction Band EC]
        EfS[Fermi Level EF near EC]
        EvS[Valence Band EV]
    end
```

## 4.3 Energy Band Diagram — After Contact (Schottky Rectifying, n-type, $\phi_M > \phi_S$)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph METAL[Metal]
        EcM[Conduction Band]
        EfM[Fermi Level EF common to both]
    end
    subgraph SC[Semiconductor n-type with band bending]
        EcSC[EC bends upward]
        EfSC[Fermi aligns with metal]
        EvSC[EV bends upward]
        D1[Depletion region width W]
        D2[Neutral bulk n region]
    end
    METAL --- SC
    EcM -. Barrier phiBn .-> EcSC
```

## 4.4 Energy Band Diagram — Forward Bias (n-type Schottky)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FW[Forward Bias V greater 0]
        B1[Barrier lowered by qV]
        B2[Majority electrons flow SC to M]
        B3[Current increases exponentially]
    end
```

## 4.5 Energy Band Diagram — Ohmic Contact (n-type, $\phi_M < \phi_S$)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OH[Ohmic Contact, n-type]
        O1[Electrons accumulate at interface]
        O2[No depletion region]
        O3[Accumulation layer of high conductivity]
        O4[Linear I-V in both polarities]
    end
```

## 4.6 Process Flow — Realisation of an Ohmic Contact on n-type Si

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start with n-type Si wafer] --> P2[Heavy doping NDD greater 10 to power 19 per cubic cm]
    P2 --> P3[Deposit low work function metal e.g. Ti or Al]
    P3 --> P4[Alloy or sinter at 400 to 450 degree C]
    P4 --> P5[Spike silicide forms low barrier ohmic]
    P5 --> P6[Verify linear I-V on test structure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above deliberately use **block-level functional architecture** (per the protocol safeguard) rather than attempting pixel-perfect band diagrams. In your exam, sketching the *qualitative* band shapes — flat in metal, bent in semiconductor, depletion width marked, barrier height labelled — is sufficient for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between an ohmic contact and a rectifying metal–semiconductor contact. State the conditions on the work functions for an n-type semiconductor that lead to each type.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Ohmic contact:** A metal–semiconductor contact that conducts equally well in both bias polarities with negligible contact resistance and a linear I–V characteristic. *(1 mark)*
2. **Rectifying contact:** A contact that allows large current in forward bias and very small current in reverse bias — behaves like a diode (Schottky diode). *(1 mark)*
3. **Conditions for n-type semiconductor:**
   - Ohmic: $\phi_M < \phi_S$ (metal work function less than semiconductor work function) → electrons accumulate.
   - Rectifying: $\phi_M > \phi_S$ → depletion region forms with Schottky barrier $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi$. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
**Define Schottky barrier height. With the help of relevant expressions, explain how it is determined for metal contacts on n-type and p-type semiconductors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Schottky barrier height** $\phi_B$ is the energy difference between the metal Fermi level and the semiconductor band edge (conduction for n-type, valence for p-type) at the interface, measured in eV. *(1 mark)*

- For **n-type:** $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi$ *(1 mark)*
- For **p-type:** $\phi_{Bp} = E_g - (\phi_M - \chi) = E_g - \phi_{Bn}$ *(1 mark)*

It is independent of doping concentration (Schottky–Mott rule).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Internal Choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full Question

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the formation of a **Schottky barrier** between a metal and an n-type semiconductor when $\phi_M > \phi_S$. Derive the expression for the **depletion width** $W$ in terms of $V_{bi}$, $\varepsilon_s$, $q$, and $N_D$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a metal–n-type silicon Schottky contact, the barrier height is $\phi_{Bn} = 0.67$ eV, the donor concentration is $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, the silicon permittivity is $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, and $V_T = kT/q = 0.0259$ V. Calculate:
   (i) The built-in potential $V_{bi}$ given $E_C - E_F = 0.26$ eV.  
   (ii) The depletion width at zero bias.  
   (iii) The maximum electric field at the interface. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Formation & Derivation (7 marks)**

*Formation (3 marks):*
- Draw the metal and the n-type semiconductor with **separated** energy bands: metal has $E_{F,M}$, SC has $E_{F,S}$ near $E_C$. Vacuum levels aligned. *(1 mark)*
- On contact, a single $E_F$ must be established. Since $\phi_M > \phi_S$, electrons flow from the SC into the metal until equilibrium. *(1 mark)*
- A positively charged depletion region of width $W$ forms in the SC; bands bend **upward** towards the interface. Barrier $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi$. *(1 mark)*

*Derivation of $W$ (4 marks):*

Poisson's equation in the depletion region:

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Integrating with boundary conditions $E(W) = 0$ and $V(W) = 0$:

$$V(x) = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} \left( Wx - \frac{x^2}{2} \right) - \frac{q N_D W^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

Setting $V(0) = -V_{bi}$:

$$-V_{bi} = -\frac{q N_D W^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\,W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}\,}$$

*Generalisation to applied bias: replace $V_{bi}$ with $(V_{bi} - V)$ for forward bias.* *(extra credit point)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$\phi_{Bn} = 0.67$ eV, $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³ $= 10^{22}$ m⁻³, $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $E_C - E_F = 0.26$ eV, $V_T = 0.0259$ V.

**(i) Built-in potential (2 marks):**

$$V_{bi} = \phi_{Bn} - (E_C - E_F)/q = 0.67 - 0.26 = 0.41 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Depletion width at zero bias (3 marks):**

Convert units: $N_D = 10^{22}$ m⁻³, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 0.41}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times 10^{22}}}$$

Numerator:
$$2 \times 1.0359 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.41 = 8.494 \times 10^{-11}$$

Denominator:
$$1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{22} = 1.6 \times 10^{3} = 1600$$

Ratio:
$$\frac{8.494 \times 10^{-11}}{1.6 \times 10^{3}} = 5.309 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^2$$

$$W = \sqrt{5.309 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.304 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 0.23 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) Maximum electric field (2 marks):**

$$\vert E_{max} \vert = \frac{q N_D W}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{22} \times 2.304 \times 10^{-7}}{1.0359 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$= \frac{3.686 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0359 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.56 \times 10^{6} \text{ V/m}$$

$$\boxed{\,\vert E_{max} \vert \approx 3.56 \times 10^{5} \text{ V/cm}\,}$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain the **thermionic emission theory** of current transport in a Schottky barrier diode. Derive the expression for the saturation current $I_S$ in terms of barrier height $\phi_{Bn}$, temperature $T$, and Richardson constant $A^*$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Schottky diode is fabricated on n-type silicon with $\phi_{Bn} = 0.65$ eV and area $A = 10^{-6}$ cm². Given $A^* = 112$ A/m²·K² and $T = 300$ K, compute:
   (i) The reverse saturation current $I_S$.  
   (ii) The forward current at $V = 0.3$ V.  
   (iii) The **ideality factor** discussion — why is it close to 1 for SBDs and closer to 2 for p–n junctions at low current? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Thermionic Emission (7 marks)**

The TE theory assumes:
- The barrier height $\phi_{Bn} \gg kT$ (true for typical SBDs).
- Electrons crossing the barrier from SC to M obey a Maxwell–Boltzmann energy distribution.
- The current is limited by emission *over* the barrier, not by drift/diffusion in the depletion region.
- The metal–SC interface is at $x = 0$; the depletion region extends into the SC.

The electron flux from SC to metal (per unit area per second) is:

$$J_{S \to M} = \int_{E_F + q\phi_{Bn}}^{\infty} q v_x \, dn$$

Evaluating this for an MB distribution gives:

$$J_{S \to M} = A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right) \exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right)$$

The flux from metal to SC is *bias-independent* because $\phi_{Bn}$ is fixed by $\phi_M - \chi$:

$$J_{M \to S} = -A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)$$

Net current density:

$$J = A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right) \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

$$\boxed{\,I = I_S \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]\,}, \quad I_S = A \, A^* T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right)$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical + Conceptual (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\phi_{Bn} = 0.65$ eV, $A = 10^{-6}$ cm² $= 10^{-10}$ m², $A^* = 112$ A/m²·K², $T = 300$ K, $V_T = 0.0259$ V.

**(i) Saturation current (2 marks):**

Exponential factor:
$$\exp\left(-\frac{q \phi_{Bn}}{kT}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{0.65}{0.0259}\right) = \exp(-25.10) = 1.276 \times 10^{-11}$$

Prefactor:
$$A A^* T^2 = 10^{-10} \times 112 \times (300)^2 = 10^{-10} \times 112 \times 9 \times 10^{4} = 10^{-10} \times 1.008 \times 10^{7} = 1.008 \times 10^{-3}$$

Therefore:
$$I_S = 1.008 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.276 \times 10^{-11} = 1.286 \times 10^{-14} \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Forward current at $V = 0.3$ V (2 marks):**

$$\exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) = \exp\left(\frac{0.3}{0.0259}\right) = \exp(11.58) \approx 1.07 \times 10^{5}$$

Since this is $\gg 1$:

$$I \approx I_S \times 1.07 \times 10^{5} = 1.286 \times 10^{-14} \times 1.07 \times 10^{5} = 1.376 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A} \approx 1.38 \text{ nA}$$

**(iii) Ideality factor (3 marks):**

The general diode equation includes an ideality factor $n$:

$$I = I_S \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

- For an **SBD**, current flows almost entirely by thermionic emission of majority carriers over the barrier. There is **no minority-carrier recombination** in the depletion region. Hence $n \approx 1.02 - 1.10$.
- For a **p–n junction** at low forward bias, current is dominated by **recombination in the depletion region** (Shockley–Read–Hall), which has a different voltage dependence, giving $n \approx 2$. At high bias, diffusion current dominates and $n \to 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** $N_D$ in **cm⁻³ must be converted to m⁻³** (multiply by $10^6$). Many students forget and get $W$ off by a factor of 1000.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{bi}$ and $\phi_{Bn}$:** $V_{bi} = \phi_{Bn} - V_n$, where $V_n = (E_C - E_F)/q$. They are *not* equal.
> 3. **Exponential argument:** In $I_S$, the exponent is $-q\phi_{Bn}/kT = -\phi_{Bn}/V_T$ (in V), **not** $-\phi_{Bn}/(kT)$ in raw J/K.
> 4. **Ohmic vs rectifying condition:** Students often say "ohmic if heavily doped" — incomplete. The *real* condition involves BOTH doping *and* barrier height, captured in $\rho_c \propto \exp(q\phi_{Bn}/kT)$.
> 5. **Band diagram labelling:** Always label $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_F$, $\phi_{Bn}$, $V_{bi}$, and $W$. A diagram without labels gets only partial credit.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Ohmic contact** = low-resistance, linear, non-rectifying; needed at every terminal of a transistor.
- [x] **Rectifying contact** = Schottky barrier; forms when $\phi_M > \phi_S$ (n-type) or $\phi_M < \phi_S$ (p-type).
- [x] **Schottky barrier height** for n-type: $\phi_{Bn} = \phi_M - \chi$ — *independent of doping* (Schottky–Mott rule).
- [x] **Built-in potential** $V_{bi} = \phi_{Bn} - (E_C - E_F)/q$.
- [x] **Depletion width:** $W = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)/(qN_D)}$.
- [x] **Maximum field:** $E_{max} = \sqrt{2qN_D(V_{bi} - V)/\varepsilon_s}$ at the metal–SC interface.
- [x] **Capacitance–voltage:** $1/C^2$ vs $V$ is a straight line → extract $N_D$ and $V_{bi}$.
- [x] **Thermionic emission current:** $I = I_S[\exp(qV/kT) - 1]$, with $I_S = AA^*T^2\exp(-q\phi_{Bn}/kT)$.
- [x] **Richardson constants:** n-Si ≈ 112; n-GaAs ≈ 8.16 A/m²·K².
- [x] **Current transport mechanisms:** TE, FE (tunneling), TFE, and recombination — *name all four*.
- [x] **Schottky diode** is a **majority-carrier** device → no minority-carrier storage → **picosecond switching**.
- [x] **Ohmic contact** is realised by **heavy doping** (so $W$ is thin → tunneling) and/or by **low-barrier metal** (low $\phi_{Bn}$).
- [x] **Modern silicide ohmics:** TiSi₂, CoSi₂, NiSi — give $\rho_c \sim 10^{-7}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$.
- [x] **Schottky vs p–n:** SBD has lower $V_{on}$ (~0.3 V), higher $I_S$, no reverse recovery loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the band diagram", always show **three panels** — before contact, after contact (equilibrium), and under forward bias. Mention $\phi_M$, $\chi$, $\phi_{Bn}$, $V_{bi}$, $W$ on the diagram. This is worth 2–3 marks by itself in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current-Voltage (I-V) Characteristics** of a MOSFET define the functional relationship between the drain current ($I_D$) flowing through the channel and the terminal voltages ($V_{GS}$, $V_{DS}$) applied between the gate-source and drain-source terminals. The I-V curve mathematically captures how the device transitions through three operational regimes: **Cut-off**, **Triode (Linear)**, and **Saturation (Active)**.

The I-V characteristics form the foundational transfer function that governs the behavior of every Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET) used in modern CMOS digital logic, analog amplifiers, RF mixers, and power management circuits.

## 1.2 Device Topology Recap (NMOS Reference Structure)

For a standard **n-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET (NMOS)** built on a p-type substrate:

- **Source (S):** Heavily doped n+ region, typically grounded.
- **Drain (D):** Heavily doped n+ region, where electrons exit the channel.
- **Gate (G):** Polysilicon or metal electrode, separated from the channel by a thin **SiO₂** gate oxide (thickness $t_{ox}$).
- **Body/Substrate (B):** p-type silicon wafer, typically tied to ground (or to the most negative supply for PMOS).
- **Channel:** Inversion layer of electrons formed just beneath the gate oxide when $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Oxide permittivity of SiO₂: $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times \varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{3.45 \times 10^{-11} \ F/m}$
> - Free-space permittivity: $\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ F/m}$
> - Semiconductor permittivity (Silicon): $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-10} \ F/m}$
> - Elementary charge: $q = \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ C}$
> - Electron mobility (typical): $\mu_n = \mathbf{500 \ cm^2/(V \cdot s)}$
> - Hole mobility (typical): $\mu_p = \mathbf{200 \ cm^2/(V \cdot s)}$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The "Smart Water Hose"

Imagine a **flexible water hose** (the channel) lying on a flat garden bed, with water flowing from the source (tap) to the drain (sprinkler). Now, place a **squeezing clamp** (the gate) on top of the hose.

| Action | Real-World Effect | MOSFET Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Squeeze lightly** (clamp barely touching) | Water flow is zero — clamp doesn't deform the hose. | **Cut-off Region**: $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$, no inversion layer, $I_D = 0$. |
| **Squeeze moderately** (clamp presses the hose flat) | Water flows, and the harder you push water pressure (drain voltage), the more flow. | **Triode/Linear Region**: Channel exists; $I_D$ increases linearly with $V_{DS}$. |
| **Squeeze very hard** (clamp pinches the hose shut at the drain end) | No matter how much pressure you apply at the tap, the flow is **capped** because the hose is physically choked. | **Saturation Region**: Channel pinched off at the drain; $I_D$ becomes independent of $V_{DS}$ (idealized). |

This physical intuition explains why $I_D$ is fundamentally controlled by **two independent knobs**: the **gate "squeeze"** ($V_{GS}$) and the **drain "pressure"** ($V_{DS}$).

## 1.4 The Three Operational Regimes — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Decision Rule for MOSFET Region Identification:**
> 1. If $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ → **CUT-OFF** ($I_D = 0$).
> 2. If $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ AND $V_{DS} < (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ → **TRIODE / LINEAR**.
> 3. If $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ AND $V_{DS} \geq (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ → **SATURATION**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Threshold Voltage ($V_{TH}$) — The Gateway Parameter

The threshold voltage is the **minimum gate-source voltage** required to create a conducting inversion layer (channel) at the Si-SiO₂ interface.

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot q \cdot \varepsilon_{si} \cdot N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$$

Where:
- $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$ is the flat-band voltage.
- $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ is the Fermi potential.
- $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ is the oxide capacitance per unit area (**F/m²**).
- $N_A$ is the p-type substrate doping concentration.
- $Q_{ox}$ is the fixed oxide charge per unit area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $V_{TH}$ matters in I-V characteristics:** It serves as the **activation threshold** in every I-V equation. All drain current expressions contain the term $(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ raised to a power or used as a multiplier. Without strong inversion, there is **no conducting channel**, and hence **no drain current** — regardless of how large $V_{DS}$ becomes.

## 2.2 The Three Operational Regimes — Detailed Physics

### Regime 1: Cut-off (Subthreshold) — $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$

- No conducting channel exists beneath the gate oxide.
- The semiconductor surface is in **depletion** or **weak inversion**.
- **Drain current is theoretically zero** (in ideal long-channel models); in reality, a small subthreshold leakage current flows:
$$I_D \approx I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V_{GS} - V_{TH})}{n k T}\right)$$
- **Utility:** This is the "OFF" state in digital CMOS logic. The transistor behaves as an **open switch**.

### Regime 2: Triode / Linear / Ohmic — $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} < (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$

- A continuous inversion layer spans from source to drain.
- The channel behaves as a **voltage-controlled resistor**.
- The drain current increases approximately linearly with small $V_{DS}$.
- **Utility:** MOSFET acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor** or analog switch (transmission gate in CMOS).

### Regime 3: Saturation / Active — $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} \geq (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$

- The channel **pinches off** at the drain end when the local gate-to-channel voltage drops to $V_{TH}$.
- Beyond pinch-off, additional $V_{DS}$ drops across the **depletion region** near the drain, not across the channel.
- The drain current becomes (ideally) **independent of $V_{DS}$** — it saturates.
- **Utility:** MOSFET acts as a **constant-current source**; this is the standard operating region for analog amplifiers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below assume a **long-channel n-channel enhancement MOSFET** in the standard reference configuration.

| # | Parameter / Equation | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxide Capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | F/m² |
| 2 | Process Transconductance (k') | $k'_n = \mu_n \, C_{ox}$ | A/V² |
| 3 | Conduction Parameter | $\beta_n = k'_n \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$ | A/V² |
| 4 | **Cut-off** Current | $I_D = 0$ | A (ideal) |
| 5 | **Triode** Drain Current | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | A |
| 6 | **Triode** On-Resistance | $R_{on} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})}$ | Ω (for $V_{DS} \to 0$) |
| 7 | **Saturation** Drain Current (Ideal) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | A |
| 8 | **Saturation** Current (with CLM) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2 (1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | A |
| 9 | Overdrive Voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | V |
| 10 | Transconductance $g_m$ (Sat.) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH}) = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$ | A/V or S |
| 11 | Output Resistance $r_o$ | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Ω |
| 12 | Pinch-off Voltage Condition | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_{OV}$ | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the symbols $k'_n$ (process transconductance, A/V²) and $\beta_n$ (conduction parameter, A/V²). Remember: $\beta_n = k'_n \cdot (W/L)$. Many textbook problems give you $k'_n$ and ask you to compute $I_D$ — failing to multiply by $W/L$ is a guaranteed mark loss.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Operating Regime | Why I-V Characteristics Matter |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital CMOS Inverter** | Cut-off ↔ Saturation switching | Determines **propagation delay**, **noise margin**, and **dynamic power** ($P = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$). |
| **Operational Amplifier Input Stage** | Saturation | $g_m$ from I-V characteristics sets the **open-loop gain**. |
| **RF Power Amplifier** | Saturation (near edge) | $I_D$-$V_{DS}$ curves define the **load line** and **PAE** (power-added efficiency). |
| **Analog Switch / Mux** | Triode | Low $R_{on}$ (from triode I-V) ensures minimal signal distortion. |
| **Current Mirror** | Saturation | Matched $I_D$ in two MOSFETs relies on identical $(V_{GS}-V_{TH})$ and $W/L$. |
| **Voltage-Controlled Resistor (VCR)** | Triode (deep) | Triode $I_D$-$V_{DS}$ is linear, slope set by $V_{GS}$. Used in automatic gain control. |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Triode-Region Drain Current

### Starting Point: Channel Charge Density

In the triode region, a continuous n-type inversion layer exists between source and drain. The **inversion charge per unit area** $Q_I(y)$ at any point $y$ along the channel (measured from the source) is governed by the local gate-to-channel voltage $V_{GC}(y) = V_{GS} - V(y)$, where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential:

$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\!\left[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TH}\right]$$

The negative sign indicates electron charge (n-channel). At the source end ($y=0$), $V(0) = 0$, giving $Q_I(0) = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$. At the drain end ($y=L$), $V(L) = V_{DS}$, giving $Q_I(L) = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{DS} - V_{TH})$.

### Drift Current Element

The differential drain current $dI_D$ flowing through a slice of channel of length $dy$ is the channel charge per unit length times the electron drift velocity $v_d = -\mu_n \, E(y)$:

$$dI_D = -W \cdot Q_I(y) \cdot v_d(y) \, dy$$

The electric field along the channel is $E(y) = -\dfrac{dV(y)}{dy}$. Substituting:

$$dI_D = W \cdot C_{ox}\!\left[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TH}\right] \cdot \mu_n \frac{dV(y)}{dy} \, dy$$

### Integrating Across the Channel

Since $I_D$ is constant along the channel (KCL, ignoring generation/recombination in the short channel), we can write:

$$I_D \, dy = \mu_n C_{ox} W \!\left[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TH}\right] dV(y)$$

Integrating from source ($y=0$, $V=0$) to drain ($y=L$, $V=V_{DS}$):

$$\int_0^L I_D \, dy = \int_0^{V_{DS}} \mu_n C_{ox} W \!\left[V_{GS} - V - V_{TH}\right] dV$$

$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V - \frac{V^2}{2}\right]_0^{V_{DS}}$$

$$\boxed{I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]}$$

This is the **complete triode-region I-V equation**. Valid for $0 \leq V_{DS} \leq (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Saturation-Region Drain Current (Ideal)

Saturation begins at the boundary condition $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. Substituting $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2}{2}\right]$$

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2}{2}\right]$$

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In saturation, $I_D$ depends **quadratically** on the overdrive voltage $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ and is **independent** of $V_{DS}$ (ideal long-channel model). This quadratic dependence is the foundation of analog amplifier design.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Transconductance $g_m$

The transconductance is defined as:

$$g_m \equiv \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS} = \text{const}}$$

In saturation, $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$. Differentiating with respect to $V_{GS}$:

$$g_m = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$$

$$\boxed{g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** $g_m$ is the **amplification strength** of the MOSFET. Designers increase $g_m$ by enlarging $W/L$ or by biasing the device at a higher overdrive voltage. Doubling $W$ doubles $g_m$ and hence the small-signal voltage gain of a common-source amplifier.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor has $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 10$, and $V_{TH} = 0.5\ \text{V}$. The device is biased at $V_{GS} = 1.5\ \text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the regime:** We need to know $V_{DS}$ to classify the region. Assume $V_{DS} = 2\ \text{V}$.

Compute overdrive voltage: $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0\ \text{V}$.

Check: $V_{DS} = 2\ \text{V} > V_{OV} = 1.0\ \text{V}$ → **Saturation region**.

**Step 2 — Compute drain current in saturation:**

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot (1.0\ \text{V})^2$$

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1000\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 1\ \text{V}^2 = 500\ \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute transconductance:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV} = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot 1.0\ \text{V} = 1000\ \mu\text{A/V} = 1\ \text{mA/V}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using the alternative $g_m$ formula:**

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \cdot 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot 500\ \mu\text{A}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1000 \cdot 500 \times 10^{-12}}\ \text{A/V} = \sqrt{10^{-6}}\ \text{A/V} = 1\ \text{mA/V} \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $I_D = 500\ \mu\text{A}$, $g_m = 1\ \text{mA/V}$.

## 3.5 Channel Length Modulation (CLM) — Real Device Correction

In **real short-channel MOSFETs**, the effective channel length shrinks as $V_{DS}$ increases beyond $V_{DS,sat}$, because the drain-side depletion region encroaches into the channel. The saturation current therefore increases linearly with $V_{DS}$:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

Where $\lambda$ (units: V⁻¹) is the **channel-length modulation parameter**, typically $0.01 \leq \lambda \leq 0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$ for sub-micron devices. The output resistance is:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Trap:** When a problem says "ideal MOSFET" or "long-channel device," **ignore CLM** and set $\lambda = 0$. If it says "real" or "short-channel" or gives a value of $\lambda$, you **must** include the $(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ factor. Missing this in the answer is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the three operating regions of an enhancement-type n-channel MOSFET. State the conditions for the drain current $I_D$ in each region.**

**Model Answer:**

The three operating regions of an n-channel enhancement MOSFET are:

1. **Cut-off Region:** $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$. The drain current is zero (ideal), as no inversion layer is formed.
   $$I_D = 0$$

2. **Triode (Linear) Region:** $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} < (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$. A continuous channel exists and the device acts as a voltage-controlled resistor.
   $$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

3. **Saturation (Active) Region:** $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} \geq (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$. The channel is pinched off at the drain end and $I_D$ becomes independent of $V_{DS}$.
   $$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$$

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the transconductance ($g_m$) of a MOSFET in saturation? Derive its expression.**

**Model Answer:**

The transconductance $g_m$ represents the **efficiency of conversion** of the input gate-source voltage variation into output drain current variation. It is the fundamental gain parameter of the MOSFET in analog amplifier design and is defined as:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}=\text{const}}$$

For an n-channel MOSFET operating in saturation, $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$. Differentiating:

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})$$

This can also be expressed in terms of $I_D$ as:

$$g_m = \sqrt{2\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$$

A higher $g_m$ yields a **larger small-signal voltage gain** in common-source amplifier configurations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Choice A: Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the complete expression for the drain current $I_D$ of an n-channel enhancement MOSFET operating in the triode region, starting from the inversion charge per unit area. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NMOS transistor has the following parameters: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, $L = 1\ \mu\text{m}$, and $V_{TH} = 0.7\ \text{V}$. The transistor is biased with $V_{GS} = 2\ \text{V}$ and $V_{DS} = 1.5\ \text{V}$. Find: (i) the operating region, (ii) the drain current $I_D$, and (iii) the transconductance $g_m$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Long-channel device (no short-channel effects).
2. Gradual channel approximation (GCA) holds: electric field is vertical near the surface and longitudinal along the channel.
3. Drift current dominates; diffusion current is neglected.
4. Threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ is constant along the channel.
5. No channel length modulation ($\lambda = 0$).
6. The inversion charge per unit area at any point $y$ along the channel is:
$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TH}\right]$$

**Derivation:** The drift current at any point $y$ is:

$$I_D = -W \cdot Q_I(y) \cdot v_d(y) = W \cdot C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TH}\right] \cdot \mu_n \frac{dV}{dy}$$

Since $I_D$ is constant along the channel:

$$I_D \, dy = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TH}\right] dV$$

Integrating from $y=0$ (where $V=0$) to $y=L$ (where $V=V_{DS}$):

$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]}$$

This is the triode-region drain current equation, valid for $V_{DS} \leq (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$
- $W/L = 10\ \mu\text{m}/1\ \mu\text{m} = 10$
- $V_{TH} = 0.7\ \text{V}$
- $V_{GS} = 2\ \text{V}$, $V_{DS} = 1.5\ \text{V}$

**(i) Operating Region:**
First, check: $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 2 - 0.7 = 1.3\ \text{V}$.
Since $V_{DS} = 1.5\ \text{V} > V_{OV} = 1.3\ \text{V}$, the device is in **saturation region**.

**(ii) Drain Current:**
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot (1.3\ \text{V})^2$$
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 500\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 1.69\ \text{V}^2$$
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 845\ \mu\text{A} = 422.5\ \mu\text{A}$$

**(iii) Transconductance:**
$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV} = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot 1.3\ \text{V}$$
$$g_m = 650\ \mu\text{A/V} = 0.65\ \text{mA/V}$$

**Verification:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 \cdot 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot 422.5\ \mu\text{A}} = \sqrt{422500 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 650\ \mu\text{A/V}$ ✓

---

### Choice B: Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the I-V characteristics of an n-channel enhancement MOSFET. Mark the triode, saturation, and cut-off regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NMOS device is operating in saturation with $I_D = 0.5\ \text{mA}$. The device parameters are: $k'_n = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 25$, and $V_{TH} = 0.4\ \text{V}$. Determine: (i) the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$, (ii) the required gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$, and (iii) the transconductance $g_m$ in mA/V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Sketch (Block Representation):**

The output I-V characteristic is a family of curves with $V_{DS}$ on the x-axis and $I_D$ on the y-axis. Each curve corresponds to a different fixed value of $V_{GS}$.

| Region | Condition | Shape of Curve |
|---|---|---|
| Cut-off | $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ | Horizontal line on the x-axis ($I_D = 0$) |
| Triode | $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Initially linear, rising from origin, then curving |
| Saturation | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Flat (horizontal) region; $I_D$ nearly constant |

At the **knee** of each curve (where $V_{DS} = V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$), the device transitions from triode to saturation. Higher $V_{GS}$ curves lie above lower $V_{GS}$ curves, with the saturation current $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}k'_n (W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ increasing quadratically with overdrive voltage.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $I_D = 0.5\ \text{mA} = 500\ \mu\text{A}$
- $k'_n = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$
- $W/L = 25$
- $V_{TH} = 0.4\ \text{V}$

**(i) Overdrive Voltage $V_{OV}$:**
Using the saturation-region equation:
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV}^2$$
$$500\ \mu\text{A} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 25 \cdot V_{OV}^2$$
$$500 = 1250 \cdot V_{OV}^2$$
$$V_{OV}^2 = 0.4\ \text{V}^2 \implies V_{OV} = 0.632\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Gate-Source Voltage $V_{GS}$:**
$$V_{GS} = V_{OV} + V_{TH} = 0.632 + 0.4 = 1.032\ \text{V}$$

**(iii) Transconductance $g_m$:**
$$g_m = k'_n \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV} = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.632\ \text{V}$$
$$g_m = 1580\ \mu\text{A/V} = 1.58\ \text{mA/V}$$

**Cross-check:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 k'_n (W/L) I_D} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 25 \cdot 500\ \mu\text{A}}$
$= \sqrt{2,500,000 \times 10^{-12}} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.58 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A/V} = 1.58\ \text{mA/V}$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in I-V Characteristic Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $W/L$ factor:** Many students plug in only $\mu_n C_{ox}$ and forget to multiply by $W/L$ when computing $I_D$. This loses **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Using triode formula when saturation applies:** Always check $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$ FIRST. Using the full quadratic triode formula in saturation is a **conceptual error** that loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring channel length modulation:** If the problem says "ideal" or "long-channel," set $\lambda = 0$. If the problem gives a value of $\lambda$ or says "real device," include the $(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ multiplier. Forgetting CLM when required loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** $\mu_n C_{ox}$ may be given in $\mu\text{A/V}^2$ or $\text{mA/V}^2$ or $\text{A/V}^2$. Convert to a consistent unit system (typically SI: A/V²) before plugging in.
> 5. **Sign errors for PMOS:** For a p-channel MOSFET, $V_{GS} < 0$, $V_{TH} < 0$, and the current flows from source to drain (opposite direction). Many students blindly apply the NMOS equation to PMOS without sign adjustment — guaranteed 1-2 mark loss.
> 6. **Skipping region identification:** Always state the region explicitly. The KTU answer key requires you to justify your choice of formula with the region condition. Skipping this loses **1 mark** even if the final answer is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The I-V characteristics of a MOSFET describe $I_D$ as a function of $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$, with three regimes: **Cut-off**, **Triode**, **Saturation**.
- ✅ The **threshold voltage $V_{TH}$** is the minimum $V_{GS}$ required to form a conducting inversion layer.
- ✅ **Cut-off Condition:** $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ → $I_D = 0$.
- ✅ **Triode Condition:** $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ AND $V_{DS} < V_{OV}$ → $I_D = k'_n (W/L)\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$.
- ✅ **Saturation Condition:** $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ AND $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$ → $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2$.
- ✅ **Overdrive voltage** $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ — appears in every I-V equation.
- ✅ **Conduction parameter** $\beta_n = k'_n \cdot (W/L) = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)$ — always multiply $\mu_n C_{ox}$ by $W/L$.
- ✅ **Transconductance** $g_m = k'_n (W/L) V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 k'_n (W/L) I_D}$ — gain parameter in saturation.
- ✅ **Pinch-off voltage** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ — the boundary between triode and saturation.
- ✅ **Channel length modulation (CLM):** Real devices exhibit $I_D = I_{D,sat}(1+\lambda V_{DS})$; output resistance $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$.
- ✅ **Subthreshold conduction:** Below $V_{TH}$, an exponential leakage current exists — important for low-power CMOS design.
- ✅ **Body effect:** $V_{TH}$ increases when the source-body junction is reverse-biased; modifies I-V via an effective $V_{TH}$.
- ✅ **PMOS mirror rule:** All voltages and currents are negated; same equation structure with absolute values.
- ✅ **For numerical problems:** Always (1) identify the region, (2) select the appropriate equation, (3) substitute with consistent units, (4) verify the answer using a secondary formula.
- ✅ **For derivations:** State assumptions, derive the channel charge, apply drift current, integrate along the channel, and arrive at the final boxed expression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal MOS Capacitor: Band Diagrams at Equilibrium
topics.content:
