> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ideal MOS Capacitor: Band Diagrams at Equilibrium # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor (MOS) Capacitor** is the foundational building block of all modern CMOS integrated circuits. It consists of a three-layer sandwich structure: a **metal gate electrode**, a thin **insulating oxide layer** (classically $\text{SiO}_2$), and a **semiconductor substrate** (typically silicon). Physically, it behaves as a **parallel-plate capacitor** whose lower "plate" is a semiconductor whose charge can be modulated by an applied gate voltage. An **Ideal MOS Capacitor** is defined by three simultaneous conditions: 1. **Zero Work Function Difference**: The metal work function equals the semiconductor work function, $\Phi_m = \Phi_s \Rightarrow \Phi_{ms} = 0$. 2. **Perfect Insulator**: The oxide contains **no trapped charges**, no mobile ions, and no fixed charges ($\rho_{ox} = 0$). 3. **No Interface States**: The semiconductor–oxide interface is atomically perfect with **zero interface trap density** ($D_{it} = 0$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Ideal Conditions (Frequent 3-mark Question)** > The word "ideal" in PCECT302 strictly means: **(i) $\Phi_{ms} = 0$, (ii) $Q_{ox} = 0$, and (iii) $D_{it} = 0$**. Memorize this triplet — it appears in nearly every University Exam. ## 1.2 Physical Structure (Layer-by-Layer) | Layer | Material | Function | Typical Thickness | |:------|:---------|:---------|:------------------| | Top | Metal (Al, poly-Si) | Gate electrode | $\sim 100\,\text{nm}$ | | Middle | $\text{SiO}_2$ | Gate dielectric | $1.5 \text{ to } 10\,\text{nm}$ | | Bottom | Si (p or n) | Active channel | Bulk wafer | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Dam" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Pump**: Imagine a **hydroelectric dam**. > - The **metal gate** is the *upstream reservoir* (electrons injected from the wire). > - The **oxide** is the *impenetrable dam wall* — water (carriers) **cannot pass through**, only electrostatic pressure transmits. > - The **semiconductor surface** is the *downstream river bed*. As the reservoir level rises, the river surface (band edge) **bends** in response. > - At **equilibrium** (no extra water released), the water levels on both sides are connected by an invisible **siphon line** (the constant Fermi level $E_F$). The oxide thus acts as a **one-way electrostatic gate** — it blocks *DC current* completely but allows the metal's electric field to penetrate and **re-shape the semiconductor's energy bands**. ## 1.4 Why "At Equilibrium" Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thermal Equilibrium** $\equiv$ **Zero applied bias AND zero net current**. Under this condition, the **Fermi level $E_F$ must be FLAT (constant) across the entire structure** — from deep inside the metal, through the oxide, and into the bulk of the semiconductor. This single rule governs every band diagram in this module. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Critical Energy References Every MOS band diagram is built from these five energies (measured from the vacuum level $E_{vac}$): - **$E_c$** — Conduction band edge (bottom of conduction band) - **$E_v$** — Valence band edge (top of valence band) - **$E_F$** — Fermi level (constant at equilibrium) - **$E_i$** — Intrinsic Fermi level (lies near midgap) - **$E_{vac}$** — Vacuum level (reference, $E_{vac} - E_c = \chi$) ## 2.2 Work Function, Electron Affinity, and the Bands For the **semiconductor** (substrate): $$\Phi_s = \chi + \left(E_c - E_F\right)$$ For the **metal** (gate): $$\Phi_m = \chi_m + \left(E_c^{metal} - E_F^{metal}\right)$$ > Here, $\chi$ is the **electron affinity** (energy required to lift an electron from $E_c$ to $E_{vac}$). For Si: $\chi_{Si} = 4.05\,\text{eV}$. ## 2.3 The Three Reference Energy Differences | Quantity | Definition | Physical Meaning | Unit | |:---------|:-----------|:-----------------|:-----| | $E_g$ | $E_c - E_v$ | Band gap of semiconductor | eV | | $q\phi_F$ | $E_F - E_i$ (bulk) | Position of $E_F$ relative to midgap | eV | | $q\phi_{fp}$ | $E_i - E_F$ (for p-type) | Bulk potential of p-substrate | eV | For a p-type substrate doped $N_a$: $$q\phi_{fp} = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_a}{n_i}\right)$$ ## 2.4 The Three Operating Regimes of an Ideal MOS Capacitor When a gate voltage $V_G$ is swept (relative to the substrate), three distinct surface conditions appear. **At equilibrium ($V_G = 0$ and $\Phi_{ms} = 0$)**, the surface is in the **flat-band** condition. | Regime | Gate Polarity (p-substrate) | Surface Carrier Density | Band Bending | |:-------|:---------------------------|:------------------------|:-------------| | **Accumulation** | $V_G < 0$ (negative) | $p_s > N_a$ (holes pile up) | Bands bend **upward** near surface | | **Depletion** | $V_G > 0$ (small +ve) | $p_s < N_a$, mobile holes pushed away | Bands bend **downward** | | **Inversion** | $V_G > V_T$ (large +ve) | $n_s > p_s$ (electron layer forms) | Bands bend strongly **downward** ($> q\phi_{fp}$) | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Clarifier**: Although the syllabus groups accumulation/depletion/inversion as "equilibrium band diagrams", strictly they occur under *quasi-static* applied bias. **At true zero-bias equilibrium with an ideal structure, the bands are flat.** The diagrams showing accumulation/depletion/inversion illustrate the **sequence of band shapes** as $V_G$ varies — examiners often test this transition. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in Exam | |:--|:--------|:------------|:--------------------| | 1 | $\Phi_{ms} = \Phi_m - \Phi_s$ | Work-function difference | Find contact potential | | 2 | $V_{FB} = \Phi_{ms} - \dfrac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$ | Flat-band voltage (general) | Reduce to $0$ for ideal | | 3 | $V_{FB}^{\text{ideal}} = 0$ | Flat-band voltage for **ideal** MOS | Direct 1-line answer | | 4 | $q\psi_s = E_{i,\text{surf}} - E_{i,\text{bulk}}$ | Surface potential definition | Surface band bending | | 5 | $V_{ox} + \psi_s = V_G - V_{FB}$ | Voltage partition rule | Gauss-law loop | | 6 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Oxide capacitance per unit area | Used in $V_{ox} = Q_s/C_{ox}$ | | 7 | $E_g = 1.12\,\text{eV}$ (Si at 300 K) | Silicon band gap | Given/assumed value | | 8 | $\chi_{Si} = 4.05\,\text{eV}$ | Silicon electron affinity | Standard constant | | 9 | $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ (Si) | Intrinsic carrier concentration | Used in $\phi_{fp}$ formula | | 10 | $q\phi_{fp} = kT \ln(N_a/n_i)$ | Bulk Fermi potential of p-Si | p-type substrate problems | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip**: In the formula table above, vertical bars are deliberately replaced with `\vert` or written as fractions. **Never** write $|x|$ inside a markdown table — it breaks the column separator. Same applies in your answer sheets if you use tables; prefer inline math. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Work Function Difference $\Phi_{ms}$ for p-type Substrate We derive the most-tested expression: $\Phi_{ms}$ for a metal gate on **p-type silicon**. **Step 1 — Write metal work function from vacuum reference:** $$\Phi_m = E_{vac} - E_F^{metal}$$ This is the energy needed to extract an electron from the metal's Fermi level to vacuum. **Step 2 — Write semiconductor work function from vacuum reference:** $$\Phi_s = E_{vac} - E_F^{semi} = \chi_{Si} + \left(E_c - E_F\right)_{semi}$$ Since the vacuum level is universal, $E_{vac} - E_c = \chi_{Si}$ (electron affinity of Si). **Step 3 — Subtract the two:** $$\Phi_{ms} = \Phi_m - \Phi_s$$ **Step 4 — Express the semiconductor's $\left(E_c - E_F\right)$ in terms of doping:** For a p-type substrate at equilibrium, $E_F$ lies **below** midgap. The bulk Fermi level is located at $E_i - q\phi_{fp}$, so: $$\left(E_c - E_F\right)_{\text{bulk}} = E_g - q\phi_{fp} \quad \text{since } E_c - E_i = E_g/2$$ **Step 5 — Combine with $\phi_{fp}$ formula:** $$q\phi_{fp} = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_a}{n_i}\right) \cdot q = kT\ln\!\left(\frac{N_a}{n_i}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Final expression for p-type silicon:** $$\boxed{\;\Phi_{ms} = \Phi_m - \left[\chi_{Si} + \left(E_g/2 + q\phi_{fp}\right)\right]\;}$$ This is the **standard KTU answer** for the metal–p-silicon work-function difference. ## 3.2 Derivation: The Flat-Band Condition The flat-band condition is defined as the gate voltage at which **no band bending** occurs anywhere in the semiconductor ($\psi_s = 0$). **Step 1 — Start from the voltage partition across the structure:** $$V_G = V_{ox} + \psi_s + \Phi_{ms}/q$$ **Step 2 — At flat band, $\psi_s = 0$ by definition:** $$V_G\big\vert_{\psi_s=0} = V_{FB}$$ **Step 3 — The oxide supports the remaining voltage through its capacitance:** $$V_{ox} = \frac{Q_s}{C_{ox}}$$ where $Q_s$ is the semiconductor surface charge density. At flat band, $Q_s = 0$, so $V_{ox} = 0$. **Step 4 — Therefore:** $$\boxed{\;V_{FB} = \Phi_{ms} - \frac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}\;}$$ **Step 5 — For the IDEAL case** ($\Phi_{ms} = 0$ and $Q_{ox} = 0$): $$\boxed{\;V_{FB}^{\text{ideal}} = 0\;\text{V}\;}$$ **Physical Interpretation**: Zero applied gate voltage produces zero band bending — the bands are *literally* flat across the MOS stack. This is the defining signature of an ideal capacitor. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Energy Levels in the Three Regimes For a **p-type** substrate with $V_G$ varying (qualitative band-edge trajectories at the surface): | Regime | Surface $E_c$ | Surface $E_v$ | Surface $E_i$ | Bending Direction | Surface Charge | |:-------|:--------------|:--------------|:--------------|:------------------|:---------------| | Accumulation ($V_G < 0$) | Moves **up** | Moves **up** | Moves **up** | Upward toward bulk $E_F$ | $+Q_s = +qN_a$ (holes) | | Flat band ($V_G = 0$) | Horizontal | Horizontal | Horizontal | None | $Q_s = 0$ | | Depletion ($V_G > 0$, small) | Moves **down** | Moves **down** | Moves **down** | Downward (mild) | Negative ionized acceptors | | Inversion ($V_G > V_T$) | Moves **far down** | Moves **far down** | Crosses $E_F$ at surface | Strong downward ( $>$ $q\phi_{fp}$) | Electrons dominate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note on Sign Conventions**: In all KTU problems, "downward bending" means the bands at the surface are **lower in energy** than in the bulk. The intrinsic level $E_i$ bending is the **most reliable visual indicator** — examiners often ask students to "show $E_i$ bending" as proof of understanding. ## 3.4 Sample Numerical (KTU-Style Quick Problem) > **Problem**: An ideal MOS capacitor has $\Phi_m = 4.1\,\text{eV}$, $\chi_{Si} = 4.05\,\text{eV}$, $E_g = 1.12\,\text{eV}$, $N_a = 10^{16}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$, $T = 300\,\text{K}$ ($kT = 0.0259\,\text{eV}$). Find $\Phi_{ms}$. **Solution Steps:** First compute $q\phi_{fp}$: $$q\phi_{fp} = (0.0259)\ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(6.667 \times 10^{5})$$ $$= 0.0259 \times 13.41 = 0.347\,\text{eV}$$ Then compute $\Phi_s$: $$\Phi_s = 4.05 + (1.12/2 + 0.347) = 4.05 + 0.907 = 4.957\,\text{eV}$$ Finally: $$\Phi_{ms} = 4.1 - 4.957 = -0.857\,\text{eV}$$ > A **negative** $\Phi_{ms}$ for a p-type substrate means the metal work function is *smaller* than the semiconductor work function — the bands bend downward at the surface even at zero applied bias (built-in depletion). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note**: A precise energy-band diagram (energy vs. position with curved band edges) is not natively supported in Mermaid's flow chart grammar. Below we provide a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps the **interactions and energy references** in an ideal MOS capacitor. This block diagram is what examiners accept when "physical drawing is not feasible" in textual answers. ## 4.1 Block Architecture Flow — Ideal MOS Capacitor Energy Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Metal Gate
Work Function Phi m] -->|Field lines
through oxide| B[Gate Oxide
SiO2
No charge Qox 0] B -->|Electrostatic coupling| C[Semiconductor Surface
Si p type
Surface potential psi s] C -->|Bulk region| D[Semiconductor Bulk
Equilibrium Ef constant] D -->|Back contact| E[Ohmic Substrate Contact] A -.->|Phi ms = Phi m minus Phi s| F[Flat Band Condition
VFB 0 for ideal] classDef metal fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef oxide fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef semi fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef ref fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff class A metal class B oxide class C,D semi class E,F ref ``` ## 4.2 Reading Guide for the Energy Band Diagram (Textual Reconstruction) Since the actual MOS band diagram is a 2D plot of **energy (vertical) vs. position (horizontal)**, here is the **block-level layout** in plain text that you can redraw in your exam: **Left side (deep inside Metal):** - $E_{vac}$ at top - $E_F^{metal}$ is a horizontal line, $\Phi_m$ below $E_{vac}$ **Middle (Oxide region):** - All bands appear as **vertical dashed lines** (oxide has no $E_c$ or $E_v$ in the semiconductor sense; $E_{vac}$ is flat across the oxide) - The metal $E_F$ on the left and semiconductor $E_F$ on the right are at the **same height** (equilibrium) **Right side (Semiconductor, going from surface → bulk):** - $E_c$, $E_v$, $E_i$ as three curves - $E_F$ is a **horizontal straight line** joining the metal $E_F$ (equilibrium requirement) - $E_{vac}$ is shown with a small step at the oxide–semiconductor interface (because $\chi_{Si} \neq \chi_{ox}$) **Key features to label in your sketch:** 1. $\Phi_m$ — from metal $E_F$ to $E_{vac}$ 2. $\chi_{Si}$ — from semiconductor $E_c$ (at surface) to $E_{vac}$ 3. $E_g$ — vertical distance between $E_c$ and $E_v$ 4. $q\phi_{fp}$ — distance between bulk $E_i$ and bulk $E_F$ 5. $q\psi_s$ — vertical distance between bulk $E_i$ and surface $E_i$ (this is the **bending**) ## 4.3 Three-Region Block View — Accumulation / Depletion / Inversion ```mermaid flowchart TB P[Ideal MOS p substrate] --> Q{Apply Gate Voltage Vg} Q -->|Vg less than 0| R1[Accumulation
Bands bend up
Holes accumulate at surface] Q -->|Vg equals 0| R2[Flat Band
Bands horizontal
No surface charge] Q -->|Vg greater than 0 small| R3[Depletion
Bands bend down
Ionized acceptors] Q -->|Vg greater than VT| R4[Inversion
Bands bend strongly down
Electron layer forms] classDef pos fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef neu fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef neg fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff class R1 pos class R2 neu class R3,R4 neg ``` ## 4.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Diagram Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > The MOS energy band diagram at equilibrium is the **starting point for designing every transistor on a chip**. Engineers use it to: > - Compute the **threshold voltage $V_T$** (the gate voltage at which the surface inverts). > - Predict **leakage current** in nanometer-scale CMOS (subthreshold swing is set by how steeply the bands bend). > - Choose **metal gate materials** (TiN, TaN, W) to engineer $\Phi_m$ and tune $V_T$ for low-power logic. > - Diagnose **oxide traps and mobile ions** in reliability testing — any non-ideal flat-band shift is a fingerprint of $Q_{ox}$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. Define an ideal MOS capacitor. List the three ideal conditions.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **ideal MOS capacitor** is a Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor structure in which: 1. **No work-function difference** exists between the metal gate and the semiconductor: $\Phi_{ms} = \Phi_m - \Phi_s = 0$. 2. **The oxide is charge-free**: $Q_{ox} = 0$ (no fixed, mobile, or trapped charges). 3. **The semiconductor–oxide interface is trap-free**: $D_{it} = 0$. Under these conditions, the **flat-band voltage $V_{FB} = 0\,\text{V}$**, and at zero applied bias the energy bands are perfectly horizontal across the device. *(3 marks — 1 mark per condition)* --- ### **Q2. What is the flat-band condition? Why is it called the "reference" state of an MOS capacitor?** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **flat-band condition** is the gate bias at which there is **no band bending** in the semiconductor, i.e., the surface potential $\psi_s = 0$. The applied voltage required is the **flat-band voltage** $V_{FB}$, given by $V_{FB} = \Phi_{ms} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$. It is called the *reference state* because: - It is the **starting point** for analyzing accumulation, depletion, and inversion. - All device parameters ($V_T$, $C$-$V$ curves) are measured **relative to $V_{FB}$**. - For an ideal MOS, $V_{FB} = 0$, making the device symmetric and the math simplest. *(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > KTU 2024 ESE Rule: *Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts. Students must answer either full Question A or full Question B. Attempting both forfeits marks.* --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** #### **A(a)** Draw the energy band diagram of an **ideal MOS capacitor with p-type silicon substrate at thermal equilibrium**, with $V_G = 0$. Label all five energy references. Explain each region. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Draw the three regions horizontally:** - Left: Metal gate (block) - Middle: $\text{SiO}_2$ oxide (narrow block) - Right: p-type Si (wider block, surface on left, bulk on right) **Step 2 — Mark the vacuum level $E_{vac}$** as a horizontal line on the metal side and a parallel line on the semiconductor side, with a small offset at the oxide interface. **Step 3 — Draw $E_F$ as one continuous horizontal line** crossing all three regions (this enforces equilibrium). **Step 4 — In the semiconductor**, draw $E_c$ (top), $E_v$ (bottom), and $E_i$ (middle). Since $\Phi_{ms} = 0$ (ideal) and $V_G = 0$: - $E_c$, $E_v$, $E_i$ are all **parallel and horizontal** (flat bands). - $E_F$ lies **below** $E_i$ by $q\phi_{fp}$ because the substrate is p-type. **Step 5 — Label these distances:** - $\Phi_m$ : from $E_F$ (metal) to $E_{vac}$ on the metal side - $\chi_{Si}$ : from $E_c$ (surface) to $E_{vac}$ on the semiconductor side - $E_g = 1.12\,\text{eV}$ : vertical gap between $E_c$ and $E_v$ - $q\phi_{fp}$ : distance from bulk $E_i$ down to $E_F$ - $q\psi_s = 0$ : explicitly mark **zero** surface potential (this is the flat-band condition) **Step 6 — Brief explanation (1 mark):** Since the device is ideal and unbiased, no charge is induced at the surface, no field exists in the oxide, and consequently no band bending occurs. The energy bands are **perfectly flat**, with $E_F$ constant at $0.347\,\text{eV}$ below $E_i$ for $N_a = 10^{16}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$. **Mark split:** Diagram (3) + Labels (2) + Explanation (2) = **7 marks** --- #### **A(b)** Discuss the **three regions of operation** of an ideal MOS capacitor with **p-type substrate** as gate voltage is varied from large negative to large positive values. Include band-bending sketches. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **(i) Accumulation ($V_G < 0$, typically $V_G \ll 0$):** - Negative gate voltage repels electrons in the semiconductor and **attracts holes** to the oxide interface. - $p_s > N_a$ at the surface — a thin layer of **majority carriers** accumulates. - Bands bend **upward** at the surface (toward $E_F$). - The device behaves like a parallel-plate capacitor with the **oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$** alone (high-frequency $C = C_{ox}$). **(ii) Depletion ($V_G > 0$ but $V_G < V_T$):** - Positive gate voltage pushes holes away, leaving behind a region of **ionized acceptors** ($N_a^-$). - A **space-charge region** (depletion layer) forms, width $W$. - Bands bend **downward** by an amount $q\psi_s$ where $0 < q\psi_s < q\phi_{fp}$. - Effective capacitance: $C = C_{ox} C_s/(C_{ox} + C_s)$ — series combination of oxide and depletion capacitances. **(iii) Inversion ($V_G > V_T$):** - The surface potential exceeds $q\phi_{fp}$ — the bands bend so much that **$E_i$ crosses $E_F$** at the surface. - A thin layer of **minority carriers (electrons)** forms an "inversion layer" with $n_s > p_s$. - Bands bend **strongly downward** by more than $q\phi_{fp}$. - This is the **ON state** of the MOSFET channel; current can flow. **Mark split:** Region (i) (2) + Region (ii) (2) + Region (iii) (2) + Sketches (1) = **7 marks** --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **B(a)** Derive the expression for the **work-function difference $\Phi_{ms}$** between a metal gate and a p-type silicon substrate, starting from the definition of work function. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition of metal work function:** $$\Phi_m = E_{vac} - E_F^{m}$$ **Step 2 — Definition of semiconductor work function (using electron affinity $\chi_{Si}$):** $$\Phi_s = \chi_{Si} + \left(E_c - E_F\right)_{semi}$$ **Step 3 — Subtract:** $$\Phi_{ms} = \Phi_m - \Phi_s = \Phi_m - \chi_{Si} - \left(E_c - E_F\right)$$ **Step 4 — Express $(E_c - E_F)$ for p-type substrate.** Since $E_F$ is below midgap: $$E_c - E_F = \frac{E_g}{2} + q\phi_{fp}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\phi_{fp}$ expression:** $$q\phi_{fp} = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_a}{n_i}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Final answer:** $$\boxed{\;\Phi_{ms} = \Phi_m - \chi_{Si} - \frac{E_g}{2} - kT\ln\!\left(\frac{N_a}{n_i}\right)\;}$$ This is the **standard KTU result** — expected verbatim or in equivalent form. **Mark split:** Definitions (2) + Subtraction (1) + $\phi_{fp}$ substitution (2) + Final formula (2) = **7 marks** --- #### **B(b)** Explain the **flat-band voltage $V_{FB}$** for an MOS capacitor. Derive $V_{FB}$ from the voltage-balance equation for (i) the ideal case and (ii) the case with oxide charge $Q_{ox}$. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 mark):** The flat-band voltage $V_{FB}$ is the gate voltage required to make the semiconductor surface potential $\psi_s = 0$ (i.e., to flatten the energy bands at the oxide–semiconductor interface). **Voltage partition across the MOS stack (2 marks):** Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law from the metal gate to the bulk semiconductor: $$V_G = V_{ox} + \psi_s + \Phi_{ms}/q$$ where $V_{ox}$ is the voltage drop across the oxide and $\Phi_{ms}/q$ is the built-in contact potential (in volts). **Case (i) — Ideal MOS (2 marks):** With $\Phi_{ms} = 0$, $Q_{ox} = 0$, the surface charge $Q_s = 0$ at flat band, so $V_{ox} = Q_s/C_{ox} = 0$. Hence: $$V_{FB}^{\text{ideal}} = 0\,\text{V}$$ **Case (ii) — With oxide charge (2 marks):** If $Q_{ox} \neq 0$, an additional voltage is needed to balance the field from $Q_{ox}$. The oxide drop becomes $V_{ox} = -Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$ at flat band (the negative sign follows from the sign of induced image charge). Substituting: $$V_{FB} = \Phi_{ms} - \frac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$$ **Engineering significance:** A non-zero $V_{FB}$ shifts the entire $C$-$V$ curve along the voltage axis and alters the threshold voltage $V_T$ — a critical effect in CMOS scaling. **Mark split:** Definition (1) + Voltage equation (2) + Ideal case (2) + Non-ideal case (2) = **7 marks** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write** > 1. **"Flat band" does not mean $E_F$ is flat** — it means $E_c$ and $E_v$ are flat. $E_F$ is **always** flat at equilibrium. Mixing these loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Sign of $\Phi_{ms}$**: For Al gate on p-Si, $\Phi_{ms} < 0$. For n$^+$-poly on p-Si, $\Phi_{ms} > 0$. Always compute, never assume. > 3. **"Equilibrium band diagrams"** in KTU questions often include the three regimes — don't limit your answer to $V_G = 0$ only. > 4. **Units**: $q\phi_{fp}$ is in **eV**, $\phi_{fp}$ is in **volts**. Examiners *do* deduct marks for unit slip-ups. > 5. **Work-function difference $\Phi_{ms}$ is an energy (eV), but $V_{FB}$ is a voltage (V)**. Do not divide by $q$ twice. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print-this-list: 30-second Revision Card for the Day of Exam** **Core Definitions to Memorize:** - ☐ Ideal MOS = $\Phi_{ms} = 0$ **AND** $Q_{ox} = 0$ **AND** $D_{it} = 0$ - ☐ Flat band = $\psi_s = 0$ → $V_{FB} = \Phi_{ms} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$ → $V_{FB}^{\text{ideal}} = 0$ - ☐ Surface potential: $q\psi_s = E_{i,\text{surf}} - E_{i,\text{bulk}}$ **Five Energy References (draw all five in every diagram):** - ☐ $E_c$, $E_v$, $E_F$, $E_i$, $E_{vac}$ **Constants (write in margin on exam day):** - ☐ $E_g(\text{Si}) = 1.12\,\text{eV}$, $\chi_{Si} = 4.05\,\text{eV}$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ **Three Regimes of p-type MOS (in order of $V_G$):** - ☐ $V_G \ll 0$ → **Accumulation** (bands bend up, holes pile up) - ☐ $V_G = 0$ → **Flat band** (bands horizontal, $Q_s = 0$) - ☐ $V_G \gg 0$ → **Depletion** → **Inversion** (bands bend down, electrons at surface) **Critical Direction of Band Bending (most-tested visual):** - ☐ p-sub + $V_G > 0$ → bands bend **DOWN** (electrons are attracted) - ☐ p-sub + $V_G < 0$ → bands bend **UP** (holes are attracted) - ☐ n-sub: **opposite** of the above **Voltage-Balance Equation (Kirchhoff across MOS):** - ☐ $V_G = V_{ox} + \psi_s + \Phi_{ms}/q$ — must appear in any long answer **Key Formulas to Derive Cold:** - ☐ $\Phi_s = \chi + (E_g/2 + q\phi_{fp})$ for p-type - ☐ $\Phi_{ms} = \Phi_m - \chi_{Si} - E_g/2 - kT\ln(N_a/n_i)$ - ☐ $V_{FB}^{\text{ideal}} = 0$ **One-Sentence Summary of the Topic:** > The ideal MOS capacitor at equilibrium has perfectly flat energy bands because the metal and semiconductor work functions are equal and the oxide is charge-free; the moment $V_G$ deviates from zero, the bands bend — upward for accumulation, downward for depletion and inversion — and the **flat-band voltage remains the universal reference point** for all subsequent device analysis.

topics.title: Accumulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Accumulation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!NOTE] > **Accumulation** is the operating condition of the Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (MOS) structure in which a gate bias of the same polarity as the majority carriers of the semiconductor body is applied, causing a large concentration of majority carriers to be drawn toward the oxide-semiconductor interface. For an **NMOS device** (p-type substrate), accumulation occurs when the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ is more negative than the flat-band voltage $V_{FB}$ (i.e., $V_{GS} < V_{FB}$). For a **PMOS device** (n-type substrate), accumulation occurs when $V_{GS} > V_{FB}$ (positive side). Under accumulation: - The surface potential $\phi_s$ has the **same sign as** the gate bias (relative to the bulk). - The depletion region effectively **vanishes**; the depletion approximation is no longer valid. - A thin, highly conductive sheet of majority carriers is formed right at the Si–SiO₂ interface. - The structure behaves as a parallel-plate capacitor with the oxide as the dielectric and the accumulated carriers as the "plate" on the semiconductor side. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnet on a Fridge" Picture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine the semiconductor surface is a smooth floor covered with marbles (majority carriers). The metal gate is the ceiling. When you apply a voltage of the *same sign* as the marbles, the marbles are *electrostatically pulled* toward the ceiling — they pile up right under the oxide, like iron filings snapping to a magnet held just above a sheet of paper. Because these are *majority* carriers, no inversion occurs; the surface simply becomes "extra-rich" in the carriers that were already abundant in the bulk. The device has **no channel**, and therefore **no drain current** can flow. It is firmly in the **OFF state**. ### 1.3 Why Accumulation Matters in KTU Examinations > [!IMPORTANT] > Accumulation is the **default OFF state** of every MOSFET. Without it, the very concept of a "threshold voltage" (the boundary between accumulation and inversion) would have no physical meaning. The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes this concept under **CO2**: *Analyze the structure and operation of MOS devices.* ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Electron charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ **F/cm** - **Relative permittivity of silicon:** $\varepsilon_{rs} = 11.7$ - **Relative permittivity of SiO₂:** $\varepsilon_{ro} = 3.9$ - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 0.02585$ **V** - **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si:** $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³** (at 300 K) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Operation > [!NOTE] > The following logical chain is the **canonical KTU answer skeleton** for any question on "operation of MOS structure in accumulation." **Step 1 — Apply a gate bias of majority-carrier polarity.** For an NMOS (p-substrate, holes are majority), a negative $V_{GS}$ is applied. The metal gate acquires negative surface charge. **Step 2 — Electrostatic induction at the interface.** By Gauss's law, the semiconductor must develop a balancing positive sheet of charge at the Si–SiO₂ interface. Since the substrate is p-type, this positive sheet is composed of **holes** (majority carriers). **Step 3 — Surface potential becomes negative.** The band diagram bends **upward** at the surface. The surface potential $\phi_s$ takes a **negative value** (for the p-type substrate, $\phi_s < 0$ in accumulation). **Step 4 — The depletion region collapses.** In the depletion regime, ionized acceptors ($N_A^-$) form a space-charge region. In accumulation, the surface becomes so rich in holes that the ionized acceptors are **neutralized** by mobile holes. The depletion width $W \rightarrow 0$. **Step 5 — No inversion, no channel.** Electrons (minority carriers) are *repelled* from the surface. There is **no n-type layer** to connect source and drain. Hence $I_D = 0$. **Step 6 — Capacitive behavior.** All the applied gate voltage drops across the oxide. The structure looks like a capacitor $C_{ox}$ storing charge $Q_s$ on the semiconductor side. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Why? | |---|---| | Apply negative $V_{GS}$ | Like charges **repel**; negative gate repels electrons but **attracts** positive holes in p-substrate. | | Holes accumulate at interface | Holes need only drift a small distance; they form a 2-D "sheet" of positive charge. | | $\phi_s < 0$ | The electric field points from bulk to gate; integrating $E$ from bulk to surface gives a negative potential drop. | | Depletion region shrinks | Mobile holes neutralize ionized $N_A^-$ acceptors; net space charge at the surface becomes positive, not negative. | | No channel | A channel requires an **inversion layer** (electrons for NMOS); accumulation does *not* invert the surface type. | | Capacitor model | The charge on the gate ($Q_G$) and the charge in the semiconductor ($Q_s$) are equal and opposite; the only insulator between them is SiO₂. | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Memorize this table — it covers 90 % of accumulation-region numerical problems in the 2024 Scheme ESE papers. | # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Sign | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fermi potential (p-substrate) | $\phi_F = V_T \ln(N_A / n_i)$ | $N_A$ in cm⁻³ | V | | 2 | Work-function difference (n⁺ poly / p-Si) | $\phi_{MS} = -\left(\dfrac{E_g}{2q} + \phi_F\right)$ | Standard NMOS gate | V | | 3 | Flat-band voltage | $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$ | Always | V | | 4 | Gate–semiconductor relation | $V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_s - \dfrac{Q_s}{C_{ox}}$ | General | V | | 5 | Oxide capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ro}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | $t_{ox}$ in cm | F/cm² | | 6 | Strong-accumulation charge | $Q_s \approx -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{FB})$ | $\vert\phi_s\vert \gg V_T$ and $V_{GS} \ll V_{FB}$ | C/cm² | | 7 | Accumulation hole density at surface | $p_s \approx N_A \exp\left(-\dfrac{q\phi_s}{kT}\right)$ | $\phi_s < 0$ | cm⁻³ | | 8 | Total semiconductor charge (depletion) | $Q_s = \mp\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{rs}\varepsilon_0 q N_A \,\vert\phi_s\vert}$ | $\vert\phi_s\vert \le \vert\phi_F\vert$ | C/cm² | | 9 | Threshold condition | $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ | Boundary of strong inversion | V | | 10 | Sub-threshold swing factor | $n \approx 1 + \dfrac{C_{D}}{C_{ox}}$ | Weak-inversion to accumulation | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > The **vertical pipe symbol** has been intentionally replaced with `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CMOS digital logic:** The OFF state of both NMOS and PMOS in a CMOS inverter is the accumulation mode. Leakage current is governed by the **weak-accumulation → weak-inversion** transition. - **MOS capacitors (MOSCAPs):** Used as **charge-storage elements** in DRAM and analog sample-and-hold circuits. Accumulation provides maximum capacitance, equal to $C_{ox}$. - **Biochemical sensors:** Accumulation-mode MOSFETs sense pH, ion concentrations, and biomolecules through shifts in $V_{FB}$. - **Photodetectors & solar cells:** The accumulation layer modulates surface recombination velocity, affecting device efficiency. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Semiconductor Surface Charge in Accumulation We begin with the fundamental gate–semiconductor voltage relation (this is the **master equation** the KTU Board Examiner loves to test): $$ V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_s - \frac{Q_s}{C_{ox}} $$ **Step 1 — Identify the region of interest.** We are in the **accumulation regime**, which means for an NMOS (p-substrate), $\phi_s < 0$. Also, the magnitude of $\phi_s$ is small compared to the applied $V_{GS}$ in *strong* accumulation. **Step 2 — Express the charge $Q_s$ in accumulation using the depletion-style formula but with $\phi_s < 0$.** $$ Q_s = \mp \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{rs}\varepsilon_0 q N_A \, \vert\phi_s\vert} $$ The **upper sign ($-$)** applies for p-type substrate, so: $$ Q_s = - \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{rs}\varepsilon_0 q N_A \, \vert\phi_s\vert} $$ But wait — in accumulation, $Q_s$ is **positive** (mobile holes). Hence the *correct* sign in the gate equation uses the semiconductor charge convention, where a positive $Q_s$ in the formula above is **subtracted** from the bulk. For accumulated holes the *magnitude* of the surface charge density is: $$ \vert Q_s \vert = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{rs}\varepsilon_0 q N_A \, \vert\phi_s\vert} $$ **Step 3 — Use the exponential form (Boltzmann statistics) valid in accumulation.** For a p-type semiconductor, the hole concentration at the surface is: $$ p(0) = N_A \exp\!\left(-\frac{q\phi_s}{kT}\right) $$ Because the surface is *rich* in holes, the charge per unit area stored in the thin accumulation layer is: $$ Q_s = q \int_0^{\infty} \!\! \left[p(x) - N_A\right] dx $$ Using the standard textbook approximation (Neamen, *Semiconductor Physics and Devices*, Ch. 10), the integration yields: $$ Q_s \approx \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{rs}\varepsilon_0 kT\, n_i}}{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-q\phi_s}{2kT}\right) \quad \text{(for p-type in accumulation)} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for the surface potential $\phi_s$ as a function of $V_{GS}$.** Substituting the charge expression into the master equation, in *strong accumulation* ($V_{GS} \ll V_{FB}$), the term $\phi_s$ in the master equation is small compared to $(V_{GS} - V_{FB})$, so we approximate: $$ V_{GS} - V_{FB} \approx -\frac{Q_s}{C_{ox}} $$ **Step 5 — Final compact result.** $$ \boxed{\,Q_s \approx -C_{ox}\left(V_{GS} - V_{FB}\right) \quad \text{(strong accumulation, NMOS)}\,} $$ This shows that in strong accumulation, the semiconductor surface charge is **linearly proportional** to the gate overdrive beyond flat band, exactly like a parallel-plate capacitor. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Given:** NMOS capacitor with p-type substrate, $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $t_{ox} = 20$ nm, $V_{FB} = -0.9$ V. Applied gate bias $V_{GS} = -3$ V. Find the semiconductor surface charge density and confirm the regime. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute oxide capacitance.** $$ C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ro}\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{20 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ C_{ox} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-13}}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.727 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $Q_s$ using the strong-accumulation formula.** $$ Q_s = -C_{ox}\left(V_{GS} - V_{FB}\right) = -1.727 \times 10^{-7}\bigl(-3 - (-0.9)\bigr) $$ $$ Q_s = -1.727 \times 10^{-7} \times (-2.1) = 3.627 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C/cm}^2 $$ The **positive sign** confirms that holes (positive carriers) have accumulated at the surface — consistent with NMOS accumulation. **Step 3 — Verify regime using the master equation.** We need to check whether the depletion-style $Q_s$ gives a consistent $\phi_s$. The total semiconductor charge in depletion/weak-accumulation is: $$ Q_s = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{rs}\varepsilon_0 q N_A \vert\phi_s\vert} $$ Setting $3.627 \times 10^{-7} = \sqrt{2(11.7)(8.854 \times 10^{-14})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{16})\vert\phi_s\vert}$: $$ (3.627 \times 10^{-7})^2 = (3.314 \times 10^{-15})\vert\phi_s\vert $$ $$ \vert\phi_s\vert = \frac{1.316 \times 10^{-13}}{3.314 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 39.7 \text{ V} $$ > [!WARNING] > A surface potential of **~40 V** is **physically impossible** under the depletion approximation. This tells us the depletion model has *broken down* — we are firmly in **strong accumulation**, exactly where the linear $Q_s = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{FB})$ formula is the correct description. This is a **classic KTU examiner trick**: ask the student to identify the regime by checking the validity of the depletion approximation. **Step 4 — Cross-check using the master equation.** $$ V_{GS} - V_{FB} = \phi_s - \frac{Q_s}{C_{ox}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -2.1 \text{ V} = \phi_s - \frac{3.627 \times 10^{-7}}{1.727 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ -2.1 = \phi_s - 2.1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \phi_s \approx 0 \text{ V} $$ The surface potential is essentially **zero** (clamped near the bulk Fermi level) — physically, this means the band bending is so small at the surface that almost all the gate voltage is dropped across the oxide. This is the hallmark of strong accumulation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 MOS Energy-Band Diagram in Accumulation (NMOS, p-substrate) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Metal Gate
VGS less than VFB (negative)"] -->|"Fermi level EF_m pulled down"| B["SiO2 Oxide Layer
Voltage drop approx VGS minus VFB"] B -->|"High hole density (p-type)"| C["Accumulation Layer
Bands bend UPWARD at surface
phi_s negative and small"] C -->|"Bulk p-Si, NA = 1e16 cm-3"| D["Neutral p-type Substrate
phi equals phi_F (Fermi potential)"] D -->|"Reference: bulk Fermi level"| E["Vacuum Level Reference"] E --> A ``` ### 4.2 Charge Distribution Flow in Accumulation ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1["Negative Charge on Metal Gate
Q_G negative"] -->|"Mirrored across oxide"| Q2["Positive Sheet of Holes at Si-SiO2 Interface
Q_s positive, magnitude equals Q_G"] Q2 -->|"Depletion region collapses"| Q3["No Ionized Acceptors at Surface
W depletion approx 0"] Q3 -->|"Majority carrier excess"| Q4["Surface Hole Density p_s greater than NA
Boltzmann: p_s equals NA exp(-q phi_s over kT)"] Q4 -->|"Result: I_D equals 0"| Q5["Device in OFF State
Acts as Capacitor C_ox"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MOSCAP in Accumulation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bias_Network["Bias Network Block"] V1["DC Source: VGS less than VFB"] end subgraph MOS_Capacitor["MOS Capacitor Block"] M1["Metal Plate: carries negative Q_G"] OX["Dielectric: SiO2 thickness t_ox, permittivity 3.9"] M2["Semiconductor Plate: positive holes at interface"] end subgraph Output_Regime["Output Regime Classification"] R1["phi_s negative, less than 0"] R2["W depletion approx 0 nm"] R3["Q_s equals minus C_ox times VGS minus VFB"] end V1 --> M1 M2 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 -->|"I_D = 0"| OFF["DEVICE STATE: OFF / ACCUMULATION"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]** **Q1. Define accumulation in a MOS structure. What gate bias condition produces it for an NMOS capacitor with a p-type substrate?** **Model Answer:** Accumulation is the operating condition of a MOS structure in which a gate bias of the **same polarity as the majority carriers** of the semiconductor body is applied, drawing a large concentration of majority carriers to the oxide-semiconductor interface. For an **NMOS** with p-type substrate (holes are majority), accumulation occurs when $V_{GS} < V_{FB}$ (i.e., a negative gate-to-source bias more negative than the flat-band voltage). The surface becomes enriched with holes, no inversion layer forms, and the drain current $I_D = 0$. --- **Q2. Why does the depletion approximation fail in the accumulation regime?** **Model Answer:** The depletion approximation assumes the mobile carrier density is negligible compared to the ionized dopant density, and that the space-charge region is bounded by ionized acceptors or donors. In accumulation, however, the surface is flooded with **mobile majority carriers** (holes for NMOS), whose concentration can be several orders of magnitude larger than $N_A$. The ionized acceptors are **neutralized** by these mobile holes, so the depletion width $W \to 0$. The correct description becomes an **exponential (Boltzmann) sheet of holes**, with $Q_s = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{FB})$ in strong accumulation. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** With the help of the energy-band diagram and charge distribution, explain the **accumulation mode of operation** of a MOS capacitor having a p-type semiconductor substrate. Clearly state the condition for accumulation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An ideal NMOS capacitor has the following parameters: - Substrate doping: $N_A = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻³ - Oxide thickness: $t_{ox} = 10$ nm - Oxide charge: $Q_{ox} = 0$ - Flat-band voltage: $V_{FB} = -0.75$ V - Applied gate bias: $V_{GS} = -2.5$ V Calculate (i) the oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$, (ii) the semiconductor surface charge density $Q_s$, and (iii) verify the operating regime. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Explanation:** 1. **Condition:** For p-type substrate, $V_{GS} < V_{FB}$. 2. **Energy band diagram:** The metal Fermi level is pulled *below* the semiconductor Fermi level. Bands bend **upward** at the surface: $E_c$ and $E_v$ move up, $E_i$ also moves up. Surface potential $\phi_s < 0$. 3. **Charge distribution:** Negative charge resides on the metal gate. A matching **positive sheet of holes** forms at the Si–SiO₂ interface. The depletion region has effectively collapsed ($W \approx 0$). 4. **Capacitive behavior:** The structure acts as a parallel-plate capacitor with $C_{ox}$ as the dielectric. 5. **No channel:** Electrons (minority carriers) are repelled; hence no n-type inversion layer, and $I_D = 0$. **Energy-band diagram (Mermaid sketch):** ```mermaid flowchart LR L1["Metal: EF_m low"] -->|"Vacuum level"| L2["SiO2: voltage drop"] L2 -->|"Bands bend up"| L3["Si surface: EF_p high, phi_s negative"] L3 -->|"Neutral bulk"| L4["p-Si bulk: EF at phi_F"] ``` **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Oxide capacitance:** $$ C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ro}\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{10 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ C_{ox} = 3.453 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2 $$ **(ii) Semiconductor surface charge density (strong accumulation approximation):** $$ Q_s = -C_{ox}\left(V_{GS} - V_{FB}\right) $$ $$ Q_s = -3.453 \times 10^{-7} \times \left(-2.5 - (-0.75)\right) = -3.453 \times 10^{-7} \times (-1.75) $$ $$ Q_s = 6.043 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C/cm}^2 $$ The **positive** sign indicates hole accumulation at the Si–SiO₂ interface, as expected for NMOS in accumulation. **(iii) Regime verification:** Using the master equation $V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_s - Q_s/C_{ox}$: $$ -2.5 = -0.75 + \phi_s - \frac{6.043 \times 10^{-7}}{3.453 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ -2.5 = -0.75 + \phi_s - 1.75 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \phi_s \approx 0 \text{ V} $$ The surface potential is **clamped to nearly zero**, confirming that the depletion-region formula would yield an unphysical $\phi_s$ and that the device is in **strong accumulation**. ✓ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Style]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **semiconductor surface charge density in the accumulation regime** for an NMOS capacitor. Show explicitly why the depletion approximation breaks down. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare the three regimes of MOS operation — **accumulation, depletion, and inversion** — in a tabular form, clearly indicating (i) gate bias condition, (ii) surface potential sign, (iii) dominant charge type at the surface, (iv) typical $I_D$ behaviour, and (v) one engineering application. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** Start with the master equation and the hole density at the surface: $$ V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_s - \frac{Q_s}{C_{ox}} $$ In the **p-type substrate**, the hole concentration at the surface is: $$ p(0) = N_A \exp\!\left(-\frac{q\phi_s}{kT}\right) $$ When $\phi_s < 0$ (accumulation), $p(0) \gg N_A$. The space charge near the surface is dominated by this excess mobile hole density. Integrating over the thin accumulation layer gives: $$ Q_s \approx \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{rs}\varepsilon_0 kT\, n_i}}{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-q\phi_s}{2kT}\right) $$ **Breakdown of the depletion approximation:** The depletion model assumes: $$ p(x) \ll N_A \;\;\text{and}\;\; n(x) \ll N_A $$ In accumulation, both inequalities **violate** because $p(0)$ exceeds $N_A$ by orders of magnitude. The depletion width $W$ becomes undefined because the charge neutrality is restored by *mobile* holes, not by ionized dopants. The correct description uses **Boltzmann statistics**, leading to: $$ Q_s = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{FB}) \quad \text{(strong accumulation)} $$ **(b) Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Accumulation | Depletion | Inversion | |---|---|---|---| | **Gate bias (NMOS, p-sub)** | $V_{GS} < V_{FB}$ | $V_{FB} < V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ | | **Surface potential $\phi_s$** | $\phi_s < 0$ (small $\vert\phi_s\vert$) | $0 < \phi_s < 2\phi_F$ | $\phi_s \ge 2\phi_F$ | | **Dominant surface charge** | Mobile majority carriers (holes) | Ionized acceptors $N_A^-$ | Mobile minority carriers (electrons) | | **Drain current $I_D$** | Zero (no channel) | Essentially zero (sub-threshold leakage) | Conduction: $I_D \propto (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | | **Engineering application** | DRAM storage capacitor; OFF state of CMOS | Varactor diodes; charge-coupled devices | Active channel in MOSFETs; CMOS logic | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign confusion in $Q_s$:** Many students write $Q_s = +C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{FB})$ and forget that for NMOS in accumulation, $V_{GS} - V_{FB} < 0$, which would *incorrectly* yield a negative charge. Always state the physical interpretation. > 2. **Confusing accumulation with weak inversion:** Accumulation has $\phi_s < 0$; weak inversion has $0 < \phi_s < 2\phi_F$. The current is exponentially small in weak inversion but *truly* zero (DC) in strong accumulation. > 3. **Forgetting the depletion-width collapse:** Students often write $W > 0$ in accumulation, which contradicts the physical picture. State explicitly that $W \to 0$. > 4. **Unit mismatches:** $N_A$ must be in cm⁻³, $t_{ox}$ in cm, $C_{ox}$ in F/cm², $Q_s$ in C/cm². KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for wrong or missing units. > 5. **Skipping the regime-verification step:** When a numerical problem gives a "depletion-style" answer with unphysical $\phi_s$ (> 1 V), the student must recognize that the device is in strong accumulation and switch to the linear $C_{ox}$ formula. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Accumulation (Module 3, PCECT302)** - **Definition:** Accumulation = majority carriers pile up at the Si–SiO₂ interface under a gate bias of the same polarity as the bulk majority carriers. - **NMOS condition:** $V_{GS} < V_{FB}$ (typically a *negative* gate bias). - **PMOS condition:** $V_{GS} > V_{FB}$ (typically a *positive* gate bias). - **Surface potential:** $\phi_s < 0$ (NMOS, p-sub) or $\phi_s > 0$ (PMOS, n-sub); small in magnitude in strong accumulation. - **Depletion width:** $W \to 0$ — the depletion approximation is invalid. - **Master equation (always valid):** $V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_s - Q_s/C_{ox}$. - **Strong-accumulation charge:** $Q_s = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{FB})$. - **Capacitance behaviour:** $C = C_{ox}$ at high-frequency MOS-C measurement in accumulation (maximum capacitance). - **No drain current:** $I_D = 0$ because no inversion (channel) layer exists. - **Fermi potential:** $\phi_F = V_T \ln(N_A / n_i)$ for p-type; always positive for p-substrate. - **Flat-band voltage:** $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$. - **Work-function difference (n⁺ poly / p-Si):** $\phi_{MS} = -(E_g/2q + \phi_F) < 0$. - **Hole density at the surface:** $p(0) = N_A \exp(-q\phi_s / kT)$ — grows exponentially as $\phi_s$ becomes more negative. - **Physical picture:** Like a magnet pulling iron filings to a surface; majority carriers form a thin, highly conductive 2-D sheet. - **Engineering uses:** OFF state in CMOS, MOSCAP-based DRAM, ion-sensitive bio-sensors, varactor tuning in RF circuits. - **KTU Board Favourite Sub-questions:** (i) "State the condition for accumulation and draw the energy band diagram." (3 marks) (ii) "Derive $Q_s$ in strong accumulation." (7 marks) (iii) "Compare accumulation, depletion, and inversion." (7 marks) - **Common pitfall:** Writing $\phi_s > 0$ for NMOS accumulation — this is the *opposite* of the correct sign. - **Quick self-test:** If $\phi_s$ comes out > 1 V from a depletion-style calculation, **switch to the strong-accumulation formula immediately**; you are in the wrong regime.

topics.title: Depletion and Inversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Depletion and Inversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **DEPLETION REGION (in MOS Structure):**
> The depletion region in a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (MOS) structure is a thin layer beneath the oxide-semiconductor interface that is swept free of mobile charge carriers (electrons and holes) by the transverse electric field induced by the applied gate-to-substrate voltage. The fixed ionized acceptor (in p-type substrate) or donor (in n-type substrate) atoms remain, creating a space-charge region characterized by a built-in potential barrier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INVERSION LAYER (Surface Inversion):**
> Inversion is the condition in a MOS structure when the surface potential $\phi_s$ under the gate oxide has been pulled/pushed so strongly that the band bending at the semiconductor surface causes the local majority carrier type to flip. For a p-type substrate, strong inversion occurs when $\phi_s \ge 2\phi_F$, at which point the electron concentration at the surface ($n_s$) becomes numerically equal to or greater than the bulk hole concentration ($p_{p0}$), creating a thin, highly conductive n-type sheet — the **inversion layer** — that forms the channel of the MOSFET.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Magnetic Pebbles in a Sandbox" Analogy

Imagine a sandbox (the p-type silicon substrate) filled with tiny red marbles (holes — majority carriers). Above the sandbox, there is a thin glass plate (the SiO₂ oxide) and a flat metal lid (the gate). Now you turn a knob (apply a gate voltage $V_{GS}$):

- **$V_{GS} = 0$ (Flat-Band):** The marbles lie flat. Nothing has moved.
- **$V_{GS} < 0$ (Accumulation):** The knob pushes *more* red marbles up against the glass. The surface becomes a "traffic jam" of majority carriers.
- **$V_{GS} > 0$ (Depletion):** The knob now *repels* red marbles downward, away from the glass. The top layer of the sandbox becomes empty of marbles — only the **fixed negatively charged "drains" (ionized acceptors, $N_A^-$)** remain. This empty, charged zone is the **depletion region**.
- **$V_{GS} \gg 0$ (Inversion):** The electric field at the surface becomes so strong that it actually rips electron-hole pairs apart and pulls the minority electrons *up* against the glass, forming a new "blue marble sheet" — a thin n-type layer on top of the p-type substrate. The surface has been **inverted** from p-type to n-type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants for Silicon at T = 300 K** (Must memorize for KTU exams)
> - Thermal Voltage: $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ (often taken as **26 mV**)
> - Electron Charge: $q = \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ C}$
> - Silicon Permittivity: $\varepsilon_s = 11.7\,\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-12}\ F/cm}$
> - Oxide (SiO₂) Permittivity: $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9\,\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ F/cm}$
> - Intrinsic Carrier Concentration: $n_i \approx \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{10}\ cm^{-3}}$
> - Silicon Bandgap: $E_g = \mathbf{1.12\ eV}$
> - Silicon Electron Affinity: $\chi_s = \mathbf{4.05\ eV}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operating Regimes of the MOS Capacitor

A MOS capacitor (the heart of the MOSFET) moves through three regimes as $V_{GS}$ is varied (assuming p-type substrate, $V_{GB}$ is gate-to-bulk voltage):

1. **Accumulation** ($V_{GS} < V_{FB}$): Holes pile up at the oxide interface. No depletion.
2. **Depletion** ($V_{FB} < V_{GS} < V_T$): Holes are pushed away; ionized acceptors form a depletion layer of width $W$.
3. **Inversion** ($V_{GS} > V_T$): A thin n-channel forms at the surface.

The boundary between depletion and inversion is defined by the **Threshold Voltage $V_T$** — a key KTU high-yield parameter.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Progression

**Step 1 — Surface Potential $\phi_s$ is the master variable.**
The applied gate voltage drops across two series capacitors: the oxide ($C_{ox}$) and the semiconductor depletion layer ($C_s = \varepsilon_s / W$).

**Step 2 — Band Bending.**
When $\phi_s > 0$, the energy bands at the surface bend **downward** (in an energy band diagram). When $\phi_s = 0$, bands are flat. When $\phi_s = \phi_F$ (the bulk Fermi potential), intrinsic level $\phi_i$ crosses the Fermi level $E_F$ at the surface — this is the **onset of inversion**.

**Step 3 — Strong Inversion Criterion.**
Strong inversion is defined (per KTU syllabus) as the point where $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$. At this point:
$$n_s = n_i\, e^{q\phi_s / kT} = n_i\, e^{2q\phi_F / kT} = N_A = p_{p0}$$

The minority electron concentration at the surface has become equal to the bulk doping — a true **type-flip**.

**Step 4 — Depletion Width at Inversion.**
Solving Poisson's equation in the depletion approximation gives the **maximum depletion width** $W_{m}$ at the brink of strong inversion:
$$W_{m} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s\,(2\phi_F)}{q\,N_A}}$$

**Step 5 — Beyond Threshold.**
Once $V_{GS} > V_T$, additional gate voltage does **not** significantly widen the depletion region (it is pinned near $W_m$). Instead, it drops across the oxide to build up the **inversion charge** $Q_n$:
$$Q_n = -C_{ox}\,(V_{GS} - V_T)$$

This is the mobile charge that conducts current from source to drain in a MOSFET.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | V (≈ 0.0259 V) | At T = 300 K |
| 2 | Bulk Fermi Potential | $\phi_F = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | V | Positive for p-type |
| 3 | Surface Electron Density | $n_s = n_i\, e^{\phi_s / V_T}$ | cm⁻³ | When $\phi_s = 0$, $n_s = n_i$ |
| 4 | Surface Hole Density | $p_s = n_i\, e^{-\phi_s / V_T}$ | cm⁻³ | When $\phi_s = 0$, $p_s = n_i$ |
| 5 | Onset of Inversion | $\phi_s = \phi_F$ | — | $n_s = p_s = n_i$ (intrinsic surface) |
| 6 | Strong Inversion | $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ | — | $n_s = N_A$ |
| 7 | Depletion Charge Density | $\vert Q_d \vert = q\,N_A\,W$ | C/cm² | Per unit area |
| 8 | Max Depletion Width | $W_m = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\varepsilon_s\,\phi_F}{q\,N_A}}$ | cm | At strong inversion onset |
| 9 | Oxide Capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | F/cm² | $t_{ox}$ = oxide thickness |
| 10 | Threshold Voltage (Ideal) | $V_T = \dfrac{2\phi_F \,q\,N_A\,W_m}{C_{ox}} + 2\phi_F$ | V | With $V_{FB}=0$ (ideal) |
| 11 | Full Threshold Voltage | $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_s\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$ | V | KTU-standard form |
| 12 | Inversion Charge (Above $V_T$) | $\vert Q_n \vert = C_{ox}\,(V_{GS} - V_T)$ | C/cm² | Mobile channel charge |
| 13 | Flat-Band Voltage | $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ss}}{C_{ox}}$ | V | Work-function + oxide charges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The depletion–inversion physics determines the **threshold voltage** of every CMOS transistor in every modern chip. Without precise control of depletion width and inversion charge, transistors would not switch predictably. This is the foundation of all digital logic, DRAM cell operation, and CMOS image sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depletion Charge Density from Poisson's Equation

We solve the 1-D Poisson equation in the depletion region of a p-type substrate:

$$\dfrac{d^2 \phi(x)}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step A — Charge density in depletion region.**
For $0 \le x \le W$, mobile carriers are negligible, so only ionized acceptors exist:
$$\rho(x) = -q\,N_A \quad (N_A\ \text{is constant, acceptors are negative ions})$$

**Step B — Substitute into Poisson's equation:**
$$\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = \dfrac{q\,N_A}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step C — Integrate once (electric field):**
$$\dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \dfrac{q\,N_A\,x}{\varepsilon_s} + C_1$$

**Step D — Boundary condition:** The field vanishes at the edge of the depletion region, $x = W$, so $C_1 = -\dfrac{q\,N_A\,W}{\varepsilon_s}$.

**Step E — Integrate again (potential):**
$$\phi(x) = \dfrac{q\,N_A}{\varepsilon_s}\left(\dfrac{x^2}{2} - W\,x\right) + C_2$$

**Step F — Boundary condition:** $\phi(W) = 0$ (potential referenced to bulk neutral region), so $C_2 = \dfrac{q\,N_A\,W^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$.

**Step G — Surface potential at $x = 0$:**
$$\phi_s = \phi(0) = \dfrac{q\,N_A\,W^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step H — Solve for $W$ in terms of $\phi_s$:**
$$W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\varepsilon_s\,\phi_s}{q\,N_A}}$$

**Step I — Depletion charge per unit area:**
$$\vert Q_d \vert = q\,N_A\,W = \sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_s\,q\,N_A\,\phi_s}$$

This is the **space-charge density** of the depletion region.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold Voltage (Full Derivation)

We apply the voltage balance around the MOS capacitor at strong inversion ($\phi_s = 2\phi_F$):

$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_s + \dfrac{\vert Q_{d,\max} \vert}{C_{ox}} \quad \text{(neglecting $Q_n$ at threshold)}$$

**Step A — Write the depletion charge at strong inversion:**
$$\vert Q_{d,\max} \vert = \sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_s\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)} = \sqrt{4\,\varepsilon_s\,q\,N_A\,\phi_F}$$

**Step B — Substitute into voltage equation:**
$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{4\,\varepsilon_s\,q\,N_A\,\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step C — Simplify the square root** to obtain the **KTU-standard threshold voltage formula:**

$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_s\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step D — Identify the three terms:**
- $V_{FB}$ = flat-band voltage (work-function difference + oxide-trapped charge)
- $2\phi_F$ = surface potential term (band-bending cost)
- Third term = voltage drop across oxide to support the depletion charge

**Step E — Compute $C_{ox}$ explicitly:**
$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{t_{ox}}\ \text{F/cm}^2 \quad (\text{with } t_{ox}\ \text{in cm})$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical — KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A p-type silicon MOS capacitor has $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $t_{ox} = 50$ nm = $5 \times 10^{-6}$ cm, and $V_{FB} = -0.5$ V. Find: (i) $\phi_F$, (ii) $W_m$, (iii) $C_{ox}$, and (iv) $V_T$. Take $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, $T = 300$ K.

**Solution:**

**(i) Fermi potential:**
$$\phi_F = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right)$$
$$= 0.0259 \times \ln(6.667 \times 10^5) = 0.0259 \times 13.41 = 0.347\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Maximum depletion width:**
$$W_m = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\varepsilon_s\,(2\phi_F)}{q\,N_A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.694}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16}}}$$
$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{1.443 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{9.02 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 3.00 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{cm} = 0.30\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) Oxide capacitance:**
$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.9 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

**(iv) Threshold voltage:**
$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_s\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

The depletion term:
$$\sqrt{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16} \times 0.694} = \sqrt{2.31 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 4.81 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{C/cm}^2$$

Divided by $C_{ox}$:
$$\dfrac{4.81 \times 10^{-8}}{6.9 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.697\ \text{V}$$

Therefore:
$$V_T = -0.5 + 0.694 + 0.697 \approx 0.89\ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The threshold voltage here is **0.89 V** — typical for an n-channel MOSFET with a 50 nm gate oxide. The body-effect term (last term) is the largest contributor; reducing $N_A$ or $t_{ox}$ lowers $V_T$, which is the trend in modern sub-22 nm CMOS nodes.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Depletion vs. Inversion

| Property | Depletion Region | Inversion Layer |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| Mobile carriers present? | **No** (swept away) | **Yes** (minority electrons at surface) |
| Mobile charge type | None | Electrons (n-type sheet) |
| Space charge | Fixed ionized dopants ($N_A^-$) | Mobile electrons + fixed dopants |
| Thickness $W$ | $0.1 - 1\ \mu m$ typical | $1 - 10$ nm typical |
| Formed by | Moderate positive $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} \ge V_T$ |
| Governing equation | Poisson (constant $\rho$) | Schrödinger (quantum confinement in modern nodes) |
| Reacts to $V_{GS}$ increase | Widens (until $W_m$) | Charge density $Q_n$ grows |
| Carrier type vs bulk | Same as bulk (still p) | **Opposite** of bulk (n on p) |
| Conductive? | **No** (insulating) | **Yes** (forms MOSFET channel) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Band-Bending Evolution Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

This flowchart maps the three operating regimes of the MOS structure by tracking the surface potential $\phi_s$, the depletion width $W$, and the carrier type at the surface.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start0[VGS equals 0 V] --> flatband
    flatband[FLAT BAND: phi_s equals 0, W equals 0, surface is neutral]
    flatband --> cond1
    cond1{Is VGS less than VFB?}
    cond1 -- YES --> accum
    cond1 -- NO --> cond2
    accum[ACCUMULATION: holes pile up at oxide interface, phi_s less than 0]
    accum --> next1[Increase VGS toward positive]
    next1 --> cond2
    cond2{Is VGS between VFB and VT?}
    cond2 -- YES --> deplete
    cond2 -- NO --> cond3
    deplete[DEPLETION: holes repelled, ionized acceptors form space charge, W grows as sqrt of phi_s]
    deplete --> next2[Increase VGS further]
    next2 --> cond3
    cond3{Is VGS greater than VT?}
    cond3 -- YES --> invert
    cond3 -- NO --> deplete
    invert[INVERSION: phi_s equals 2 phi_F, n-type electron sheet forms at surface, W pinned at Wm, channel conductive]
    invert --> next3[Beyond VT, Qn equals Cox times VGS minus VT]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: MOSFET Cross-Section (Functional Flow)

Since a true physical cross-section of a MOSFET is complex to render in Mermaid, the following is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping the gate, oxide, and substrate regions that govern depletion and inversion formation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gateBlock[BLOCK A: METAL GATE] --> oxideBlock
    oxideBlock[BLOCK B: SIO2 OXIDE LAYER, permittivity epsilon_ox, thickness t_ox] --> semiBlock
    semiBlock[BLOCK C: P TYPE SILICON SUBSTRATE, doping NA] --> depletionBlock
    depletionBlock[BLOCK D: DEPLETION REGION, width W, ionized acceptors, charge Qd] --> inversionBlock
    inversionBlock[BLOCK E: INVERSION LAYER, charge Qn, channel for MOSFET conduction]
    gateBlock -. VGS gate voltage .-> oxideBlock
    oxideBlock -. C_ox per unit area .-> semiBlock
    semiBlock -. phi_F bulk fermi potential .-> depletionBlock
    depletionBlock -. phi_s surface potential .-> inversionBlock
```

### 4.3 Charge Density vs. Surface Potential — Conceptual Plot (ASCII Schematic)

```
Vertical Axis: Charge density |Q|  (C/cm²)
   ▲
   │              Inversion
   │              charge Qn
   │             ◄──────────►
   │             │   Sloped   │
   │             │   mobile   │
   │             │   charge   │
   │             │            │
   │   ◄─────────┼────────────┤  Strong
   │    Depletion│  Wm (max)  │  Inversion
   │     region  │            │  (phi_s = 2 phi_F)
   │    Qd slope │            │
   │    ∝ √φ_s   │            │
   │             │            │
   │   ──────────┼────────────┤─►  Horizontal Axis: surface potential φ_s
   │             │            │
   │   0    φ_F  │   2φ_F     │   3φ_F
   │             │            │
   │   Flat-Band │   Onset    │   Strong
   │             │   of       │   Inversion
   │             │   Inversion│
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the plot:** The leftmost region (0 to $\phi_F$) shows pure depletion charge $\vert Q_d \vert$ growing as $\sqrt{\phi_s}$. Past $\phi_F$ (onset of inversion), the mobile inversion charge $Q_n$ begins to dominate. Once $\phi_s \ge 2\phi_F$, $W$ saturates at $W_m$ and additional surface potential drops across the oxide to increase $Q_n$ linearly with $(V_{GS} - V_T)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
Define the term **strong inversion** in a MOS structure and write the mathematical condition relating surface potential $\phi_s$ to the bulk Fermi potential $\phi_F$.

**Model Answer:**
Strong inversion is the operating condition of a MOS structure in which the surface of the semiconductor has been converted from its original majority-carrier type to the opposite type (e.g., a p-type surface becomes n-type) due to band-bending caused by the applied gate voltage.
**Mathematical condition:** $\phi_s \ge 2\phi_F$
At this point the surface electron concentration $n_s = n_i\,e^{2\phi_F / V_T} = N_A$, which equals the bulk hole concentration. The inversion layer thickness is typically a few nanometres and supports the channel conduction in a MOSFET. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
What is the **maximum depletion width** $W_m$ in a MOS capacitor? Derive its expression in terms of substrate doping $N_A$, permittivity $\varepsilon_s$, and bulk Fermi potential $\phi_F$.

**Model Answer:**
The maximum depletion width $W_m$ is the depletion-region width at the onset of strong inversion, beyond which the depletion region stops widening and inversion charge accumulates.
**Expression:**
$$W_m = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s\,(2\phi_F)}{q\,N_A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\varepsilon_s\,\phi_F}{q\,N_A}}$$
It is derived by setting $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ in the depletion-approximation solution $W = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s \phi_s / (q N_A)}$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 📗 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the **onset of inversion** ($\phi_s = \phi_F$) with **strong inversion** ($\phi_s = 2\phi_F$). Threshold voltage is defined at **strong inversion**, not onset.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting units — $N_A$ in cm⁻³, $W$ in cm, $\varepsilon_s$ in F/cm — yields numbers off by factors of 10 or more.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting the $V_{FB}$ term in the $V_T$ expression. KTU awards 2–3 marks specifically for stating the complete threshold-voltage formula.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing up depletion charge $Q_d$ (fixed) and inversion charge $Q_n$ (mobile). In the saturation region, only $Q_n$ flows.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation + Numerical**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Equivalent Style]**

**(a)** Starting from Poisson's equation in the depletion region, derive the expression for the **depletion-region width $W$** in a p-type MOS capacitor in terms of surface potential $\phi_s$, permittivity $\varepsilon_s$, electronic charge $q$, and acceptor concentration $N_A$. Hence obtain the **maximum depletion width $W_m$** at the threshold of strong inversion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an n-channel MOS capacitor with $N_A = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻³, oxide thickness $t_{ox} = 100$ nm, and flat-band voltage $V_{FB} = -1.0$ V, calculate the **threshold voltage $V_T$** at T = 300 K. Use $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Poisson's equation in depletion region** (x = 0 at surface, x = W at depletion edge):
$$\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = \dfrac{q\,N_A}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once with boundary condition $E(W) = 0$:**
$$E(x) = -\dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \dfrac{q\,N_A}{\varepsilon_s}\,(W - x)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate again with $\phi(W) = 0$:**
$$\phi(x) = \dfrac{q\,N_A}{\varepsilon_s}\,\left(W\,x - \dfrac{x^2}{2}\right) - \dfrac{q\,N_A\,W^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4 — Surface potential at $x = 0$:**
$$\phi_s = \dfrac{q\,N_A\,W^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $W$:**
$$W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s\,\phi_s}{q\,N_A}}$$

**Step 6 — At strong inversion, $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$:**
$$W_m = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\varepsilon_s\,\phi_F}{q\,N_A}}$$

**(Full 7 marks awarded for clear derivation with boundary conditions.)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Thermal voltage:** $V_T = 0.0259$ V at T = 300 K.

**Step 2 — Fermi potential:**
$$\phi_F = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5 \times 10^{15}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(3.33 \times 10^5)$$
$$= 0.0259 \times 12.717 = 0.3294\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Surface potential at threshold:** $2\phi_F = 0.6588$ V.

**Step 4 — Oxide capacitance:**
$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{100 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Depletion term in $V_T$:**
$$\sqrt{2\varepsilon_s q N_A (2\phi_F)} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^{15} \times 0.6588}$$
$$= \sqrt{1.096 \times 10^{-15}} = 3.31 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{C/cm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Divided by $C_{ox}$:**
$$\dfrac{3.31 \times 10^{-8}}{3.45 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.960\ \text{V}$$

**Step 7 — Final threshold voltage:**
$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_s q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$
$$V_T = -1.0 + 0.6588 + 0.960 = 0.619\ \text{V} \approx \mathbf{0.62\ V}$$

**(7 marks — full credit if all 7 steps are shown with units.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Concept + Comparison (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Equivalent Style]**

**(a)** Explain with the help of band diagrams how the **MOS structure transitions from accumulation → depletion → inversion** as gate voltage $V_{GS}$ is varied (assume p-type substrate). Define $\phi_F$, $\phi_s$, $V_{FB}$, and $V_T$ in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **depletion** and **inversion** regions in a MOS structure under the following heads: (i) mobile carrier presence, (ii) charge type, (iii) thickness/width, (iv) response to increasing $V_{GS}$, (v) electrical conductivity, and (vi) governing physical equation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

For a p-type MOS capacitor (n-type gate or n⁺ poly gate):

1. **Flat-Band ($V_{GS} = V_{FB}$):** Energy bands are flat throughout the semiconductor. $\phi_s = 0$.

2. **Accumulation ($V_{GS} < V_{FB}$):** Negative gate voltage attracts holes to the surface. Bands bend **upward** at the surface. $p_s > N_A$.

3. **Depletion ($V_{FB} < V_{GS} < V_T$):** Positive gate voltage repels holes. Ionized acceptors $N_A^-$ form a depletion region of width $W$. Bands bend **downward** by $\phi_s = \dfrac{q N_A W^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$.

4. **Inversion ($V_{GS} > V_T$):** Bands bend so far that intrinsic level $\phi_i$ at the surface lies **below** the Fermi level $E_F$. Surface becomes n-type. Condition: $\phi_s \ge 2\phi_F$, where $\phi_F = V_T \ln(N_A/n_i)$.

**Definitions:**
- $\phi_F$ = bulk Fermi potential (eV/V), positive for p-type
- $\phi_s$ = surface potential (band bending)
- $V_{FB}$ = flat-band voltage
- $V_T$ = threshold voltage (gate voltage at strong inversion)

**(7 marks — 2 marks for each regime explanation, 3 marks for definitions.)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

| Head | Depletion Region | Inversion Layer |
|------|------------------|------------------|
| (i) Mobile carriers | Absent (swept away) | Present (minority electrons) |
| (ii) Charge type | Fixed ionized $N_A^-$ (immobile) | Mobile electrons $Q_n$ (mobile) |
| (iii) Width | $W = 0.1 - 1\ \mu m$ | $1 - 10$ nm (thin sheet) |
| (iv) Response to $V_{GS}$ | $W \propto \sqrt{\phi_s}$ — grows | $Q_n \propto (V_{GS} - V_T)$ — grows; $W$ pinned at $W_m$ |
| (v) Conductivity | **Insulating** | **Highly conductive** (MOSFET channel) |
| (vi) Governing equation | Poisson's equation with constant $\rho$ | Schrödinger equation (quantum) + Poisson |

**(7 marks — 1 mark per correctly contrasted head, plus 1 mark for the quantum-mechanical comment in (vi).)**

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets:**
> - **Three regimes** of MOS capacitor: **Accumulation → Depletion → Inversion** (in order of increasing positive $V_{GS}$ for p-type).
> - **Onset of inversion** (intrinsic surface) occurs at $\phi_s = \phi_F$. **Strong inversion** occurs at $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$.
> - **Threshold voltage** is the gate voltage at which $\phi_s$ first reaches $2\phi_F$ — this is the **KTU-defining** condition.
> - **Maximum depletion width** is **pinned** at $W_m$ once inversion begins; further $V_{GS}$ drops across the oxide to charge $Q_n$.
> - **Inversion charge** = $Q_n = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_T)$ — this is the **mobile channel charge** that flows from source to drain.
> - **Depletion charge** = $|Q_d| = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s q N_A \phi_s}$ — fixed ionized dopants, **does not** contribute to current.
> - **Flat-band voltage** $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ss}/C_{ox}$ — must be **included** in $V_T$ expression (KTU awards marks for this).
> - **Fermi potential** $\phi_F = V_T \ln(N_A/n_i)$ — for n-type substrate, take $\phi_F$ negative.
> - **Quantum effects** matter in modern sub-22 nm nodes: classical $Q_n$ formula overestimates charge by 10–30%.
> - **Body effect:** Increasing source-bulk reverse bias $V_{SB}$ widens the depletion region and **increases** $V_T$ — critical in analog CMOS design.
> - **Memory aid for definitions:** "**D**epletion = **D**rained of carriers; **I**nversion = **I**nverted carrier type at surface."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surface Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Surface Potential** (denoted $\phi_s$ or $\psi_s$) is defined as the electrostatic potential at the surface of a semiconductor (at the oxide–semiconductor interface in a MOS structure) measured with respect to the electrostatic potential in the neutral bulk of the semiconductor. Mathematically:

$$\phi_s = V_s - V_{bulk}$$

where $V_s$ is the band-bending potential at the semiconductor surface and $V_{bulk}$ is the bulk potential. The sign convention in KTU standard texts (Neamen, Pierret, Streetman) typically uses $\phi_s > 0$ for downward band bending (n-type) and $\phi_s < 0$ for upward band bending.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Surface potential is the single most important parameter in MOS physics. **EVERY** charge, field, and capacitance relation in a MOS capacitor is expressed as a function of $\phi_s$. Mastering $\phi_s$ is mastering the MOS device.

### Physical Significance

Surface potential quantifies **how much the energy bands at the semiconductor surface have bent** relative to the bulk due to the applied gate voltage and the work-function difference. The band bending $q\phi_s$ is the vertical displacement of the conduction and valence band edges at $x=0$ (the Si–SiO₂ interface).

### Conceptual Analogy (The "Hill and Valley" Picture)

Imagine a flat agricultural plain (the bulk semiconductor) where the ground is perfectly level. Now imagine the boundary between this plain and a raised highway (the oxide). The "Surface Potential" is the **height of the embankment** at the edge of the plain — how much the ground has been raised or lowered at the boundary.

- If the surface potential is **positive** $\rightarrow$ the plain is pushed **down** at the edge (downward band bending for n-type, accumulation of electrons).
- If the surface potential is **negative** $\rightarrow$ the plain is pushed **up** at the edge (upward band bending, depletion/inversion).
- A **negative gate voltage** on a p-type substrate pushes the potential **upward** (more negative $\phi_s$), analogous to pushing the embankment higher.

### Physical Constants Used (Bolded as Required)

| Symbol | Value | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **q** | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** | Elementary charge |
| **k** | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** | Boltzmann constant |
| **ε₀** | $8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ **F/cm** | Vacuum permittivity |
| **ε_s (Si)** | $11.7 \varepsilon_0$ | Silicon permittivity |
| **n_i (Si)** | $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³** | Intrinsic carrier concentration (300 K) |
| **V_T = kT/q** | $\approx 0.02585$ **V** | Thermal voltage at 300 K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Fermi Potential ($\phi_F$):**
> The Fermi potential in a p-type semiconductor (doped with $N_A$ acceptors) is:
> $$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) > 0$$
