| $\tau = B_1 / \varepsilon_m$ | "Lucky electron" parameter | dimensionless | Exponential sensitivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units reminder for KTU exams:**
> - ε<sub>si</sub> = 11.7 × 8.854 × 10<sup>-14</sup> F/cm = **1.04 × 10<sup>-12</sup> F/cm**
> - ε<sub>ox</sub> = 3.9 × 8.854 × 10<sup>-14</sup> F/cm = **3.45 × 10<sup>-13</sup> F/cm**
> - q = **1.6 × 10<sup>-19</sup> C**
> - kT/q at 300 K = **0.02585 V** (thermal voltage)
> - φ<sub>F</sub> for N<sub>A</sub> = 10<sup>16</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup> ≈ **0.35 V**

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Body Effect on Threshold Voltage

**Starting point — depletion charge under the gate:**

$$Q_B = -\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

**Gate oxide capacitance per unit area:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$$

**Threshold condition** (gate voltage that makes surface potential ψ<sub>s</sub> = 2φ<sub>F</sub>):

**Step 1:** Write the gate voltage as the sum of flat-band, surface band-bending, and the voltage across the oxide needed to balance depletion charge:

$$V_G = V_{FB} + \psi_s - \frac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply inversion criterion ψ<sub>s</sub> = 2φ<sub>F</sub>:

$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F - \frac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Q<sub>B</sub>:

$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 4:** Split into zero-bias and bias-dependent parts:

$$\boxed{V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)}$$

**Textual interpretation:** The square-root term is the depletion width at the surface. Increasing V<sub>SB</sub> widens the depletion region, increases the magnitude of |Q<sub>B</sub>|, and hence raises V<sub>th</sub>.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DIBL-Induced V<sub>th</sub> Shift (Approximate)

**Step 1 — 2-D Poisson's equation in the channel:**

For a short-channel device, we cannot assume 1-D vertical electrostatics. The 2-D potential φ(x,y) satisfies:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions:**
- At y = 0 (Si–SiO<sub>2</sub> interface): φ(x,0) = surface potential ψ<sub>s</sub>(x)
- At drain: φ(L, 0) = V<sub>DS</sub> + V<sub>bi</sub>
- At source: φ(0, 0) = V<sub>bi</sub>

**Step 3 — Solve for the minimum surface potential** (where the source-side barrier sits):

$$\psi_{s,min} \approx \psi_{s,long} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{r_j}{L}\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{2W_S}{r_j}} - 1\right)\right] - \eta \, V_{DS}$$

**Step 4 — Threshold voltage shift:**

The gate voltage that achieves ψ<sub>s,min</sub> = 2φ<sub>F</sub> is the new V<sub>th</sub>:

$$V_{th}^{short} = V_{th0} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{th}^{DIBL} = -\eta \cdot V_{DS}}$$

**Interpretation:** The DIBL coefficient η is essentially the "leakage of gate control" to the drain. For long channels, η → 0. For sub-100 nm, η can reach **50–100 mV/V**, leading to severe off-state leakage.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Substrate Current Model (Lucky Electron Picture)

**Step 1 — Maximum lateral field at the drain pinch-off:**

$$\varepsilon_m = \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l}$$

where **l** is the velocity-saturation region length (typically a few hundred Å).

**Step 2 — Probability that an electron gains enough energy to cause impact ionization:**

Following the "lucky electron" model, the probability an electron travels **l** without scattering:

$$P \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Impact-ionization generation rate per unit length:**

$$\alpha(\varepsilon) = A_i \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_i}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate along the high-field region** to get the substrate current:

$$I_{sub} = I_D \int_0^{\varepsilon_m} A_i \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_i}{\varepsilon}\right) d\varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Closed-form approximation (used in KTU problems):**

$$\boxed{I_{sub} \approx C_1 \, I_D \, V_{DS} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_1}{\varepsilon_m}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- I<sub>sub</sub> is **exponentially** sensitive to ε<sub>m</sub>.
- Since ε<sub>m</sub> depends on V<sub>DS</sub> – V<sub>DS,sat</sub>, a small increase in V<sub>DD</sub> produces a large increase in I<sub>sub</sub> and therefore in HCE degradation.
- I<sub>sub</sub> peaks at V<sub>GS</sub> ≈ V<sub>DS</sub>/2 — this is the standard **HCE worst-case bias condition** used for accelerated life testing.

---

### 3.4 Hot-Carrier-Induced V<sub>th</sub> Degradation

Empirically (from accelerated stress tests):

$$\Delta V_{th}(t) = A \cdot \log\!\left(1 + \frac{t}{\tau_0}\right)$$

- The **logarithmic** time dependence arises because the most damaging interface states form early, and subsequent degradation proceeds slowly.
- **Device lifetime** is defined as the time to reach ΔV<sub>th</sub> = 50 mV (or 10% I<sub>D</sub> degradation):

$$\tau_{life} \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{B_2}{\varepsilon_m}\right)$$

This **Arrhenius-like** dependence on ε<sub>m</sub> is why a small reduction in V<sub>DD</sub> dramatically extends device lifetime.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Exam Style)

**Problem:** An NMOS has N<sub>A</sub> = 10<sup>16</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>, t<sub>ox</sub> = 10 nm, source-body bias V<sub>SB</sub> = 2 V, n<sub>i</sub> = 1.5 × 10<sup>10</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>. Find γ and V<sub>th</sub> assuming V<sub>FB</sub> = −0.9 V.

**Step 1 — Fermi potential:**

$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.02585 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right)$$

$$= 0.02585 \cdot \ln(6.67 \times 10^5) = 0.02585 \cdot 13.41 = 0.347 \, V$$

**Step 2 — Oxide capacitance:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{10 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-7} \, F/cm^2$$

**Step 3 — Body-effect coefficient:**

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 10^{16}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{3.33 \times 10^{-15}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{5.77 \times 10^{-8}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.167 \, V^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Zero-bias V<sub>th</sub>:**

$$V_{th0} = -0.9 + 2(0.347) + \gamma \sqrt{2\phi_F}$$

$$= -0.9 + 0.694 + 0.167 \cdot \sqrt{0.694} = -0.9 + 0.694 + 0.167 \cdot 0.833 = -0.069 \, V$$

**Step 5 — Threshold with V<sub>SB</sub> = 2 V:**

$$V_{th} = -0.069 + 0.167\left(\sqrt{0.694 + 2} - \sqrt{0.694}\right)$$

$$= -0.069 + 0.167\left(\sqrt{2.694} - 0.833\right) = -0.069 + 0.167(1.641 - 0.833)$$

$$= -0.069 + 0.167(0.808) = -0.069 + 0.135 = 0.066 \, V$$

**Result:** V<sub>th</sub> shifted from **−0.069 V to 0.066 V** — a **134 mV increase** due to the body effect.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Threshold Voltage Variation Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Long Channel Vth Reference Vth0"] --> BE["Body Effect Delta Vth BE via VSB"]
    Start --> SCE["Short Channel Effect Delta Vth SCE via L"]
    Start --> NWE["Narrow Width Effect Delta Vth NWE via W"]
    Start --> DIBL["Drain Induced Barrier Lowering Delta Vth DIBL via VDS"]
    Start --> OX["Oxide Charge Effect Delta Vth OX via Qox"]
    Start --> T["Temperature Effect Delta Vth T via T"]

    BE --> Sum["Effective Vth = Vth0 plus all Delta Vth terms"]
    SCE --> Sum
    NWE --> Sum
    DIBL --> Sum
    OX --> Sum
    T --> Sum
    Sum --> Final["Vth effective drives device operation and reliability"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hot Carrier Effect Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["High VDS Applied to Channel"] --> B["Carriers Accelerated by Lateral Field near Drain"]
    B --> C["Carrier Effective Temperature Rises Beyond Lattice"]
    C --> D1["Impact Ionization near Drain Pinch Off"]
    C --> D2["Injection into Gate Oxide Hot Carrier Injection"]
    C --> D3["Interface Trap Generation at Si SiO2 Boundary"]

    D1 --> E1["Substrate Current Isub measurable"]
    D2 --> E2["Oxide Trapped Charge Qot causes Delta Vth shift"]
    D3 --> E3["Mobility Degradation gm drop and IDSAT reduction"]

    E1 --> F["Device Reliability Degradation Over Time"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G["End of Life Criteria Delta Vth or Delta ID reached"]
```

### 4.3 Flow Diagram — V<sub>th</sub> Roll-off Characteristic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["Long Channel Region: Vth equals Vth0 and is constant"]
    L1["Moderate Channel Length: Vth begins to decrease roll off begins"]
    L2["Short Channel: Vth drops sharply with L"]
    L3["Deep Submicron: DIBL dominates, Vth becomes VDS dependent"]
    L4["Below Minimum L: Device cannot be turned off, severe leakage"]

    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

### 4.4 Architecture — Hot Carrier Degradation Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Pulse Generator stress VDS high VGS half VDS"] --> DUT["Device Under Test NMOS in saturation"]
    DUT --> I1["Measure Isub as HCE monitor"]
    DUT --> I2["Measure IDSAT and Vth shift"]
    DUT --> I3["Measure gm and mobility"]

    I1 --> Logic["Compare with End of Life Criteria"]
    I2 --> Logic
    I3 --> Logic
    Logic --> Result["Predict Lifetime using Arrhenius Extrapolation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Mermaid for KTU students:** These block diagrams help you visualize how multiple ΔV<sub>th</sub> mechanisms add up, and how HCE forms a closed-loop reliability problem. In KTU exams, drawing such flow diagrams (along with the equations) often earns **extra presentation marks**.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Body Effect** in a MOSFET. Write the expression for threshold voltage including the body-effect term.

**Model Answer:**
The body effect is the phenomenon in which the threshold voltage V<sub>th</sub> of a MOSFET changes when a voltage V<sub>SB</sub> is applied between the source and the body (substrate). A positive V<sub>SB</sub> reverse-biases the source–body junction, widens the surface depletion region, and requires a larger gate voltage to achieve inversion. Hence V<sub>th</sub> increases with V<sub>SB</sub>.

$$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

where γ is the body-effect coefficient depending on substrate doping and oxide thickness.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)**? Why is it significant in short-channel MOSFETs?

**Model Answer:**
DIBL is a short-channel effect in which a high drain voltage V<sub>DS</sub> lowers the potential barrier at the source, reducing the effective threshold voltage. The shift is approximated as:

$$\Delta V_{th}^{DIBL} = -\eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

**Significance:** In short-channel devices (sub-100 nm), DIBL causes V<sub>th</sub> to become V<sub>DS</sub>-dependent, increases **off-state leakage current**, worsens static power dissipation, and limits further scaling of CMOS technology.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **threshold voltage of a long-channel NMOS** with source-body bias V<sub>SB</sub>. Clearly state the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **short-channel effect (SCE)** and **DIBL** in a MOSFET. Discuss how each affects V<sub>th</sub> as channel length L is reduced. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Surface potential condition for inversion:**
For an NMOS on a p-type substrate, the onset of strong inversion occurs when the surface potential ψ<sub>s</sub> = 2φ<sub>F</sub>, where

$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Depletion charge at the surface:**

$$Q_B = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (\psi_s)} = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

(The additional V<sub>SB</sub> arises because the source is at +V<sub>SB</sub> relative to the body.)

**Step 3 — Gate voltage equation** (KVL across the MOS structure):

$$V_G = V_{FB} + \psi_s - \frac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply inversion condition ψ<sub>s</sub> = 2φ<sub>F</sub>:**

$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 5 — Define zero-bias V<sub>th0</sub> and body coefficient γ:**

$$V_{th0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}, \quad \gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Final form:**

$$\boxed{V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)}$$

**Term meanings:** V<sub>FB</sub> = flat-band voltage; 2φ<sub>F</sub> = band-bending at inversion; γ = body-effect coefficient; V<sub>SB</sub> = source-to-body bias; C<sub>ox</sub> = oxide capacitance per unit area.

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Short-Channel Effect (Charge Sharing):**
When L is comparable to the source/drain depletion width, the gate no longer controls the entire channel depletion charge. The depletion regions from source and drain "share" charge with the gate-controlled region. Less charge is required for inversion, so V<sub>th</sub> **decreases** as L decreases:

$$\Delta V_{th}^{SCE} \approx -\frac{r_j}{L}\left[\sqrt{1 + \frac{2W}{r_j}} - 1\right] \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

**DIBL:**
For very short channels, the drain electric field penetrates to the source and reduces the source-side potential barrier. The barrier lowering makes it easier for carriers to leave the source, reducing V<sub>th</sub>:

$$\Delta V_{th}^{DIBL} = -\eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

**Sketch description:** Draw a 2-D cross-section of the MOSFET showing depletion regions extending from source/drain into the channel, with the dotted line showing the channel potential. Mark that the barrier is highest at long L and progressively flattens as L shrinks.

**Trend:** As L → L<sub>min</sub>, both SCE and DIBL contribute, and V<sub>th</sub> falls sharply — the "V<sub>th</sub> roll-off" curve.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **physical mechanism of Hot Carrier Effects (HCE)** in a short-channel MOSFET. Discuss impact ionization and the role of substrate current. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **lucky-electron substrate current model**:

$$I_{sub} = C_1 \, I_D \, V_{DS} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_1}{\varepsilon_m}\right)$$

Explain why I<sub>sub</sub> peaks at V<sub>GS</sub> ≈ V<sub>DS</sub>/2. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Physical Mechanism:**

1. In saturation, the channel is pinched off near the drain. A high lateral electric field ε exists in the pinch-off region (length *l*).
2. Electrons entering this region gain kinetic energy from the field. Their effective temperature T<sub>e</sub> exceeds the lattice temperature — they become "hot."
3. Some hot electrons gain energy > 1.5 e<sub>g</sub> (~1.7 eV) and cause **impact ionization**, generating electron–hole pairs.
4. The generated holes flow to the substrate, forming the measurable **substrate current I<sub>sub</sub>**.
5. Some hot electrons (energy > 3.1 eV) may be injected into the gate oxide, becoming **trapped charge** or creating **interface states** at the Si–SiO<sub>2</sub> boundary.
6. Trapped charge shifts V<sub>th</sub>; interface states reduce mobility and I<sub>D,sat</sub>. These are the *degradation* symptoms.

**Key physical reason:** ε<sub>m</sub> is the critical driver — exponential sensitivity makes HCE a *reliability* issue.

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Maximum lateral field:**

$$\varepsilon_m = \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l}$$

**Step 2 — Probability that an electron gains sufficient energy without scattering** (lucky electron model):

$$P \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_1}{\varepsilon_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Impact-ionization coefficient:**

$$\alpha(\varepsilon) = A_i \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_i}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Integration along the high-field region:**

$$I_{sub} = I_D \int \alpha(\varepsilon) \, dy = C_1 \, I_D \, V_{DS} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_1}{\varepsilon_m}\right)$$

**Why I<sub>sub</sub> peaks at V<sub>GS</sub> ≈ V<sub>DS</sub>/2:**

- At very low V<sub>GS</sub>: I<sub>D</sub> is small ⇒ I<sub>sub</sub> is small.
- At very high V<sub>GS</sub> ≈ V<sub>DS</sub>: channel is long, V<sub>DS,sat</sub> ≈ V<sub>DS</sub>, so ε<sub>m</sub> is small ⇒ exponential is tiny.
- The **maximum** of I<sub>sub</sub> occurs at the trade-off: moderate I<sub>D</sub> with high ε<sub>m</sub>, which happens at **V<sub>GS</sub> ≈ V<sub>DS</sub>/2** (i.e., V<sub>DS</sub> − V<sub>DS,sat</sub> ≈ V<sub>DS</sub>/2 is maximized).

This is the **industry-standard HCE worst-case bias** for accelerated life testing.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of φ<sub>F</sub>** — students often compute φ<sub>F</sub> in eV and plug it into a V equation. Always convert using (kT/q)·ln(...).
> 2. **Mixing up signs** — body effect *increases* V<sub>th</sub> (positive ΔV), DIBL and SCE *decrease* V<sub>th</sub> (negative ΔV). The net V<sub>th</sub> = V<sub>th0</sub> ± sum.
> 3. **Confusing "hot carrier" with "high-energy carrier"** — a "hot" carrier is one with energy >> thermal, not merely "mobile."
> 4. **Stating V<sub>th</sub> roll-off occurs only at very short L** — it actually *begins* around L ≈ 1 µm and becomes severe by L < 0.25 µm.
> 5. **Omitting the exponential term in I<sub>sub</sub>** — the dominant contribution is the exponential; I<sub>D</sub>·V<sub>DS</sub> is just a prefactor.
> 6. **Mixing up short-channel and narrow-width effects** — SCE depends on L, NWE depends on W. They have *opposite* effects on V<sub>th</sub>.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Threshold Voltage Variations & Hot Carrier Effects**
>
> ✅ V<sub>th</sub> is *not* constant — it shifts with V<sub>SB</sub>, L, W, V<sub>DS</sub>, T, and oxide charge.
>
> ✅ **Body effect** ↑V<sub>th</sub> with V<sub>SB</sub>; characterized by coefficient γ.
>
> ✅ **Short-channel effect (SCE)** ↓V<sub>th</sub> with L due to charge sharing — begins at L ~ 1 µm.
>
> ✅ **Narrow-width effect (NWE)** ↑V<sub>th</sub> with 1/W due to fringing fields at channel edges.
>
> ✅ **DIBL** ↓V<sub>th</sub> with V<sub>DS</sub>; dominant in sub-100 nm devices.
>
> ✅ **Hot carriers** are electrons/holes with energy >> thermal; created by high lateral field near drain.
>
> ✅ **Substrate current I<sub>sub</sub>** is the HCE monitor; peaks at V<sub>GS</sub> ≈ V<sub>DS</sub>/2.
>
> ✅ **Substrate current model:** I<sub>sub</sub> = C₁·I<sub>D</sub>·V<sub>DS</sub>·exp(−B₁/ε<sub>m</sub>) — exponential sensitivity to ε<sub>m</sub>.
>
> ✅ HCE causes **oxide trapping** (ΔV<sub>th</sub>) and **interface states** (Δμ, ΔI<sub>D,sat</sub>).
>
> ✅ Device lifetime follows Arrhenius-like law: τ ∝ exp(B₂/ε<sub>m</sub>); small V<sub>DD</sub> reduction gives huge lifetime gain.
>
> ✅ Physical constants to memorize: q, kT/q, ε<sub>si</sub>, ε<sub>ox</sub>, φ<sub>F</sub> formula.
>
> ✅ KTU-favorite derivations: (i) V<sub>th</sub> with body effect, (ii) DIBL approximation, (iii) I<sub>sub</sub> model.
>
> ✅ Drawing the **V<sub>th</sub> roll-off curve** vs L is a common 7-mark question — practice it.
>
> ✅ Worst-case HCE condition: V<sub>GS</sub> = V<sub>DS</sub>/2, at maximum V<sub>DS</sub>.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MESFET and FinFET: Structure, Operation and Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT302 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MESFET and FinFET: Structure, Operation and Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 MESFET — Metal Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **MESFET** (Metal–Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor) is a unipolar field-effect transistor that uses a **Schottky barrier gate** (metal–semiconductor junction) to control the conduction channel, in place of the conventional MOS (Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor) gate structure. It is fabricated almost exclusively on **III–V compound semiconductors** such as **GaAs (Gallium Arsenide)** and **InP (Indium Phosphide)**, where the absence of a stable native oxide rules out the standard MOSFET construction.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose Valve" Model

Imagine a flexible garden hose carrying water (the **electrons** in the channel). Instead of pinching the hose with your fingers, you wrap a stiff metal collar (the **Schottky gate**) around it. By tightening the collar, you constrict the cross-section of the hose, thereby reducing the flow of water (the **drain current**). A negative voltage on the collar squeezes the channel shut (depletion mode), while a positive voltage releases it (enhancement). This is precisely how the MESFET modulates current — the reverse-biased Schottky junction expands a depletion region that "squeezes" the conducting channel.

### Key Structural Features

- **Channel:** A thin n-type epitaxial layer grown on a semi-insulating GaAs substrate.
- **Schottky Gate:** A metal contact (e.g., Ti/Pt/Au) forming a rectifying junction with the semiconductor.
- **Source & Drain:** Ohmic contacts at the two ends of the channel.
- **Substrate:** Semi-insulating GaAs, which provides excellent electrical isolation and minimizes parasitic capacitance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GaAs?** GaAs has an electron mobility of approximately **8500 cm²/V·s**, nearly **6× higher** than Si (≈ 1400 cm²/V·s). This makes MESFETs exceptionally fast, with cutoff frequencies well into the **GHz range**.

---

## 1.2 FinFET — Fin-Shaped Field Effect Transistor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **FinFET** is a multi-gate, non-planar MOSFET in which the conducting channel is fashioned as a thin vertical "fin" of silicon (or another semiconductor) that is wrapped on multiple sides by an insulated gate electrode. This 3D gate-all-around (or tri-gate) geometry provides superior electrostatic control of the channel, allowing the gate length to be aggressively scaled down to the **sub-22 nm regime** while suppressing the deleterious short-channel effects (SCEs) that plague planar CMOS.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Vertical Flag on a Pole" Model

Picture a tall, thin silicon "fin" rising vertically from the substrate like a flagpole. The gate, which is wrapped around this fin like a flag wrapped around a pole, controls the channel from **both vertical faces and the top** (in a tri-gate) or from **all sides** (in a true gate-all-around or GAA device). The thin fin body (typically **10–20 nm wide**) ensures that the gate's electric field fully depletes the channel, providing a "lever" of electrostatic control that the old planar MOSFET could never achieve.

### Key Structural Features

- **Fin Body:** Thin vertical silicon fin, the channel itself.
- **Gate:** Wraps around the fin (tri-gate or double-gate geometry).
- **Fin Width (W_fin):** Determines the effective channel width; smaller is better for SCE suppression.
- **Fin Height (H_fin):** Typically 30–60 nm; provides the vertical surface for current conduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Impact:** Intel's 22 nm "Ivy Bridge" (2011) and 14 nm, 10 nm, 7 nm, and 5 nm technology nodes all use FinFET (or its successor GAA) architectures, making it the **workhorse of modern nanoscale CMOS**.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | MESFET | FinFET |
|---|---|---|
| **Gate Structure** | Schottky (metal–SC) junction | Insulated MOS gate around a fin |
| **Semiconductor** | GaAs, InP, SiC | Si, SiGe, III–V on Si |
| **Mode of Operation** | Normally-on (depletion) | Normally-off (enhancement) |
| **Channel Geometry** | Planar, lateral | Non-planar, vertical fin |
| **Primary Advantage** | High electron mobility, RF speed | Superior SCE control, scalability |
| **Typical Application** | RF amplifiers, MMICs | Digital CMOS logic, SRAM, SoCs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MESFET — Operation in Detail

### 2.1.1 Working Principle

The MESFET operation rests on the **Schottky barrier** between the metal gate and the n-type channel. Applying a reverse-bias voltage $V_{GS}$ to the gate widens the depletion region beneath the gate, which in turn **reduces the effective channel thickness**. The drain current $I_D$ flowing from drain to source is controlled by the remaining undepleted channel cross-section.

### 2.1.2 Three Operational Regimes

1. **Pinch-off (Saturation) Regime:** When the depletion region extends through the entire channel thickness $a$, the drain current saturates to a near-constant value — analogous to velocity saturation in a MOSFET.
2. **Triode (Linear/Ohmic) Regime:** For small $V_{DS}$, the device behaves like a voltage-controlled resistor.
3. **Cut-off Regime:** When $V_{GS} = V_{bi}$ (built-in potential), the channel is fully depleted and $I_D \approx 0$.

### 2.1.3 Threshold (Pinch-off) Voltage

The voltage at which the depletion region just punches through the channel:

$$V_{TH} = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

Where:
- $V_{bi}$ = built-in potential of the Schottky junction
- $q$ = electronic charge ($1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C)
- $N_D$ = donor concentration (cm$^{-3}$)
- $a$ = channel thickness (m)
- $\varepsilon_s$ = permittivity of the semiconductor

### 2.1.4 Drain Current Model (Junction FET Approximation)

For a uniform-doped MESFET in the saturation region, the drain current is given by Shockley's classic square-law:

$$I_{D,sat} = I_{P} \left[ 1 - 3 \left(\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_{P}}\right) + 2 \left(\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_{P}}\right)^{3/2} \right]$$

with the **pinch-off current**:

$$I_{P} = \frac{q^{2} N_{D}^{2} \mu_n \varepsilon_s Z a^{3}}{3 \varepsilon_s L} = \frac{q N_{D}^{2} \mu_n Z a^{3}}{6 \varepsilon_s L}$$

For the simpler triode (linear) region:

$$I_D \approx \frac{q N_D \mu_n Z a}{L} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_P}} \right] V_{DS}$$

---

## 2.2 FinFET — Operation in Detail

### 2.2.1 Working Principle

The FinFET is a **multi-gate MOSFET** in which the gate voltage induces an inversion layer (or accumulates carriers) along the vertical sidewalls and the top of the fin. The fin's narrow width ensures **volume inversion** and **full depletion**, providing excellent subthreshold swing and minimal drain-induced barrier lowering (DIBL).

### 2.2.2 Effective Channel Width

Because current flows along **both vertical sidewalls** (and optionally the fin top), the effective channel width is:

$$W_{eff} = 2 H_{fin} + W_{fin}$$

Where:
- $H_{fin}$ = fin height
- $W_{fin}$ = fin width

For a tri-gate FinFET, the **top surface** is also gated, so the factor is $2H_{fin} + W_{fin}$.

### 2.2.3 Threshold Voltage Model

A simplified long-channel FinFET threshold voltage:

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{Q_{dep}}{C_{ox}}$$

Where:
- $V_{FB}$ = flat-band voltage
- $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(N_A / n_i)$ = Fermi potential
- $Q_{dep} = q N_A W_{fin}$ = depletion charge per unit length
- $C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ = gate-oxide capacitance per unit area

### 2.2.4 Drain Current (Strong Inversion)

The saturation drain current in a FinFET follows the classical MOS square law, scaled by the effective width:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W_{eff}}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^{2} (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

For **short-channel FinFETs**, velocity saturation causes the current to become more linear in $(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$.

### 2.2.5 Subthreshold Swing

The subthreshold swing of an ideal FinFET approaches the **Boltzmann limit** of **60 mV/decade** at 300 K, with multi-gate geometry pushing it to nearly **65–70 mV/dec** in practice. The general relation is:

$$S = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \left( 1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does FinFET beat planar MOSFET?** The multi-gate geometry drastically increases the gate's electrostatic coupling to the channel, raising $C_{ox}$ relative to $C_{dep}$ and pushing the subthreshold swing closer to its theoretical limit.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table for quick revision before the exam. All quantities are in SI units unless noted.**

| Device | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| MESFET | $V_{TH} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}$ | Pinch-off voltage |
| MESFET | $I_{D,sat} = I_P \left[ 1 - 3 u + 2 u^{3/2} \right]$, $u = \dfrac{V_{bi}-V_{GS}}{V_P}$ | Saturation current |
| MESFET | $I_P = \dfrac{q N_D^{2} \mu_n Z a^{3}}{6 \varepsilon_s L}$ | Pinch-off current |
| MESFET | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{V_{DS}}$ | Transconductance |
| FinFET | $W_{eff} = 2 H_{fin} + W_{fin}$ | Effective channel width |
| FinFET | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W_{eff}}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^{2}$ | Saturation current |
| FinFET | $V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{Q_{dep}}{C_{ox}}$ | Threshold voltage |
| FinFET | $S = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \left( 1 + \dfrac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}} \right)$ | Subthreshold swing |
| Both | $v_{sat} \approx 10^{7}$ cm/s | Saturation velocity (GaAs/Si) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MESFETs** dominate **monolithic microwave integrated circuits (MMICs)** for radar, satellite receivers, and 5G front-end modules because GaAs's high mobility yields **low noise figures (NF < 1 dB at 10 GHz)**.
- **FinFETs** are the **backbone of modern CPUs, GPUs, and mobile SoCs**, enabling billions of transistors per chip at nodes down to **3 nm** with acceptable leakage and dynamic power.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: MESFET Pinch-off Voltage $V_P$

### Given
- Channel thickness: $a$ (m)
- Donor concentration: $N_D$ (m$^{-3}$)
- Built-in potential: $V_{bi}$ (V)
- Semiconductor permittivity: $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ (F/m)

### Step 1 — Depletion Region Width
For a Schottky junction under reverse bias $V_R = V_{bi} - V_{GS}$, the depletion width is:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}}$$

This is derived by solving Poisson's equation in 1D with the boundary condition that the field vanishes at the edge of the depletion region.

### Step 2 — Pinch-off Condition
Pinch-off occurs when the depletion region equals the channel thickness: $W = a$.

$$\sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}} = a$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $V_{GS} = V_{TH}$

Squaring both sides:

$$\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{TH})}{q N_D} = a^{2}$$

Solving for $V_{TH}$:

$$\boxed{V_{TH} = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}}$$

This is the **pinch-off voltage** $V_P$ of the MESFET.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: MESFET Saturation Current $I_{D,sat}$

### Step 1 — Channel Conductance
The conductance of a partially depleted channel of depth $h(x)$ is:

$$G(x) = q N_D \mu_n Z \frac{h(x)}{L}$$

The undepleted depth at position $x$ along the channel is:

$$h(x) = a - W(x) = a \left[ 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V(x)}{V_P}} \right]$$

Here $V(x)$ is the channel potential at position $x$ (varying from 0 at source to $V_{DS}$ at drain).

### Step 2 — Current Continuity
For a constant current along the channel:

$$I_D = q N_D \mu_n Z h(x) \frac{dV(x)}{dx}$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Along the Channel

$$\int_0^{L} I_D \, dx = q N_D \mu_n Z \int_0^{V_{DS}} h(V) \, dV$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D L &= q N_D \mu_n Z \int_0^{V_{DS}} a \left[ 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V}{V_P}} \right] dV
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral
Let $u = \frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V}{V_P}$, so $dV = V_P du$. The limits become $u_1 = \frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_P}$ and $u_2 = \frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V_{DS}}{V_P}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D L &= q N_D \mu_n Z a V_P \int_{u_1}^{u_2} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{u} \right] du \\
&= q N_D \mu_n Z a V_P \left[ u - \frac{2}{3} u^{3/2} \right]_{u_1}^{u_2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Introduce $I_P$
Define the pinch-off current:

$$I_P = \frac{q N_D \mu_n Z a V_P}{3 L} = \frac{q^{2} N_D^{2} \mu_n Z a^{3}}{6 \varepsilon_s L}$$

This follows from $V_P = \dfrac{q N_D a^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}$.

### Step 6 — Final $I_D$ Expression (Triode Region)
Dividing through by $L$ and rearranging:

$$\boxed{I_D = I_P \left[ 3(u_2 - u_1) - 2(u_2^{3/2} - u_1^{3/2}) \right]}$$

### Step 7 — Saturation Limit
In saturation, the channel pinches off at the drain: $u_2 = 1$. Setting $u_2 = 1$:

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = I_P \left[ 3(1 - u_1) - 2(1 - u_1^{3/2}) \right] = I_P \left[ 1 - 3u_1 + 2 u_1^{3/2} \right]}$$

with $u_1 = \dfrac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_P}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: FinFET Effective Width and Aspect Ratio

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup
A single fin of width $W_{fin}$ and height $H_{fin}$ is wrapped by the gate on **two vertical sidewalls** and the **top surface** (in a tri-gate).

### Step 2 — Effective Channel Width
Each sidewall contributes $H_{fin}$, and the top contributes $W_{fin}$:

$$W_{eff} = 2 H_{fin} + W_{fin}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example
For a typical 14 nm node FinFET:
- $H_{fin} = 42$ nm
- $W_{fin} = 8$ nm

$$W_{eff} = 2(42) + 8 = 92 \text{ nm per fin}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** To increase drive current per unit area, modern chips use **multiple parallel fins** ("multi-fin" or "multi-finger" layouts), where $N_{fin}$ fins yield $W_{eff,total} = N_{fin} \cdot (2H_{fin} + W_{fin})$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Subthreshold Swing Reduction in FinFET

### Step 1 — Capacitance Ratio
For a multi-gate device, the gate capacitance per unit length is enhanced because the gate is wrapped around the channel from multiple sides. The total oxide capacitance per unit channel length is:

$$C_{ox,total} = n \cdot \varepsilon_{ox} \cdot \frac{H_{fin}}{t_{ox}}$$

where $n$ is the number of effective gates ($n = 2$ for double-gate, $n = 3$ for tri-gate).

### Step 2 — Depletion Capacitance
The depletion capacitance per unit length is:

$$C_{dep} = \frac{\varepsilon_s}{W_{fin}}$$

### Step 3 — Subthreshold Swing Formula

$$S = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \left( 1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox,total}} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Limiting Behavior
As $C_{ox,total} \gg C_{dep}$ (i.e., the fin is very thin and the oxide is very thin), the swing approaches the **Boltzmann limit**:

$$S \to \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) = 60 \text{ mV/dec at 300 K}$$

For a 14 nm FinFET with $t_{ox} = 1.2$ nm, $H_{fin} = 42$ nm, $W_{fin} = 8$ nm:

$$C_{ox,total} = 2 \cdot \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1.2 \times 10^{-9}} \cdot 42 \times 10^{-9} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}$$

$$C_{dep} = \frac{11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{8 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.29 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}$$

$$S \approx 60 \text{ mV/dec} \times \left( 1 + \frac{1.29}{2.4} \right) \approx 92 \text{ mV/dec}$$

(Real measured values are even better, ~ 70 mV/dec, because of quantum confinement and the additional top-gate capacitance.)

---

## 3.5 Quick-Reference Derivations Matrix

| # | Derivation | Key Assumption | Final Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MESFET $V_P$ | Abrupt junction, $W = a$ at pinch-off | $V_{TH} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}$ |
| 2 | MESFET $I_{D,sat}$ | Gradual-channel, uniform doping | $I_{D,sat} = I_P \left[ 1 - 3u_1 + 2u_1^{3/2} \right]$ |
| 3 | FinFET $W_{eff}$ | Tri-gate, uniform inversion | $W_{eff} = 2H_{fin} + W_{fin}$ |
| 4 | FinFET $S$ | Long-channel, single-domain | $S = 60 \text{ mV/dec} \cdot (1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox})$ |
| 5 | FinFET $I_{D,sat}$ | Square-law, strong inversion | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W_{eff}}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^{2}$ |

## 4.3 Functional Operation Flow — MESFET

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    F --> G[Triode Region: IDS proportional to VDS]

```

```mermaid
    D --> E[Saturation: Velocity Saturation of Carriers in Thin Fin]
    E --> F[Short Channel Effects Suppressed due to Volume Inversion]
    F --> G[Low Leakage OFF State due to Narrow Fin Body]
    G --> H[High Drive Current ON State due to Multiple Parallel Fins]
    H --> I[Ultra-Low Power Digital CMOS Operation]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define MESFET. Why is it preferred over MOSFET for high-frequency applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A MESFET (Metal–Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor) is a unipolar field-effect transistor that uses a **Schottky barrier junction** between a metal gate and a doped semiconductor channel to control the current flow. Unlike a MOSFET, the MESFET has no gate oxide — the gate forms a **rectifying metal–semiconductor contact** directly with the channel.

It is preferred for high-frequency applications because:
1. It is fabricated on **III–V semiconductors** like GaAs, which has **6× higher electron mobility** than Si, enabling faster carrier transit.
2. The absence of a thin gate oxide eliminates oxide-related leakage and trapped charges, giving **lower noise figures**.
3. The semi-insulating GaAs substrate provides **excellent isolation** and **low parasitic capacitance**, extending the cutoff frequency $f_T$ well into the GHz range.

*(Examiner's tip: mention Schottky junction + GaAs mobility + cutoff frequency for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**List any three advantages of FinFET over conventional planar MOSFET.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Superior Short-Channel Effect (SCE) Suppression:** The multi-gate geometry (gate on both sidewalls and top) provides tight electrostatic control, suppressing drain-induced barrier lowering (DIBL) and threshold voltage roll-off.
2. **Higher Drive Current per Unit Area:** The 3D fin structure increases the effective channel width ($W_{eff} = 2H_{fin} + W_{fin}$), boosting ON current without increasing footprint.
3. **Lower Subthreshold Swing:** Approaches the Boltzmann limit of 60 mV/decade, enabling low OFF-state leakage and excellent power efficiency.
4. **Better Scalability:** Allows gate lengths to be aggressively scaled (sub-10 nm) without compromising performance.
5. **Reduced Random Dopant Fluctuation (RDF):** The undoped or lightly doped fin body minimizes $V_{TH}$ variability.

*(Any three advantages carry 1 mark each. The 4th and 5th points are bonus content.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`
**Question A (a) [7 marks]:**
With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **structure and principle of operation of a MESFET**. Derive an expression for the **pinch-off voltage** of a MESFET.

**Model Solution:**

**Structure (2 marks):**
A MESFET consists of:
- A **semi-insulating GaAs substrate**.
- A thin **n-type epitaxial channel layer** of thickness $a$ and donor concentration $N_D$.
- A **Schottky metal gate** (e.g., Ti/Pt/Au) forming a rectifying junction on top of the channel.
- **Ohmic source and drain contacts** at the two ends of the channel.
- Optionally, a **buffer layer** between the channel and the substrate to prevent substrate leakage.

**Operation (2 marks):**
A reverse-bias voltage $V_{GS}$ applied to the gate widens the depletion region beneath it. This reduces the effective conducting cross-section of the channel, thereby modulating the drain current $I_D$. When the depletion region equals the channel thickness, the channel is pinched off and $I_D \approx 0$. The device is therefore a **normally-on (depletion-mode)** device, controlled by a Schottky junction.

**Derivation of Pinch-off Voltage (3 marks):**
The depletion width for a Schottky junction under reverse bias $V_R = V_{bi} - V_{GS}$ is:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}}$$

Pinch-off occurs when $W = a$:

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{TH})}{q N_D}}$$

Squaring and solving:

$$\boxed{V_{TH} = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}}$$

---

**Question A (b) [7 marks]:**
Derive the **drain current expression** for a MESFET in the **saturation region**. State the **advantages and disadvantages** of MESFET.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Following the gradual-channel approximation, the channel depth at position $x$ is:

$$h(x) = a - W(x) = a \left[ 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V(x)}{V_P}} \right]$$

The current continuity gives:

$$I_D = q N_D \mu_n Z h(x) \frac{dV}{dx}$$

Integrating from source ($x=0$, $V=0$) to drain ($x=L$, $V=V_{DS}$):

$$I_D L = q N_D \mu_n Z a \int_0^{V_{DS}} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V}{V_P}} \right] dV$$

Introducing the pinch-off current $I_P = \dfrac{q N_D \mu_n Z a V_P}{3 L} = \dfrac{q^2 N_D^2 \mu_n Z a^3}{6 \varepsilon_s L}$ and the dimensionless variables $u_1 = \dfrac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_P}$, $u_2 = \dfrac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V_{DS}}{V_P}$:

$$I_D = I_P \left[ 3(u_2 - u_1) - 2(u_2^{3/2} - u_1^{3/2}) \right]$$

In saturation, $u_2 = 1$:

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = I_P \left[ 1 - 3u_1 + 2 u_1^{3/2} \right]}$$

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- High electron mobility in GaAs → high speed and low noise.
- No gate oxide → no oxide-trapping issues.
- Semi-insulating substrate → low parasitic capacitance.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Normally-on (depletion mode) — not ideal for low-power digital logic.
- Schottky gate has higher gate leakage than MOS gate.
- Difficult integration with Si CMOS process.
- GaAs is more expensive and fragile than Si.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`
**Question B (a) [7 marks]:**
Explain the **structure and operation of a FinFET** with a suitable diagram. Discuss how the **multi-gate geometry improves short-channel performance**.

**Model Solution:**

**Structure (2 marks):**
A FinFET is a non-planar, multi-gate MOSFET in which a thin vertical silicon **fin** of width $W_{fin}$ and height $H_{fin}$ serves as the channel body. The fin is etched from a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) or bulk-Si substrate. A thin gate dielectric (SiO$_2$ or high-$\kappa$ HfO$_2$) is grown over the fin, and a metal gate electrode (e.g., TiN, W) wraps around the fin, gating it from the **two sidewalls and the top** (tri-gate) or all around (gate-all-around). Source and drain are formed by epitaxial growth or ion implantation at the fin ends.

**Operation (2 marks):**
Applying $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ inverts the silicon surface along the fin sidewalls (and top), forming a continuous conducting channel. Current flows from drain to source along the fin. The narrow fin body ensures the gate's electric field fully depletes the channel, providing strong electrostatic control and a steep subthreshold slope.

**Multi-gate advantage for SCEs (3 marks):**
1. **Enhanced gate coupling:** Multiple gates raise $C_{ox}$ relative to $C_{dep}$, lowering the subthreshold swing toward the 60 mV/dec limit.
2. **Volume inversion:** The thin fin body becomes fully depleted; the inversion layer is no longer confined to the surface, improving carrier mobility.
3. **Suppressed DIBL and $V_{TH}$ roll-off:** Better gate-to-channel coupling means the drain field cannot easily penetrate to the source, reducing short-channel leakage.
4. **Better $V_{TH}$ control:** The threshold voltage is set primarily by the fin geometry (work-function engineering) rather than random channel doping, reducing variability.

---

**Question B (b) [7 marks]:**
Derive the **effective channel width** and the **drain current in saturation** for a FinFET. Compare the **subthreshold swing** of FinFET and planar MOSFET.

**Model Solution:**

**Effective Width (2 marks):**
For a tri-gate FinFET, the gate controls current on both sidewalls (each contributing $H_{fin}$) and the top surface (contributing $W_{fin}$):

$$\boxed{W_{eff} = 2 H_{fin} + W_{fin}}$$

For $N_{fin}$ parallel fins, the total effective width is $W_{eff,total} = N_{fin} (2H_{fin} + W_{fin})$.

**Saturation Drain Current (3 marks):**
The FinFET follows the standard MOS square-law in strong inversion:

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W_{eff}}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^{2} (1 + \lambda V_{DS})}$$

The effective width $W_{eff}$ multiplies the per-unit-width current, dramatically boosting drive current per unit footprint.

**Subthreshold Swing Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Planar MOSFET | FinFET |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Single-gate | Multi-gate (2 or 3) |
| $C_{ox}/C_{dep}$ ratio | Limited by single gate | Greatly enhanced |
| Typical Swing | 90–110 mV/dec | 65–75 mV/dec |
| Limit | ~ 90 mV/dec at 22 nm | Approaches 60 mV/dec |

The subthreshold swing formula in both cases is $S = (kT/q) \ln(10) \left[ 1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox} \right]$, but the FinFET's $C_{ox}$ is multiplied by 2 or 3, pushing $S$ much closer to the Boltzmann limit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this topic (examiner feedback patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting the built-in potential $V_{bi}$** in the MESFET threshold voltage formula — students often write only $-qN_D a^2 / 2\varepsilon_s$ and miss the $V_{bi}$ term. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing pinch-off voltage $V_P$ with threshold voltage $V_{TH}$** — in MESFET, the gate-bias that just pinches off the channel at the **source end** is $V_{TH}$, while $V_P$ is the drain-bias that pinches off the **drain end**. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Drawing the FinFET as a planar device** — examiners specifically look for the **vertical fin and gate wrapping around it**. A planar diagram loses **at least 2 marks** in a structure question.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $W_{eff}$** — the most common FinFET error. Always write $2H_{fin} + W_{fin}$, not $H_{fin} + W_{fin}$. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Omitting units and sign conventions** in derivations — even a correct formula can lose marks if the units of $N_D$ (m$^{-3}$ vs cm$^{-3}$) are inconsistent. **Cost: 0.5–1 mark.**
> 6. **Writing $g_m$ without the operating-point condition** — $g_m$ is a partial derivative at constant $V_{DS}$. Always specify. **Cost: 0.5 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MESFET** = Metal–Semiconductor FET with a **Schottky gate** on a doped channel (usually GaAs); a **normally-on**, depletion-mode device used in **RF and microwave** circuits.
- **FinFET** = Non-planar, multi-gate MOSFET with a **vertical silicon fin** wrapped by the gate; a **normally-off**, enhancement-mode device used in **nanoscale digital CMOS**.
- **MESFET Pinch-off Voltage:** $V_{TH} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$ — set by the channel thickness $a$ and donor density $N_D$.
- **MESFET Saturation Current:** $I_{D,sat} = I_P [1 - 3u_1 + 2u_1^{3/2}]$, with $I_P = \dfrac{q^2 N_D^2 \mu_n Z a^3}{6 \varepsilon_s L}$ — proportional to $a^3$ and $N_D^2$.
- **FinFET Effective Width:** $W_{eff} = 2H_{fin} + W_{fin}$ — both sidewalls and the fin top conduct current in parallel.
- **FinFET Saturation Current:** $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W_{eff}}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ — multiplied across multiple fins for higher drive.
- **Subthreshold Swing:** $S = 60 \text{ mV/dec} \cdot (1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox})$ — multi-gate geometry reduces $C_{dep}/C_{ox}$, pushing $S$ toward the **Boltzmann limit of 60 mV/dec**.
- **Why MESFET is fast:** GaAs electron mobility ≈ **8500 cm²/V·s** (vs 1400 for Si) → high $f_T$, low noise figure.
- **Why FinFET scales:** Excellent electrostatic control, volume inversion, low SCE, low $V_{TH}$ variability.
- **KTU must-know units:** $N_D$ in m$^{-3}$, $a$ in m, $\varepsilon_s$ in F/m, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $kT/q = 25.85$ mV at 300 K.
- **KTU exam hot keywords:** Schottky barrier, depletion region, pinch-off, gradual channel, normally-on, gate-all-around, DIBL, subthreshold swing, multi-fin layout, high-$\kappa$ metal gate.
